The user approved, and the archive.rs core module, parser.rs integration, background loop triggering, and SSH device support were implemented. All 321 tests passed.)
✅ Diagnosing and Fixing GLM/DeepSeek Pricing Display Bugs

The user reported GLM5.1 and DeepSeek-Reasoner displaying $0 despite OpenRouter support. The pricing lookup chain was traced through pricing.rs/litellm.rs/openrouter.rs, and the root cause was identified as the PricingCache lacking a version field, allowing old cached data (before OpenRouter) to remain valid for 7 days, preventing new data from taking effect. A version field with CACHE_VERSION=2 was added to automatically invalidate old cached data on the next startup.)
✅ Optimizing Archive Implementation Using /optimize Skill

After compression, the newly implemented archive function was tested using the /optimize skill. Five optimizations were applied: extracting frontier checks into ArchiveFrontier::is_up_to_date() (eliminating approximately 40 lines of duplication), adding boundary checks for string slicing, refactoring 3 match patterns to let…else, adding timestamp parsing failure logs, and pre-allocating collections. All 325 tests passed, with zero clippy warnings.)

summary: 跨四台设备的高强度技术工作日：完成BetterSSH全栈项目从零到生产、优化多项目开发工具链、深度重构MimicGen增广系统、构建NeurIPS学术写作框架、扩展语音识别后端
title: Bug Journal 2026-04-11

content:
Daily Report — 2026-04-10 Daily Overview Completed Work: Completed strategic planning (MIHD paper framework, 367-line blueprint), addressed critical infrastructure issues (Screenpipe version compatibility, LIBERO data conversion errors, NaN training loss problems), implemented production features (OpenClaw Sprint 2+3, including 22 code review fixes), and optimized monitoring tools (TokenMonitor pricing unification, OpenRouter API integration).
Completion Method: Used strategic approaches: parallel agents for large-scale documentation and research; analyzed data pipeline issues through root cause analysis; conducted hierarchical code reviews (from severe to moderate to minor); reverse engineered Claude Code’s internal logic to improve pricing accuracy; integrated multi-source APIs and established rollback layers.
Impact: The MIHD project has evolved from a conceptual stage to an executable blueprint with full reproducibility. MeetingHelper and LifeCopilot are ready for production use after comprehensive testing. LIBERO and battery training pipelines have been resolved. TokenMonitor’s cost calculations are consistent with Claude Code’s, while the model coverage has increased from 2500 to 3000+.
DCC
Completed Work: MIHD benchmark paper strategic planning: framework design (targeted as Nature Methods Analysis, defining 4 tasks without tag terms) and complete blueprint documentation (367 lines → 2246 lines, covering paper plan, architecture, API specifications).
Completion Method: Through multiple rounds of strategic discussions, identified core arguments (frozen FM + unsupervised fusion paradigm evaluation) → wrote plans.md file → used ccplan skills and 3 parallel agents (paper plan, repository blueprint, module specifications) for detailed expansion.
Impact: Established a complete technical and narrative foundation for the MIHD paper, resolved ambiguity in positioning, and created a reproducible one-to-one implementation guide.
MacBook
Completed Work: Deployment and troubleshooting of MeetingHelper: fixed Screenpipe 0.2.13 model loading failure (Protobuf error), installed relevant skills, synchronized error_recovery data from tianhe (196MB), and optimized journal club markdown format.
Completion Method: Diagnosed that Homebrew Screenpipe was obsolete → uninstalled and switched to npx; downloaded SCP from tianhe; cleaned up markdown structure.
Impact: Resolved Screenpipe compatibility issues, established a reliable meeting recording environment, and completed cross-cluster data synchronization.
TzJsDesktop
Completed Work: Three main projects: (1) journal club research on 3 space transcriptomics papers; (2) implementation of OpenClaw Sprint 2+3 and comprehensive code review fixes (22 issues); (3) optimization of TokenMonitor (7 errors), CI fixes, release v0.7.2, and integration of pricing system with OpenRouter API.
Completion Method: Parallel agent research for paper analysis; systematic code reviews → step-by-step verification of repair processes; initialized git repository for meeting assistance tools; reverse engineered Claude Code’s/cost implementation; multi-source pricing acquisition, prioritizing LiteLLM merging.
Impact: Journal club materials are ready for comparative analysis; LifeCopilot meets production quality, 70/70 test passes, no technical debt issues; meeting assistance tools are open-sourced on GitHub; TokenMonitor’s costs are consistent with Claude Code’s, with global model coverage including Chinese and Western ecosystems.
tianhe
Completed Work: Data pipeline debugging and training setup: (1) fixed observation.dones type error in LIBERO→LeRobot conversion; (2) verified WHU classification dataset’s unlabeled pixel processing; (3) installed 113 academic writing skills; (4) prepared battery datasets with uniform time sampling; (5) debugged NaN training loss issues; (6) implemented comprehensive training logging.
Completion Method: In-depth analysis of LeRobot 0.4.0’s feature mapping mechanism, inserted.item() conversion; confirmed ignore_index=-1 correctness throughout the pipeline; configured network agents to access GitHub; applied linear interpolation for uniform sampling; tracked NaN propagation through StandardScaler to identify corrupted data; integrated Python logging module.
Impact: Resolved LIBERO data conversion pipeline issues, ensured correct type handling; verified training logic; deployed complete academic writing toolchain; generated clean 221K line battery datasets with continuous experimental tracking for reproducibility.
Multi-project development day: completed MIHD benchmark paper blueprint, resolved MeetingHelper and Screenpipe compatibility issues, debugged LIBERO/battery training pipelines, implemented OpenClaw functions, no technical debt issues, improved TokenMonitor pricing accuracy, making it consistent with Claude Code
Tasks Architecture and Strategy - ✅ MIHD benchmark test paper framework design and complete documentation—defined the paper’s positioning (Nature Methods Analysis, frozen FM algorithm + unsupervised fusion paradigm evaluation), established 4 core tasks, selected tagless terminology, and expanded the plan document from 367 lines to 2246 lines, using a parallel agent strategy, covering paper plan, code architecture, module API references, configuration patterns, data formats, and reproducibility guidelines. ✅ Fixing Observation.dones Type Error in LIBERO→LeRobot—resolved type conflicts in LeRobot 0.4.0, where features shaped (1,) were mapped to scalar values, but validation required ndarray. This was solved by inserting.item() conversion before save_episode(). ✅ Unifying Battery Dataset Time Sampling and NaN Loss Debugging—generated unified sampled datasets (4 points per cycle obtained through linear interpolation), identified erroneous rows causing NaN propagation in StandardScaler (Battery 31, 833 cycles), removed these erroneous rows, resulting in a clean 221K line dataset. ✅ Implementation of OpenClaw Sprint 2+3 and Comprehensive Code Review Fixes—implemented data lifecycle management, session persistence, thinking search, dream service, and circuit breaker functions. Fixed 22 code review issues (4 severe, 7 moderate, 8 minor), including concurrency issues, outdated APIs, Pydantic v2 migration, etc. All 70 tests passed. ✅ TokenMonitor Code Optimization and Pricing Unification—resolved 7 performance/type safety issues (redundant cloning, unnecessary parsing). Unified cache layer pricing to match Claude Code’s (1.25 times rate), added network search tracking (0.01 USD per request), integrated OpenRouter API for comprehensive coverage of over 3000 models (including 92 Chinese models), and implemented 7-day auto-refresh function. ✅ MeetingHelper Screenpipe Compatibility Fix—diagnosed and resolved Screenpipe 0.2.13 (Homebrew) model loading failure (Protobuf parsing error). Identified outdated version status, uninstalled Homebrew version, cleaned model cache, switched to npx solution, and updated all documentation. ✅ Journal Club Research on 3 Space Transcriptomics Papers—used parallel agents to obtain and analyze papers from STAIG (Nature Commun), QueST (arXiv), and Loki (Nature Methods). Generated comparative analysis reports covering graph contrast learning development (node-level → subgraph-level → cross-modal). ✅ Academic Writing Skill Installation (113 Skills)—installed AI-research-SKILLs (94 skills), humanizer (1 skill), and Anthropics official skills (18 skills) on Tianjin cluster. Configured network agents to solve GitHub access issues, created comprehensive user documentation. ✅ GitHub Release of MeetingHelper—applied 6 optimization measures (MCP tool, Windows health check script, bash reliability, placeholder URL, .gitignore, mkdir command), initialized git repository, submitted 12 files, pushed to TzJ2006/MeetingHelper, and created a 450-line tutorial. ✅ Release of TokenMonitor v0.7.2—resolved CI failure issues (clippy warning, cargo fmt), merged feature branches, manually upgraded version (Windows release script failed), created tags, pushed to GitHub, and updated PR #5 Implementation and Fixes ✅ Implementation of Battery Training Logging System—integrated Python logging module, continuously recording experimental parameters, metrics at each stage (training/test loss, learning rate), early stopping information, and final test results. These records are stored in train.log file under checkpoint directory. ✅ Verification of WHU Classification Dataset—verified end-to-end processing of unlabeled pixels (-1) and invalid categories (17, 19): confirmed remap_labels function can handle -1 uniformly, CrossEntropyLoss can correctly ignore these categories, and genConfusionMatrix can properly mask these categories. ✅ Cross-Cluster Synchronization of error_recovery Data—downloaded recovery_collection_portable.tar.gz (196MB) from tianhe to MacBook via SCP, replaced the old version, extracted and recorded structural changes. ✅ Local Installation of Gadget Skills—copied 5 skills (ccplan, summarize, optimize, cchypothesis, slurm-gpu) from gadget/skills/ to ~/.claude/skills/. Problems and Solutions Key Issues 1. Save_episode Error in LeRobot 0.4.0: observation.dones field shape is (1,), which can only be converted to Python scalar Solution: The root cause is LeRobot’s get_hf_features_from_features() method mapping data shaped (1,) to scalar values, while validate_frame() requires ndarray. A loop was added before save_episode() to convert all dones arrays to Python integers.
Key Insight: When framework verification and coding logic conflict, the best insertion point is in the buffer between them, rather than modifying feature definitions or bypassing verification.
2. “Protobuf Parsing Failure” Error When Starting Screenpipe 0.2.13, Loading Wespeaker_en_voxceleb_CAM++.onnx Model Solution: Discovered that the Homebrew version was outdated and unable to build. Uninstalled the version, deleted corrupted model cache (~/Library/Caches/screenpipe/models/), switched to npx screenpipe@latest or official desktop application, and updated all documentation.
Key Insight: An outdated Homebrew formula is a serious issue. When encountering underlying dependency problems, check the upstream maintenance status instead of debugging outdated versions.
3. Despite Normal Data, Battery Training Losses Became NaN From First Iteration Solution: Systematically traced the entire process and found that due to corrupted rows (Battery 31, 833 loops), there were 4 NaN values in the loop columns. StandardScaler calculated the mean and standard deviation of NaN values, causing all scaled values to become NaN. This corrupted row was removed.
Key Insight: NaN values propagate through normalization: a single NaN in training data can disrupt the statistical properties of StandardScaler, causing all subsequent scaled values to become NaN. Ensure data cleanliness before normalization operations.
4. TokenMonitor Costs Not Consistent with Claude Code’s/cost Output Solution: Found that TokenMonitor uses 2.0 times the 1-hour cache token rate, while Claude Code uses a uniform 1.25 times rate. Changed the pricing of cache_write_1h in pricing.rs to match Claude Code’s.
Key Insight: Claude Code’s/cost intentionally simplifies pricing (uniform rate), although the API provides detailed segmentation. The simplicity of the UI justifies the aggregation of data—not every field needs to be displayed.
5. CircuitBreaker HALF_OPEN Concurrency Issues: Outside lock checks allow multiple programs to enter simultaneously Solution: Moved state checks into the “async with self._lock” block to ensure atomicity of state checks.
Key Insight: Concurrent state machines must execute state checks and transitions within the same lock. Any outside lock checks will introduce a window of concurrency.
6. MIHD Paper Positioning Unclear—Initially defined as “Fusion Strategy Benchmark,” core contribution description not clear Solution: Relocated to “Frozen FM + Unsupervised Fusion Paradigm Evaluation,” answering whether this approach can replace fine-tuning methods. The narrative shifted from method proposal to system boundary exploration.
Key Insight: The benchmark paper evaluates the paradigm, not the specific method. The core value lies in system boundary exploration, not claims of cutting-edge technology. Precise problem definition determines the correct narrative framework.
7. LiteLLM Pricing Database Lacks Major Chinese AI Models (GLM, Qwen, DeepSeek)—There is a systemic gap in the Chinese AI ecosystem Solution: Merged OpenRouter API (including 92 Chinese model information) with LiteLLM, providing simultaneous coverage: OpenRouter provides basic coverage, LiteLLM covers when available. Added 7-day auto-refresh function.
Key Insight: Global AI pricing requires multi-source strategies and considers geographical diversity. Western databases systematically lack Chinese models; Chinese sources also lack Western models. Redundancy is necessary rather than optional.
8. Initial Uniform Sampling Used Fixed 0-20 Second Range, Resulting in Invalid IR Values (16, 15, 14…), Instead of Expected 0-0.024 Range Solution: Changed strategy: interpolated within the actual price time range for each cycle (usually 10-13 seconds), avoiding forcing data into a fixed global range. This avoids extrapolating beyond observed data points.
Key Insight: Interpolation and extrapolation: Forcing data into a fixed global range beyond observed data points leads to invalid values. Use sample-adaptive ranges to maintain interpolation accuracy.
9. The Term “Zero Sample” Can Be Misunderstood—STAIG Fusion Training Spent 550 Cycles, If Really “Zero Sample,” Reviewers Would Raise Questions Solution: Changed to “No Tags/Annotations,” emphasizing the lack of manual annotation required. Clearly stated that “zero sample” refers to the encoder level (frozen FM), while fusion is unsupervised, using spatial coordinates as free signals.
Key Insight: Precise terminology avoids confusion among reviewers. Hierarchical definition (frozen encoder + unsupervised fusion) avoids ambiguity and highlights the actual value (no annotation costs).
10. GitHub Access Failure on Tianjin Cluster: “Unable to Connect to 172.16.31.200 Port 3138: Host Not Found” Solution: Diagnosed that the proxy configuration was outdated (172.16.31.200). User provided “source /app/bin/proxy.sh” command. Updated git config http/https.proxy to new proxy address (99.72.0.200:3138), npx openskills installation succeeded.
Key Insight: High-performance cluster network proxies often change. When encountering network errors, verify the validity of the proxy (env | grep proxy, git config –list), rather than assuming the configuration is correct.
Human-AI Methodology Strategic Level Strategic Repositioning of MIHD Paper Role Method Human The user directly pointed out: “Since FM is already so powerful, can we use zero-sample FM to do what requires fine-tuning?"—immediately established core value proposition and 4 specific tasks AI The AI initially followed the existing draft structure (“Fusion Strategy Benchmark”), focusing on technical details rather than strategic positioning. Adjusted based on user feedback Difference Analysis: Humans excel at high-level narration and problem definition—the user’s direct questions clearly revealed the paper’s core arguments. The AI initially focused too much on technical execution details, rather than strategic positioning, demonstrating humans’ advantage in high-level storytelling.Difference Analysis: Users were able to identify that the problem might lie in an earlier version, rather than configuration issues. Artificial intelligence focuses too much on technical fixes within the existing environment. This reflects human sensitivity to maintaining the state of ecosystems, while artificial intelligence pays too much attention to technical details.
Code Review Fix Priority Strategy Role Method Human Requires all issues to be fixed at once Artificial Intelligence Adopts a phased verification approach: CRITICAL→Verification Test→HIGH→Verification→MEDIUM→Verification→LOW, thereby reducing the risk of regression Difference Analysis: Artificial intelligence adopted a defensive layered strategy to avoid cascading failures when fixing 22 issues in 11 files.
Parallelization Strategy for Large Document Generation Role Method Human When encountering difficulties, users immediately suggest “breaking it down into smaller parts and processing them in parallel” Artificial Intelligence Attempts to complete it in a single thread, but hesitates when dealing with a document of 2246 lines Difference Analysis: Humans were able to identify opportunities for parallelization faster. Artificial intelligence knows about parallel tools, but tends to think sequentially in practice and requires clear user guidance to utilize concurrency.
Clarification of Data Structure Requirements for Uniform Sampling Role Method Human Users intuitively want to “flatten” data into a sequence, implicitly understanding that existing data loaders can handle simple row-order data without code changes Artificial Intelligence Initially proposed retaining a time structure of 41 points per cycle (3D: battery × cycle × price time), which could retain more information, but required modifying the architecture of the data loader Difference Analysis: Humans have an intuitive understanding of system constraints (data loaders expect 2D table data). Artificial intelligence initially overlooked this limitation, and the proposed solution required significant code changes. After clarification through the AskUserQuestion, artificial intelligence adopted a simpler and more practical method based on existing infrastructure.
Prioritizing Cost Accuracy Over Feature Integrity Role Method Human Users immediately demanded verification that costs matched exactly with the /cost command, willing to skip UI improvements (selecting the “Data Pipeline Only” option) to ensure correctness first Artificial Intelligence Initially proposed a comprehensive plan including displaying cache levels. Only after the user explicitly selected this option was it adjusted to only data range Difference Analysis: Humans exhibit a strong instinct to “ensure correctness before pursuing aesthetics,” while artificial intelligence defaults to a feature-complete implementation. The user’s judgment avoided expanding the scope.
Limitations of Artificial Intelligence Key Limitations - LeRobot Type Problem Solving: Attempted 5 different type conversions (int→np.int64→np.asarray(0-dim)→np.asarray(1-dim)→.item()) and failed to find contradictions directly by analyzing the validate_frame and get_hf_features_from_features source code during the first analysis. Insufficient sensitivity to strategic problem definition - Initially followed the existing MIHD draft structure without questioning “what is the core story,” requiring the user to directly specify “What I really want to do…” to re-establish the correct narrative direction. In the Screenpipe issue, spent a lot of time trying to replace model files and data type conversion solutions, failing to recognize early on that the Homebrew formula was no longer in use (although indicated in the brew information output), leading to multiple ineffective attempts. Before proposing a public 50,000/t-hour cache token, did not actively investigate whether both tokens were used simultaneously in actual logs. Only after the user asked did they check the actual usage pattern. Initially misunderstood the structure requirements of the battery dataset, proposing to retain 41 time points per cycle as a high-dimensional input, while the user wanted simple row-level flattening. A clear AskUserQuestion clarification was needed to understand this limitation. When studying the sources of pricing, did not immediately integrate a multi-source merging strategy. Clear instructions from the user were needed to design a backup architecture prioritizing LiteLLM. General Limitations Lack of active parallelization thinking in large-scale tasks - Tried using a single-threaded method to generate a 2246-line document until it was clearly suggested to parallelize it. Unable to independently discover cluster-specific operation specifications (such as /app/bin/proxy.sh), relying on users to provide this implicit knowledge. Learning Outcomes Key Learning Outcomes In LeRobot 0.4.0, there are contradictions in the shape=(1,) feature processing design: validate_frame requires ndarray, while get_hf_features_from_features maps it to scalar values. The solution is to insert a type conversion layer between validation and encoding. NaN propagation mechanism of StandardScaler: Any row containing NaN in any feature column will cause StandardScaler.fit() to calculate NaN statistics (mean/stddev), and propagate this to all scaled data through (x - NaN) / NaN = NaN. It is necessary to verify for NaN/Inf situations before normalization. Benchmark papers evaluate paradigms rather than methods - Papers in Nature Methods Analysis do not require SOTA, but need systematic answers to “how far this method can go.” The narrative shifts from “Our method is the best” to “What are the boundaries and bottlenecks of this paradigm?” The fact that Homebrew formula is no longer in use is an important signal - When encountering low-level dependency issues, one should first check the maintenance status of upstream versions instead of debugging deprecated versions. Development in graph contrast learning in spatial transcriptomics: From STAIG (node-level) to QueST (subgraph-level), then to Loki (cross-modal), showing a trend from “one method per problem” to “one base model applicable to multiple tasks.” Hierarchical repair scheme for code review: From CRITICAL (safe/competitive) to HIGH (deprecated APIs/private access), then to MEDIUM (types/styles), and finally to LOW (code inspectors). Testing and verification are required after each level to avoid cascading failures. Correctness invariant of Asycio state machines: State checks and transitions must be atomic within the same lock region; any checks outside the lock region will introduce a race condition window. Global AI pricing needs a multi-source strategy with geographical diversity - Western databases like LiteLLM systematically lack Chinese models; Chinese data sources also lack Western models. Redundancy is essential, not optional. Performance patterns of Rust: Avoid unnecessary clone(), use borrowing structures for serialization, prioritize line counting over full deserialization when only metadata is needed. Term selection is important: No-label vs zero-shot - “Zero-shot” can be misinterpreted as “no training at all,” while no-label/no-annotation accurately conveys the core advantage of “no need for manual annotation.” Hierarchical definitions (zero-shot encoder + fusion self-supervised) can avoid ambiguity. Parallel proxy strategies significantly accelerate the generation of large-scale documents - Dividing the 2246-line document into 3 independent sections (paper plan, repository structure, module specifications), each section generated and assembled by a separate proxy. This also accelerated the research: 4 proxies completed in 14 seconds, while sequential processing took 45-60 seconds. “Free signal” framework of self-supervised learning - Spatial transcriptomics coordinates are inherent structured information of the Visium chip. Treating spatial graphs as “free supervision signals” clarifies the value of no-label data and enhances its usefulness (without additional annotation costs). Interpolation and extrapolation in time resampling: Linear interpolation should remain within the range of observed data. Forcing data into a fixed global range will lead to invalid values in extrapolation. Using per-sample adaptive ranges can avoid artifacts caused by extrapolation. End-to-end verification is required for the correctness of multi-stage data processing - Special values like -1 must be consistent across all stages (preprocessing → dataset → loss function → evaluation). In Claude Code, /cost intentionally simplifies pricing display (uniform 1.25x cache rate), although the API provides detailed 50,000/t-hour breakdown. The simplicity of the UI justifies data aggregation - not every field needs to be made public. HPC cluster network proxies often change - Establishing standard inspection workflows: env | grep proxy + git config –list + test connection, rather than assuming the correctness of configurations. Migration mode of Pydantic v2: dict→model_dump(), but test simulations must provide the same interface via auxiliary functions like make_settingsMock(). JSONL session logs do not contain all billed attributes - The fast mode level is invisible in logs, although it affects actual costs, limiting which cost tracking tools can reconstruct data from historical data. Practical Learning Outcomes Weekly updates to pricing (7-day validity), combined with background checks, achieved the optimal balance for LLM cost tracking: frequency high enough to capture new models and rate changes, yet low enough to avoid API quota issues. Dialogue Summary MIHD ✅ Benchmark paper framework design and complete blueprint documentation 14:48:32.241 | claude_code Through multiple rounds of discussion, users and AI clarified the core focus of the MIHD paper: shifting from “fusion strategy benchmark” to “frozen FM and self-supervised fusion paradigm evaluation, replacing fine-tuning methods.” Key decisions included adopting the style of Nature Methods Analysis, covering 4 core tasks (DLPFC/HD clustering, cross-sample queries, downstream tasks), using the term “no-label,” and defining 3 levels of self-supervision. Subsequently, using the ccplan skill, the planning document was expanded from 367 lines to 2246 lines, covering paper strategy, code architecture, API signatures, configuration patterns, data formats, etc. The complete technical specifications were implemented to achieve 1:1 reproducibility.
MeetingHelper**✅ Fixed Screenpipe startup timeout issues and deployment problems** 04:43:00.851 | claude_code Due to changes in the Screenpipe 0.3.278 CLI interface, MeetingHelper’s startup script timed out. The screenpipe record subcommand was needed to solve the problem. AI modified the start.sh file and tested it successfully. Later, it was found that Screenpipe v0.2.13 (Homebrew) had a problem with speaker separation models (Protobuf parsing failure). Various solutions were tried, and it was determined that the Homebrew version was outdated. A npx-based startup script was created, all documents were updated, the outdated Homebrew version was uninstalled, and the model cache was cleared. 5 changes and solutions related to this issue were submitted.
Academic Discussion ✅ Journal club research: 3 spatial transcriptomics papers 05:02:21.800 | claude_code Users requested analysis of papers on STAIG (Nature Commun), QueST (arXiv), and Loki (Nature Methods). 4 parallel proxies were initiated to obtain content and explore research tools. A comprehensive journal club report was generated, covering the development of graph learning in spatial transcriptomics: node-level → subgraph-level → cross-modal. Although there was a minor issue with the bash path, causing incorrect directories, the actual report was still correctly placed in the outputs/reports/ directory.
BOSS Data Conversion ✅ Fixed LIBERO→LeRobot observation.dones type error issue 11:53:38.763 | claude_code Users reported a TypeError during the conversion process: “Only 0-dimensional arrays can be converted to Python scalars.” AI identified the root cause: LeRobot 0.4.0 mapped shapes of (1,) to scalar values, but the passed data was np.array([x]). 5 different type conversions were tried, but none worked because of problems with validate_frame or HF encoding. The final solution was to insert a loop before save_episode(), converting all dones arrays to Python integers using item(). Users were a bit impatient during the iteration, but the problem was eventually solved.
Chen Lu Academic Writing Skills ✅ Installed 113 academic writing skills in Tianjin 03:37:44.872 | claude_code Users provided the URL of the awesome-ai-research-writing repository and requested the installation of relevant skills. Due to proxy issues, the initial installation failed (172.16.31.200:3138 was inaccessible). Users provided commands to analyze/app/bin/proxy.sh. AI updated the git proxy to 99.72.0.200:3138. 3 sources were installed using npx openskills: zechenzhangAGI/AI-research-SKILLs (94 skills), blader/humanizer (1 skill), and anthropics/skills (18 skills). A total of 113 skills were installed. A comprehensive usage guide was created, covering research workflows, core skills (ml-paper-writing, academic-plotting, humanizer, doc-coauthoring), along with example prompts and troubleshooting methods.
Crossformer Battery Resistance Prediction ✅ Unified time sampling + NaN loss debugging + logging 02:55:41.738 | claude_code Users needed to train Crossformer on uniformly sampled internal battery resistance data. AI used linear interpolation to generate a unified time-sampled dataset within the actual charging time range. NaN losses occurred during training; AI systematically traced the root cause, finding that a single corrupted row (battery 31, cycle 833) contaminated the StandardScaler statistics. After data correction, users requested comprehensive logging. AI implemented a Python logging module in exp_crossformer.py to record experimental parameters, metrics for each epoch, and test results, saving them to the persistent train.log file.
LifeCopilot ✅ OpenClaw Sprint 2+3 implementation + code review fixes 00:19:42.904 | claude_code Sprint 2 (data lifecycle, session persistence) and Sprint 3 (thought search, dream service, circuit breaker) were implemented. By using the before-action-after pattern, time-sensitive test failures were resolved. The python-reviewer proxy identified 22 issues: 4 CRITICAL (CircuitBreaker competition, private API, ISO time comparison, silent exceptions), 7 HIGH (deprecated get_event_loop, private methods, Pydantic v2), 8 MEDIUM (type annotations, fuzzy matching), 3 LOW (ruff). A hierarchical approach was used to systematically solve these issues, from CRITICAL to HIGH to MEDIUM to LOW, with testing and verification after each step. The final result was 0 errors for ruff, with 70/70 test passes.
meeting-helper ✅ Optimization + GitHub release + tutorial writing 00:56:22.836 | claude_code The project was analyzed, and 6 optimization points were identified (MCP presentation tool missing, Windows health check script, reliability of bash $?), placeholder URLs, .gitignore, mkdir). The user invoked the ccplan, but this was just a direct fix task. All fixes were applied, Git was initialized, 12 files were submitted, and then successfully pushed to TzJ2006/MeetingHelper. A comprehensive 450-line tutorial was generated, covering 15 chapters from installation to advanced use.
TokenMonitor ✅ Unified cache pricing integration with Claude Code + integration with OpenRouter API 00:36:12.391 | claude_code Users requested research on the /cost implementation in Claude Code. AI found that it used local calculations from the API usage fields, and discovered that the 1-hour cache layer of TokenMonitor had a 1.25 times higher usage rate than Claude Code. A data pipeline was implemented to show the breakdown of the cache layer, and the pricing was unified to 1.25 times to match the /cost. A network search tracking function costing $0.01 per request was added. Users also asked about authoritative pricing sources, pointing out that LiteLLM lacked Chinese models. AI conducted research and recommended OpenRouter (350 models, 92 of which were Chinese). The user specified a strategy to prioritize LiteLLM and set up a 7-day auto-refresh. AI implemented the openrouter.rs module, modified litellm.rs to enable concurrent retrieval and merging, and added a background TTL check. Now the pricing covers the global AI ecosystem (more than 3000 models). A PR #5 was created, containing a change set of 12 files.
✅ Code optimization + CI fixes + v0.7.2 release 05:22:50.725 | claude_code The user triggered the /optimize skill. AI identified 7 issues, including TypeScript type mismatches (fileCount→entryCount), redundant HashMap cloning, unnecessary JSON parsing, and lack of a backup plan. All issues were fixed simultaneously. The feat/cache-tier-web-search-pricing was merged into the main code (resolved the conflict between Windows file mode and core.fileMode=false). Clippy warnings (unused variables) and cargo fmt failures in CI were fixed. On the Windows system, the release script failed; manually upgraded the version to 0.7.2, submitted, marked, and pushed to GitHub, updating PR #5.
WHU Classification Dataset**✅ Verifying the handling of unmarked pixels during training** 07:36:33.776 | claude_code The user opened the process_WHU.py file and asked whether unmarked areas (-1) would incorrectly participate in training. The AI tracked the entire process: (1) process_WHU.py only discarded patches entirely composed of {-1,17,19}; (2) WHUDataset.remapLabels mapped -1/17/19 to -1; (3) CrossEntropyLoss(ignore_index=-1) correctly ignored these areas; (4) Training OA calculations included -1 pixels (merely an aesthetic issue). The user also asked about testing scenarios—the AI confirmed that the genConfusionMatrix filter correctly filtered out -1s. The user rejected any fixes to OA calculations.
Token usage Total: $103.74 — 106 million tokens
summary: 多项目开发日：完成了MIHD基准测试论文的撰写工作，解决了MeetingHelper与Screenpipe之间的兼容性问题，调试了LIBERO和电池训练流程，实现了OpenClaw功能，同时避免了技术上的债务问题。此外，还提升了TokenMonitor的定价准确性，以更好地满足Claude的需求。
title: Bug Journal 2026-04-10

content:
Daily Report — 2026-04-09 Daily Overview Completed Work: Work was carried out in parallel across several areas: research computing (robot learning data pipelines), infrastructure maintenance (proxies/permissions/multi-instance isolation), content pipeline conversion (Gadget bilingual architecture), and two major new projects (LifeCopilot architecture upgrade + conference assistance tool creation). Additionally, TokenMonitor was improved. Completion Methods: tianhe: Used h5py for HDF5 validation, implemented Python scripts, and standardized Node environment variables. TzJsDesktop: Adopted a systematic multi-stage refactoring approach, parallelized proxy verification, and used ccplan for architectural planning (completed Phase 0-11 of two workflows). TDD was used for comprehensive testing, Win32 API was used for window operations, and time zone-aware date conversion was implemented. Impact: A manual collection backup plan was established for 626 MimicGen failures; flexible multi-data set contrast learning was achieved; a universal Node CLI proxy solution was developed; Gadget was converted into a bilingual pipeline with English as the primary language; LifeCopilot was equipped with production-level health monitoring and automatic recovery functions (10 new modules added); deployable conference assistance tools were created and integrated with Screenpipe; issues related to time zones and interface flickering in TokenMonitor were resolved. tianhe Completed Work: Analysis of MimicGen results was completed, manual collection workflows were implemented, BOSS multi-data sets were refactored, and issues with Node.js proxy configuration were fixed. Completion Method: Directly used h5py for HDF5 file validation, wrote three Python remote operation collection scripts, modified three training pipeline files to achieve multi-source enhancement, and standardized shell proxy environment variables (case synchronization + setting NODEUSE_ENV_PROXY=1). Impact: A complete manual collection workflow was provided for 626 failed scenarios; flexible sampling of single and dual data sets was achieved; a universal solution was provided for all Node CLI tools that require external API access, accessed through enterprise proxies. TzJsDesktop Completed Work: All sessions on this device (11 in total): Gadget English conversion (divided into four stages and parallel validation), unified GitHub workspace permissions, setting up multi-instance isolation for Claude Code, completing two complete ccplan workflows (LifeCopilot OpenClaw integration + conference assistance tool creation), updating Gadget documentation, debugging TokenMonitor (3 debugs: using Tauri CLI, port settings, time zone settings, animation processing). Completion Method: Adopted a four-stage refactoring approach, parallelized proxy verification, identified 6 remaining problem categories; structured planning using ccplan (research → adversarial review → feasibility assessment → implementation → testing); used Agent tools for parallel code library research; TDD was used for 70 Rust tests and 219 front-end tests; Win32 API was used for hiding/showing functionality to handle WebView2 asynchronous rendering issues; CSS transition time adjustments were analyzed. Impact: The bilingual workflow became more refined (increased international coverage); approximately 200 permission prompts were reduced; GLM and Claude could be used simultaneously; LifeCopilot added 10 new modules (health manager, configuration monitor, data lifecycle management, dream service, circuit breaker, etc.), and passed 70 tests; conference assistance tools achieved end-to-end functionality (configuration, pipeline, MCP, installation script, documentation); TokenMonitor’s time zone function eliminated date boundary issues across time zone-ridden SSH servers; animation fixes improved the aesthetics of the interface. Cross-device full-stack development day: Optimized the robot learning pipeline on tianhe (MimicGen analysis + manual collection workflow + multi-data set contrast learning), resolved infrastructure issues (Node proxy, Claude Code permissions), performed large-scale Gadget refactoring (using Ollama for Chinese to English conversion), completed two major architecture upgrades (LifeCopilot OpenClaw integration, passed 70 tests; developed conference assistance tools from scratch, implemented through Screenpipe and MCP), and enhanced TokenMonitor’s functionality, including time zone-aware SSH handling and interface animation fixes
Tasks Architecture and Strategy - ✅ Reconstruct the gadget summarize module to output in English—Convert the 4 core files (summarizer.py, formatter.py, weekly_summary.py, monthly_summary.py) from Chinese LLM prompts and Markdown rendering to English. All SUMMIARY_PROMPT/CHUNK_MERGE_PROMPT/WEEKLY_SUMMARY_PROMPT/MONTHLY_SUMMARY_PROMPT templates now require English output. ✅ Reconstruct the gadget research module to output in English—Converted 8 files in the scout and profiler subsystems: DEFAULT_LANGUAGE=‘en’, all prompts became English, Markdown rendering tags also changed to English, and label_en attribute was added to ResearcherTier enumeration. ✅ Integration of LifeCopilot OpenClaw core functions—Integrated 7 functions through ccplan workflows: service health monitoring + automatic restart (using health_manager.py and 4-state FSM), CronScheduler concurrency control + replay (task locking + global Semaphore), configuration hot reload (using config_watcher.py and watchfiles), JSONL lifecycle management (using data_lifecycle.py), session history storage (reconstructed through SessionManager), thinking search + dream integration (using DreamingService and InsightStore), circuit breaker mechanism (close → open → half-open). 10 new module files were added, 15 files were modified, and 70 tests passed. ✅ Create a conference assistance AI assistant—Created a complete conference assistant through ccplan’s phases 0-11. Studied existing solutions like Zoom AI, Screenpipe, Meetily, and chose Screenpipe+MCP+Claude Code CLI architecture. Implemented 5 functions: Screenpipe configuration (using Whisper large-v3-turbo+bilingual support+custom vocabulary), custom conference summary Pipe, MCP setup script, CLAUDE.md Q&A system prompts (including 27 MCP tool guides), cross-platform installation script (for MacOS/Windows). Also included a complete ECL planning document. ✅ Implement manual collection workflow scripts—Created three scripts for manual successful demonstration collection: identify_failed_scenes.py (parses logs, outputs 626 scene IDs), collect_success_from_error.py (uses SpaceMouse for remote operations and tracking progress), convert_teleop_to_hdf5.py (converts NPZ to HDF5 and merges them). ✅ Refactor PairedDataset to support multiple enhancement datasets—Modified build_paired_mapping.py (support for multiple inputs and source labels), paired_dataset.py (based on enhancement dataset list and source selection), config.py (support for enhancement dataset ID and source list) to sample from multiple OSS enhancement datasets simultaneously. ✅ Fix Node.js proxy issues—Resolved issues where npx ccusage and all Node CLI tools couldn’t access external data through enterprise proxies. This was because Node 24’s –use-env-proxy only read uppercase HTTP_PROXY/HTTPS_PROXY/NO_PROXY. Modified.bashrc and.bashrcy to synchronize uppercase/lowercase variables and exported NODEUSE_ENV_PROXY=1. ✅ Fix residual Chinese issues in LLM prompts and output—Used 3 parallel verification proxies to identify 6 problem categories: merging prompt upper and lower strings, matplotlib chart labels, DEEP_EVAL comments, overview.md template, JSON fix instructions, configuration initialization prompts. All 6 problem categories were fixed. ✅ Handle time zone issues with SSH remote servers—Implemented time zone-aware date filtering for SSH remote servers. Added TZ_OFFSET detection, local time zone conversion for all date operations, time zone persistence, and UI display functions. Fixed 4 date extraction errors and Codex since filter issues. Added 8 new unit tests. All 307 Rust code and 219 front-end tests passed. ✅ Analyze and verify MimicGen-generated results—Analyzed successful MimicGen demonstrations (1282 successes/953 failures), identified 626 failed scenarios requiring manual collection. Fixed errors in report scripts by directly checking HDF5 files to correctly report pick place/coffee quantities. ✅ Fix visual flickering issues when floating balls collapse—Resolved visual flickering issues when floating balls collapsed on Windows (only ’left’ direction supported). This was caused by asynchronous rendering during SetWindowPos on WebView2. Solution was to hide the window before resizing and show it after resizing. ✅ Fix movement issues in floating ball collapse animations—Resolved issue where floating balls moved from left to right during collapse animation. This was due to the .expanded class being removed in CSS, causing a jump during the 250 ms transition. Solution was to remove the expanded condition from the direction-based justify-content rule. Implementation and Fixes ✅ Optimize code for gadget summarize/research modules—7 optimizations implemented: extracted render_content_list(), removed unnecessary OrderedDict imports, replaced loops and break statements with next(), extracted truncate() and field() helper functions, added load_profile() with error handling, added None protection. Approximately 50 lines of duplicate code were removed. ✅ Unify Claude Code permissions in GitHub workspace settings—Created/updated settings.local.json files for 6 sub-directories to maintain consistent permissions: automatically allowing all built-in tools and common commands, requiring confirmation only for destructive operations like rm/rmdir/del/rd. ✅ Claude Code multi-instance isolation strategy—Provided 3 solutions for running GLM 5.1 and official Claude Code simultaneously: (1) CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR environment variable (recommended); (2) wrapper scripts with hard-coded environment variables; (3) symbolic link switching. This isolated ~/.claude-glm/ and ~/.claude directories. ✅ Update Gadget CLAUDE.md documentation—Updated 5 key sections to reflect the migration to the Ollama translation architecture: environment setup (added Ollama installation), architecture (added common/ollama.py, translation.py, bilingual.py), cross-module dependencies (updated dependency graph), translation architecture (new chapters), testing (added pytest command). ✅ Configure permissions for meeting-helper project—Modified.claude/settings.local.json file, adopting a three-tier permission strategy: automatically allowing over 60 common commands, requiring confirmation for deletion/git push operations, rejecting deletion-rmf/git push–force/git reset–hard/git clean-f operations. ✅ Fix Tauri CLI failure issues in TokenMonitor—Resolved the issue where “tauri” was not recognized due to lack of Windows native binding (@tauri-apps/cli-win32-x64-msvc). Removed node_modules and package-lock.json, reinstalled to trigger correct platform dependency resolution. Issues and Solutions Critical Issues 1. npx ccusage and all Node.js CLI tools cannot access external data, although http_proxy/https_proxy environment variables work properly, and the local proxy runs at 127.0.0.1:10087 Solution: It was found that Node 24’s –use-env-proxy feature only reads uppercase HTTP_PROXY/HTTPS_PROXY/NO_PROXY, ignoring lowercase forms. Modified.bashrc and.bashrcy to export both uppercase and lowercase variables in the proxy_on/net_on functions, and set NODEUSE_ENV_PROXY=1 to enable Node’s native fetch() proxy support.
Key Insight: Node.js 24 and above have an built-in environment variable proxy feature through the –use-env-proxy flag/NODEUSE_ENV_PROXY environment variable, but it strictly requires using uppercase variable names. This is a simpler solution than injecting an undici ProxyAgent or wrapping individual CLI tools, and it applies to all Node CLI tools, not just specific tools.
2. After converting LLM prompts and Markdown rendering to English, 6 types of residual Chinese text were found during verification, which would disrupt the English-first processing flow: LLM prompt upper and lower strings, matplotlib chart labels, DEEP_EVAL comments, project templates, JSON fix instructions, configuration default values Solution: Used 3 parallel verification proxies (one for each module) to systematically find Chinese patterns and verify the consistency of the entire process. All 6 types of issues were fixed in the summary, research, and website modules.
Key Insight: LLM prompt translation not only needs to check the main PROMPT constants, but also needs to check: (1) upper and lower strings injected during execution; (2) non-text outputs (charts); (3) code comments in prompts; (4) generated templates; (5) error recovery prompts. Parallel proxy verification is more thorough than single grep and can detect strings injected at runtime.
3. Remote SSH servers in different time zones caused date filtering errors—The timestamp in the records was 2024-01-01T23:00:00+00:00 (UTC), which would be considered January 1st instead of February 1st when the local time zone was UTC+8. Solution: Added a parse_remote_ts_to_local_date() helper function to convert fixed-offset timestamps to the local time zone before extracting the date. Modified 4 call points to use time zone-aware conversion methods. Fixed the isolation_filter in Codex to include the UTC offset for correct cross-time zone string comparison.
Key Insight: DateTime::date_naive() extracts the date in server time zone, not the local time. It must first be converted to the local time zone using .with_timezone(&chrono::Local) before calling date_naive(). This is important for any distributed system with cross-time zone data.
4. Meeting-helper requires Chinese-English mixed transcription, but Whisper performs language detection for each piece of text (about 30 seconds), resulting in poor transcription quality for sentences like “We need to implement this feature.” Solution: Multi-layer mitigation measures: (1) Add a custom vocabulary to screenpipe-settings.json for common bilingual terms; (2) Add a retranscription workflow in CLAUDE.md to guide users to use bilingual prompts POST /audio/retranscribe API when transcription quality is poor; (3) Add a step 4 in the pipeline to check transcription quality and trigger retranscription for mixed segments; (4) Clearly document this limitation to manage user expectations.
Key Insight: Whisper’s architecture limitations (language detection per piece of text) cannot be fully addressed through model parameters. A more effective strategy is to use a custom vocabulary + secondary transcription + user feedback loop to gradually improve quality, while honestly disclosing limitations in the documentation, rather than making excessive promises.
5. LifeCopilot’s background coordinator manages over 18 backend services, but there is no health check mechanism. When services crash, only errors are recorded, and they do not restart automatically, resulting in some system failures unnoticed by users. Solution: Migrated the health monitoring mode from OpenClaw: created health_manager.py, implemented ServiceHealthManager (4-state FSM: HEALTHY→DEGRADED→FAILED→STOPPED, restart budget of 10 times per hour, exponential backoff time of 1→2→4→8→60 seconds). Modified BackgroundCoordinator to integrate health management: perform health checks every 5 minutes, restart automatically if failed, mark as degraded when budget runs out, and notify via EventBus. Added a GET /api/health endpoint to expose all service statuses.
Key Insight: Migrating from a production-verified open-source project like OpenClaw’s health monitoring is more reliable than designing from scratch. The key is understanding the principles behind the original design (why use 4-state FSM? Why limit the number of restarts? Why use exponential backoff time?) and adapting it to the context of the target project.
6. Floating ball folding animations on Windows have visual flickering issues, but this only occurs when the direction is ’left’ (the ball is on the right side). When the direction is ‘right’, there is no flickering.Solutions: Root cause: SetWindowPos moves and resizes the window simultaneously, but WebView2 rendering is asynchronous. When the ball’s position jumps during resizing (x=96→x=0), there is a 1-2 frame interval where outdated content appears incorrectly cropped. Fix: Use ShowWindow(SW_HIDE) and ShowWindow(SW_SHOWNOACTIVATE) before and after SetWindowPos, so WebView2 does not render intermediate frames. This applies only to collapsed states to minimize side effects. Key insight: The native window geometry change (SetWindowPos) is synchronous, but WebView2’s content rendering is asynchronous. When the left edge of the window moves to the left (during ’left’ collapse), the content coordinate system of WebView changes, causing visual flickering during the 1-2 frame rendering delay. There is no flickering during ‘right’ collapse, as the left edge remains at x=0—only the right edge shrinks.
7. Errors in the reporting script caused pickplace and coffee to read the outdated full_run_200attempts.log file, which showed 398/260 numbers that did not match the actual values of 168/85. Solution: Bypass log parsing and directly use h5py to check the HDF5 file to obtain the actual number of demonstrations.
Key insight: Reports based on logs may deviate from actual data; the HDF5 file is an authoritative source for data verification.
8. The floating ball’s collapse animation causes the ball to move left and then right, instead of staying still. Solution: Root cause: Setting expanded=false immediately removes the .shell.expanded class, causing the CSS justify-content to change from flex-end/flex-start to center. During the 250 milliseconds width transition of the capsule, the ball moves towards the center. Fix: Remove the expanded condition from the direction-based justify-content rules, making them apply throughout the transition.
Key insight: CSS transitions interact with native window size changes. Front-end CSS transitions (250 milliseconds) complete before back-end window size changes (t=260 milliseconds). If CSS rules change immediately based on the expanded state, the ball will float during the transition. Direction-based positioning must remain unchanged throughout the transition to fix the ball’s position.
Methods of Humans and Artificial Intelligence Strategic Level Strategy of Searching First, Then Implementing Role Method Human Users emphasize “searching for existing open-source solutions first” in meeting assistance, and interrupt the AI when it starts需求 analysis: “Can you use websearch to research existing solutions first?” Artificial Intelligence Initially, the AI wanted to directly conduct需求 analysis for ccplan Phase 0, preparing to design the architecture from scratch. After being interrupted, the AI conducted in-depth research on Screenpipe, Meetily, and Natively, and adjusted the direction of the entire architecture (from custom audio capture to Screenpipe+MCP). Difference analysis: Human experience tells us that “don’t reinvent the wheel” is a golden rule in software engineering, preferring to search first → evaluate → reuse. The AI also knows this principle (mentioned in ccplan skills), but tends to have the mindset “I can solve this problem myself” and skip the research phase. Human intervention corrected this tendency, teaching the AI that necessary research must be done first.
Node.js CLI Proxy Repair Strategy | Role | Method | | Human | Users described the issue as “npx ccusage@latest cannot pass through the proxy” and requested a “one-time solution,” possibly considering wrapping the npx command, modifying the ccusage source code, or creating a custom startup script. | | Artificial Intelligence | The AI investigated the entire dependency chain: checked the ccusage source code to confirm it uses the native fetch() function without a custom scheduler, then checked the Node version (24.12.0), found –use-env-proxy support, and noticed that variable requirements must be in uppercase, while users only used lowercase variables. Therefore, a solution was proposed to unify variable casing in proxy helper functions. |
Difference analysis: Humans focused on solutions for specific tools; the AI found the root cause at the Node runtime level, allowing for a universal solution for all Node CLI tools. The AI’s solution is simpler (just modifying 3 lines of shell functions), compared to wrapping or patching individual tools. The key is to check the tool’s dependencies before proposing a solution.
Scope of English translation in content generation process Role Method Human The initial request was: “Modify the JSON report to have generate_markdown() output only the English version, and then translate it into Chinese at the website level.” This indicates that only the Markdown rendering layer needs to be modified. Artificial Intelligence The AI realized that this would require changing the entire LLM prompt chain (not just rendering), and actively checked related modules for similar patterns. During the validation phase, the AI used 3 parallel proxies to find additional Chinese content in 6 other categories (upper and lower text strings, chart labels, templates, etc.), which were not obvious from the initial request. Difference analysis: Humans specified the desired behavior but did not detail all necessary changes; the AI inferred the full technical scope (prompt → rendering → charts → templates → error messages). The AI’s multi-stage approach (translation → validation → fixing remaining issues → optimization) is more thorough than the user’s initial step-by-step requests. This shows that the AI has strong capabilities in dependency analysis and completeness checking.
Realistic limitations of mixed Chinese-English transcription Role Method Human After the meeting assistant v1 was completed, the user asked, “Can you address the key risks?” — specifically pointing out the CRITICAL risks recorded in ECL due to poor sentence-level language conversion quality. Artificial Intelligence The AI was overly optimistic about Whisper large-v3-turbo’s multilingual capabilities during phases 0-9 (DEC-003 confidence = 0.75), thinking it could “solve” this problem. It didn’t take action until the user explicitly asked to “address key risks.” Only then did the AI adopt a more practical mitigation strategy (retranscribing API + custom vocabulary list + documentation). Difference analysis: The AI tends to be technically optimistic (“a better model will solve this problem”), while humans know from experience that some technical limitations are structural (each language detection in Whisper cannot be changed by adjustment). Human intervention forced the AI to shift from a “problem-solving” mindset to one of “mitigating risks + managing expectations,” which is more mature for dealing with inherent technical limitations.
Necessity of testing hidden/show solutions Role Method Human Users requested to test the direct SetWindowPos operation before adding hidden/show functionality, indicating that “it’s possible to try” — this is a pragmatic approach, verifying whether such a solution is truly needed. Artificial Intelligence Initially, the AI based on theoretical WebView2 asynchronous rendering behavior proposed hidden/show as the final solution, but did not test whether directly adjusting the size would cause flickering. Difference analysis: Humans prefer empirical testing (“let’s give it a try”) rather than theoretical analysis. This shows that direction is important—only flickering occurs in the left direction, making the solution more targeted. The AI made repairs without establishing a baseline. For edge cases, empirical testing is better than theoretical analysis.
Limitations of Artificial Intelligence Key Limitations- The initial English translation ( Stages 1-2 ) omitted 6 types of Chinese text: the upper and lower strings in the merge prompt, matplotlib chart labels, prompt comments, templates, error recovery prompts, and default configuration values. These issues were only discovered when users requested “check the entire process”. The first batch of translations by AI focused on obvious areas (prompt constants, Markdown tags), but neglected the strings injected during execution and non-text outputs. AI did not anticipate the user requests in Stage 11 of the meeting assistant, such as re-transcribing workflows, Q&A records, and speaker management. Although the adversarial review in Stage 5 identified limitations in mixed Chinese-English transcription, no measures were proposed to address this issue. Although Screenpipe’s speech system was studied, it was not included in the feature list. Q&A interactions were designed, but recording requirements were not considered. AI’s forward planning still had blind spots—it could identify problems, but might not propose optimal solutions; it could study features, but might not actively recommend them. AI had insufficient understanding of Whisper’s language detection mechanism. In Stages 2-4 of the meeting assistant, the “mixed Chinese-English language” was marked with a confidence level of 0.75, indicating that Whisper large-v3-turbo with bilingual configuration could handle it well. However, Whisper detects each piece of language individually (about 30 seconds), not sentences, so it is not suitable for intra-sentence language conversion. This critical limitation was only discovered during the adversarial review in Stage 5 (by the code review sub-agent). AI often relied on the intuition that “the model is powerful” when evaluating technical feasibility, ignoring architectural limitations. AI misinterpreted old log files (full_run_200attempts.log). Since pickplace and coffee lacked run_200att_*.log files, it incorrectly reported the results of 398/260. Before summarizing, it failed to verify based on the actual HDF5 output. For data verification tasks, AI should always rely on authoritative sources (HDF5 files) rather than potentially outdated intermediate logs. AI proposed a CSS pre-positioning solution (moving the ball to the left and then adjusting the window size) to solve the problem of flickering floating balls, but it did not analyze IPC time limits thoroughly. It was only after user complaints that AI realized that CSS changes and Win32 SetWindowPos could not synchronize within Tauri’s IPC boundaries—they always separated by one frame or more, making the solution impossible. AI did not anticipate the second animation error (moving from left to right during folding), which arose from the first fix. Only user testing revealed the CSS justify-content issue caused by immediately removing the expanded class during the transition period. AI was unable to complete interactive shell verification on Tianjin servers: when testing Node’s fetch() function through the proxy, it encountered “TypeError: fetch failed” errors, along with EPER errors. AI correctly identified this as a sandbox proxy connection issue rather than a code issue, but could not complete verification in a real environment. Therefore, it had to rely on theoretical correctness verification (such as bash -n syntax checking + environment variable verification), rather than end-to-end testing. Over-reliance on file content caching led to multiple errors where “the file has been modified after reading”. AI should have been more cautious: even if a file was just accessed, it should have been read again immediately before editing, especially in workflows with linters/formatters or multiple agents working concurrently. Key learnings The adversarial review of ccplan (Stage 5 adversarial filtering) was very valuable. In the meeting assistance system, the code review sub-agent, acting as an adversarial critic, identified 6 serious issues overlooked by the main planner, including limitations in mixed Chinese-English transcription (Whisper’s block-based detection leads to poor quality of language conversion within sentences), long meeting context overflow, speaker recognition limitations, installation complexity, and privacy compliance issues. These issues were not fully identified in the single-perspective planning of Stages 0-4. An important realization is that complex project planning must incorporate independent critical perspectives (adversarial reviews), focusing on what “the planner might overlook,” rather than whether the plan is correct. MimicGen had a low success rate in incorrect scenarios (the overall successful demonstration was 57.4%, and the successful demonstration with human enhancement was 9.57%). When the success rate of automatic generation stagnated, manual intervention was needed to ensure comprehensive coverage. Node.js 24 and later versions have built-in support for environment variables proxies (the –use-env-proxy flag or NODEUSE_ENV_PROXY=1). However, it only reads uppercase HTTP_PROXY/HTTPS_PROXY/NO_PROXY variables, not their lowercase forms. A common pitfall is that proxy setting scripts only export lowercase forms. The solution is to handle both cases in the proxy helper functions. This is a common solution for all Node CLI tools. When refactoring LLM-driven pipelines from one language to another, verification must cover: (1) static prompt templates; (2) runtime context injection; (3) non-text outputs (charts/pictures); (4) code annotations in prompts; (5) generated templates/frameworks; (6) error recovery logic. Parallel proxy verification patterns (one for each subsystem) are more thorough than single-pass grep. Migrating proven architecture patterns from mature open-source projects is more efficient and reliable than designing from scratch. The integration of LifeCopilot with OpenClaw shows that health monitoring (4-state FSM + restart budget + exponential backoff), circuit breakers (close → open → half-open), configuration hot reload (watchfiles + EventBus), and speaker recording (voice embedding + calendar assistance) all use production-level implementations. The key is to understand the principles of the original design (why 4 states instead of 3? Why limit the number of restarts? Why use exponential backoff?) and then adapt it to the target environment. The workflow of “understanding → migrating → adapting” significantly reduces design risks, as the source project has already identified potential problems. The MCP (Model Context Protocol) serves as a standard interface between AI assistants and external tools, greatly simplifying integration complexity. The meeting assistance system demonstrates the power of MCP: Screenpipe uses 27 MCP tools to implement all functions (listing meetings, accessing meetings, searching for content, listing speakers, etc.). Claude Code CLI can achieve full conference Q&A functionality without any custom code (real-time queries, historical searches, speaker management). This proves the value of standard protocols in the AI tool ecosystem—tool providers only need to implement one MCP server, and all AI assistants that support MCP (Claude, Codex, etc.) can use it directly. Compared to REST APIs or custom integrations, each AI assistant requires separate adaptation. Details of date and time zone handling: The date_naive() method on DateTime extracts the date from the source timezone, not the local timezone. For remote timestamp filtering, it is necessary to convert the date to the local timezone using with_timezone(&chrono::Local) before extraction. This is crucial for any distributed system that processes data across time zones. Multi-data set comparative learning requires a mapping-level source marker and a list-based architecture at the dataset level. This allows for flexible sampling from heterogeneous enhancement sources (either single or multiple OSS perturbations). The hybrid architecture of JSONL (only appending logs) and memory caching is highly effective for scenarios requiring both persistence and performance. Multiple subsystems in LifeCopilot use this architecture: session history (deque(maxlen=100) + data/sessions/{user_id}.jsonl), audit logs, idea records, and mismatch records. Key design points include: (1) using atomic operations for writing (write tmp → os.replace) to prevent data corruption during crashes; (2) lazy loading during reading (reading the last N records upon first access) to avoid slow startup; (3) using bounded buffers in memory (e.g., deque(maxlen)) to prevent memory leaks; (4) using ISO 8601 UTC format for timestamps to ensure timezone safety; (5) regular archiving (data_lifecycle.py cleans up based on retention days) to prevent unlimited data growth. This architecture is the best practice for large-scale validation in fields like logging systems, event tracing, and audit tracking. WebView2 rendering is asynchronous compared to native window geometry changes. SetWindowPos synchronizes window size/position changes, but Chromium needs 1-2 frames to render new content. For transparent windows with complex layouts, this asynchronous gap can cause visible flickering. Displaying the window before geometry changes (SW_HIDE) and after geometry changes (SW_SHOWNOACTIVATE) can prevent intermediate frames from being synthesized by the DWM. The anchor behavior of SetWindowPos: The top-left corner is fixed, so reducing the size means the right/bottom edges move inward. When the content requires a visual anchor pointing to the right (e.g., direction=‘left’ floating balls), the width difference of the x-position must be adjusted simultaneously. This is a fundamental Win32 API constraint that affects all window size adjustments. CSS transitions do not explicitly interact with React/Svelte state changes. Deleting a class (e.g., expanded) immediately changes the calculated style, but the transition may still be ongoing. For multi-step animations involving CSS and native operations, CSS rules must remain unchanged during the transition or a separate “transitioning” state must be used. Practical learnings - Character ratio language detection (CJK ratio exceeding 5%) is sufficient for technical content classification. English technical documents containing a small amount of Chinese terms typically have a CJK ratio below 1%, so there is still a large margin for error. In content classification tasks, complex natural language processing-based language detection techniques are not required. Code optimization patterns: Extract reusable helper functions for complex parsing, and then build higher-level functions from them. For example, the pattern of parse_remote_ts → parse_remote_ts_to_local_date eliminates duplicate code and makes it easier to maintain than copying and pasting RFC3339 format strings. Performance optimization should reduce redundant work while maintaining code readability. Configuring loose permission settings for exploratory projects (automatically allowing most operations) can improve development experience while ensuring security. The meeting-helper.claude/settings.local.json file demonstrates this strategy: adding over 60 common commands (read/write/edit/global search/grep/WebFetch/git status/npm/python, etc.) to the automatically allowed list; only confirming destructive operations (such as deleting files, git pushing, git reset –hard); and directly rejecting extremely dangerous operations (such as recursive forced deletion, git push –force, git clean -f). This three-tier permission strategy prevents frequent interruptions and prevents irreversible errors (such as deletion, remote pushing requires manual confirmation), making it ideal for exploratory development environments. Session summaries error-recovery-benchmark: Analyzed the successful demonstration generation process of MimicGen (739/1365 scenarios succeeded, 626 failed). Errors found in the report were that pickplace/coffee read outdated logs. HDF5 checks confirmed the actual number. Three manual operation processes were implemented: scenario identification, using SpaceMouse for collection and tracking progress, and converting NPZ to HDF5. BOSS: Reconstructed PairedDataset and built a pipeline that supports sampling from multiple OSS enhancement datasets simultaneously. Modified the build_paired_mapping.py file to handle multiple inputs, and updated the aug_datasets list and aug_repo_ids configuration files. Copied 220 BDDL files (5 variations × 44 tasks) and generated mapping JSON files. Corrected the naming convention: con → single. System maintenance: Fixed Node.js proxy configuration issues, allowing the CLI tool to communicate properly through the enterprise proxy. Users reported failures on Tianhe servers. AI monitored the entire process and confirmed that ccusage used the native fetch() function instead of a custom scheduler. Node 24 supports the –use-env-proxy option, but only reads uppercase HTTP_PROXY/HTTPS_PROXY variables. Modified the proxy helper functions in .bashrc and .bashrcy to synchronize uppercase/lowercase variables, and set NODEUSE_ENV_PROXY=1. Verified the syntax correctness and accuracy of environment variable transmission. Actual testing was hindered by sandbox proxy limitations. Final status: the code is ready for users to view ~/.bashrc and verify. Implemented general fixes for all Node CLI tools. Claude Code multi-instance isolation: Configured isolation between GLM 5.1 and official Claude Code for users. Users need to run GLM 5.1 and official Claude Code simultaneously without conflict. AI explained the root cause (shared ~/.claude/ directory) and provided three solutions: (1) using CLAUDE_CONFIG_DIR environment variable (recommended), (2) using a wrapper script, (3) switching symbolic links. Now users can use different configuration directories for each instance. GitHub workspace permissions: Unified permissions for GitHub sub-projects. Users wanted to avoid repeating confirmation operations in all GitHub sub-projects. AI explained the permissions boundaries of each repository and developed a unified permission scheme for 6 directories (except rm/del/rmdir/rd, all other operations are allowed). Project-specific MCP server configurations were retained. gadget: Reconstructed the summary and research pipelines, making the output mainly in English and updating the documentation. This reconstruction involved two conversations. During the first meeting, users requested changing the article generation from mainly in Chinese to mainly in English and using Ollama for website-level translation. Four steps were performed: (1) converting 4 summary files to Markdown format, (2) converting 8 research files to Markdown format, (3) using 3 parallel verification tools to identify 6 remaining Chinese problem categories (such as LLM upper and lower strings, chart labels, prompt comments, templates, repair prompts, default configuration values), and solving all problems, (4) optimizing the code and adding 7 improvements (extracting helper functions, eliminating duplicate code, adding error handling). During the second meeting, the CLAUDE.md file was updated to reflect the migration of Ollama translation architecture. 5 key sections were modified (environment setup, architecture, cross-module dependencies, translation architecture, testing). Final verification confirmed that all changes were compiled successfully, with no Chinese content in the prompts/outputs, and the documentation was accurate. LifeCopilot: Integrated OpenClaw core functions and enhanced them. Two rounds of optimization plans were carried out. In the first round, users requested integration of OpenClaw functions. The complete workflow was executed: parallel research (2 Explore agents scanning the code library) → users selected “core enhancement functions” (health monitoring + configuration hot reload + timed concurrent control), and these functions were implemented → ECL files were generated (openclaw-core-enhancements.yaml, containing 3 functions, 8 function modules, 15 configuration items, 4 event bus events) → These functions were implemented in 3 stages (timed scheduler concurrency + configuration monitor + basic health monitoring → background coordinator integration + timed persistence + automatic restart + CONFIG_RELOADED handling → GET /api/health endpoint) → 3 test files were written (70 test cases) → Compilation and test results were verified (70/70 passed). In the second round, users asked about further optimizations, triggering the second round of optimization plans. AI identified 7 optimization directions → users selected all directions and implemented them → a 4-Sprint plan was generated (openclaw-extended-enhancements.yaml) → Sprint 1-3 were implemented (data_lifecycle.py general JSONL cleaner, SessionManager JSONL persistence + memory caching, ThoughtStore.search() keyword matching, DreamingService three-stage AI integration, InsightStore creation, CircuitBreaker three-state FSM). Sprint 4 (DM pairing) was marked as the topic of future meetings. The final results included 10 new module files (health_manager, config_watcher, data_lifecycle, dreaming_service, insightstore, circuit_breaker, etc.), 15 files were modified, and 70 tests passed. meeting-helper: ✅ Creating an AI meeting assistant from scratch + permission optimization 19:21:47.872 | claude_code Completed the ccplan workflow for both sessions, Stages 0-11. Session 1: Users requested an AI meeting assistant (recording, transcription, summary, Q&A, screen shots, cross-platform macOS+Windows, Claude Code CLI backend). Research work was performed in Stages 0-1 (parallel agents studied Zoom AI, Teams, Otter.ai commercial solutions, as well as Screenpipe/Meetily/ native open-source solutions, determining Screenpipe as the best base solution) → User selected Strategy A (building based on Screenpipe) and requested cloning deep learning-related repositories → 2 exploration agents were dispatched for parallel research (Screenpipe’s Pipe mechanism, MCP server, meeting detection; Meetily’s dual-channel audio, VAD, BlockNote editor) → Requirements from the second stage were verified → Requirements from the fourth stage were crystallized (8 requirements, 7 functions, 5 modules) → Adversarial review and feasibility testing were conducted in Stages 5 to 7 (code review agents identified 6 problems, including key Chinese-English mixed transcription limitations; exploration agents verified 5 key assumptions) → Approval was obtained in the ninth stage → 5 functions were implemented in the tenth stage (screenpipe-settings.json Whisper configuration + bilingual meeting summary Pipe, setup-mcp.sh configuration script, CLAUDE.md Q&A system prompts, install.sh/ps1 cross-platform installation + health check) → Feedback loop was established in the eleventh stage (users requested additional key risk mitigation measures + Q&A logging + speaker management, AI added re-transcription workflows, custom vocabulary lists, Q&A logs, speaker MCP tool integration). Final architecture: Screenpipe backend (audio + screen shots + Whisper transcription + SQLite FTS5) → screenpipe-mcp (27 tools) → Claude Code CLI (real-time interaction) + meeting summary Pipe (bulk summarization after meetings). Complete ECL planning documentation was included (meeting-helper.yaml). Session 2: Users complained about frequent Claude Code confirmation prompts. The.claude/settings.local.json was updated with a three-tier permission strategy: allowing 60 common commands (automatically allowed), requesting rm/git push, rejecting rm -rf/git push –force/git reset –hard/git clean -f. This configuration applied to all sessions, including subfolders.### TokenMonitor ✅ Tauri CLI fixes + 404 error in development server + SSH timezone handling + floating ball animation fix 2026-04-09 (multiple sessions) | claude_code Four debugging/optimization tasks were completed within one day. Session 1: npm run tauri dev failed because “tauri” was not recognized. It was diagnosed that the Windows native binding (@tauri-apps/cli-win32-x64-msvc) was missing. The node_modules and package-lock.json files were deleted, and re-installation was performed to trigger the correct platform dependency resolution. Session 2: npm run tauri dev failed because Vite’s “port 1420 is already in use”. Using netstat, PID 42676 was found (remaining node.exe size: 101MB). It was recommended to terminate it using taskkill. Session 3: Timezone-aware date filtering for SSH remote servers was implemented. The root issue was that remote JSONL timestamps included the server’s timezone offset (RFC 3339 FixedOffset), but date filtering used date_naive() to extract the server date instead of the local date, resulting in errors.
