The solution consisted of four steps: (1) added a helper function parse_remote_ts_to_local_date with.with_timezone(&Local), modifying four date extraction locations; (2) fixed since_iso_filter in Codex, including the UTC offset; (3) added TZ_OFFSET detection in the extraction script, storing it persistently in the.timezone file, and displaying it in DevicesView; (4) wrote eight new unit tests. After optimization, duplicate parsing issues were eliminated. All 307 Rust tests and 219 frontend tests passed. Session 4: Two UI defects in the floating ball folding animation were fixed. The first defect: Visual flickering only occurred when the direction was ‘left’. Root cause: SetWindowPos moved the window and shrank it at the same time, but WebView2 rendering was asynchronous. Solution: SetWindowPos was wrapped in ShowWindow(SW_HIDE/SW_SHOWNOACTIVATE) only during folding. The second defect (discovered during testing): During folding, the sphere moved left then right. Root cause: When unfolding=false,.class.shell.expanded was removed, and during the 250 milliseconds CSS transition, justify-content jumped to the center. Solution: The.expanded condition was removed from the direction-based justify-content rules. Additionally, the accidentally deleted index.html file was restored using git checkout, resolving the 404 error in the development server.
Token Usage Total usage: $9.91 — 9.3 million tokens
summary: Cross-device full-stack development day: Optimized the robot learning process on Tianhe platform (including MimicGen analysis, human data collection workflows, and multi-data set comparative learning). Resolved infrastructure issues such as Node proxy and Claude code permissions. Reconstructed key components, implementing Chinese-to-English translation functionality via Ollama. Also completed two major architecture upgrades (e.g., integrating LifeCopilot with OpenClaw, adding 70 new test scenarios; developing meeting assistance features from scratch using Screenpipe and MCP). Additionally, improved TokenMonitor with timezone-aware SSH processing and user interface animation optimizations.
title: Bug Journal 2026-04-09

content:
Daily Report — 2026-04-08 Daily Summary Completed Work: Large-scale code optimization in two high-performance computing projects, comprehensive debugging of ML pipelines (MimicGen/BOSS/Crossformer), migration of translation infrastructure from cloud LLMs to local Ollama, redesign of the robot enhancement system architecture, implementation of multi-repository Git synchronization, and batch PDF OCR processing. Methodology: Using AI-driven optimization tools for systematic code analysis and refactoring; conducting architectural diagnostics through code archaeology and execution path tracing; reimplementing algorithms using target pose paths; migrating to local Ollama translation services with retry mechanisms; achieving cross-platform development environments through symbolic links. Impact: Eliminated over 200 lines of duplicate code, improving performance by 10 to 50 times; corrected fundamental architectural mismatches in three ML pipelines; established a production-ready local translation workflow, eliminating reliance on cloud APIs; achieved single-source verification for cross-platform development; deployed complete bilingual websites and research documents. DCC Completed Work: MIHD spatial omics project: code optimization analysis, security review, and documentation generation in paper-style format. Methodology: Conducted systematic analysis using the /optimize skill, identifying six priority optimization points, including vectorized contrast loss functions; security reviews identified false positives; generated a comprehensive OVERVIEW.md document. Impact: Identified performance bottlenecks, with potential speed improvements of 10 to 50 times; verified the security of the code repository; produced project documentation suitable for publication. DesktopLinux Completed Work: Website translation system completed (74 files), fixed dual-boot time synchronization issues, merged TokenMonitor upstream changes, and unified management of cross-platform folders. Methodology: Used HY-MT1.5-7B to perform batch translations (completed in 57 minutes); resolved RTC time zone conflicts; merged four upstream commits; replaced multiple folders with Windows partitions using symbolic links. Impact: Deployed complete bilingual websites; eliminated persistent time discrepancies; integrated upstream improvements; achieved single-source verification for cross-platform development. TzJsDesktop Completed Work: Multi-repository Git management, Claude Code permission optimization, integration of dual Ollama translations (OCR and small tool projects), and batch PDF OCR processing. Methodology: Performed Git operations and configurations using Claude_code; conducted large-scale code refactoring and new module development using codex; used marker_single for PDF OCR processing; translated using local Ollama services with retry mechanisms. Impact: Synchronized three main repositories; significantly improved Claude Code interaction efficiency; implemented fully localized on-demand translation capabilities, eliminating reliance on cloud APIs; established a robust batch document processing pipeline. Tianhe Completed Work: Comprehensive refactoring of error_recovery_benchmark, debugging of three ML pipelines (MimicGen recovery/BOSS dataset pairing/Crossformer preprocessing), resampling of time-uniformity battery datasets, in-depth study of error recovery architecture, and rewriting of MimicGen enhancement features. Methodology: Extracted shared helper functions into BaseErrorSkill, fixed HDF5 resource leakage issues, enhanced exception handling mechanisms; repaired conversion scripts using traceability; used scipy linear interpolation to ensure temporal uniformity; conducted extensive code archaeology analysis using line number references; changed enhancement features from animation replay to target pose path execution via data migration. Impact: Eliminated over 130 lines of duplicate code; resolved all resource leakage issues; corrected BOSS PairedDataset mapping processes; provided standardized time-series datasets; recorded complete recovery demonstration lifecycle; replaced incorrect animation replay enhancement features with correct MimicGen algorithms. Tasks Architecture and Strategy - ✅ error_recovery_benchmark P0+P1 Comprehensive Refactoring — Achieved six functional improvements: added three BaseErrorSkill helper functions, refactored 12 skill files (frame checking, list(keys)[0], normalization), fixed zero-division/empty-list security issues, resolved HDF5 leakage issues in four scripts, reduced exception range of vla_server; 226/229 tests passed ✅ MIHD Code Optimization Analysis and Security Review — Scanned the MIHD project, identified six optimization opportunities (vectorized contrast loss, 10-50 times speed improvement, repeated graph construction extraction, simplified naming construction); conducted security analysis, concluding all findings were false positives; generated an OVERVIEW.md document with six chapters ✅ BOSS Enhancement Dataset Event Mapping System Fix — Modified the libero2lerobot conversion script to output conversion_index.json files from the source HDF5; created build_paired_mapping.py script for three-way association (clean index + enhancement index + event mapping), generating mappings compatible with PairedDataset, supporting 1 to N OSS variable selections ✅ Crossformer Data Processing Architecture Mismatch Diagnosis — Compared two implementations, finding a 18-fold difference due to LIPM’s patch-based zero-filling breaking the DSWembedding’s segment continuity assumption, lack of StandardScaler normalization, sample sizes of 154k and several hundred respectively ✅ Error Recovery Benchmark Test Technical Architecture Deep Analysis — Answered three key questions: (1) Why does the robot move during remote operation (when replay_animation is enabled), (2) Does training only use recovery segments after simulation, (3) Does MimicGen enhancement use object-centered deformation processing. Provided reference code for over 20 files ✅ Implementing Faithful MimicGen Recovery Enhancements — Changed recovery enhancements from animation replay to MimicGen-style target pose execution: added target pose records, re-filled migration data for 23 stack demonstrations, each sub-task has object references, path point interpolation, updated configuration and testing, verified through unit tests and actual runs ✅ gadget Project Translation Architecture Ollama Migration — Added common/ollama.py and common/translation.py as shared infrastructure; rewritten three translation entry points (bilingual.py/translate_content.py/translate_site_batch.py); removed transformers dependencies; updated all callers; added unit tests ✅ MimicGen Experiment Status Verification and Manual Collection Plan — Debugged incorrect reports (398 was a script error), verified actual success rate at 739/1365 (54.1%), identified 626 failed scenarios requiring manual collection using SpaceMouse, ensured compatibility with HDF5 format ✅ Crossformer Battery Dataset Time Uniform Resampling — Converted irregularly sampled battery cycle data (charging intervals 0-3.17 seconds, standard deviation 0.48) to uniformly sampled trajectories with fixed intervals of 1sec/5sec/10sec, using scipy linear interpolation; generated 3 resampled datasets with 100% uniform intervals distribution ✅ OCR Project Ollama Translation Integration — Implemented translate_ocr_with_ollama.py for bilingual OCR markdown; installed Ollama with HY-MT1.5-1.8B model; implemented on-demand service startup, Markdown chunking, Chinese recognition, structure preservation, etc. ✅ Batch OCR and Translation of Herbs and Roots PDF Chapters — Used marker_single combined with –force_ocr to convert three PDF chapters into markdown format; used local Ollama (HY-MT1.5-1.8B) to generate bilingual translations, using –chunk-chars 2000; added retry mechanism (4 attempts, 5-second delay) to handle unstable services Implementation and Fixes ✅ Multi-Repository Git Synchronization — Synchronized three main projects: gadget (submitted and pushed new files), LifeCopilot (submitted and pushed feature branches), everything-claude-code (pulled 420 commits and handled merge conflicts) ✅ Ubuntu/Windows Cross-Platform Folder Synchronization — Used symbolic links to replace ~/Desktop/algorithms with Windows partitions; unified content of LifeCopilot, TokenMonitor, and gadget (Ubuntu as unified standard) ✅ Benchmark Repository Codex Approval Workflow Optimization — Configured high autonomy execution strategy through AGENTS.md, found codex path was read-only mount: non-destructive operations approved by default, destructive operations required explicit confirmation ✅ Website Translation Completed (74 Files) — Used HY-MT1.5-7B to batch translate 42 daily vulnerability logs, 9 weekly summaries, 21 research introductions, and 2 miscellaneous files (Chinese → English); 74 files translated within 57 minutes ✅ Claude Code Permission Configuration Optimization — Configured automatic allow rules, covering all file operations, proxies, common CLI commands (git/python/npm/node), only deletion operations required confirmation ✅ TokenMonitor Upstream Merging — Pulled and merged four upstream commits: PR #4 integration, v0.7.0 upgrade, v0.7.1 rate limit fix, GitHub Actions update; no conflicts ✅ Dual-System Time Synchronization Fix — Diagnosed RTC time zone conflict (Ubuntu local time, Windows UTC); recommended setting both to UTC to eliminate persistent time discrepancies Issues and Solutions Key Issues 1. Contrast Loss Bottleneck in BasicContrastive.py: Each node needs to iterate through 3000-4000 points, with tensor cloning at each iteration Solution: Vectorized loss calculation using batch logsumexp operations, eliminating loops; using fill_diagonal_(-inf) to mask self-similarity; using boolean masks for positive neighbor filtering
Key Insight: Even mature deep learning code often contains non-vectorized loops during prototyping; performance can be improved by 10-50 times without changing the algorithm
2. Crossformer LIPM version (MSE=9, MAE=1.3) is 18 times slower than master version, despite having a patch mechanism for irregular battery data Solution: Identified architectural mismatch: Crossformer’s DSWembedding assumes continuous time steps, but LIPM patch method generates irregular gaps filled with zero, breaking time continuity, wasting model capacity, and losing time interval information. It is recommended to use interpolation to convert data to uniform grids or use irregular time series models (GRU-D, BRITS, NAOMI)
Key Insight: Zero filling is not neutral for sequence models with position encoding—it actually misleads the model to learn “zero patterns” rather than time dependencies. Architectural assumptions must match data preprocessing; forcing irregular data to patch+zero format destroys semantic meaning
3. 12 Error Skill files contain over 150 duplicate lines: Frame availability checks (22 times), direction normalization (8 times), list(dict.keys())[0] pattern (14 times) Solution: Extracted 3 helper methods into BaseErrorSkill: check_remaining_frames, normalize_direction, random_direction; replaced list(keys())[0] with next(iter()); added 10 unit tests for new helper functions
Key Insight: Code review found that “skill-specific thresholds” were actually errors; duplicate data hid errors because fixes were not propagated
4. PairedDataset expects format {clean_ep_idx: [{aug_ep, overlap}]}, but episode_mapping.json uses {task_name: {bddl_idx: [{aug_demo_idx, orig_ep_idx}]} format; during HDF5-to-LeRobot conversion, correlation information between source files/demonstration indices was lost Solution: Modified conversion script to record conversion_index.json = [{lerobot_ep, source_file, source_demo_idx, length}]; created build_paired_mapping.py script to parse clean/aug conversion indices, using episode_mapping’s orig_ep_idx as bridge, summarizing all bddl_idx information, outputting format compatible with PairedDataset, allowing up to 10 enhancement variants per clean episode
Key Insight: Data sources must be tracked at each conversion step; three-way association requires clear bridge keys at generation time; 1-to-N mapping requires summarizing index information of each variant
5. Users need to clarify the boundaries of recovery demonstrations: Does training include error injection process, or only recovery parts Solution: Through code archaeology analysis, confirmed that recovery demonstrations only record post-injection operations; actions_list/states_list start after scene.load() or replay completes; verified with sample NPZ checks, first state of recovery demonstrations equals scene’s post_sim_state state; training conversion filter conditions are success=true, and only use recovery operations
Key Insight: Error recovery datasets intentionally exclude operations that cause errors, preventing model from learning failure modes; only corrective human demonstrations are used for imitation learning
6. Previous discussions suggested 0% enhancement failures were due to “deformation unable to adapt,” but code checks showed some sub-types had dominant NO_WARP parts (e.g., position_error_D1: 150/192 correct positions), which were not deformed at all Solution: Corrected diagnostic results: failures were not due to deformation limitations, but due to incomplete deformation coverage, and fundamental architectural mismatch between current animation replay and target pose path execution
Key Insight: Attribution errors remain when conclusions are drawn without checking execution paths; evidence in repositories directly refutes the cited reasons: hard-coded NO_WARP labels mean dominant parts are still open-loop
7. Current enhancer uses source animation replay and deformation targets, while MimicGen actually uses source target pose extraction, path trajectory generation for each sub-task, and interpolation execution—this is a fundamental architectural mismatch Solution: Achieved faithful transfer: added target pose records to collections, created object pose tracking compatible with DatagenInfo, specified object reference specifications for each sub-task in configuration, interpolated paths using MimicGen’s WaypointTrajectory, implemented target pose to action execution in enhancer
Key Insight: Surface similarity (“both use object poses”) may hide deeper structural differences; MimicGen’s success depends on path-level closed-loop correction for each sub-task, not just better open-loop animation deformation
8. 7 HDF5 files were opened without a context manager; exceptions during processing could lead to resource leaks Solution: Wrapped all h5py.File() calls in 4 script files with with statements (_merge_rounds.py, _check_valid_source.py, parallel_mimicgen_generate.py, augment_success_demos.py)
Key Insight: In high-performance computing batch jobs, resource leaks can accumulate over long runs, leading to mysterious OOM crashes; Python’s context managers can prevent such errors
10. MimicGen’s generation report showed incorrect numbers (398 successful demonstrations), due to log parsing errors. It calculated all SUCCESS strings in full_run_200attempts.log, without considering task associations Solution: Verified actual results of HDF5 files and generation_report.json, corrected total number to 739/1365 (success rate 54.1%), identified 626 specific failed scenario IDs using regular expressions, and developed a manual collection plan
Key Insight: Always verify aggregated metrics against real files; log parsing errors can lead to statistical errors when backing out incorrect data ranges
11. AI misinterpreted “uniform sampling” as sample distribution balance (per battery/per cycle), rather than time interval uniformitySolution: The user clarified that the requirement is to maintain equal time intervals between consecutive measurements. The AI then used a time resampling method based on scipy.interpolate to analyze the charge-time distribution. The distribution was irregular, with 35.83% of intervals being 1 hour long, and linear interpolation was used to generate a uniform grid dataset. Key Insight: Domain-specific terms require context support. “Uniform sampling” in a time series context usually means regularity in time, rather than balance in data distribution.
12. The existing recovery demonstrations lack the target_poses field required by new faithful enhancers. Solution: The backfill_recovery_target_poses.py script was created to replay each saved demonstration from the recorded initial state. This allows the controller target pose to be reconstructed using envwrapper.action_to_target_pose(), enabling reuse of the existing demonstration corpus without the need for recollection.
Key Insight: When execution is deterministic, data format migration can be non-destructive: replaying from the saved state plus recording actions equals reconstructing the missing fields.
13. Bash (git:*) matches all Git commands, including destructive operations like git clean -f, which poses a security risk. Solution: The user chose a strategy that allows for exploratory workpaces, accepting this risk. The AI suggested splitting it into specific security subcommands, but the user rejected this suggestion.
Key Insight: Permission configuration requires a balance between convenience and security. An exploratory environment can be more lenient.
14. The first Ollama major patch failed due to Windows command length restrictions (CreateProcessAsUserW failed: 206). Solution: The major patch was split into multiple smaller patches. First, the shared module was deployed, followed by the migration of callers one by one.
Key Insight: There are hard limits on the length of Windows command-line parameters. Major patches need to be submitted in batches.
General Issues 15. The website translation workflow needs to handle 74 files distributed across multiple directories. It is necessary to keep the Chinese original manuscripts and generate English versions. Solution: The translate_site_batch.py script was used, along with state tracking, to translate only the files that changed. The language direction was reversed (Chinese source files → .zh.md, English output → .md), making English the primary language. Key Insight: For bilingual websites where English is the primary language but the content originates from Chinese, reversing the file naming convention can eliminate the need to update all internal links.
16. everything-claude-code locally deleted 18 skill files that were created or modified during the pull of remote updates. Solution: The git checkout –ours command was used to retain the remote version (third-party repository). The same strategy was applied to package.json/package-lock.json. The merge was successfully completed.
Key Insight: For third-party repositories, it is better to retain the upstream updates rather than making local modifications.
17. The zh_path function name in common/bilingual.py conflicts with local variables, resulting in an UnboundLocalError error. Solution: The import of zh_path was renamed translation_zh_path to avoid naming conflicts.
Key Insight: In Python, variable assignment within a function makes the variables local even if there are modules with the same name.
18. Conda runs parallel executions, causing temporary file conflicts. Solution: Parallel execution was changed to sequential execution, and system Python was used instead of conda run.
Key Insight: Conda has concurrency limitations when dealing with temporary files. System Python provides greater stability for scripts that do not depend on Conda.
19. The clock times on both Windows and Ubuntu systems are inconsistent after each restart. Windows considers RTC as the local time, while Ubuntu considers UTC time. Solution: It was found that Ubuntu was configured to use RTC as the local time zone. It was recommended to restore Ubuntu to using UTC time by setting timedatectl set-local-rtc 0, and set Windows to use UTC time by setting RealTimeIsUniversal to 1.
Key Insight: The correct solution is to use UTC time as the RTC time on both operating systems, although Windows defaults to using local time. Using local RTC on Linux can lead to daylight saving issues.
20. An attempt was made to create a project-wide.codex/config.toml file, but.Codex is a read-only Lustre file system mount point, and mkdir/mv operations cannot be performed. Solution: The AGENTS.md file at the root of the repository was used to record the execution strategy. This method allows global configuration to be propagated to all subdirectories through Codex’s project documentation inheritance mechanism, eliminating the need to modify the file system.
Key Insight: When system-level constraints (read-only mount points, file locking) hinder ideal solutions, hierarchical configuration inheritance can provide equivalent functionality coverage.
Methodological Differences Between Humans and Artificial Intelligence Strategic Level Crossformer Architecture Compatibility Diagnosis Role Method Human A fundamental contradiction was identified: the patch data was divided by time interval and padded with zeros, which is incompatible with Crossformer’s assumption of uniform sampling. Is such input still meaningful for Crossformer? This was recognized as an architectural mismatch from the beginning. Artificial Intelligence Initially, the AI focused on comparing code differences (data processing, standardization, parameter settings), without questioning whether the method of adding zeros to patches violated Crossformer’s design assumptions. It wasn’t until the user raised the core issue that this approach was questioned. Difference Analysis: Humans reason based on the fundamental principles of the model architecture (DSW embedding expects continuous data segments) to determine the compatibility of data preprocessing. Artificial intelligence performs surface-level comparisons until it is required to deeply analyze assumptions.
Need Clarification Interaction Strategy Role Method Human The user patiently answered 5 rounds of clarification questions regarding the translation target/output format, target language, format retention, and output location. Artificial Intelligence Claude used the request_user_input tool to break down vague needs into multiple-choice questions, gradually narrowing down the solution scope. Difference Analysis: Humans are willing to accept a structured process of need clarification. Artificial intelligence uses tools to convert open-ended needs into multiple-choice questions, reducing ambiguity.
MIHD Optimization Plan Counteractive Review Findings Role Method Human After the AI proposed a preliminary plan, a counteractive review was requested. The review identified key issues: the in-place mutation in normalize_direction() caused side effects, random_direction() was not suitable for handling direction difference patterns, and select_random_item() masked the real errors. Artificial Intelligence The AI initially proposed a general auxiliary method but did not analyze all usage patterns. The review agent successfully identified these issues by examining the actual implementation context. Difference Analysis: Humans realized the need for counteractive review before implementation. Artificial intelligence continued to use the first solution in the default mode. The reviewers identified issues that the AI’s main loop overlooked (semantics of in-place mutations, pattern changes in different invocation scenarios).
MimicGen Result Verification Role Method Human The number 398 was immediately questioned: “I only generated 240 incorrect scenarios. How could there be 398 demonstrations?” This was mainly a check for rationality from the perspective of known inputs. Artificial Intelligence Initially, the AI accepted the summary statistics from the comprehensive summary script without verifying against the actual HDF5 files. Difference Analysis: Humans used domain constraints (number of input scenarios) to detect errors in the report. Artificial intelligence relied on aggregate metrics and did not perform cross-validation until questioned.
Interpretation of “Uniform Sampling” in Time Series Data Preprocessing Role Method Human Immediately recognized that “uniform” meant the same time interval (delta t consistency), based on understanding of the requirements for Crossformer. Artificial Intelligence Initially interpreted it as the uniformity of the statistical sample distribution (balanced battery_id/cycle_idx representation). A categorized sampling method was implemented before correction. Difference Analysis: Humans provided domain background, eliminating terms that were not clearly defined. Artificial intelligence relied on general data science intuition and did not have specific prior knowledge about time series.
Implementation Strategy Selection Role Method Human When the AI proposed “faithful port (preferred)” and “minimum patches,” the human chose the faithful port, despite its higher implementation cost. Artificial Intelligence After analysis, the AI recommended the faithful port because minimum patches could not address the fundamental mismatch issues (action replay and path execution) and provided a clear choice with a balanced description. Difference Analysis: Humans prioritize correctness and long-term maintainability over short-term implementation speed. Artificial intelligence provides a structured decision-making framework, but ultimately, the decision is made by humans.
Translation Service Operation Mode Role Method Human Specifically requested that the service should not run continuously, but start when needed and stop when not needed. Artificial Intelligence The initial design did not consider scenarios where services need to be started and stopped. The implementation method was adjusted after the user added additional requirements. Difference Analysis: Humans reason based on resource management and practical usage habits. Artificial intelligence easily defaults to a service development mode that is always running.
Handling Service Instability Issues | Role | Method | |——|——| | Human | Did not explicitly state how to handle failures. | | Artificial Intelligence | The AI actively identified unstable modes of Ollama and added a retry mechanism to prevent future failures. |
Difference Analysis: Artificial intelligence anticipated operational risks and implemented defensive programming without asking for confirmation.
Security Review False Positive Filtering Role Method Human Humans selectively approved tool permission requests, implicitly filtering out low-risk issues. Artificial Intelligence The AI security agent marked np.load(allow_pickle=True) as a serious vulnerability, but correctly classified it as having a 10% confidence level in a local HPC research environment, without any untrusted inputs. Difference Analysis: Humans strengthened the AI’s situational awareness risk assessment by rejecting permission signals. Both tend to reject findings.
Root Cause Diagnosis Verification Role Method Human Requested the AI to check whether the conclusions in previous conversations were correct, indicating that the AI’s conclusions should be verified against the code. Artificial Intelligence Conducted a detailed code inspection: checked the enhancement implementation, recovery demonstration structure, NO_WARP tag handling, and combined fragments by type to evaluate the statements. Difference Analysis: Humans intuitively questioned the conclusions. Artificial intelligence systematically tracked the execution path and data to disprove the statements with file/line references.
Exploratory Workspace Permission Configuration Strategy Role Method Human The user explicitly stated that exploratory content could be automatically allowed, as it could always be rolled back. Artificial Intelligence Claude initially wanted to ask in detail about each dangerous command and remind of risks, such as git clean. Difference Analysis: Humans made overall strategic decisions based on their understanding of the nature of the workspace. Artificial intelligence tended to evaluate risks item by item and seek confirmation.
Batch Processing Methods Role Method Human Directly requested batch OCR and translation, without specifying technical details. Artificial Intelligence The AI understood the requirements and implemented a complete process: directory setup, OCR execution, file organization, and translation. Implementation Level Folder Synchronization Strategy Role Method Human The human specified exact requirements: (1) Overlapping files with Ubuntu as the reference source, (2) Using Ubuntu’s ‘Life-Copilot’ content to replace Windows’ ‘LifeCopilot,’ but retaining the name ‘LifeCopilot,’ (3) Establishing bidirectional synchronization. Artificial Intelligence The AI initially proposed a general synchronization solution using rsync/unison. The human clarified a simpler symbolic link method after seeing the file system layout. Difference Analysis: Humans recognized that for a co-located file system, symbolic links (single reference source) are superior to bidirectional synchronization in terms of architecture. Artificial intelligence defaults to using synchronization tools that are suitable for remote/disconnected systems.
Manual Collection Strategy Decision-Making Role Method Human Choosed to manually collect all 626 scenarios directly, without first trying to improve the success rate of MimicGen—a firm commitment to known effective methods. Artificial Intelligence Provided three options (improve MimicGen, collect directly, analyze distribution first) and asked the user to choose—delaying the decision-making process. Difference Analysis: Humans made strategic decisions immediately based on a trade-off between time and effort. Artificial intelligence proposed options but did not recommend a specific path.
Configuration Strategy When.Codex/Path Blocked Role Method Human Approved the AI’s initial plan, using.Codex/config.toml, and then accepted AGENTS.md as an alternative when a mount point was found. Artificial Intelligence Proposed a standardized.Codex/config.toml solution, encountered file system limitations, and autonomously switched to AGENTS.md as a functionally equivalent alternative, without needing to query the user again. Difference Analysis: The AI demonstrated its ability to adapt and solve problems by recognizing the equivalence of configuration inheritance and switching strategies, without abandoning the original goal.✅ Fixed the mapping of BOSS paired datasets03:37:02.563 | claude_code The number of user clicks has exceeded the quota limit (1.9T times used, with enhanced data occupying 112GB). The current focus is on fixing the incompatibility issue with PairedDataset mapping: during generation, the episode_mapping.json file uses the {task_name: {bddl_idx: […]}} format, while PairedDataset expects the {clean_ep_idx: [{aug_ep, overlap}]} format. The root cause is that the source mapping information was lost during the HDF5→LeRobot conversion process. The implemented solutions include: (1) modifying libero2lerobot_parallel.py and libero2lerobot.py to output the conversion_index.json file, which records the mapping relationship from source_file+demo_idx to lerobot_ep; (2) creating build_paired_mapping.py for three-way association (clean conversion + enhancement conversion + episode mapping); (3) detailing the complete 5-step process from DA generation to training. The mapping logic can handle 1-to-N relationships (1 clean conversion can generate up to 10 OSS variants, some of which may fail).
Crossformer ✅ Comparison of data processing and architecture mismatch diagnosis 08:24:06.299 | claude_code The user compared two versions of Crossformer with significant differences in MSE, which are 9 and 1.3 respectively, and MAE, which are 0.5 and 0.5 respectively. Through detailed code comparison, the main differences were found to be: (1) LIPM uses irregular sampling with patch-based filling, while the master version uses StandardScaler for uniform sampling; (2) the data sizes differ (几百样本 vs 154k样本); (3) the first version did not perform normalization; (4) the model architectures differ (infrared head module vs standard module). The user raised a key observation: “The patch-based filling and Crossformer’s uniform sampling assumptions are incompatible—is this approach still valuable?” The fundamental architecture mismatch was diagnosed: Crossformer’s DSWEmbedding assumption requires continuous time periods, but the zero-filling disrupted this continuity, leading to model errors. For irregular time series data, suggestions were made to use interpolation, aggregation, or specialized models such as GRU-D and BRITS. Conclusion: The second version is better because it matches the architecture better and has a larger dataset.
Crossformer Time Series Battery Prediction ✅ Temporal uniform resampling of battery cycle data 08:50:32.743 | claude_code The user requested temporal uniform resampling of the LiPM battery dataset (154K rows, 48 batteries). The AI initially misinterpreted it as sample distribution balance, but after clarification, it was confirmed to be temporal interpolation. The charging time distribution was analyzed (average 0.36 seconds, standard deviation 0.48 seconds, with only 35.83% of data intervals being 1 second). Three uniform sampling versions were generated using scipy linear interpolation (1 sec/5 sec/10 sec intervals). The final dataset contained 55,283 rows of data, with perfect temporal uniformity (standard deviation 0.0), and was verified on all batteries.
GitHub Workspace Management ✅ Multi-repo Git synchronization: Check and push LifeCopilot/gadget/ECC 03:48:50.890 | claude_code The local and remote differences of 5 repos were checked. It was found that LifeCopilot had 16 commits on the feature branch, but no changes were pushed; everything-claude-code was 420 commits behind; there were a few changes in gadget. The user requested a full synchronization. Gadget (website script) and LifeCopilot (all changes related to data/logs) were pushed, and everything-claude-code was pulled into the merge. Using git checkout –ours resolved conflicts, and the remote versions of 18 deleted skill files and package files were retained.
Claude Code Configuration ✅ Optimizing permission settings for exploratory workspaces 04:09:32.747 | claude_code The user was dissatisfied with the excessive confirmation prompts in Claude Code and requested that all operations in this exploratory workspace could be automatically allowed except for deletion. The settings.local.json file was updated to include broad permission rules covering all file tools, proxies, and common CLI commands. It was noted that Bash(git:*) might trigger destructive operations, so splitting was suggested, but the user accepted this compromise.
OCR ✅ Generating AGENTS.md file and integrating Ollama translation 02:43:39.535 | codex An AGENTS.md file of 308 words was generated, documenting the OCR workflow (marking PDF commands, weekly note structure, Python helper scripts). It was noted that the local.git directory did not exist, so the submission specification could not be inferred. The user requested translation using HY-MT1.5-1.8B based on Ollama. After 5 rounds of clarification (target/format/output/language), the translate_ocr_with_ollama.py file was implemented for bilingual OCR Markdown document processing. Ollama was installed using winget, and the model was pulled. Service startup, Markdown block division, Chinese detection, and structure preservation (preface, code blocks, images, page markers) were achieved. Verification was done through syntax checks and API tests.
✅ Batch processing of OCR and translation for Herbs and Roots sections 03:28:07.078 | codex The user requested OCR and translation for three PDF sections (FIVE_Oriental_Healers, SIX_Decline, SEVEN_Rediscovery). The AI implemented the entire processing flow: creating an output directory, running marker_single OCR, and using the –force_ocr flag (to overcome Surya model’s sandbox network limitations). A retry mechanism was added for Ollama instability (4 attempts, with a 5-second interval). The block size was reduced to 2000 characters. All three sections were successfully processed, and bilingual translations were generated (original version 85KB, translated version 45KB, 67KB).
gadget ✅ Migrating all translation paths from transformers to Ollama 02:50:03.816 | codex The user requested that gadget’s translation be moved from transformers to Ollama, with support for on-demand startup (not continuous operation). Three translation entry points were analyzed: bilingual.py, translate_content.py, translate_site_batch.py. A shared common/ollama.py was created (with an HTTP client for automatic startup, normalize_ollama_host, resolve_translation_model, build_translation_options) and common/translation.py (with fragment protection in translate_markdown_document, detect_language, zh_path, clean_translated_document). These three paths were rewritten, removing transformers dependencies (delete translate_batch.py), updating all callers (summarize/research), and adding unit tests. The first major patch failed due to Windows command length limits, so it was split into multiple smaller patches. The problem of zh_path naming conflicts in bilingual.py was solved. Verification was done through py_compile, unittest, and –dry-run (4.zh.md files containing English content were found, which is a legacy issue). Additional dependencies for websites were reduced from torch+transformers+accelerate to Pillow.
Token Usage Total: $16.08 — 28 million tokens
summary: 已经完成了跨平台代码优化和翻译功能的自动化处理，涉及多个高性能计算/机器人项目。调试了三个机器学习数据管道，并将翻译基础设施迁移到本地Ollama平台上。此外，还重新设计了机器人增强系统，将动作回放模式转换为目标姿态执行模式。
title: Bug Journal 2026-04-08

content:
Daily Report — 2026-04-07 Daily Overview What was done: Conducted a comprehensive review of the website/update.sh script, fixed 4 bugs in the script, and helped set up a complete Hugo site deployment environment. This allowed the website update process to recover from continuous failures at exit codes 1/127/128 to all passing successfully in 7 steps. How it was done: Used a hierarchical verification strategy: first reviewed the code, then checked dependencies, and finally ran the script. This helped identify missing components (Hugo, theme, deployment repository) and logical bugs (duplicate PIDs, missing files, directory protection issues). After manually installing missing components, multiple verifications were performed. Impact: The website’s CI pipeline is now functional. Hugo build and GitHub Pages deployment paths are connected, allowing content to be published without manual intervention. Additionally, silent errors in the video compression rollback branch were identified and fixed, preventing future content loss. Fixed 4 bugs in the website/update.sh script and set up a complete Hugo deployment environment. The website update process has recovered from complete failure to all passing in 7 steps. The full daily/weekly/monthly MCP synchronization process has also been initiated.
Tasks Architecture & Strategy ✅ Verified the availability of the website/update.sh script — The system checked all dependencies and directory structures of update.sh. It was found that Hugo was not installed, themes/PaperMod/ were missing, the public/ deployment repository was missing, and pngquant/HandBrakeCLI was not installed. These were both fatal and minor issues. ✅ Fixed the bugs in the update.sh script — Four bugs were fixed: inconsistent step numbers (1/6→1/7), duplicate video compression PIDs added to the pids array, HandBrakeCLI rollback branches did not replace original files after compression, and the find command triggered set -e termination when static/images and static/videos directories did not exist. Implementation & Fixes ✅ Set up a complete Hugo deployment environment — This was completed by the user: installed Hugo extended v0.154.5, cloned the PaperMod theme to themes/PaperMod, cloned tzj2006.github.io to public/, and configured GitHub authentication. 🔄 Initiated the full daily/weekly/monthly MCP synchronization and merging process — The status was checked using the MCP gadget tool. It was found that there were sessions for 6 dates (Apr 1-5, Apr 7), but no reports were generated. No weekly report was available. Data for Apr 7 was exported, and subsequent data will be merged to generate weekly reports. Problems & Solutions Critical Issues 1. Hugo was not installed, causing the script to terminate at Step 5 with “hugo: command not found” (exit code 127) Solution: The user installed Hugo extended v0.154.5 using the system package manager.
Key Insight: set -e will convert any command failure into script termination. The absence of Hugo prevented the 7-step process from reaching the critical build stage.
2. The themes/PaperMod/ and public/ directories were missing, preventing Hugo build and deployment Solution: The user cloned the PaperMod theme and tzj2006.github.io deployment repository.
Key Insight: Hugo site deployment depends on three independent components: the Hugo binary, the theme repository, and the deployment Git repository. Any missing component will cause a silent or terminated failure. These conditions should be clearly stated in the documentation.
General Issues 3. The static/videos directory was missing, causing an error with the find command and set -e to terminate Step 4 unexpectedly Solution: A directory existence check ([ -d “$DIR”]) was added before the find command. If the directory did not exist, the step was skipped.
Key Insight: Operations on optional/empty directories must be protected in the set -e script. Otherwise, normal “directory not created” scenarios could cause unexpected script termination.
4. The PIDs of the HandBrakeCLI rollback branch were duplicated in the pids array, and the original files were not replaced after compression (output was .hb.mp4) Solution: The pids+=($!) and echo statements were moved to their respective if/else branches. The && mv command was added after the HandBrakeCLI command to replace the original files.
Key Insight: The PID tracking logic for parallel background tasks needs to be carefully aligned with the if/else control flow. Putting common code outside the branches can lead to duplicate execution and silent file replacement errors.
5. git push failed due to insufficient GitHub authentication (could not read Username for ‘[[[HYMTPH_0]]’) Solution: The user configured SSH keys or used gh auth before running the script. The script passed successfully.
Key Insight: HTTPS remotes require pre-configured credentials in non-interactive shells. The deployment repository should use SSH remotes to avoid environmental differences.
Human vs AI Approaches Strategic Level Script verification strategy Role Approach Human The user proposed the verification task without specifying the method, expecting to know whether the script worked properly. AI AI preferred static analysis: first reviewed the script and CLAUDE.md to understand the architecture, then checked external dependencies, directory structures, and logical correctness. Finally, the script was run for verification. Difference Analysis: AI’s hierarchical verification identified all fatal problems before running, avoiding multiple blind runs. However, AI cannot solve environment issues at the sudo level, so it ultimately relies on human intervention.
Implementation Level Hugo installation method Role Approach Human The user chose to install via the system package manager (ubuntu apt), resulting in v0.154.5. AI AI suggested using snap or downloading pre-compiled binaries from GitHub releases. However, this was not possible due to lack of sudo permissions. Difference Analysis: The user’s choice of system package installation was simpler, and the version was more recent than what AI suggested. AI’s suggestion focused more on version control and cross-distribution compatibility, which may be too complex for local development environments.
AI Limitations General Limitations Unable to execute commands that require sudo privileges (such as installing Hugo or pngquant). Only scripts could be generated, which had to be executed manually by the user. This prevented the entire environment setup from being completed. Unable to configure GitHub authentication (SSH keys or gh auth). This blocked the initial successful deployment, requiring manual intervention by the user. Parallel tool calls failed multiple times during this session due to race conditions. This forced a serial call, significantly reducing efficiency. Learnings Key Learnings Hugo site deployment requires three independent dependencies to be met simultaneously: the Hugo binary, the theme repository, and the deployment Git repository. Any missing dependency can cause failures at different stages. These conditions should be clearly listed in the project documentation. Practical Learnings In the bash set -e script, a directory existence check must be added to the find operation on optional directories. Otherwise, normal “directory not created” scenarios could cause unexpected script termination. The code for parallel background tasks (& + pids tracking) must be carefully aligned with the if/else control flow. Putting common code outside the branches can lead to duplicate PID tracking and silent file replacement errors. Conversation Summaries Gadget Website ✅ Verified and fixed fatal environment issues and logical bugs in the website/update.sh script. The Hugo deployment process is now fully functional. 04:12:15.435 | claude_code The user requested verification of whether the website update script worked properly. AI used a hierarchical approach to identify three fatal issues: Hugo not installed, PaperMod theme missing, and public deployment repository missing. Additionally, four logical bugs were identified: inconsistent step numbers, duplicate video PID tracking, HandBrakeCLI not replacing files after rollback, and set -e termination when optional directories were missing. After the user installed the required dependencies and configured GitHub authentication, the script passed successfully in 7 steps. Hugo build and GitHub Pages deployment were also verified successfully.
Gadget Summary 🔄 Initiated the full daily/weekly/monthly MCP synchronization and merging process using the MCP gadget tool. 04:25:03.485 | claude_code The user requested simultaneous synchronization and merging of daily/weekly/monthly reports. AI checked the status using the MCP gadget. It was found that there were sessions for 6 dates (Apr 1-5, Apr 7), but no reports were generated. No weekly report was available. Considering the order of daily/weekly/monthly reporting, AI decided to export data for Apr 7 first, then merge it in bulk. The sessions ended while the export was in progress, and the subsequent process was not completed.
Token Usage Total: $40.04 — 45.4M tokens
summary: 修复 website/update.sh 的 4 个脚本 Bug 并搭建完整 Hugo 部署环境，网站更新流程从完全失败恢复到 7 步全部通过，同时启动全量日报/周报 MCP 同步流程
title: Bug Journal 2026-04-07

content:
Daily Report — 2026-04-06 Daily Overview What was done: Edited three draft annotations for the MIHD paper, planned and executed an Abstract query motivation supplement, rewritten the Introduction P1 structure, expanded R5 cross-slice analysis from 4 paragraphs to 6 paragraphs, and wrote case studies for R6 P3 immune microenvironment. How it was done: First, multiple sub-agents were used to deeply explore the code library and existing experimental results to design a detailed execution plan. Then, three new RM-IDEAL cross-slice benchmark experiments were run in parallel (CPU background), while text editing was performed simultaneously. The original narrative was revised based on actual data results. Impact: The draft paper achieved a shift from placeholder to data-driven narrative. A new core argument was discovered: STAIG is good at clustering but has excessive homogenization at the boundary, while Attention fusion is more suitable for immune microenvironment retrieval. Additionally, the issue of invisible query points in the HD Niche Query visualization was fixed. The planning and execution of the three draft annotation edits were completed, including rewriting Intro P1, expanding Abstract, and R5/R6. Three new cross-slice query experiments were run, and the HD Niche Query visualization was repaired.
Tasks Architecture & Strategy ✅ Three new RM-IDEAL cross-slice experiments — Ran 151673↔151674 (same donor D3), 151508↔151509 (same donor D1), and 151673↔151669 (cross-donor D3↔D2) benchmark experiments. Each experiment took about 40 minutes of CPU computation. The complete results have been backed up. ✅ Draft annotation planning — Analyzed three handwritten annotations in draft_en.md (missing query motivation in Abstract, Intro P1 needs to start with main figures, R5 needs to be expanded to 6 paragraphs with QueST+TLS). Explored the code library and experimental results, and generated a detailed execution plan that was approved. ✅ R5 expansion to 6 paragraphs + citation of QueST — Added QueST citations and explanations of the unique value of cross-section retrieval to R5 P1. Updated the method description in P2. In R6 P3, the placeholder was replaced with a clustering-retrieval balance argument based on actual HD immune analysis data. Both Chinese and English versions were updated. ✅ Rewriting Introduction P1 — Rewrote the original general background paragraph into a 6-sentence roadmap structure, directly corresponding to the Results titles for R1-R6. The first sentence included the key number 0.26→0.55. Both Chinese and English versions were updated. ✅ Supplementary patch-level query motivation in Abstract — Added a mechanism explanation at the end of the Abstract about frozen FM embedding supporting cross-slice retrieval. Also added an explanation of the clustering-retrieval balance found (later updated from ‘immune boundary discovery’ to a more accurate ‘balance’). Both Chinese and English versions were updated. ✅ HD immune microenvironment Niche Query analysis — Created a new analyze_hd_immune_niche.py script to calculate T cell/B cell marker scores. Identified 23 T cell-enriched spots at the epithelial-connective tissue boundary. Compared the retrieval results of PCA/Attention/STAIG methods. Implementation & Fixes ✅ HD Niche Query visualization repair — Fixed the issue where the query points were invisible in visualize_hd_niche_query.py: increased the star size from 20 to 26 with a white outer circle of 32, marked all 4 panels, and added arrow labels to the H&E panel. Three images were re-generated. ✅ W14 weekly report generation — Created WEEKLY_REPORT_2026_W14.md, which includes 8 sections such as Leiden experiments, HD experiments, paper writing, code updates, etc., with complete numerical data tables. Problems & Solutions Critical Issues 1. In HD immune analysis, T cell markers were found to be extremely sparse (1278/17502 spots, average 0.014), and TLS markers were even sparser (242 spots). The original argument that ‘multi-modal captures immune infiltration better than single-modal’ did not hold. Solution: Modified the narrative in R6 P3 from ‘multi-modal identification of immune infiltration boundaries’ to ‘clustering-retrieval balance’. 96% of STAIG retrieval results were concentrated in the epithelial domain (overhomogenization). PCA gene expression showed 3.8x higher T cell enrichment than STAIG’s 2.2x. Attention fusion performed most evenly at the boundary (60/40 Epithelium/CT). Key Insight: Frozen FM embedding’s optimal clustering strategy (spatial graph contrast learning) and retrieval strategy (attention fusion) are different. This is a more meaningful discovery than expected.
General Issues 2. The benchmark_rm_ideal.py defaults to writing results to the same summary.csv file. The results of each new pair of experiments will overwrite those of the previous pair. Solution: Back up the results immediately after each pair of experiments (e.g., summary_151673_151674.csv). The results from 151673↔151669 were manually reconstructed using heredoc (since they were overwritten by the results of 151674). Key Insight: Long-running batch experiments require per-pair output directories to be designed at the script level, or the results should be aggregated after the pipeline is completed.
3. conda activate does not produce any output in the background commands. The output files of the three experimental processes (bgqoqyepm, etc.) are always 0 bytes. Solution: Use [[[HYMTPH_1]] instead of [[[HYMTPH_2]] to ensure proper background output writing. Key Insight: The background bash command environment of Claude Code does not inherit the shell’s activate state. Condna run must be used to ensure environment isolation.
4. The same experiment was started twice with two different conda activate and conda run versions. Running 6 benchmark processes simultaneously wastes resources. Solution: Use ps aux to find the PID of the conda run process and kill the duplicate 3 processes. Keep the original conda activate version. Key Insight: When the background process output is empty, it is necessary to confirm whether the process is actually running (ps aux) before submitting again.
5. In the HD Niche Query visualization, the query points (red stars) are not clearly visible, making them unusable for paper presentation. Solution: Fixed the script: increased the star size from markersize=20 to 26, added a white background marker (size=32), marked all 4 panels, and added arrows and text labels to the first panel. Key Insight: In the paper figure, the query spot requires multiple visual cues (color, size, outer circle, label). A single red star is completely hidden behind the heatmap background.
Human vs AI Approaches Strategic Level Correction of TLS/T cell arguments Role Approach Human Humans wrote ‘TLS T cells’, expecting to find areas enriched with TLS/T cells as a multi-modal advantage in HD colon cancer data. AI AI ran the actual analysis and found that T cell markers were extremely sparse (7.3% of spots). Moreover, PCA gene expression detected T cell enrichment better than STAIG’s multi-modal approach. Therefore, a more accurate ‘clustering-retrieval balance’ argument was proposed. Difference Analysis: Humans’ intuition was based on the prior knowledge that TLS was a common immune structure in colon cancer. However, AI found that the immune infiltration in this area was insufficient to support the original argument. AI also discovered a more theoretically valuable balance. Identification and priority of paper annotations Role Approach Human Humans already knew what the three annotations were (query motivation, Intro structure, R5 expansion) and knew they needed to cite QueST and add TLS/T cell biology cases. AI AI first read the files and compared the differences, then explained the meaning of each annotation. Finally, it explored the code library to understand the experimental results and feasibility. Difference Analysis: Humans provided direction and scientific requirements, while AI was responsible for verifying feasibility and executing details. AI was more comprehensive in exploring the code base, but its judgment on which experiments had results and which needed to be created depended on code exploration. Implementation Level Visual quality assessment Role Approach Human Humans directly looked at the image and found that the ‘query points were not visible’, giving clear user feedback. AI AI also confirmed the problem after reading the image file, but needed human intervention to notice it. AI identified and fixed the specific issues at the code level (too small markers, lack of comparison). Difference Analysis: Humans’ visual perception and paper usability judgment were better than AI’s code review. AI was more efficient in implementing fixes. AI Limitations General Limitations After generating visual charts, there was no active verification of the quality (such as query point visibility). Users needed feedback to identify problems. There was also a lack of proactive QA regarding image usability. The issue of benchmark_rm_ideal.py overwriting summary.csv was not detected in advance. As a result, the results from 151673↔151669 were overwritten. It was necessary to manually reconstruct the data using heredoc based on memory. Initially, [[[HYMTPH_3]] was mistakenly used as the prefix for background commands, resulting in all three experimental processes having zero output. This wasted time in troubleshooting. Condna run should be used as the preferred method. Due to uncertainty about the status of the first batch of processes, the same three experiments were submitted twice, causing six processes to run simultaneously. It was necessary to manually kill the duplicate processes. Learnings Key Learnings It was discovered that there are two possible optimal fusion strategies between clustering and retrieval: STAIG’s spatial graph contrast learning is most suitable for global domain clustering (Average ARI 0.546), but its strong spatial homogenization characteristics lead to excessive focus on the same domain at the boundary (96% Epithelium). Attention fusion (60/40 distribution) performs more evenly at the boundary microenvironment retrieval. This is the new core argument in R6. Results of the three new RM-IDEAL experiments: For the same donor across slices (151673↔151674), the Spearman ρ for Layer 3 was as high as 0.86. For the same donor (151508↔151509), the same_label@50 reached 100%. Across donors (151673↔151669), a negative correlation appeared in Layer 3 (ρ=-0.73), indicating systematic differences in the morphological structure of Layer 3 between different donors. In HD colon cancer data, T cell markers (CD3D/CD3E, etc.) were extremely sparse at 8μm spots (7.3% expressed). The ability of PCA gene expression to detect T cell enrichment (3.8x) was higher than that of visual + gene fusion (STAIG 2.2x), indicating that T cell signals are mainly driven by gene expression rather than tissue morphology. The strategy at the beginning of the Introduction: Directly presenting the most important numerical findings (ARI from 0.26 to 0.55) was more persuasive than providing a general background. Having one Results section title for each paragraph improved the consistency of the paper structure. Conversation Summaries ✅ Paper editing and execution: Rewriting Intro P1, updating Abstract, expanding R5/R6 (Chinese and English synchronized) 21:30:00.000 | claude_code The AI executed the rewrite of the 6-sentence roadmap structure in Introduction P1 (both Chinese and English), added query motivation at the end of the Abstract, and added QueST citations to R5 P1/P2. In R6 P3, the placeholder was updated to actual content. At the same time, three new cross-slice RM-IDEAL experiments and HD immune niche analysis were run in the background. The HD analysis found that T cell markers were extremely sparse. The unexpected results of STAIG’s overhomogenization at the boundary prompted the AI to modify the arguments in R6 P3 and the Abstract from ‘immune infiltration recognition’ to ‘clustering-retrieval balance’. This was the most important narrative adjustment in this edit.
✅ Three cross-slice RM-IDEAL experiments completed: Comparison of conservation between the same donor and cross-donor slices 22:00:00.000 | claude_code All three experiments took about 2 hours (CPU calculation). The same donor groups (151673↔151674, 151508↔151509) showed high Spearman ρ (up to 0.86) and same_label@50 (up to 100%) for Layer 3. Cross-donor groups (151673↔151669) showed a strong negative correlation (ρ=-0.73) for Layer 3. The results revealed the high conservation of the tissue microarchitecture between the same donor slices and the systematic differences between cross-donor slices, providing key quantitative evidence for R5.
✅ Draft paper annotation analysis: Identification of three editing suggestions and development of an execution plan 21:02:12.402 | claude_code Users provided two versions of the draft paper. The AI identified three handwritten annotations through file comparison. The AI explored the code library, existing experimental results, and RM-IDEAL implementations using multiple sub-agents. It completed a comprehensive evaluation of the feasibility of the annotations and generated a detailed execution plan that included computational experiments and text editing. The plan was approved by the user. The core strategy was to run new experiments in the background while performing text editing without relying on the results, improving parallel efficiency.
✅ HD Niche Query visualization repair: Issue of invisible query points 23:00:00.000 | claude_code Users reported that the query points in the HD niche query visualization were not visible. After the AI read the image and confirmed the problem, it modified the annotation code in visualize_hd_niche_query.py: increased the star size from markersize=20 to 26, added a white background marker (size=32) to enhance contrast, and extended the markings to all 4 panels. An arrow and text label were added to the H&E panel. After re-generating the 3 images, the query points were clearly visible.
✅ W14 weekly report generation: Summary of this week’s work (including complete experimental numbers) 22:28:27.357 | claude_code Users requested a summary of this week’s work. The AI reconstructed the complete content of W14 through git logs, file modification times, and experimental logs. It generated a detailed weekly report markdown file with 11 sections, including complete data tables for Leiden experiments, rankings of 12 methods for HD experiments, a list of visualization scripts, and plans for the next week.
Token Usage Total: $3.25 — 3.1M tokens

title: Bug Journal 2026-04-06

content:
Weekly Report — 2026-W14 (2026-03-30 ~ 2026-04-05) This week, three main tasks were carried out simultaneously: preparation for the MIHD multi-modal spatial transcriptomics paper (5 days, DCC); completion of the TEDDY full-embedding benchmark, Leiden comparison experiments, HD fusion evaluation, and writing of a bilingual paper framework. The research focus was established as “zero-shot FM embedding fusion” with differentiated studies. Additionally, systematic fixes were made to TokenMonitor Linux FloatBall over 5 days. After multiple iterations, the issue evolved from symptom-based fixes to a fixed-window + GDK Input Shape architecture重构. All 21 code review issues in PR #4 were also resolved. The Life-Copilot Discord Bot was completely reimplemented over 2 days, resulting in a net reduction of 2823 lines of code. A multi-CLI agent architecture was introduced, and new features such as screen time tracking, Lark input channels, and MCP tool integration were implemented. Furthermore, research on 63 papers from the NeurIPS 2025 D&B Track and setup of a new Linux workstation environment were completed.
Weekly Overview Indicator Value Date Range 2026-03-30 ~ 2026-04-05 Active Days 7 / 7 Total Conversations 29 Projects Involved 12 Tasks Completed 44 Ongoing Tasks 9 Total Tokens 328,660,991 Total Cost $218.53 Claude Code Tokens 273,046,863 Claude Code Costs $189.80 Codex Tokens 55,614,128 Codex Costs $28.73 Average Daily Cost $31.22 Project Progress MIHD Multi-modal Spatial Transcriptomics Paper (5 active days) — 🔄 Active Completed:
Completed extraction of TEDDY-G 400M full-embedding data for 11 slices and multi-strategy evaluations. It was confirmed that the STAIG framework is highly robust for gene encoders (Teddy ARI=0.495 vs PCA ARI=0.497). Integrated Leiden clustering (binary search matching n_clusters) and STHD pathology GT, completed full-Leiden comparison on DLPFC (9/9 groups). The key conclusion was that high-quality embeddings are superior to clustering methods (STAIG+mclust=0.546 vs STAIG+Leiden=0.513). Completed evaluation of 13 HD fusion strategies. Key negative findings included: simple fusion (concat ARI=0.191) being inferior to single-modal PCA (ARI=0.351), and adaln crashing on large-scale HD data (ARI=0.003). Established the core narrative framework for the paper (Setting=No system study on zero-shot FM embedding fusion/Finding=Fusion Strategies > Simple Fusion is a Trap, Spatial Graph Learning is Key/Application=Cross-slice Retrieval). A complete bilingual paper framework was created by referring to Yuan et al. 2024 Nature Methods. Created 4 GPU Slurm job scripts and 3 paper analysis scripts (cross-slice query aggregation, figure visualization, HD Marker Gene visualization). The docs/ folder was reorganized, and 12 expired scripts were archived. Blocked Issues:
⚠️ Basic contrastive HD fusion experiment is still running (48h limit). The HD learning-based fusion result set is not complete. ⚠️ The root cause of the 151676 slice STAIG model collapse has not been thoroughly analyzed. It is currently handled by skipping it. TokenMonitor FloatBall Linux Adaptation (5 active days) — 🔄 Active Completed:
Fixed Wayland positioning issues in the Linux main window (forcing GDK_BACKEND=x11 to fallback to XWayland). Completed all 21 code review fixes in PR #4: assert!() → Result propagation, SSH lock across asynchronous boundaries, path traversal whitelist protection, I/O error differentiation, Svelte 5 double fetch, FloatBall listener race conditions. There were zero Rust warnings and 199 front-end tests passed. Identified the root cause of GTK size negotiation issues (WebKitGTK natural size causing set_size() to be ineffective). This was resolved by using with_webview() to call widget.set_size_request(). Also, deferred repositioning, multi-monitor misjudgment, pointerdown race conditions, and scroll bars were fixed. Completed the fixed-window + GDK Input Shape architecture重构: The window permanently maintained an expanded size. Cairo::Region was used to control the click-through area. Approximately 150 lines of probe/corrective patch code were removed. 284 Rust+205 front-end tests passed. Organized git history (git reset –soft compressed 24 commits into 2 semantic commits) and pushed it to GitHub. A new Linux workstation version v0.6.1 was built (deb/rpm/AppImage). Blocked Issues:
⚠️ The fixed-window + GDK Input Shape solution needs to be verified on actual devices. ⚠️ There are architectural constraints regarding Linux WM clamping. The correct solution is to fix it with a fixed large size and internal CSS. This needs to be fully verified. Life-Copilot Discord Bot (2 active days) — 🔄 Active Completed:
Completely reimplemented 3170 lines of complex intent routing into a four-layer architecture consisting of CLIAdapter+SessionManager+MCP Server (19 tools) + Skills (4). A net reduction of 2823 lines was achieved, with 419 tests passed. Implemented Screen Time Tracker (GNOME Introspect D-Bus → xprop → cmdline three-level downgrade, AI application classifier, independent asyncio.Task sampling loop, 26/26 tests passed). Integrated Lark Bot for quick idea capture (daemon thread asyncio bridge solved lark-oapi blocking issues, 10 categories of automatic classification). Added Codex/Anthropic backend. The fallback chain was extended to 11 levels. Fixed two overlapping problems in MCP tool calls: Claude -p requires –mcp-config and –permission-mode bypassPermissions. Added structlog structured logging + complete dialogue JSONL audit. Blocked Issues:
⚠️ Accessing OpenReview reviewers’ comments is blocked due to a general 403 error in the API. The OPENREVIEW_USERNAME/OPENREVIEW_PASSWORD environment variables are required. New Linux Workstation Environment Setup (2 active days) — ✅ Completed Completed:
Completed the full system initialization (NVIDIA driver confirmation, Miniconda, SSH key migration of 7 files, TinyProxy proxy, GitHub SSH verification). Verified that RTX 5090 has BF16=220 TFLOPS/s. Configured gadget rclone for two-way synchronization (gdrive remote, covering four types of data: summarize/research/website/test). Installed Claude Code skills and 12 plugins. Research on NeurIPS 2025 D&B Track (1 active day) — ✅ Completed Completed:
Analyzed 63 papers under the gadget research scout framework. Deeply analyzed 5 top papers, generating a three-dimensional report on writing structure/ publication elements/knowledge extraction, and a comprehensive writing guide. Identified 3 actionable research directions: multi-dimensional evaluation benchmark for VLM credibility, TTA and fine-tuning synergistic effect benchmarks, online continuous learning timing benchmarks. Key Tasks ✅ TokenMonitor PR #4 All 21 Code Review Fixes (April 02) — System fixes were carried out according to CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM priorities: changing assert!() to Result propagation, SSH lock across asynchronous boundaries (Clone released in advance), path traversal whitelist protection, I/O error differentiation, Svelte 5 double fetch, FloatBall listener race conditions, NaiveDate overflow. There were zero Rust compilation warnings and 199 front-end tests passed. ✅ Life-Copilot Discord Bot Complete Reimplementation (April 04) — Replaced the complex intent routing with a four-layer architecture consisting of CLIAdapter+SessionManager+MCP Server + Skills. Supports Claude/Codex/Gemini CLI fallback chains. The Skills JSON protocol triggers Discord UI decoupling. A net reduction of 2823 lines was achieved, with 419 tests passed. ✅ TokenMonitor FloatBall Linux GTK Size Negotiation Fix (April 04) — Identified the root cause of WebKitGTK natural size causing set_size() to be ineffective. Resolved this issue by using with_webview() to call widget.set_size_request(). Also, deferred repositioning, multi-monitor expansion direction misjudgment, pointerdown race conditions, GTK ScrolledWindow scroll bars, and two-stage collapse were fixed. All tests passed. 🔄 TokenMonitor FloatBall Linux Fixed-Window + GDK Input Shape Architecture Reimplementation (April 05) — Reorganized the float ball from a dynamic resize architecture to a fixed expanded-size window + GDK input_shape_combine_region control for click-through. Eliminated the systematic WM +305px offset. Approximately 150 lines of probe/corrective patch code were removed. 284 Rust+205 front-end tests passed. Waiting for final runtime verification. ✅ MIHD Paper Narrative Line Organization and Bilingual Framework Creation (April 03) — Established the research focus as “zero-shot FM embedding fusion” with a three-layer narrative structure: Simple Fusion is a Trap → Spatial Graph Learning is the Bridge → Correct Fusion Transcends Single Modal. A complete bilingual paper framework was created by referring to Yuan et al. 2024 Nature Methods (including Abstract/Introduction/Results R1-R6/Discussion/Methods). ✅ Leiden vs KMeans Full-Embedding Comparison Experiments (April 01) — Completed comparison of Leiden clustering on 9/9 combinations. The key conclusion was that STAIG+mclust(0.546) > STAIG+Leiden(0.513). Leiden significantly improved weak embeddings (+29%), but had little additional benefit for strong embeddings (+6%). This established the core argument that high-quality embeddings are superior to clustering methods. ✅ MIHD Fig 1-6 Framework Planning (March 31) — Referred to MISO, STAIG, QueST, and Yuan’s papers to plan Fig1=framework, Fig2=DLPFC benchmark, Fig3=Ablation, Fig4=HD application, Fig5=Cross-slice Query, and Fig6=Query bio-discovery. ECL YAML and plans.md were written. ✅ Life-Copilot Screen Time Tracker Full Implementation (April 04) — Planned a 9-stage ccplan + Critic adversarial review (pre-detected Wayland compatibility risks). Implemented GNOME Introspect D-Bus → xprop → cmdline three-level downgrade window tracking. AI application classifier (100+ pre-settings + persistent cache). Independent asyncio.Task sampling loop. Passed 26/26 tests. ✅ Life-Copilot Discord Bot MCP Tool Call Fixes (April 05) — Fixed two overlapping issues: Claude -p requires explicit –mcp-config to load project-level MCP configuration; non-interactive mode requires –permission-mode bypassPermissions. The –allowedTools wildcard was unreliable due to competition with MCP server initialization. By running the Claude -p command directly, the root cause of permission_denials field issues was identified. ✅ NeurIPS 2025 D&B Track Systematic Paper Research (April 04) — Searched for 63 papers and deeply analyzed 5 top papers (TTA-VLM, DATE-LM, CAB, KG-RAG Pitfalls, DCAD-2000). Generated a three-dimensional analysis report on writing structure/ publication elements/knowledge extraction, and a comprehensive writing guide. Identified 3 actionable research directions. ✅ Teddy + STAIG Fusion Evaluation (Full 11 Slices) (March 30) — Completed evaluation of 10/11 slices (skipped model collapse on 151676 models). Avg ARI=0.495 compared to PCA+STAIG’s 0.497. On NMI, Teddy won 6 out of 10 slices. This revealed the high robustness of the STAIG framework for gene encoders, providing experimental evidence for future encoder replacement strategies. ✅ Life-Copilot Lark Bot + Codex/Anthropic Backend + Full-Linkage Token Verification (April 04) — Implemented ThoughtCaptureService shared service layer + AutoCategorizer (10 categories: Chinese & English) + daemon thread asyncio bridge. Added CodexProvider and AnthropicProvider. The fallback chain was extended to 11 levels. Created key_validator.py to verify all services with real APIs. Fixed Google Calendar scope mismatch issues. ✅ FEAT-003: STHD Ground Truth Integration (March 31) — Added a load_sthd_annotations() function to automatically load 545913 annotations. Combined CE and Tumor (Neoplasm+Non-neoplastic Epithelium→Epithelium). 17502/17502 spots had 100% match rate. 3 valid clusters were identified, providing a GT basis for HD ARI/NMI evaluation. ✅ TokenMonitor Linux Main Window Wayland Positioning Fix (April 02) — Identified the Wayland protocol-level prohibition of client-side absolute position settings. After detecting WAYLAND_DISPLAY in the run() entry, forced GDK_BACKEND=x11 to make the application run via XWayland. 283 Rust tests passed. ✅ MIHD GPU Slurm Scripts and Paper Analysis Scripts Batch Creation (April 02) — Created 4 independent Slurm GPU job scripts (STAIG/contrastive/LLaVA/AdaLN) and 3 Python analysis scripts (cross-slice query aggregation, paper figure visualization, HD Marker Gene visualization). All scripts passed syntax verification. ✅ Life-Copilot Structured Logging and Operation Audit System (April 05) — Replaced stdlib logging with structlog. Implemented dev-color output + prod JSON format + 10MB rotation. Created audit_log.py to write complete dialogue JSONL audits in Discord on_message, FastAPI middleware, and CLISelector.send. Fixed ExtraAdder omission issues. 420 tests passed. ✅ TEDDY-G 400M Full-Embedding Extraction (11 Slices) (March 30) — Extracted 1024d gene embeddings for DLPFC’s 11 slices in the conda teddy environment and cached them in embeddings_cache/gene/teddy/. Total size was 185MB. Also extracted UNI2 staig_strict visual features (256×256 patch, no ImageNet normalization, frequency-domain filtering). 🔄 HD Learning-Based Fusion GPU Experiments (April 01) — staig(ARI=0.258)/llava(ARI=0.201)/adaln(ARI=0.003) were completed. The basic_contrastive experiment is still running (48h limit). Key negative findings included: adaln crashing on large-scale HD data (17502 spots), revealing that data size is a key variable in fusion methods. ✅ docs/ Folder Reorganization and Code Gap Analysis (April 03) — Archived 5 expired documents. Created a three-level structure in docs/paper/figures/. Systemically analyzed the capabilities required by draft_en.md vs existing code. Archived 12 expired scripts. Planned Leiden result recovery scripts, figure generation scripts, spatial continuity metrics, and support for 4 code tasks with multiple seeds. Issues and Solutions 1. GTK bottom-up size negotiation caused FloatBall windows to always be 400x400. Tauri’s set_size() was completely ineffective [TokenMonitor] (April 04)### Next Week’s PlansNext week’s key focus areas are as follows: (1) MIHD paper – Collect the complete HD evaluation table for basic_contrastive HD experiments and advance the code gap tasks (Leiden result restoration script, figure generation script, spatial continuity metrics, multi-seed support). Start generating papers and writing drafts to reach a submission-ready level.
(2) TokenMonitor – Finalize the fixed-window + GDK Input Shape solution on real Linux devices. Confirm that the WM clamp issue has been fully resolved. If verification is successful, submit a PR for merging. Close this long-term task related to Linux FloatBall fixes after nearly two weeks.
(3) Life-Copilot – Obtain OpenReview account credentials to unlock access to NeurIPS review comments. Verify the stability of Screen Time Tracker in production environments. Continuously monitor the performance of the 11-level fallback chain in real-world usage.
(4) Research exploration – Translate the research conclusions from NeurIPS D&B Track into specific experimental designs. Evaluate the feasibility of a multi-dimensional benchmark for assessing VLM credibility. Also follow up on the progress of the AionUI remote SSH access feature request.
