title: Bug Journal 2026-04-05

content:
Daily Report — 2026-04-04 Daily Overview What was done: Worked on four projects simultaneously: TokenMonitor fixed fundamental issues with GTK size negotiation and a series of FloatBall Linux bugs, while continuing to debug position offsets caused by WM clamp; Gadget Research Scout completed research and writing guidelines for 63 papers presented at NeurIPS 2025 D&B Track 63; Life-Copilot reorganized the Discord Bot dialogue system into a multi-CLI agent architecture, adding new features like screen time tracking, Lark idea capture, and backend integration with Codex/Anthropic; also explored the usability of AionUI and the feasibility of remote SSH access. How it was done: Identified underlying system mechanisms such as GTK bottom-up size negotiation through runtime log analysis; used ccplan + Critic agents to systematically plan multiple functions, identifying high-value risks like Wayland compatibility and lark-oapi thread blocking in advance; accelerated large-scale refactoring with subagent parallel development; completed research paper analysis using an arXiv multi-stage LLM evaluation pipeline. Impact: Life-Copilot reduced by 2823 lines of code, gaining new capabilities like screen time awareness, Lark input channels, and an 11-level AI downgrade chain; TokenMonitor successfully ran FloatBall under Linux X11 for the first time (with controllable sizes,吸附 functionality, and no transition when folding), and accumulated a comprehensive diagnostic of Linux transparent floating window architectures; NeurIPS research provided researchers with a practical submission guide. Deeply debugged and fixed multiple system-level bugs in TokenMonitor FloatBall under Linux (fundamental GTK size negotiation issues, WM clamp position offsets), completed systematic research on 63 papers presented at NeurIPS 2025 D&B Track 63, and implemented several major features in Life-Copilot (reorganizing Discord Bot architecture by 2823 lines, fully implementing Screen Time Tracker, fast idea capture with Lark Bot, backend integration with Codex/Anthropic), while exploring the feasibility of AionUI remote SSH access.
Tasks Architecture & Strategy ✅ Fixed fundamental issues with TokenMonitor FloatBall in Linux (GTK size negotiation + series of bugs) — Identified and fixed the root cause of the GTK bottom-up size negotiation causing windows to always be 400x400: called widget.set_size_request() through with_webview() callback, removed set_resizable switch (to prevent locking before asynchronous callback execution), and made Linux permanently resizable. Synchronized fixes included missing deferred repositioning (with exponential backoff retries), misjudgment of display expansion direction on multiple monitors (fixed to monitor.position.x + width/2), pointerdown race conditions (dragGeneration + pendingPointerId), GTK ScrolledWindow scroll bars (GTK layer set_policy Never), dragging with flickering (only calling set_position once instead of resizing), CSS capsule size overflow (min(56px, 100%)), two-stage folding when folding (Linux path first shrinks window in Rust atomic way then CSS), and semantic divergence in testing and implementation. All tests passed, svelte-check zero errors. ✅ Completely reorganized Life-Copilot Discord Bot dialogue system — Replaced 3170 lines of complex intent routing (semantic/, 24 handle* methods, conversation_history) with an architecture of CLIAdapter + SessionManager + MCP Server (19 tools) + Skills (4), supporting Claude/Codex/Gemini CLI fallback chains, and decoupling Discord UI (Views/Modals) through Skills JSON protocol. Total of 2823 lines removed from the project, with 419 tests passed. 🔄 Deeply debugged TokenMonitor Linux floating window WM clamp positioning issues — Analyzed backend.log to confirm that WM clamping when closing windows with old expanded width resulted in a systematic offset of ~305px. After multiple attempts including introducing new offsets with GTK gravity, GDK move_resize, and probe corrections (dual-frame flickering), the root cause was identified as a lack of a single reliable geometric source for Linux transparent borderless Tauri floating windows (three layers of geometry not synchronized). The correct solution would be to use a fixed large-size window plus internal CSS for expansion and folding. ✅ Systematic research and comprehensive writing guidelines for NeurIPS 2025 D&B Track papers — Searched for 63 papers under the Gadget Research Scout framework, selected 20 highly relevant ones, and deeply analyzed five top papers: TTA-VLM, DATE-LM, CAB, KG-RAG Pitfalls, and DCAD-2000. Generated a three-dimensional analysis report including writing structure, publication elements, and knowledge extraction, along with a comprehensive writing guide (five-paragraph narrative, review focus points, reusable methodologies). Also generated three actionable research directions (multidimensional evaluation benchmarks for VLM credibility, benchmark for training-time fine-tuning and TTA synergy, and online continuous learning timing benchmarks). ✅ Fixed four accumulated bugs in Life-Copilot Bots — Fixed issues where MCP Server did not actually serve (created new main.py + mcp_servers.json), ClaudeCLIAdapter.is_available() lacked async function causing silent downgrade to Codex (TypeError in await synchronous method was swallowed by except), butler system commands never injected into Claude CLI (added –append-system-prompt), and multiple rounds of dialogue context were lost (_process_input never passed last_assistant_message and last_intent parameters). Added ensure_instruction_files() to generate AGENTS.md/GEMINI.md with idempotence. ✅ Fully implemented Life-Copilot Screen Time Tracker — Implemented cross-platform window tracking through a 9-stage planning process (Critic discovered key risks like Wayland compatibility during review), including AI application classifier (100+ preset values, persistent cache, async lock), date-partitioned JSONL storage, independent asyncio.Task sampling loop, and integration with PreferenceLearner/ContextAssembler/BackgroundCoordinator. 26/26 tests passed, zero regressions. ✅ Implemented Life-Copilot Lark Bot with fast idea capture platform, Codex/Anthropic backend integration, and full token verification — Planned the Lark Bot through ccplan, Critic preemptively discovered two critical issues with lark-oapi thread blocking and ThoughtStore not being a singleton, resulting in the implementation of ThoughtCaptureService shared service layer, AutoCategorizer (10 Chinese-English pairs), daemon thread asyncio bridge, and REST API endpoints. Added CodexProvider and AnthropicProvider, extending the fallback chain to 11 levels. Created key_validator.py to verify all services with real APIs, fixing Google Calendar scope mismatch (calendarList → events.list), and rechecked the cron job every 06:00. ❌ Obtaining OpenReview review comments — OpenReview API returned 403 Forbidden for unauthenticated access. NeurIPS D&B Track used a separate venue ID instead of the main conference path, causing the function to fail completely. Account credentials (OPENREVIEW_USERNAME/OPENREVIEW_PASSWORD) were required to proceed. ✅ Explored the usability of AionUI and feasibility of remote SSH access — Investigated AionUI’s model switching, thinking depth configuration, and adaptability across CLI platforms (Codex/Gemini). Confirmed that AionUI remote agents only support URL connections and cannot directly connect to the Claude Code CLI process on a headless server (CLI processes have no HTTP endpoints, which is a fundamental limitation at the architectural level). It is recommended to use tmux as a temporary alternative and submit a feature request to the AionUI team. Implementation & Fixes ✅ Fixed environmental dependencies and idempotency issues in the research pipeline — Installed the arxiv/openreview-py package and used absolute paths to call scripts; cleared 5 cache files in the insight cache directory to resolve cache hits on old results; manually deleted 4 duplicate daily reports written due to multiple pipeline executions in overview.md. Problems & Solutions Critical Issues 1. FloatBall windows always remain 400x400 in Linux, Tauri’s set_size() is ineffective Solution: GTK bottom-up size negotiation takes max(window_requested, widget_natural_size), and WebKitGTK’s natural size of approximately 200x200 causes window locks. Fix: Called widget.set_size_request() through with_webview() in the GTK main thread, removed set_resizable switch (with_webview is asynchronous, set_resizable(false) will queue up and lock the old size before the callback execution), and made Linux permanently resizable.
Key Insight: Tauri’s set_size/set_min_size/set_max_size operations follow GTK window-level hints, and GTK bottom-up negotiation ignores these hints to meet the widget’s natural size. The callback from with_webview() has not yet executed, so subsequent Tauri API calls are still FIFO asynchronous queues.
2. Visual flicker when folding in Linux: Two-stage sequence exposes intermediate states + WM clamp’s old width causes systematic coordinate offsets Solution: Two-stage sequence: macOS has a 250ms animation buffer, while Linux disables transitions, making the intermediate state glaring. In Linux, first shrink the window to 56px using Rust atomic operations, then set CSS expanded to false (at this point 100% = 56px, so the change is invisible). WM clamp issue: GTK gravity does not work on this WM (instead, it introduces systematic offsets), probe + corrective reapplication produces double-frame flickering. The fundamental solution is to use a fixed large-size window + internal CSS for expansion and folding.
Key Insight: Disabling CSS transitions requires synchronizing JS/Rust timing logic; GTK gravity is not supported by all WMs;事后补救措施本身就是可见闪烁的来源,根本解法是让WM一次性接受正确的坐标。
3. Three-layer break in Life-Copilot MCP Server: There is a Calendar token, but the agent still reports a connection failure Solution: Three places simultaneously broke: FastMCP was instantiated but not called run(), .claude/mcp_servers.json was missing, and Butler commands (including MCP tool list) never reached the Claude CLI child process. Created main.py to start stdio transport, new mcp_servers.json, and added –append-system-prompt to ClaudeAdapter. All 19 MCP tools worked normally again.
Key Insight: “Code written” and “code called” are two different things; creating an object with the factory pattern does not equal starting a service; external programs need configuration files to discover services; App verification layer and CLI tool context injection are two separate layers; token verification passing does not mean the CLI tool received the correct context.
4. Initial solution for Linux Screen Time Tracker used xdotool, which does not support Wayland (user’s machine runs GNOME 50 Wayland) Solution: Conducted feasibility checks to confirm Wayland environment, replaced the main path with GNOME Introspect D-Bus (org.gnome.Shell.Introspect.GetWindows()), used xprop as a backup for XWayland, and PID→cmdline as the final downgrade. All subprocess calls had a 5-second timeout and proc.kill().
Key Insight: GNOME 41+ provides a standard Introspect D-Bus interface, which is the correct way to obtain active windows under Wayland. This issue was actively discovered by Critic sub-agent during review. If overlooked, it would result in the function being completely unusable on user machines.
5. AionUI could not connect to the Claude Code CLI on a remote headless server via URL or other methods (e.g., SSH) Solution: AionUI remote agent architecture assumes that the agent exposes an HTTP port, while the CLI process has no HTTP endpoint. This is fundamentally incompatible. It is recommended to use tmux/screen to manage multiple sessions as a temporary alternative, and submit a feature request to the AionUI team.
Key Insight: AionUI database already has a remote_agents table (with allow_insecure field), indicating that the development team is building remote agent capabilities. SSH direct connection may be a future roadmap direction.
6. X11 WMs reject positioning of half-out-of-screen windows, causing drifting during adsorption and folding Solution: X11 WMs like Mutter/GNOME place non-decorated windows within the visible area, rejecting positions where x < 0 or x > screen_width - window_width. Linux uses edge_offset=0, while macOS/Windows use edge_offset=ball_size/2.
Key Insight: macOS and Windows allow non-decorated windows to partially exceed the screen boundary, while X11 WMs usually do not allow it. Different edge offsets are required for both platforms.
7. ClaudeCLIAdapter.is_available() lacks async function, causing Claude to silently downgrade to Codex and time out Solution: Ordinary await methods do not throw compilation errors, but runtime TypeError is swallowed by except Exception. Claude was deemed unavailable. Adding the async keyword restored Claude availability to True.
Key Insight: In Python, await synchronous methods produce TypeError. If caught by a broad except Exception, it can lead to incorrect False results, resulting in a downgrade in functionality rather than an error message. This is very difficult to troubleshoot.
8. In multiple-round dialogues, the bot cannot understand cross-round references (DualVerifier parameter connection broken) Solution: DualVerifier, SemanticRouter, and KeywordScorer all have last_assistant_message and last_intent parameters, but they are never passed during _process_input() calls. Fixing the connection is sufficient; no changes to the classification logic are needed.### TokenMonitor**✅ Complete review of FloatBall Linux code, fixing four bugs and the root issue with GTK (size negotiation + collapse sequence)**
19:42:04.443 | claude_code
After discovering several inaccuracies in the logic of FloatBall Linux, the team conducted a thorough code review and identified and fixed 4 bugs (missing deferred repositioning, incorrect direction detection for multiple monitors, incomplete handling of pointerdown contention with pendingPointerId, test bifurcation). Additionally, 3 new Linux bugs were reported. The AI resolved the root issue through geometric corrections. After obtaining the backend.log, it was confirmed that the underlying problem was GTK bottom-up size negotiation. This was fixed by calling widget.set_size_request() via with_webview(), removing the set_resizable switch, and adding GTK layer ScrolledWindow set_policy to suppress the scrollbar. Finally, the CSS capsule was changed to min(56px, 100%), and the collapse sequence was reversed (Linux path first collapses the window using Rust, then via CSS), eliminating the two-stage transition. All tests passed, and svelte-check reported zero errors.
🔄 Linux floating ball WM clamp positioning issue: Log analysis and multiple debugging sessions led to an architectural limitation
00:57:20.724 | codex
Users reported erratic positioning of the floating ball on Linux. The AI analyzed logs and confirmed that the WM clamps the position based on the old expanded width, resulting in a systematic offset of ~305px. Multiple fixes, including those related to GTK gravity (introducing new offsets), GDK move_resize, probe corrections (double-frame flickering), and transparent safety margins (disrupting mouse tracking), did not fully resolve the issue. After users requested ceasing modifications, the AI systematically identified the root cause: Linux’s transparent borderless Tauri floating windows lacked a single reliable geometric source (the three layers—WM/GTK/WebView—were not synchronized). This was an architectural limitation. The correct solution would be to use fixed-sized windows and internal CSS for expansion and collapse.
Gadget Research Scout 🔄 Comprehensive research on 63 papers from the NeurIPS 2025 D&B track, 5 in-depth analyses, and a comprehensive writing guide
20:18:09.048 | claude_code
Based on the existing nips2025-benchmark project, the team fixed the dependency on arxiv/openreview-py and cleared the insight cache. After running the pipeline three times, they completed 63 searches, 20 highly relevant filters, and 5 in-depth analyses (TTA-VLM, DATE-LM, CAB, KG-RAG Pitfalls, DCAD-2000). They also generated a comprehensive writing guide covering five aspects: writing structure, publication requirements, and knowledge extraction. Additionally, they generated 3 actionable research directions. OpenReview’s review comments failed due to API 403 errors; account authentication is required.
Life Copilot ✅ Complete architecture restructuring of Discord Bot: From intention routing to multi-CLI agent architecture (planning + full implementation)
19:40:36.464 | claude_code
Users suggested replacing the complex intention routing with Claude/Codex/Gemini CLI sub-processes, which reduced the code to 3170 lines. After planning using ccplan (including 7 critical/high risks identified during adversarial review and feasibility testing for Codex session bugs), 18 tasks were defined. This was achieved through subagent-driven-development: CLIAdapter (three CLIs + fallback chain), SessionManager (ring buffer history), MCP Server (19 tools), Skills (4 SKILL.md files), Discord UI decoupled via Skills JSON protocol, slash command migration, and code cleanup. A total of 2823 lines were removed, and 419 tests passed.
✅ Complete requirement planning and implementation for Screen Time Tracker (9 ccplan phases + three parallel sub-agents)
03:19:12.046 | claude_code
Users requested the addition of a screen time tracker function to LifeCopilot. Using ccplan, Critic identified 32 issues during the adversarial review, including Wayland compatibility issues. User approval was given for one of the solutions (integration with EventBus). Three parallel sub-agents were implemented: abstracted PlatformTracker + GNOME Introspect D-Bus main path, AI classifier (with over 100 pre-built caches), date partitioned JSONL storage, independent asyncio.Task sampling loops, and integration with PreferenceLearner/ContextAssembler/BackgroundCoordinator. 26/26 tests passed, with zero regressions.
✅ Fixing four accumulated bugs after refactoring: MCP not serving, missing Claude async, Butler not injected, and lost context during multiple rounds
22:38:00.853 | claude_code
Users reported having tokens but the agent couldn’t connect to the calendar. It was found that all three layers were broken simultaneously (MCP not running, mcp_servers.json missing, Butler commands not sent). After fixing these issues, all 19 MCP tools became functional. Additionally, the async issue in ClaudeCLIAdapter was resolved (Claude available: True). Ensure_instruction_files() was added to generate AGENTS.md/GEMINI.md with idempotence. The issue causing cross-loop references was also fixed in _process_input.
✅ Quick idea capture for Feishou Bot, backend integration with Codex/Anthropic, and establishing a full-link token verification system
03:15:58.220 | claude_code
Three functions were integrated: (1) ccplan planning for Feishou Bot, with Critic identifying lark-oapi thread blocking (critical) and ThoughtStore not being a singleton (critical). This resulted in ThoughtCaptureService + AutoCategorizer + daemon thread asyncio bridge + REST API endpoints; (2) new CodexProvider and AnthropicProvider, extending the fallback chain to 11 levels; (3) key_validator.py verified all services’ real APIs. Issues like Google Calendar scope mismatch (calendarList → events.list) were fixed. A daily cron job was scheduled to recheck at 06:00. Startup logs showed the verification status of all services.
AionUI 🔍 Exploring how to use AionUI, evaluating the feasibility of remote SSH server access, and analyzing Skills across platforms
19:36:59.842 | claude_code
Users asked about model switching (/model) and effort level instructions (/effort). The AI provided commands that were ineffective (the AI acknowledged it was just a guess). The core requirement was to manage panel access to remote headless servers using Claude Code CLI. After discussing technical solutions, it was determined that AionUI’s remote agents only support URL connections, making them incompatible with CLI processes. Additionally, it was analyzed whether Skills could be used across platforms (markdown prompts, tool name mapping required for cross-platform migration). tmux was suggested as a temporary solution, and a feature request was made. The AI was corrected that Claude context window had been updated from 200k to 1M.
Token Usage Total: $132.61 — 207.1M tokens
summary: 深度调试并修复 TokenMonitor FloatBall 在 Linux 下的多个系统级 bug（GTK 尺寸协商根本问题、WM clamp 定位偏移等），完成 NeurIPS 2025 D&B Track 63 篇论文系统性研究调研，以及 Life-Copilot 多项重大功能落地（Discord Bot 架构重构净删 2823 行、Screen Time Tracker 全量实现、飞书 Bot 快速想法捕获、Codex/Anthropic 后端接入），并探索 AionUI 远程 SSH 接入可行性。

title: Bug Journal 2026-04-03

content:
Daily Report — 2026-04-02 Daily Overview Completed tasks: DCC advanced the experimental pipeline for MIHD multimodal spatial transcriptomics papers, created GPU Slurm scripts and visualization tools; DesktopLinux fixed bugs related to window positioning and floating ball interactions in Linux/Wayland, and systematically resolved all 21 code review issues in PR #4. Also completed the organization of git history and pushed it to GitHub, as well as initialized the gadget rclone environment and configured Claude Code skills and plugins. How it was done: DCC generated Slurm and analysis scripts in batches using a parallel Agent; DesktopLinux diagnosed and fixed platform bugs by addressing root causes such as Wayland protocol restrictions and GTK resizable flags. Code review feedback was processed with priority levels of CRITICAL→HIGH→MEDIUM. 24 commits were compressed into 2 semantic commits using git reset –soft, and then pushed with force-with-lease. Additionally, the rclone gdrive remote was configured to synchronize data in the cloud. Impact: The MIHD paper experimental pipeline now has complete GPU scripts and visualization tools, which can be directly submitted to the Slurm queue. The user experience of TokenMonitor Linux has been restored, and PR #4 has eliminated high-risk issues such as assert! panics and path traversal. Rust has zero compilation warnings, and all 199 front-end tests have passed, meeting the merging criteria. DesktopLinux has been connected to the gadget cloud synchronization system, and the development toolchain is now fully configured. DCC Completed tasks: Updated the ECL weekly plan document for the MIHD project. Added four independent experiments—STAIG, contrastive, LLaVA, and AdaLN—to the pipeline_config.yaml file. Created 4 Slurm GPU scripts, and simultaneously generated three analysis scripts: cross-slice query aggregation, paper figure visualization, and HD Marker Gene visualization. How it was done: Read the existing pipeline_config.yaml and slurm templates, added new independent experiment definitions and groups, and created three Python analysis scripts using a parallel Agent with syntax validation. Impact: The paper experimental pipeline now has complete GPU scripts and visualization tools, allowing for easy submission to the Slurm queue and generation of publication-ready figures. DesktopLinux Completed tasks: Fixed bugs related to TokenMonitor Linux/Wayland’s main window positioning and floating ball movement issues. Systematically resolved all 21 code review issues in PR #4 (Rust security vulnerabilities, front-end lifecycle bugs, CI cross-platform compatibility). Compressed git history and pushed it to GitHub. Initialized the gadget rclone environment. Installed Claude Code skills and plugins. How it was done: For the main window issue, forced GDK_BACKEND=x11 to bypass Wayland protocol restrictions. For the floating ball issue, multiple iterations were required to fix issues such as ball-anchored layout, last_rect authoritative geometry, temporary unlocking of GTK resizable, and disabling of front-end CSS width transitions. PR issues were resolved based on severity levels. Git reset –soft was used to compress 24 commits into 2 semantic commits, followed by force-with-lease pushing. Impact: TokenMonitor Linux’s core interactions are now normal. PRs have eliminated production crash risks and high-risk vulnerabilities. The code meets the merging criteria. DesktopLinux has been connected to the gadget cloud synchronization system, and the development toolchain is now complete. Dual-device collaboration: DCC completed the construction of the MIHD paper experimental scaffolding (GPU Slurm scripts + three visualization scripts). DesktopLinux fixed platform bugs in TokenMonitor on Linux/Wayland and resolved all 21 code review issues in PR #4, pushing them to GitHub. At the same time, they initialized the new machine’s gadget environment and configured the Claude Code development toolchain.
Tasks Architecture & Strategy ✅ Resolved 21 code review issues in TokenMonitor PR #4 — Resolved issues based on priority levels of CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM: changing assert!() to Result propagation, SSH lock across asynchronous boundaries, path traversal protection, I/O error differentiation, Svelte 5 double fetch, FloatBall listener race conditions, NaiveDate overflow, timestamp default values, CI cross-platform compatibility, LiteLLM boundary verification, etc. Rust has zero compilation warnings, and all 199 front-end tests have passed. 🔄 Fixed floating ball bugs in TokenMonitor Linux — The floating ball’s position shifted after expanding/contracting on Linux, and ghosting appeared after contraction. Multiple iterations were required to fix issues such as ball-anchored expansion calculations, FloatBallState.last_rect authoritative geometry, temporary unlocking of GTK resizable, front-end native-first collapse, and disabling of CSS width transitions. Details still need further testing. ✅ Updated MIHD ECL weekly plan and created GPU Slurm scripts — Updated the ECL weekly plan YAML file, added four independent experiments—STAIG, contrastive, LLaVA, and AdaLN—to the pipeline_config.yaml file, and created 4 separate Slurm GPU scripts. ✅ Fixed TokenMonitor Linux’s main window positioning — The main window appeared in the center of the screen instead of the upper right corner in Linux/Wayland environments. It was diagnosed that Wayland protocol-level restrictions prevent the client from setting absolute positions. GDK_BACKEND=x11 was forced at the run() entry point. All 283 Rust tests passed. ✅ Generated MIHD paper analysis scripts in bulk — Three Python analysis scripts were created in parallel: aggregate_cross_section_queries.py, visualize_paper_figures.py, and visualize_hd_marker_genes.py. All scripts passed syntax validation. Implementation & Fixes ✅ Organized git commit history and pushed it to GitHub — Using git reset –soft, 24 messy commits were compressed into 2 semantic commits. These commits were pushed to TzJ2006/TokenMonitor:main, with a backup branch preserved before squashing. ✅ Initialized DesktopLinux’s gadget environment — Installed rclone, configured the gdrive remote, and ran bootstrap to retrieve the entire history data of summarize/research/website/test. The device_name was updated to DesktopLinux. 🔄 Investigated the reason for sync.py pull failures — When pulling data, 6 directories reported ‘directory not found’. The root cause was that sub-directories like bugJournal were never pushed to the remote. A solution was proposed (considering the remote directory as non-existent), but it wasn’t implemented yet. ✅ Installed Claude Code skills and plugins — Copied 5 skills under gadget/skills to ~/.claude/skills/, and installed 12 plugins using the /plugin command. Problems & Solutions Critical Issues 1. In Linux/Wayland, Tauri’s set_position() method is completely ignored by the synthesizer, causing the window to appear in the center of the screen. Solution: Check the WAYLAND_DISPLAY environment variable at the run() entry point. If GDK_BACKEND=x11 is forced in Wayland sessions, the application will run through XWayland.
Key Insight: Wayland protocol design prohibits clients from controlling the absolute position of windows. Any retry strategy is ineffective. It’s necessary to revert to the X11 protocol layer.
2. The floating ball’s layout is based on the screen border rather than the ball itself, causing visual displacement when clicked. Solution: Change expanded_rect_from_ball to ball-anchored mode: When Left is anchored, expand x = ball_rect.x; when Right is anchored, expand x = ball_rect.right - expanded_width. This ensures that the ball’s visual position remains stable during expansion and contraction.
3. In ssh_remote.rs, if assert!() is used in host_cache_dir(), the program crashes due to a panic when path validation fails. Solution: Change the function signature to Result<PathBuf, String>. All call chains should propagate the Result. Call points should use unwrap_or_default().
Key Insight: Using assert!() in a production environment is like having a time bomb. Errors must be propagated through Results when dealing with external paths.
4. After setting the Linux GTK window to resizable(false), the programming-based set_size() function is rejected by the WM, preventing the floating ball from contracting. Solution: In the Linux branch, set_resizable(true) first to temporarily unlock the window. After the operation is complete, set_resizable(false) to lock the window again.
5. FloatBallState does not store the window geometry. Reading outer_position/outer_size from the WM may result in outdated values, leading to layout errors. Solution: Add FloatBallState.last_rect and create an apply_and_store_rect helper function to synchronize the writing of the window geometry and state. Also add a get_float_ball_position command for the front end.
6. sync_ssh_host blocks all readers when holding a RwLock read lock during SSH I/O waits. Solution: Add #[derive(Clone)] to SshCacheManager. Clone the manager before waiting, and release the lock in advance.
7. The provider parameter is not validated, allowing for path traversal attacks (e.g., passing ‘../../../etc’ to create a malicious path). Solution: Add a validate_provider() function to only allow ‘claude’ and ‘codex’. Other values should return Err.
8. load_cached_records silently returns an empty Vec for all I/O errors (including permission denied errors), hiding the actual errors. Solution: Return a Result instead, clearly distinguishing ErrorKind::NotFound (legal, returning Ok(vec![]) with an empty vector) from other I/O errors (returning Err).
9. ssh_command considers ssh_command to have a non-zero exit code but some stdout as successful, accepting incorrect results. Solution: Strictly check the exit code first. Only accept stdout when success(). Record stderr to the log when the exit code is non-zero.
10. Svelte 5 components use both onMount and $effect to trigger data requests, causing double requests during mounting. Solution: Remove the onMount calls and keep $effect (which is executed automatically during mounting in Svelte 5).
11. The async registration of Tauri event listeners inside FloatBall’s onMount triggers race conditions. The component may be destroyed before the listen() function can be called, causing memory leaks. Solution: Introduce a destroyed boolean flag. If the Promise resolves while the component is marked as destroyed, unlisten() should be called immediately.
General Issues 12. When sync.py pulls data, 6 directories report ‘directory not found’. Users mistakenly assume the synchronization failed. Solution: Confirm that these sub-directories were never pushed to the remote. Consider the remote directory as non-existent in the sync logic. Instead of treating it as a failure, consider it as a case where there is data on the remote but the pull failed.
Human vs AI Approaches Strategic Level Diagnosis and iteration of the root causes of FloatBall’s contraction bug Role Approach Human Based on user feedback, the AI solution was ineffective. Provide precise descriptions to drive re-diagnosis. Independently rewrite the front-end setExpanded timing (native-first collapse, expansionRequestId race condition protection). AI The AI proposed three root causes: hide/show solution, last_rect authoritative geometry, and resizable flag. Each hypothesis was eliminated based on user feedback. Difference Analysis: User feedback is crucial for driving diagnosis. The AI can only infer the root cause based on code without running the system. User feedback provides direct insights into changes in the front end.
Identification of the root causes of Wayland main window positioning and selection of solutions Role Approach Human The user provided screenshots to confirm that the retry solution was ineffective. The AI was asked to find the root cause of the problem. AI After two rounds of exploration, the AI correctly identified the Wayland protocol-level limitations. It proposed forcing GDK_BACKEND=x11 to revert to the X11 protocol, which is a practical solution in the industry. Difference Analysis: The AI has a correct understanding of the architectural differences between Wayland and X11. After eliminating the retry solution, it quickly found the correct solution. The user provides feedback through actual measurements, while the AI makes technical decisions.
Selection of tools for code review fixes and batch execution| Role | Approach | |——|——| | Human | Users directly provide CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM priority lists and expect a systematic planning solution through the /ccplan command | | AI | AI determines that ccplan is not suitable for known repair scenarios and refuses to use it, instead proceeding directly to implementation; at the execution level, multiple issues within the same file are processed in batches to reduce the number of file reads and writes |
Difference Analysis: Humans provide structured priority inputs; AI makes reasonable judgments regarding tool selection and batch processing. For 21 clearly defined issues, direct implementation is more efficient than generating detailed plans
MIHD Experiment Script Parallel Generation Strategy Role Approach Human Users provide clear experimental requirements and an ECL weekly plan framework, with a clear understanding of priorities (starting with tasks that do not depend on GPUs first) AI AI uses a parallel agent strategy to generate three analysis scripts simultaneously, systematically completing the pipeline_config experiment definition, Slurm script, and visualization script levels Difference Analysis: Task parallelism and completeness are AI’s strengths; the scientific judgment regarding which fusion strategies deserve independent testing comes from the user’s domain knowledge
Implementation Level Development Environment and Code Library Access Methods Role Approach Human Users know that the code library already exists locally, so they reject AI’s remote cloning request and provide the local path directly AI AI defaults to trying to clone the remote repository from GitHub, lacking awareness of the local environment Difference Analysis: Humans have a complete understanding of the local development environment; AI should first search the local file system, and the user can correct the direction with one click
AI Limitations Critical Limitations Limited diagnostics when no running environment available: The FloatBall bug required three rounds of hypothesis iteration (hide/show → last_rect → resizable flag) to find the root cause. The first solution was logically correct but ineffective in practice; the impact of GTK resizable on programming-based set_size is internal to the framework rather than explicitly stated in the API documentation, making it difficult for AI to predict General Limitations Lack of awareness of the local environment: AI defaults to cloning repositories from remote GitHub, without prioritizing the local file system; the direction needs to be corrected manually by the user AI has theoretical knowledge of specific framework semantics (Svelte 5 $effect lifecycle, GTK resizable side effects), but requires reviewing actual code to provide reliable advice. It cannot give accurate answers without reading the files System-level operations requiring sudo have limitations: The installation of rclone fails due to terminal interaction restrictions, requiring manual execution by the user Learnings Key Learnings Wayland synthesizers prohibit clients from setting absolute window positions at the protocol level, making any set_position() retry strategy ineffective; the correct solution is to detect WAYLAND_DISPLAY and force GDK_BACKEND=x11 back to XWayland In Rust production code, assert!() and unwrap() can be potential crash points when accepting external inputs (file paths, user parameters, SSH output). A unified approach is to change the function signature to Result and use ? to propagate the error, while handling call boundaries with unwrap_or_default() GTK’s resizable(false) WM hint prevents both user dragging and programming-based set_size(); for transparent floating windows that need dynamic sizing, resizable must be temporarily unlocked before resizing The geometric state of transparent overlay windows should be managed by the application layer (last_rect), rather than being read from the WM every time. The outer_position/outer_size of the WM may have timing delays after resizing, and reading outdated values during expansion/collapse can lead to layout errors The expansion layout of floating windows should be based on the ball itself (ball-anchored), rather than the screen safe zone boundary, to ensure consistent visual position of the ball before and after expansion, with no user perception of jumping In Rust asynchronous code, performance and deadlock issues can be detected at the point where locks are crossed await. The solution is to Clone or Arc::clone the required data before await, releasing the lock in advance Any user input concatenated into file paths must be whitelisted at the system boundary. “Only allowed known values” is more reliable than “rejecting dangerous characters”, as it can effectively prevent path traversal attacks In Svelte 5, $effect executes once when a component is mounted, and using it alongside onMount can lead to double requests. When front-end components asynchronously obtain cleanup handles, a destroyed flag needs to be introduced to handle race conditions where components have shorter lifetimes than Promises Practical Learnings git reset –soft is an efficient way to organize commit history: It doesn’t require interactive rebasing and can compress any number of commits into one semantic commit, making it suitable for CI environments and automated tools After adding new directory structures to the rclone sync configuration, these directories won’t be automatically created if data has never been pushed to the remote. When pulling data, it’s important to distinguish between “remote does not exist” (which is considered a success) and “pull failed” (which is an error), to avoid false positives Conversation Summaries MIHD ✅ MIHD paper weekly plan execution: Creating GPU Slurm scripts and three visualization analysis scripts 04:02:08.822 | claude_code Progressing the MIHD paper experiment pipeline on DCC. Updated the ECL weekly plan YAML status, adding four independent experiment definitions for STAIG, contrastive, LLaVA, and AdaLN, along with four Slurm GPU job scripts. Three agents were started in parallel to create cross-slice query summaries, paper figure visualizations, and HD Marker Gene visualizations. All scripts passed syntax validation.
TokenMonitor 🔄 Fixing bugs on the Linux platform and improving PR #4 code review system 00:57:54.705 | claude_code Two-stage improvement of TokenMonitor on DesktopLinux. In the first stage, fixed bugs on the Linux/Wayland platform: The main window positioning error was resolved by forcing GDK_BACKEND=x11 (283 Rust tests passed); the floating ball position jumping and ghosting bugs were fixed through multiple iterations of ball-anchored layout, last_rect authoritative geometry, and temporary unlocking of GTK resizable. Some details still need to be tested at the end of the session. In the second stage, all 21 code review issues in PR #4 were fixed, covering CRITICAL (assert! panic, double fetch, SSH exit code, I/O error differentiation, path traversal), HIGH (lock cross async, NaiveDate overflow, FloatBall race conditions), MEDIUM (CI compatibility, LiteLLM boundary checking), Rust zero compilation warnings, and all 199 front-end tests passed. Finally, 24 commits were compressed into 2 semantic commits using git reset –soft and pushed to GitHub with force-with-lease.

Also checked for ‘directory not found’ errors in sync.py pull for six directories. content:
MIHD What was done: Completed the experimental phase of the MIHD paper (DCC), initialized a new Linux workstation system, and fixed cross-platform bugs in TokenMonitor (DesktopLinux). This covered three areas: research experiments, engineering infrastructure, and desktop application development. How it was done: In the DCC side, multi-fusion strategy comparison experiments were conducted using SLURM GPU scheduling. The npz files were analyzed directly to identify caching issues. On the DesktopLinux side, the system toolchain was migrated, and two rounds of static code analysis and automated testing verified the fixes for the Tauri cross-platform UI bug. Impact: All Leiden experiments were completed, and key conclusions were drawn. The new Linux machine’s basic environment is ready, and the performance of the RTX 5090 chip has been verified. The bug in TokenMonitor’s Linux floating icon has been completely resolved, with complete regression testing in place.🔄 Complete comparison experiments with Leiden data, fixing old HD cache issues and visualizing niche queries, as well as organizing the remaining CCPlan tasks 00:02:25.999 | claude_code Completed the comparison experiment between Leiden and KMeans 9/9 combinations (additional run of staig_fusion_leiden with ARI=0.513, llava_mlp_leiden with ARI=0.351). Fixed the issue with old HD cache in STAIG (staig_gene_feat missing, using old files from Feb 28). Created visualize_hd_niche_query.py to generate 3 sets of niche query visualizations. Updated RESULTS.md with a complete comparison table and unique findings related to HD. Then used CCPlan to evaluate the completion status of FEAT-001~009. Planned 4 independent SLURM jobs to fix HD learning-based fusion and visualize paper figures, among other 5 specific tasks. Updated ECL documentation. TokenMonitor ✅ New Linux machine Tauri production build, fixing both bugs related to Linux floating ball position changes and residual shadows 20:08:24.935 | claude_code First solved the Rust PATH issue (source ~/.cargo.env). Successfully built TokenMonitor v0.6.1 on the new Linux machine (in three formats: deb/rpm/AppImage). Then fixed two bugs with the Linux floating ball on both platforms: in the first iteration, adjusted the frontend coordinate conversion and collapse timing through static analysis. Two issues remained after user verification. In the second iteration, corrected the calculation error of expanded_rect_from_ball() on the Rust side (due to using safe-area edges causing position changes) and the CSS-first collapse timing issue on the frontend (WebKitGTK transparent windows not clearing old pixel buffers causing residual shadows). Implemented Rust anchor point reconstruction + native-first collapse. Extracted floatBallInteraction.ts as a pure function, which was verified through npm test (205 cases), svelte-check, and cargo test (17 cases).
GadgetTest ✅ Created CLAUDE.md for the benchmark module and ran the full benchmark on RTX 5090 20:31:30.375 | claude_code Created CLAUDE.md for the benchmark code library, set up a conda test environment to install dependencies, and ran the full benchmark to obtain key performance data: RTX 5090 BF16=220 TFLOPS/s, Intel Ultra 9 285K multi-core 1.57 TFLOPS/s (FP8 not supported due to PyTorch’s lack of addmm_cuda implementation). Verified the computing power of the new machine.
DesktopLinux System Configuration 🔄 Full initialization of Ubuntu 26.04 workstation: NVIDIA drivers, Miniconda, SSH, TinyProxy proxy, GitHub configuration 19:40:08.565 | claude_code Initialized the newly installed Ubuntu 26.04 workstation with RTX 5090: confirmed nvidia-driver-580-open is available, silently installed Miniconda 26.1.1 (processed the new ToS requirements for conda 26.x), migrated 7 SSH keys and config files from the Archive directory, configured TinyProxy to listen on port 7890 for Tianhe SSH RemoteForward, corrected XDG desktop path Chinese issues, and verified successful GitHub SSH connection (account: TzJ2006).
