title: Bug Journal 2026-03-20

content:
Daily Report — 2026-03-19 Daily Overview Completed Tasks: Progressed four parallel projects, including spatial transcriptomics experiments (Harmony alignment revealed semantic batch mixing trade-offs), robot error recovery process (training optimization through portable deployment, successfully passed 139 tests), architectural modification of CalendarPro/Life Copilot (multi-task classifier eliminated single-task bottlenecks, audited and archived over 2400 lines of code, fixed calendar query issues, project renamed), and expansion of knowledge management infrastructure (Gadget research tool upgraded to a five-mode system, supporting deep analysis at any scale, comprehensive analysis of Yuke Zhu’s academic career from 2012 to 2026, involving 18 to 100 papers, after multiple analysis iterations, merged historical daily reports from February to March 30 days). Implementation Methods: Conducted controlled experiments (harmony alignment after fusion, using task completion-weighted BCE loss), built core infrastructure (multi-task LLM analyzer, coordinate conversion fixes, segmented long text analysis, each segment containing 150K characters, using LLM for merging, dynamic timeout formula adjusted to 300 seconds plus 120 seconds per 50K characters), carried out systematic restructuring (audited and archived dead code in over 2400 lines of code, second-phase cleanup, redesigned five-mode research tool, modified 7 source files), and performed extensive batch processing (collected data from 15-20 researchers, including 6 rounds of Yuke Zhu’s deep analysis, ultimately achieving full-text analysis of 100 papers, using 47 paragraph segmentation methods, merged historical reports over 30 days, performed 12 JSON repairs, finally resolving mechanism fault diagnosis issues). Impact: Discovered that linear batch correction has fundamental limitations when embedding vector dimensions have inconsistent semantics; through MimicGen deformation repair, demonstrated enhancement paths from 10 to 1000 were opened up; eliminated CalendarPro’s single-task architecture limitations and fixed critical errors; achieved successful testing of portable recovery deployment; completed a complete record of Yuke Zhu’s academic career (h-index 56, citations 24,611 times, evolving from data infrastructure builder to foundational model pioneer, then to world-class model architect); research tool capabilities shifted from “quick overview” to “deep excavation,” supporting full-text analysis at any scale; integrated work archives over 30 days, establishing unified cross-device work records; identified the final mechanism deadlock causing infinite loop in LLM API costs (3 device dates permanently in finalized=False state). DCC Completed Tasks: MIHD spatial transcriptomics cross-sample alignment experiment, strategic planning for ARI improvement and batch effect reduction, preparing Claude Code demonstrations for lab meetings; using historical sessions as data sources, generated a review report covering milestones from February to March 2026 (Visium HD adaptation, RM-IDEAL evaluation, scGPT+UNI2 fusion experiment, cleaned up approximately 250K lines of dead code). Implementation Methods: Conducted post-fusion Harmony alignment in sections 151508-151673, initiated multi-agent exploration to diagnose cluster ARI gaps (0.475 compared to SOTA’s 0.66+), designed a 30-minute demonstration covering planned modes, permissions, and AI overconfidence examples using DLPFC data; reviewed historical work using the Gadget summary tool on TzJsDesktop. Impact: Important findings: Harmony improved batch mixing metrics by 36%, but due to dimension semantics inconsistency caused by each training session, small-scale semantic alignment decreased—batch correction and semantic alignment are different attributes requiring joint training; provided complete demonstration materials; decided to use a dual approach to solve ARI and batch effect problems; comprehensively recorded historical milestones from February to March. MacBook Completed Tasks: Configured error recovery collection environment, integrated debugging of SpaceMouse, remote file transfer, uploaded checkpoints to athena server; interrupted brief environmental setup attempts; used historical sessions as data sources, generated a review report covering Gadget tool development and Desktop Video 4.1 features from February to March 2026. Implementation Methods: Created a recovery_collect conda environment, uninstalled 3Dconnexion driver to release device lock, modified robosuite macros_private.py file, fixed EnvWrapper.env property errors, downloaded portable packages via scp, transferred 14GB of checkpoint files; ran setup_env.sh to create recovery_collect conda environment, installed mujoco/robosuite/mimicgen dependencies (interrupted session during environment creation); reviewed historical work using the Gadget summary tool on TzJsDesktop. Impact: SpaceMouse ran successfully; found only 11/29 types of errors produced scenarios; successfully transferred large model checkpoints for future use; environmental setup incomplete; MacBook’s historical work (Gadget summary model switched to Sonnet, Desktop Video historical features, 4.1 screen saver clock transparent glass repairs) was recorded in a structured report. TzJsDesktop – Completed Work: Mainly active devices underwent comprehensive knowledge management and system restructuring: CalendarPro/Life Copilot completed system modifications (multi-intent classifier, calendar query fixes, code library health checks, archived over 2400 lines of code, project renamed), research tools underwent major upgrades (five-mode system architecture redesign, support for segmented long text analysis), conducted in-depth analysis of academic literature (Yuke Zhu performed multiple analysis iterations, ultimately achieving full-text analysis of 100 papers under deep wide mode, using 47 segmentations and 5 retries optimizations), batch integrated historical daily reports (covering more than 30 dates from February to March 2026), fixed JSON format errors (including unescaped quotes, truncated fields, incomplete parentheses), diagnosed double-layer issues in the final processing mechanism through code review, and updated tutorial documentation with actual device names. Implementation Methods: Designed and implemented a multi-intent analyzer based on Claude CLI, replacing the two-validation + 7-layer keyword processing scheme, fixed three-layer calendar query vulnerabilities, conducted comprehensive code library audits and phased restructuring; modified 7 source files (analysis.py added_MODE_CONFIG and segmentation logic, prompts.py added TRAJECTORY_MERGE_PROMPT, cli.py/research_scout.py/config.py/models.py updated option settings) + 3 documentation files; implemented a greedy algorithm for chunking papers by year (targeting 150K characters per chunk), dynamic timeout formula (300 seconds plus 120 seconds per 50K characters), and TRAJECTORY_MERGE_PROMPT for semantic integration; conducted multiple analyses of Yuke Zhu’s work, analyzing different subsets of papers (from 1 paper to 100 full-text papers, automatically segmented into 47 chunks, merged using LLM); batch called daily_summary.py to export and merge historical data, manually fixed JSON format errors (unescaped quotes, truncated fields, incomplete parentheses); diagnosed double-layer issues in the final processing mechanism through code review; updated tutorial.md document with actual device names. Impact: Eliminated CalendarPro’s basic single-intent bottleneck (now able to handle multi-action messages), restored calendar query functionality, reduced complexity of classification code by about 200 lines, removed over 2400 useless lines of code while maintaining complete test coverage (373-424 test passes); research tool capabilities shifted from “quick overview” to “deep excavation”—deep wide mode supported full-text analysis at any scale through segmentation merging mechanism, breaking through limitations of single LLM; completed comprehensive academic trajectory analysis of Yuke Zhu, revealing his evolution from visual genomics understanding to AI2-THOR intelligent representation to RoboSuite/RoboTurk ecosystem construction, as well as his development from MineDojo/Voyager LLM-driven intelligent agents to geometric intermediate representations + GPU-native simulation + VLL alignment (2012-2025, 86-100 papers, 7 breakthrough achievements); integrated work records of over 30 days (three major upgrades of CalendarPro, ErrorRecoveryBenchmark M5-M14 milestones, MIHD benchmark Visium HD adaptation and code cleanup, Desktop Video 4.1 release); identified 3 device dates causing permanent finalized=False deadlocks (2026-03-03 MacBook, 2026-03-07 MacBook/TzJsDesktop, 2026-03-15 tianhe), leading to infinite loop in LLM API costs; recorded systematic format errors in LLM JSON generation process (special character escapes, long text truncation, incomplete nested objects). tianhe Completed Work: Optimized task completion model training, error recovery collection and portable deployment preparation, generated training scenarios with controlled distribution, fixed MimicGen deformation errors, making 10→1000 enhancement possible, designed human demonstration collection architecture, configured git proxy; used historical sessions as data sources, generating a review report covering February to March 2026 (Pi0.5 Phoenix training, BC-RNN baseline, ErrorRecoveryBenchmark M5-M14, robobrain_pi training monitoring improvements, LLaVA model conversion, LIBERO evaluation extension). Implementation Methods: Applied positive sample weighting (pos_weight=10) to balance 4% positive samples in BCE loss, created sampleactions_with_completion to reuse VLM prefix features, avoiding repeated forward passes, implemented comprehensive recovery collection restructuring, fixed SpaceMouse initialization and rendering errors, created generate_training_scenes.py, used ControlledRng for deterministic amplitude sampling (from 0/9 to 9/9), fixed _replay_with_warping, designed three-tier data pool (training 10/subtypes → enhancement 200/subtypes → evaluation 100+/subtypes), changed git protocol from SSH to HTTPS and used proxy (127.0.0.1:10087); reviewed historical work using the gadget summarize tool on TzJsDesktop. Impact: Model training stagnation issues resolved, gradient contributions became more balanced; created portable deployment package (compressed size 252MB), containing 139 passed units tests; was able to collect 1320 training scenarios with uniform amplitude distribution; fixed pos_delta, making MimicGen enhancement path no longer an obstacle for 10→1000 demonstration generation (10 seeds × 200 targets × 50% success rate); cleanly extracted 352 files via proxy; comprehensive archival of historical core work from February to March 2026.### Implementation and Fixes ✅ Optimizing the CalendarPro test suite to avoid hangs Added a pytest timeout setting of 30 seconds globally, marked external services with @pytest.mark.external, and implemented an automatic skip feature. Now all 399 tests are completed within 40 seconds, eliminating hangs.
✅ Implementing automatic restart of CalendarPro in development mode Created a dev.py script to monitor changes to src/*.py files and automatically restart the robot process. Added a shortcut with the –dev flag.
✅ Fixing JSON format issues in LLM outputs (over 12 repair cycles) Fixed malformed JSON issues in daily reports and academic analyses, including truncated strings, unclosed quotes, unescaped Chinese quotes, missing closing parentheses, and incomplete nested structures. Implemented a four-step repair process: direct json.loads → block extraction → deep bracket matching → LLM repair (from haiku to tanka then to opus upgrade). All failed cases were recorded in outputs/logs/json_repair_failed/ for manual review. Covered over 12 research cases, including RelViT, S3O, VIOLA/Coopernaut/Ditto, robomimic/MAPLE, GraspMolmo/DreamGen, Visual Genome/AI2-THOR, base models evaluation, CDL/FORGE/Acid, etc., as well as daily reports.
✅ Synchronizing the research tool documentation system Parallelly updated research/CLAUDE.md with 6 modifications: adding missing reference impacts and three unrecorded CLI parameters–affiliation/, –paper/, –homepage/. Updated research/TUTORIAL.md with 4 modifications, and parent CLAUDE.md with 1 modification, ensuring that all pattern descriptions, example commands, parameter tables, and dependency descriptions match the code.
✅ Updating Tutorial.md with actual device names and workflows Updated summarize/tutorial.md by replacing generic device name placeholders (home-server/linux-server/desktop) with actual device names used in multi-device workflows (MacBook/TzJsDesktop/tianhe/) and updating rclone remote configuration to actual values (gdrive:summarize). This aligned the example scenarios with actual user workflows. Tasks were planned but interrupted by users.
✅ Setting up a recovery benchmark environment on MacBook Started the recovery_collect conda environment via setup_env.sh, installing mujoco 2.3.2, robosuite 1.4.1, and mimicgen dependencies. Interrupted the session during environment creation, preventing further verification and testing steps.
Issues and Solutions Key Issues 1. Post-fusion Harmony improves batch mixing metrics but reduces expertise retrieval quality across samples Solution: After analyzing the results, it was concluded that Harmony’s linear correction cannot handle non-linear dimensional meanings, resulting in inconsistent training of individual parts. It is recommended to use Joint STAIG multi-part training in the next iteration.
Key Insight: Batch correction methods like Harmony assume linear offsets, but these corrections fail when independent training produces embeddings with inconsistent dimensional semantics. Batch mixing and semantic alignment are two different properties. Linear correction on independently trained embeddings cannot maintain semantic consistency across samples.
**2. CalendarPro can only handle a single intent per message, leading to loss of subsequent actions like " scheduling a meeting at 3 PM and changing dinner time to 7 PM " Solution: Replaced the dual-validation process with Claude CLI’s multi-intent analyzer, which returns a series of intentions. Updated the _process_message_queue function to iterate through intentions and combine responses. Removed the 7-layer keyword coverage system.
Key Insight: Single-intent classification is a fundamental architectural limitation, not a parsing issue. Keyword techniques (always generic, context-dependent, 7-layer coverage) are symptoms of confusion in the abstraction layer. Switching to LLM-native multi-intent functionality eliminates the need for all heuristic methods.
3. MimicGen augmentation failures: calculated pos_delta but never applied to action deformation, resulting in success rates below 5% Solution: Fixed the _replay_with_warping function by applying pos_delta to warpedactions[:, :3] before adding noise. The expected success rate should be above 50% for D0 translation errors.
Key Insight: MimicGen augmentation requires actual coordinate transformation, not just noise injection. The 10→1000 path depends on: 10 seeds × 200 targets × 50% success rate = 1000 demonstrations. Without pos_delta fixes, the success rate will drop significantly.
4. Yuke Zhu’s initial deep extensive analysis using a single 5MB prompt exceeded the LLM’s processing capacity, resulting in failure Solution: Implemented a three-tier solution: ① Added a threshold of _MAX_PROMPT_CHARS=150K to automatically trigger segmentation; ② Used a greedy algorithm that groups by year and segments each paper when the data volume exceeds a certain limit; ③ Used the LLM to merge 47 data blocks into a complete report.
Key Insight: The key to large-scale text analysis lies in “divide and conquer + semantic merging”: technically, dynamic segmentation can control the burden of a single LLM, while cognitively guiding the LLM to extract consistency across segments rather than simply concatenating prompts. 150K characters correspond to approximately 15-20 medium-length papers, which is the optimal point empirically.
5. Task completion model training stalled at a loss of about 0.035, despite grad_norm=0.7-1.0 and a positive sample ratio of 4%, with negative samples accounting for 96% Solution: Applied a positive sample weighting of pos_weight=10 to the BCE loss to balance gradient contributions, effectively increasing the positive sample ratio from 4% to about 30%.
Key Insight: In cases of low loss, the recall rate of positive samples in an imbalanced dataset does not guarantee good performance. Focusing solely on gradient magnitude is misleading, as negative samples dominate. Clear gradient balancing is required for category imbalance.
6. CalendarPro immediately returns “No events today” for queries like “This week” and “Tomorrow” after creating events Solution: Adopted a three-tier solution: (1) Adding intent classification to BASE_SYSTEM_PROMPT (instead of only using INTENT_PROMPT_FRAGMENTS in multi_intent.py), (2) Adding keyword backtracking detection in handle_query() to handle LLM failures, (3) Fixing get_today_events() to display already scheduled events in get_today_events(), (4) Adding Pending_warnings for Discord error feedback.
Key Insight: INTENT_PROMPT_FRAGMENTS and BASE_SYSTEM_PROMPT serve different code paths—the main LLM classification uses BASE_SYSTEM_PROMPT. Keyword backtracking is a reliable LLM safety net. Past events must remain visible to prevent re-creating them.
7. The code repository contains 8 fully implemented modules (approximately 2400 lines of code), which have comprehensive tests but never import during runtime, causing maintenance difficulties Solution: Adopted a systematic four-stage refactoring approach: (1) Listing all imported modules and identifying isolated modules, (2) Archiving unused modules and explaining their purpose in README files, (3) Merging duplicate modules into the specification, (4) Deleting truly useless code. After 373 tests, all passed.
Key Insight: For fully implemented modules, archiving is preferable to deletion. Isolated but still usable code should be archived in an archive directory along with relevant context information for future integration. Audit findings indicated discrepancies in configuration and errors in import statements.
**8. Due to constant check failures, duplicate dining events were created repeatedly (19 breakfast, 19 lunch, 18 dinner times)**Solution: Adjust the priority of the [[[HYMTPH_0]] function to prioritize the ISO format field of the start date, rather than the start time (displayed as ‘08:00’). Also, replace the [[[HYMTPH_1]] function with get_today_events(include_past=True), so that ongoing dining events remain visible. Key Insight: The order of field parsing is crucial—always prioritize fields in ISO format to avoid parsing failures. Past events must be kept visible to prevent duplicate creation.
9. Deadlock caused by the finalization mechanism of the widget: Historical log files marked with [[[HYMTPH_3]] exist indefinitely, preventing [[[HYMTPH_4]] from ever becoming True. As a result, [[[HYMTPH_5]] continuously calls the LLM merging function, wasting API costs. Solution: Identified the root cause: The two-level finalization mechanism (logs and reports) requires all source logs to be finalized before marking the report as completed. Only when [[[HYMTPH_6]] is performed, can the export setting [[[HYMTPH_7]] be applied. The reason historical logs remain in the False state is: (a) When [[[HYMTPH_8]] is performed, it only exports once, thus keeping it in the False state; (b) After the first export, the number of conversations changes, resulting in the False state remaining. The [[[HYMTPH_9]] flag is embedded in the JSON content and synchronized using rclone. Solution: Re-run the export operation on the affected device, as now the date has passed and the number of conversations has stabilized, so it will automatically be marked as True. Another method is to manually edit the [[[HYMTPH_10]] field in the JSON to True, and then upload it using rclone.)
Key Insight: The two-level finalization mechanism requires all source data to be finalized before marking the report as completed. The [[[HYMTPH_11]] flag in the JSON content is synchronized using rclone, ensuring consistency across devices. However, historical False entries can lead to permanent deadlocks. The key value is to save LLM API costs (during the merging phase), not the download time of rclone. Re-exporting through “data repair” can better adapt to the existing architecture without requiring “logical changes” (removing all all_sources_finalized checks would break consistency.)
10. When using a block size of 60K for the first time, 100 papers were divided into 102 blocks, with almost one paper in each block. This led to a large number of LLM calls, and each paper lacked context information. Solution: Increase the block size from 60K to 150K, adjust the splitting logic to prioritize the integrity of years, and ultimately reduce the number of blocks from 102 to 47. This significantly reduced the number of LLM calls while maintaining temporal context.
Key Insight: The granularity of splitting needs to be balanced between “LLM processing capacity limitations” and “semantic coherence”: too fine a splitting will lose temporal relationships, while too coarse a splitting will cause timeout issues. 150K is roughly equivalent to 15-20 medium-length papers, which is the optimal point based on experience.
11. Confusion of researchers with the same name in academic databases: Semantic Scholar combines multiple researchers with the same name into a single profile, leading to misleading trajectory analysis (for example, Yuke Zhu is involved in medical imaging, computer vision, and astrophysics—clearly different individuals). Solution: The AI system actively identifies confusion signals in the analysis: excessive domain spans, fragmented co-author networks, inconsistencies between citation counts and paper numbers. Warnings are marked at the beginning of the trajectory summary, suggesting manual splitting or analysis at the paper level. For Yuke Zhu, he is distinguished from robot researchers at UT Austin/NVIDIA, researchers at Mybank Technology, mathematicians at Zhejiang University, and researchers at Ant Group based on institutional affiliations and research domains.
Key Insight: Automated researcher analysis systems must include multi-dimensional signal detection modules: coauthor clustering, topic clustering, temporal continuity, and consistency of institutional affiliations. Relying solely on the identity recognition functions provided by the database is not sufficient.
12. Due to the default value of [[[HYMTPH_12]] being True, old conversation logs were incorrectly skipped during the export process. Solution: Change line 1726 in the daily_summary.py file from data.get(’_finalized’, True) to data.get(’_finalized’, False), and add the –force flag to explicitly control the re-export process.
Key Insight: The default values in the data pipeline logic must be consistent with the semantics: during the export phase, missing [[[HYMTPH_13]] should be treated as incomplete (False), while during the merging phase, it can be treated as stable (True).
13. Multiple analysis failures due to LLM timeouts (the initial 300-second timeout was insufficient for a merge of 187K, and 420 seconds was still insufficient). Solution: A dynamic timeout formula was designed: [[[HYMTPH_14]], with 300 seconds as the baseline, and an additional 120 seconds for every 50K characters added. The final merge step was successfully completed within about 660 seconds of timeout.
Key Insight: The timeout design should be linearly related to the data size, rather than being a fixed value. Large models have a relatively stable processing speed (about 200-300 characters per second), and it is a reliable strategy to estimate the time based on the prompt length plus 50% buffer time.
14. There are systematic issues with escaping double quotes in the long JSON text fields generated by the LLM, resulting in JSON parsing failures (more than 12 errors). Solution: Identify and manually escape special symbols in fields like trajectory_summary and breakthrough[].description (e.g., “let the robot…” → “let the robots…”), and for truncated JSON objects, supplement the missing right parentheses and array closing symbols based on the context.
Key Insight: When the LLM generates long JSON fields, the handling of escaped double quotes is unstable, especially in academic texts that contain frequently cited terms (e.g., “image understanding” becomes “moving while looking”). This leads to formatting errors. Solutions require: (1) explicitly requiring escaped special characters in prompts; (2) setting length limits for overly long text fields, or using multi-round segmentation; (3) adding JSON schema validation steps after generation to automatically fix common formatting errors.
15. Long JSON outputs generated by the LLM have formatting errors: truncation (especially in the last few fields), unclosed quotes, unescaped Chinese quotes (""), and missing closing brackets in nested structures, especially when containing technical descriptions, Chinese text, and deep nesting (greater than 3 levels). Solution: A four-step repair process was implemented: (1) directly attempting json.loads; (2) extracting json code blocks; (3) repairing unclosed structures based on depth-based bracket matching; (4) fixing complex errors through model upgrades, from haiku to tanka to epic poetry. Special JSON repair prompts instruct the AI to extract and repair JSON, returning only pure JSON without explanations. All failures are recorded in outputs/logs/json_repair_failed/ for schema analysis and future optimization.
Key Insight: Long JSON files generated by the LLM show systematic failure patterns in different sessions, even with the same prompts (e.g., string escape issues, truncation issues, nesting errors). A single repair strategy is insufficient to address these issues; a layered fault tolerance mechanism is needed. Chinese quotes """ are semantically valid, but must be escaped to ASCII double quotes in JSON string values. Production systems need to validate the generated JSON and support automatic repair retry functions.
16. For highly productive researchers (with more than 130 papers), analyzing the entire corpus at once results in prompt length errors or insufficient analysis. The analysis of Yuke Zhu’s 206 papers failed due to “too long prompts.” Solution: An iterative analysis strategy based on year segmentation was adopted: papers were grouped by publication year, and 1-3 representative papers were analyzed in each round to uncover their breakthroughs (descriptions, why previous methods were not used, impacts, technical insights). The complexity of each call was controlled while maintaining the depth of analysis. Paper selection: based on year grouping + citation count ranking + conference award recognition (best paper/top pick/focus/oral presentation recognized by LLM), with a maximum of 10 papers per year.
Key Insight: The year-based segmentation strategy is better than analyzing the entire corpus at once (too long prompts/insufficient analysis) or randomly sampling (missing temporal evolution). It maintains the integrity of time dimensions while considering paper quality. The “few but deep” iterative approach is more suitable for academic analysis than the “many but shallow” one.
17. Reusing the VLM prefix forward pass is required for completing the head reasoning (once in sample_actions, once in predict_task_completion). Solution: The sample_actions_with_completion() function was created, which captures the result of the first VLM’s forward pass and reuses it for completing the head, returning a tuple of actions and completion_probability.
Key Insight: Stream matching denoising is cost-effective, but unnecessary VLM forward passes increase measurable overhead. The unified method eliminates redundancy with minimal restructuring.
18. Despite a normal physical connection, the SpaceMouse device still cannot be opened due to an exclusive driver lock. Solution: The 3Dconnexion driver process was terminated, and the macros_private.py file of robosuite was modified to set SPACEMOUSE_PRODUCT_ID to 0xc63a, allowing access to the physical device.
Key Insight: macOS systems can exclusively use HID devices. The macros_private.py file was used to override the mode, rather than modifying the main configuration.
19. Daily report merging task: How to avoid copying all tasks individually and instead create a comprehensive view. Solution: Specific operations include: (1) combining similar tasks into one entry, retaining the latest status; (2) integrating human-machine analysis for the same topics; (3) combining multiple sessions for the same project into one conversation summary; (4) retaining content with higher importance during deduplication. This requires the LLM to understand the causal relationships between tasks (e.g., “implementing a feature” → “fixing an error” → “verifying the fix” is considered a stage of the same work process) as well as topic similarity.
Key Insight: Direct concatenation leads to information redundancy. Synthesis requires identifying causal relationships between tasks (whether sequential or truly parallel) as well as topic similarity (repeated debugging of the same module should be treated as one task), not just string matching.
Methodology for Humans and AI Strategic Level Identifying subtle default value errors in the data pipeline Role Method Human The user analyzed the logic of the export/merge stages and identified semantic inconsistencies: the export should default to False (incomplete), and the merge should default to True (stable) AI The AI initially focused on adding the –force flag without questioning the existing defaults. It wasn’t until the user pointed out line 1726 that the AI realized this mistake. Difference Analysis: Humans use domain knowledge to infer the semantics of the pipeline, while AI focuses on implementing the required functions without reviewing the existing logic.
Balancing the splitting strategy Role Method Human The user provided high-level requirements (“add blocks and merge”), but did not specify details about the splitting algorithm or specific block sizes AI The AI chose the algorithm “greedy year-based grouping + block splitting when a single year is too large”. The initial block size was 60K (too small), and later adjusted to 150K (based on experience). The AI independently decided to use the LLM instead of simple string concatenation for merging Difference Analysis: The user provided high-level requirements, while the AI handled the details of the algorithm and parameter adjustments. The initial block size of the AI was too conservative, resulting in 102 blocks. Later, with the user’s intervention (indirectly indicated by timeout failures), the AI adjusted it to 150K.
Identifying dimension mismatch issues during batch fusion Role Method Human The user accepted Claude’s analysis, believing that Harmony improved batch mixing, but reduced semantic consistency. The user agreed to the suggestion of Joint STAIG training AI The AI systematically compared batch metrics and cross-sample query metrics, identifying contradictory improvements/degradation, and providing a mechanistic explanation (linear corrections cannot solve nonlinear dimension mismatch issues) Difference Analysis: In this case, the AI provided a deeper analysis than the human requested—human trust and agreed to the next steps. The AI’s systematic metric breakdown revealed hidden trade-offs.
Architectural decisions for multi-intent analyzer Role Method Human The human immediately recognized that the limitation of a single intent was the root cause and proposed a solution using Claude CLI, bypassing the gradual improvement of the existing dual verification system AI The AI first explored the existing architecture comprehensively, then proposed a solution. The human’s intuition was correct—the complexity of dual verification did not solve the fundamental multi-intent problem Delegating and executing audit tasks Role Method Human The human refused the AI’s attempt to delegate the audit task to 3 parallel exploration agents, insisting on using the read/grep/glob tools directly for auditing AI The AI initially tried to perform the audit by creating 3 agents (src/audit, tests/audit, duplication finder). The human stopped calling all three agents Difference Analysis: The human understood that over-delegation leads to indirectness and loses the “overview” needed for architectural judgment. Using tools directly allows the discovery content to be kept within the main context, enabling the creation of coherent reports. This provides a lesson on when to avoid using agents.
Strategies for generating training and testing error scenarios Role Method Human The user realized that training and testing scenarios needed to be separated into different directories, and demanded a uniform distribution of difficulty levels (not random) to achieve controllable difficulty progression AI The AI initially focused on using random scenarios from the same directory to replicate the existing process. Later, it had to switch to stratified sampling and control the random number generator Difference Analysis: The human understood the rigor of experiments required training/test separation and controllable variables. The AI initially ignored data contamination and experimental design issues.
Academic trajectory analysis framework: “Why it’s feasible now” as the core insight Role Method Human The user designed a comprehensive analysis framework, including trajectory summaries, breakthrough points (must include “why it’s feasible now” field), research themes, methodological evolution, and domain impact. The user explicitly asked for “why it’s feasible now” analysis, rather than just technical descriptions AI The AI initially focused on describing technical solutions without delving into historical prerequisites. The user’s pattern constraints were needed to enforce structured “why it’s feasible now” analysis Difference Analysis: The user understood that the core value of academic analysis lies in “judging why it’s feasible now”, rather than just purely technical summaries. The AI defaulted to a technical overview, lacking historical insights, and needed structured prompts to guide it.
Determining error repair strategies: Data repair or logical change Role Method Human The human believed that the problem was a permanent historical error and advocated for repairing it by running the export program again on the affected device, rather than changing the logic of finalizing merge AI The AI initially proposed modifying the finalization logic of the merge stage (removing all_sources_finalized check), trying to solve the root problem Difference Analysis: The human’s approach: “repairing the data” (re-running the export to automatically mark the correct flags, while respecting the existing architecture). The AI’s approach: “changing the logic” (modifying the code so that merging no longer depends on the finalization state of source logs). The human prevented the AI from making changes, stating that only one export operation was needed to solve the problem.
Iterative researcher analysis strategy: Multi-angle sampling to verify robustness Role Method Human The human chose to analyze the same researcher multiple times, using subsets of papers from different years (2022/2023/2025/2026), implicitly testing whether the tool could infer overall research directions from partial information AI The AI independently generated trajectory summaries and breakthrough points for each analysis session, lacking consistency checks across sessions. It failed to clearly explain the inheritance relationships between papers from different periods Difference Analysis: The human implicitly tested the tool’s robustness through multiple sampling. The AI treated each analysis session as an independent task, without establishing a causal link between sessions.### MIHD Space Transcriptomics**✅ Post-fusion and harmony alignment experiments, strategic planning for ARI improvement and batch effect reduction, preparation for Claude code demonstration** 02:04:13.480 | claude_code The benchmark_rm_ideal.py file has been modified to include the --harmony flag, which is used for batch correction during post-fusion processes, adjusting between embedding loading and cross-sample evaluation. Experiments have been conducted to compare sections 151508-151673 with and without Harmony. The main findings are: Harmony improved the batch mixing metrics (ASW_batch increased by 36%, graph_connectivity increased by 32%), but reduced the accuracy of semantic alignment (Spearman r decreased from 0.189 to 0.023). It was concluded that Harmony’s linear correction cannot handle nonlinear dimension mismatches from independent training per section. It is recommended to conduct Joint STAIG multi-section training next. Multi-agent exploration was initiated to diagnose the clustering ARI gap (0.475 compared to SOTA’s 0.66+) and to address the cross-sample batch effect problem. A dual-track approach was decided upon. A comprehensive 30-minute Claude code demonstration was prepared for Friday’s lab meeting, including the planning mode, permissions, CLAUDE.md rules, an example of AI overconfidence using DLPFC space data, and the delivery of complete demonstration materials and presentation scripts.
Error Recovery (Algorithms and Benchmarks) ✅ Environment setup, model training optimization for task completion, comprehensive recovery collection restructuring, training scenario generation, MimicGen deformation error fixing 04:16:46.552 | claude_code The recovery_collect function was set up in the conda environment using the mujoco/robosuite stack. The error in EnvWrapper.env was fixed. The locking issue with the SpaceMouse device was resolved by terminating the 3Dconnexion driver and configuring product_id=0xc63a. The device was successfully connected, but only 11 out of 29 types of errors could generate scenarios. Training stagnation was identified (loss was approximately 0.035, with positive samples accounting for 4%). A weighted BCE loss with pos_weight=10 was implemented to balance gradients. The predict_task_completion inference method was added, optimizing the model by creating sample_actions_with_completion, thereby reusing the VLM prefix features and avoiding redundant forward passes. Major reconstructions included: fixing the SpaceMouse initialization issues (removing environmental variable parameters, using input2action), adding rendering support, and implementing dynamic PROJECT_ROOT resolution settings. A portable deployment package was created (compressed from 171MB to 252MB), including setup/run scripts and tutorials. All 139 unit tests passed. The generate_training_scenes.py function was implemented to generate exactly 10 scenarios per task×subtype, with these scenarios evenly distributed, ranging from 0.0 to 1.0. The ControlledRng class was created to make the uniform() function deterministic (based on fractional interpolation), while keeping the randn() function to maintain diversity in directionality. In-depth technical discussions were held, clarifying the data collection architecture: collecting 5-10 examples/subtypes, no failed examples, starting with 1-2 tasks. It was found that RecoverySegmenter is suitable for Phoenix MCM/MPM (not MimicGen). Key errors were identified: pos_delta was calculated in _replay_with_warping but not applied. The deformation issue was fixed, resulting in 1000 enhanced examples with 10 seeds×200 targets×50% success rate. It was determined that 3 data pools are needed: training (10), enhancement (200), and evaluation (more than 100).
CalendarPro/Life Copilot ✅ Complete system restructuring: multi-intent classifier, calendar query fixes, code base audit and second-phase cleanup, fix of duplicate meals issues, project renaming, development mode implementation, test suite optimization 00:53:36.473 | claude_code The complete restructuring of the intent classification system was completed. The Claude CLI MultiIntentAnalyzer replaced double verification and the 7-layer keyword technique, supporting multi-intent decomposition. The MultiIntentAnalyzer class was created, using a 4-stage JSON parsing approach. The MultiIntentResult/AnalysisContext model was added, and the always_general_keywords and context_dependent_keywords collections were removed. The process_input was simplified from 7 steps to 4 steps. Errors in the CLI were fixed. Weekly query functionality was added. 30 new tests were written, and all 424 tests passed. Users pointed out a fundamental limitation: double verification only returned one intent, and complex messages would lose subsequent actions. The existing architecture was analyzed, identifying the 7-layer keyword technique, and then a two-tier solution was designed. A comprehensive implementation plan was developed. dev.py was implemented, using watchfiles to monitor src/*.py files, and multiprocessing.spawn for process isolation. The –dev flag was added. Debounce, grace periods, and ignore filters were configured. The pytest tests/hung indefinitely. A pytest-timeout dependency was added, with a global timeout of 30 seconds. External service tests were marked as @pytest.mark.external, using skipif for automatic detection. The Typer CLI functions were renamed to avoid collection. Now all 399 tests pass within 40 seconds. The grep/glob/read tools were used to systematically map 29K lines of code, distributed across more than 90 files (executed directly after the user rejected proxy delegation). Eight isolated modules were identified (approximately 2400 lines of code), a duplicate parse_datetime_with_timezone function, an outdated broker_models.py, 3 isolated configuration settings, and corrupted browser_service imports. A detailed phased restructuring plan was generated. All 4 phases were implemented: archiving 8 source modules + 9 test files + check_skills_status.py file, with instructions provided in README; merging the duplicate parse_datetime_with_timezone function; removing broker_models.py and 3 isolated configuration settings; fixing the corrupted browser imports. 373 tests verified the success. CLAUDE.md was updated. Users reported 19 duplicate Breakfast events, 19 Lunch events, and 18 Dinner events. The root cause was identified: _check_meal_exists used display-format time strings, but failed fromisoformat(), while schedule_essentials excluded past events. These two errors were fixed, and a cleanup script was created to remove 53 duplicates, passing the tests. Users requested the plan for renaming, and then the work was interrupted to expand the scope, including conda environments, folders, repositories, and Discord applications. The code base was reviewed, with approximately 62 changes made in 15 files (code, documentation, scripts, configuration), providing a list of 6 manual checks. Users executed .init to trigger improvements to CLAUDE.md. 4 new architecture modules were added, and multiple details were corrected. Subsequently, users reported calendar query errors (queries for this week/day always returned “no activities today”). This was fixed through a three-tier solution: adding guidance for Query Classification in BASE_SYSTEM_PROMPT, adding keyword fallback in handle_query(), adding include_past parameters in get_today_events(), and adding a Discord error feedback mechanism. All tests passed, and both documentation and functionality were perfected.**
Gadget (Summary + Research Tools) ✅ Comprehensive knowledge management infrastructure expansion: upgrade of the 5-mode research tool system, including long-text analysis capabilities, Yuke Zhu’s complete academic trajectory analysis (more than 10 iterations, over 100 papers), integration of batch historical daily reports (more than 30 dates), finalization of mechanism failure diagnosis plans, JSON repair processes 04:10:40.556 | claude_code
Users encountered the ‘ModuleNotFoundError: common’ error when running the daily_summary.py export file. Upon diagnosis, it was found that the relevant package was missing. Therefore, the package was installed using the ‘pip install -e .’ command in editable mode. It was possible to export the date information of 23 conversation logs. Users requested that all date information be re-exported. It was discovered that the _finalized default=True option was used during the export phase (line 1726), causing old logs to be incorrectly skipped. The default setting for the export phase was changed to False, and the --force flag was added for clearer control. For 17 dates, forced exports were performed, and all data was successfully uploaded to rclone. Through git stash, rebase, and push workflows, the local and remote gadget repositories were synchronized. The gadget/summarize daily_summary.py tool was used to perform large-scale batch processing on approximately 30 historical conversation logs from February to mid-March 2026. This included work records from multiple devices (DCC/MacBook/TzJsDesktop/tianhe) and multiple projects (MIHD, error-recovery-benchmark, CalendarPro, Desktop Video, gadget). Each generated report included a structured daily_overview (global and per-device information), tasks, problems and solutions, comparison between humans and artificial intelligence, limitations of artificial intelligence, learning outcomes, and conversation summaries. There were 12 JSON format errors that required specialized repairs. It was diagnosed that there was a serious deadlock issue with the finalization mechanism: 3 device-date combinations had permanent _finalized=False situations, preventing reports from being completed and causing an infinite loop in the artificial intelligence API costs. The solution was to re-execute the export operation or manually edit the JSON file. The (finalized) suffix was added to the export output for diagnostic purposes. Users provided a detailed implementation plan, requiring the expansion of 2 modes (fast/detail) to 5 modes (fast/normal/in-depth/exhaustive/in-depth-exhaustive), and adding automatic segmentation and merging for the extensive and in-depth-exhaustive modes. Artificial intelligence modified 7 source files (analysis.py added _MODE_CONFIG and chunking logic, prompts.py added TRAJECTORY_MERGE_PROMPT, cli.py/research_scout.py/config.py/models.py updated with options), and modified 3 documentation files. A dynamic timeout formula (300 seconds + 120 seconds/50K characters) and a segmentation algorithm with 150K characters/block were implemented. Yuke Zhu’s in-depth-exhaustive analysis was successfully completed: 100 full-text papers were retrieved from ArXiv, automatically segmented into 47 time blocks, each time block analyzed by artificial intelligence, and finally combined into a complete trajectory report (including 7 breakthroughs, research topic evolution, mentor relationships, etc.). After 5 iterations, the chunk size was increased from 60K to 150K, the timeout formula was optimized, and a complete academic figure report was generated. A comprehensive academic analysis of Yuke Zhu was conducted, with more than 10 iterations, analyzing 18 to 100 representative papers, covering Visual Genome (2016) → robomimic/MAPLE (2021) → CDL/MineDojo/VIMA (2022) → Voyager/S3O/LIBERO (2023) → DexMimicGen/Sirius-Fleet/PRESTO/LongVILA (2024) → DEAS/Cosmos-Predict2.5/QLIP/ASAP/GraspMolmo/DreamDojo (2025-2026). Each analysis produced a detailed trajectory summary, including dimensions such as why before/impact/technical insights, research topics, methodology evolution, and domain impact. It was found that his development trajectory progressed from “data infrastructure builder” (robosuite/ROBOMIMIC) to “basic model pioneer” (MineDojo/VIMA/Voyager) to “world model architect” (Cosmos/DreamDojo). Researchers with the same name were actively detected and marked. There were 9 JSON repair cycles due to truncated or malformed outputs. An attempt to analyze all 206 papers was made, but it failed due to the “prompt too long” error, confirming the necessity of using an annual stratified sampling strategy. Users ran the .init command to verify the accuracy of research/CLAUDE.md. The assistant identified and repaired two minor errors. Users subsequently proposed expanding the researcher analysis mode from 2 to 5, and setting different paper limits and processing depths. After clarifying that paper selection should maintain citation counts and awards as the basis, the assistant developed an implementation plan involving modifications to analysis.py, cli.py, and prompts.py. Approximately 16 historical dates were processed, generating structured daily reports from 2026-02-08 to 2026-03-17. Malformed JSON files generated by artificial intelligence were repaired, including truncated, unclosed quotes, unescaped Chinese quotes, and missing closing parentheses. A four-stage repair process was applied. research/CLAUDE.md was updated (6 changes), research/TUTORIAL.md was updated (4 changes), and parent CLAUDE.md was updated (1 change). At the same time, agents were processed in parallel, ensuring that descriptions, example commands, and parameter tables for all modes were consistent with the code.
Token Usage Total cost: $10.05 — 12 million tokens
summary: Cross-device research and development have been conducted on multiple projects: spatial transcriptomics alignment experiments have revealed the trade-offs involved in semantic batch mixing; the robot error recovery process, from training optimization to portable deployment, has been validated through 139 tests. CalendarPro/Life Copilot has completed architectural reengineering, eliminating the bottleneck caused by single intent classifiers. Additionally, the knowledge management infrastructure has been expanded, including tools for improving the 5-mode system, capable of handling long-text analysis tasks. Yuke Zhu analyzed his academic trajectory from 2012 to 2026, publishing over 100 papers and undergoing more than ten analytical iterations. Furthermore, bulk integration processing was performed on 30 days of historical data from February to March 2026.
title: Bug Journal 2026-03-19

content:
Daily Report — 2026-03-18 Daily Overview Completed Work: Expanded the intent classification system of CalendarPro by adding a new BATCH_UPDATE intent to handle users’ multi-task status reports. Implementation Method: Implemented semantic routing in 8 files, used 21 expressions for keyword scoring, included LLM prompt fragments for detecting completion verbs, and calendar integration handlers. Impact: Resolved the serious user experience issue where users couldn’t efficiently report multiple task statuses simultaneously, and eliminated the “I don’t understand” default response during batch updates. Added the BATCH_UPDATE intent in CalendarPro to handle multi-task status reports, and conducted comprehensive test coverage and documentation updates.
Tasks Architecture and Strategy ✅ Implement BATCH_UPDATE intent functionality — Added a new intent type in 8 files: enumeration definitions, semantic routing (21 expressions), keyword scorer with enhancements for 2 or more completion verbs, LLM prompt fragments for updating array patterns, context assembly mapping, double verification default responses, discord_bot handler with calendar operations at the task level, and improved GENERAL default responses. ✅ Diagnose failures in batch update classification — Launched 3 parallel exploration agents to understand the intent routing system (24 types), task completion handling, and general intent protection mechanisms. Found the root cause: no BATCH_UPDATE intent exists, semantic router score is about 0.20, which is below the threshold of 0.50, LLM classifier defaults to GENERAL, and clarifying types trigger the hardcoded “I don’t understand” template. ✅ Develop implementation plan for batch update functionality — Designed a comprehensive 8-step implementation plan covering intent type enumeration, semantic routing, keyword patterns, LLM prompts, context assembly, double verification default values, discord_bot handler with calendar integration, improved GENERAL default responses, route JSON regeneration, and test suites. Implementation and Fixes ✅ Create comprehensive test suite for BATCH_UPDATE — Created 21 tests covering keyword scoring, enumeration, handlers (completion/cancellation/rearrangement/notes/multi-tasks/null tasks/permissions), route registration, context mapping, prompt fragments, and double verification default responses. All tests passed without regression issues. ✅ Update CLAUDE.md document — Redrew the CLAUDE.md document, focusing on subtle architectural details: removed redundant file lists, added a “common multi-file changes” section, provided a step-by-step guide for adding AI providers/intents/services/events, documented isolation of singleton tests, and integrated architectural patterns. Problems and Solutions Key Problems 1. Bots cannot classify multi-task status updates (e.g., “A is completed, B is also resolved, C needs time adjustment”), and still revert to “I don’t understand” even with substantial user input. Solution: Added BATCH_UPDATE intent, including semantic routing (21 expressions), enhanced keyword scorer for 2 or more completion verbs, LLM prompt fragments for learning update array patterns, and handlers for searching calendars, deleting completed tasks, rearranging/annotating other tasks, recording completion status, and publishing EventBus events. Also fixed the GENERAL default response by using meaningful LLM responses instead of discarding them.
Key Insight: Intent classification failures usually stem from three levels of defects: (1) no enumeration members, (2) no semantic routing examples, (3) no LLM prompt fragments. Solving these three levels along with keyword scoring can create powerful classification capabilities. GENERAL default responses should retain useful LLM responses rather than blindly using templates.
2. The semantic router scores for batch updates are about 0.20, below the threshold of 0.50, resulting in LOW_CONFIDENCE classification. Solution: Created BATCH_UPDATE_ROUTE with multiple Chinese/English expressions (“A is completed, B is also done”, “Tasks A and B are completed, C needs rearrangement”) and added keyword scoring rules: 2 or more different completion keywords → increase score by 0.30 to capture batch update features.
Key Insight: Embedding-based semantic routing cannot recognize multi-entity status patterns. Clear keyword counting (2 or more completion verbs) is a simple but effective batch detection heuristic that can complement embedding similarity.
3. The LLM classifier doesn’t have batch updates in the allowed intent list, defaults to GENERAL with clarifying types, which triggers the hardcoded “I don’t understand” template, thereby discarding useful AI-generated responses. Solution: Added batch updates to INTENT_PROMPT_FRAGMENTS, including update array patterns (task name, operation, notes, new time), and added it to the allowed intent list in build_prompt(). Improved the GENERAL handler by checking if clarifying responses have meaningful LLM content (>20 characters, not template phrases), and used it instead of the hardcoded template.
Key Insight: Reverting to the template should be a last resort, not the default option. When the LLM generates meaningful responses, even if the confidence is low, they should be retained. Template overrides waste the LLM’s reasoning ability, leading to worse user experiences.
Human and Artificial Intelligence Approaches Strategic Level Diagnostic strategy for batch update failures Role Method Human User demonstrated a real Discord conversation failure case (“Hello → Bot replies with task summary → User provides batch status → Bot says I don’t understand”), and asked “How do we solve this problem?”, providing specific symptoms but not specifying diagnostic methods Artificial Intelligence Claude launched 3 parallel exploration agents to systematically understand: (1) all 24 intent types and routes, (2) task completion handling, (3) general intent protection mechanisms. Then read key files to verify details, and use Plan agent to integrate findings into an implementation plan Difference Analysis: Humans provided “what” (symptoms) and “why we care” (user experience problems), but didn’t provide “how to investigate” methods. Artificial intelligence chose a parallel exploration approach based on agents, rather than reading files sequentially, showing strategic use of sub-agents for broad discovery. Humans might have used a more linear approach of “reading this file, then that file”.
Implementation method for BATCH_UPDATE functionality Role Method Human Before asking Claude to execute the plan, the user provided a fully structured 8-step implementation plan, including exact file paths, line numbers, code snippets, data patterns, and test requirements Artificial Intelligence Claude strictly followed the plan, reading files in parallel, editing in order where dependencies existed, regenerating derived data (route JSON), and writing comprehensive tests Difference Analysis: Humans designed the initial architecture and specified specific changes; artificial intelligence executed the plan as a reliable executor. This shows that humans play the role of “architects”, while artificial intelligence acts as a “builder”, which is very efficient for complex multi-file changes, as humans have already thought through the system.
Limitations of Artificial Intelligence General limitation - In the test_batch_update.py file, Claude initially tried to import the DiscordBot class, causing an import error, which was later changed to testing the processing function separately. It should check the import structure first or use a better simulation model. When trying to run the entire test suite for the first time, Claude used pytest tests/ -v --timeout=30 -x, but failed due to the lack of the pytest-timeout plugin installed. Therefore, it was changed to running only some tests instead of installing the plugin or canceling the initial flag. Learning Points Key Learnings Multi-level intent classification requires coordinated updates of 8 or more files: enumeration files (models.py), semantic routing files (intent_routes.py), keyword scorers (keyword_scorer.py), LLM prompt files (prompts.py), context assembly files (context_assembler.py), double verification default values (dual_verify.py), handler + routing files (discord_bot.py), and derived data (route JSON). Missing any layer will lead to classification failure. Parallel agent exploration (3 agents responsible for intent routing, task handling, and overall protection) is more efficient than sequential file reading, suitable for complex diagnostic tasks. Each agent provides a focused perspective, ultimately leading to a complete understanding. Backtracks in AI systems should retain useful LLM-generated content, rather than blindly overwriting it. Before replacing the hardcoded backtrack content, it’s important to check if the LLM response has meaningful content (>20 characters, not template phrases). Keyword scoring can capture patterns that embedded similarity cannot capture: 2 or more different completion verbs is a simple batch update heuristic that can complement semantic routing functions. In neural systems, explicitly detecting patterns remains valuable. Practical Learnings When implementing the functions outlined in the user’s plan, it’s better to read all affected files in parallel, rather than individually. This helps understand dependencies and enables Claude to edit files more efficiently in batches. Conversation Summary ✅ Implemented BATCH_UPDATE intent to handle multi-task status reports 23:05:55.704 | claude_code The user provided an 8-step implementation plan, requesting the addition of BATCH_UPDATE intent in the CalendarPro classification system. Claude executed the plan: added enumeration members, defined semantic routing with 21 statements, added keyword scorer, used enhancements for 2 or more completion verbs, provided LLM prompt fragments, designed context assembly map, set double verification default values, wrote discord_bot handler with calendar operations, and improved GENERAL backtrack content to retain useful LLM responses. Created 21 test cases to cover all components, all tests passed without regression issues. Regenerated route JSON with 25 routes.
✅ Diagnosed why bots fail in batch status updates 22:35:46.066 | claude_code The user demonstrated a real Discord failure scenario: the bot couldn’t understand multi-task status updates (“A is checked, B and C are resolved”), so it replied with “I don’t understand”. Claude went all out and launched 3 parallel exploration agents to handle intent system (24 types), task handling, and overall protection issues. Read key files to verify details. Used Plan agent to integrate findings into a root cause analysis: no BATCH_UPDATE intent exists, semantic router score is low (about 0.20), LLM defaults to GENERAL clarification, triggering the hardcoded template. Created an 8-step implementation plan and saved it in the.claude/plans/ directory. This plan was later executed in the first session.
✅ Updated CLAUDE.md document to better guide users 21:55:02.204 | claude_code The user ran the/init command, requesting Claude to improve the CLAUDE.md document. Claude analyzed the existing document, understood the code library structure, and rewrote CLAUDE.md, focusing on subtle architectural design: removed redundant file lists, added a “common multi-file changes” section, provided a step-by-step guide for adding AI providers/intents/services/events, documented isolation of singleton tests, and integrated architectural explanations about multi-file relationships. The final version emphasized the pattern that requires multiple files to be understood.

content:
Daily Report — 2026-03-17 Daily Overview Completed Work: Completed large-scale MIHD refactoring (including 9 optimizations, from O(N²) to O(Nlogk), achieved comprehensive understanding of QCVLA, and proposed 11 improvements through cross-project analysis (FLARE + 9 VLA papers). Fixed critical VLA inference errors, separated loss monitoring functions, generated 2920 error benchmark scenarios, optimized data conversion processes, configured remote development environments, and enhanced GPU monitoring tools. Methodology: Used parallel proxy-based code review for MIHD refactoring, conducted iterative Q&A and literature research on QCVLA, systematically debugged and redesigned the VLA system, implemented multi-task error injection processes, enabled non-blocking I/O for interactive monitoring, and automated server settings via SSH. Impact: MIHD: Eliminated code duplication and potential errors, avoided HD data memory shortages. QCVLA: Identified P0-level fast solutions and proposed perturbation and bridging enhancement methods to address Dagger saturation issues. VLA: Stabilized the inference system, implemented independent action_loss/task_loss tracking. 90% of error benchmark testing was completed (D0 samples needed to be supplemented). GPU monitoring tools became more accessible. Remote servers were ready for distributed development. DCC Completed Work: Conducted a comprehensive code review and refactoring of the MIHD multimodal integration codebase, involving 21 modified Python files, focusing on eliminating redundancy and performance bottlenecks. Methodology: Implemented the /simplify workflow, using three parallel proxies for reviews (code reuse, quality, efficiency), and carried out 9 targeted fixes, including centralizing NEEDS_COORDS_FUSIONS constants, improving device resolution, replacing O(N²) cdist with cKDTree, removing legacy wrappers, and reusing DataPreparer instances. Impact: Eliminated 8 redundant device resolution checks, fixed the issue of missing adaln_attention in spatial coordinate fusion lists (potential error), avoided memory shortages for 17K+ high-definition data through algorithm optimization, and improved maintainability by centralizing constant management. MacBook Completed Work: Conducted in-depth research on the potential bridge architecture of QCVLA, planning cross-project improvements through research on 9 papers, FLARE integration analysis, and remote server environment configuration. Methodology: Created the CLAUDE.md document, learned about the 7-step process and issues through iterative Q&A, searched for literature related to VLA efficiency/robustness (such as VLA-Cache, AC²-VLA, Spec-VLA, KERV, etc.), extracted transferable techniques from FLARE papers (perturbation enhancement, hard fault mining, phase-aware scheduling, visual condition bridging, modular LoRA), and automated the setup of athena.egr.duke.edu via SSH+Node.js+Claude/Codex. Impact: Fully understood the technical details of the bridge mechanism (calculation savings of 57.6%, performance degradation of 4%). Identified P0-level fast solutions (making existing Action-Aware Channel Importance Loss possible) and valuable P1-level improvements (for adaptive scheduling Bridge Confidence Head). Proposed perturbation and bridging methods inspired by FLARE to address Dagger saturation issues (+1pp steady growth). Servers were ready for distributed development. tianhe Completed Work: Fixed critical errors in VLA inference, separated training losses, generated error benchmark data (2920 scenarios), optimized CALVIN dataset conversion, integrated LIBERO custom test suite, fixed duplicate issues displayed in GPU monitoring, diagnosed RoboCasa MimicGen download failures, and resolved git proxy and wandb configuration issues. Methodology: Identified and fixed 4 inference errors through code review (no check order, prompt reset, log threshold, state update); modified the compute_loss function to return a tuple (loss_array, aux_dict), set has_aux=True; ran the 6-task error injection process (taking 3.5 hours); corrected the key mapping from RLDS to LeRobot, added progress bars; created LIBERO_OBJECT_COM classes and BDDL files; implemented ENV+FD verification and gpumon.py parent chain deduplication; added non-blocking keyboard navigation functions (arrow keys, command scrolling); tracked RoboCasa errors to box_links_ds.json via registry; tested proxies and updated local git configuration; provided a wandb API key environment variable solution. Impact: VLA inference system stabilized (eliminated crashes and infinite loops), training process can now monitor action_loss/task_loss separately in wandb, 90% of error benchmark datasets were completed (2920 scenarios generated, some D0 samples needed to be supplemented), dataset conversion process became robust and user-friendly, LIBERO test framework was expanded, GPU monitoring tools reduced the number of displayed processes from 35 to 8, with interaction functions similar to nvitop, RoboCasa analysis identified issues with upstream data publishing (no MimicGen links in box_links_ds.json), and git and wandb configuration issues were resolved. Cross-device collaboration was carried out in MIHD spatio-molecular learning refactoring, in-depth research was conducted on QCVLA potential bridges, improvement measures inspired by FLARE were adopted, issues with VLA training/inference infrastructure were fixed, error benchmark data was generated, and system tool functionality was enhanced.
Tasks Architecture and Strategy - ✅ QCVLA project documentation creation and improvement plan—Created initial CLAUDE.md file, covering the 7-step process, SingleStepDiT architecture, and key issues to be addressed. Also generated improve_analysis.md file, which includes 11 suggestions from FLARE and content from 9 papers. Recorded perturbation and bridging data augmentation strategies. Analyzed FLARE papers and extracted transferable techniques (perturbation enhancement, hard fault mining, phase-aware scheduling, visual condition bridging, modular LoRA). ✅ VLA inference system error fixing—Identified and fixed 4 critical errors in vla_infer.py: None check order errors could cause crashes, prompts not resetting after tasks completed leading to infinite loops, inconsistent debug log thresholds (no exceptions in parts 3/4), task completion status not updating to idle state. ✅ MIHD codebase refactoring and optimization—Executed /simplify operations on 21 modified Python files, implementing 9 key fixes, including: centralizing NEEDS_COORDS_FUSIONS (fixed missing adaln_attention issues), improving device resolution (removed 8 duplicates), replacing O(N²) cdist algorithm with cKDTree (avoiding HD OOM issues), removing unused import statements and outdated wrappers, reusing DataPreparer instances. And updated CLAUDE.md file to reflect architectural changes. ✅ Training loss monitoring separation—Modified BaseModel compute_loss function to return tuple [loss_array, aux_dict]. Implemented independent action_loss and task_loss return methods in pi0/pi0_fast models. Updated training script to use has_aux=True to parse logs and save them to wandb. ✅ Error recovery benchmark data generation—Completed error injection process for 6 tasks (pick_place, coffee, stack, stack_three, threading, three_piece_assembly), generating 2920 error scenarios. Some D0 sample counts were insufficient (<10), requiring re-running the process to supplement. Only collision_empty could be injected in the threading task, further analysis was needed. 🔄 Error benchmark D0 sample supplementation—Some D0-level error types had fewer than 10 samples (e.g., collision_self: 2, drop_at_wrong_place: 8), requiring re-running the process to supplement. Threading task needed analysis to determine why only collision_empty could be injected. ✅ CALVIN dataset conversion script optimization—Corrected dataset names (calvin_abc vs calvin_abc_d) and key mappings (rgb_static vs image) in RLDS to LeRobot conversion script, removed non-existent fields, added progress bars and overwrite confirmation logic. ✅ LIBERO custom test suite integration—Added libero_object_com suite to LIBERO benchmark framework, modified task_map, registered Benchmark class, created BDDL files, updated client main.py Implementation and Fixes ✅ GPU monitoring tool duplicate process fix and interactive navigation—Enabled ENV+FD verification and parent chain deduplication, reducing duplicate processes displayed by gpumon.py from 35 to 8. Added interactive features like arrow key navigation, command scrolling, and visual highlighting of selected lines. ✅ RoboCasa MimicGen dataset download failure diagnosis—Traced “This task has no dataset and the dataset type does not exist!” errors through download_datasets.py → dataset_registry_utils.py → box_links_ds.json. Confirmed MimicGen entries were zero (upstream data publishing issue). ✅ Remote server environment configuration (athena.egr.duke.edu)—Configured SSH key authentication (manually running ssh-copy-id after initial failures), automatically installed fnm and Node.js 22, installed Claude Code 2.1.77 and Codex CLI 0.115.0, transferred qcvla_bridge_pipeline project via rsync (51 files, 3.1MB). Servers were ready for distributed development. ✅ Git proxy configuration fix—Diagnosed git fetch network errors, tested multiple proxies, found localhost:9999 effective. Updated local git configuration to override unavailable global proxies. ✅ Wandb account configuration—Provided 4 directory-level Wandb account configuration options, recommended using WANDB_API_KEY environment variable or direnv method. Issues and Solutions Key Issues 1. QCVLA: The second round of Dagger only improved by 1 percentage point (60%→61% SR), indicating it was saturated. The bridge could only see the error distribution it naturally produced, limiting its diversity. Solution: Proposed perturbation and bridging data augmentation methods: During Dagger promotion, randomly inject short action sequences, followed by reverse recovery actions. This captures larger deviations that the bridge would never naturally produce, expanding the training distribution. Train the bridge (performing feature prediction in a perturbed state) and action heads (recovering action sequences) simultaneously.
Key Insight: When the model only sees its own error patterns, self-supervised training becomes saturated. Injecting synthetic but real deviations (inspired by FLARE’s perturbation strategy) can expand the distribution. Compared to Gaussian noise per frame, coherent action perturbations are more effective in robot control.
2. VLA Inference: The task was completed, but due to the current_prompt not being reset, it led to an infinite loop, sending completion messages, keeping chunk_count non-zero, and the model continued outputting high scores. Solution: Set current_prompt = "" after the task is completed, and call publish_state(“idle”) to prevent the next round from triggering immediately. Add a None check before task_completed.item() to avoid crashes in non-pi05 models.
Key Insight: The state machine needs to explicitly reset all related variables at each termination node; otherwise, unpredictable loops may occur. Multi-device compatibility (pi05 and non-pi05) requires handling boundary conditions in advance.
3. MIHD: The NEEDS_COORDS_FUSIONS tuple is duplicated in pipeline/runner.py and pipeline/evaluation_planner.py, and both lack adaln_attention (potential error), making it difficult to maintain consistency when adding new fusion strategies. Solution: Centralize NEEDS_COORDS_FUSIONS as a constant in models/Fusion.py, imported by both files. Add missing adaln_attention to the tuple, fixing the vulnerability where this fusion strategy cannot receive spatial coordinates.
Key Insight: Constant duplication across modules leads to inconsistencies and missed updates. A single source can prevent potential errors and simplify maintenance work.
4. MIHD: refine_labels_spatial_majority in utils/clustering.py uses scipy.spatial.distance.cdist, which has an O(N²) memory complexity, causing OOM on Visium HD data with over 17K points. Solution: Replace cdist with scipy.spatial.cKDTree.query, which has an O(N*k) complexity, suitable for k-nearest neighbor searches. Switch from calculating the full distance matrix to querying neighbors based on radius.
Key Insight: For large point clouds, spatial queries need to use tree-based structures (like KDTree, BallTree) to avoid memory explosion. For high-definition spatial data (10K+ points), algorithm selection is more important than implementation details.
5. During training, combining combined_action_loss and task_loss into one value and recording it in wandb prevents separate monitoring. Solution: Modified the compute_loss function to return a tuple (combined_loss, {“action_loss”: …, “task_loss”: …}), allowing the training loop to use nxn.value_and_grad(… with has_aux=True) to parse auxiliary metrics and add them to the info dictionary. Key Insight: JAX/Flax’s has_aux=True mechanism allows for elegant extraction of additional monitoring metrics from loss functions without affecting gradient calculation. All compute_loss implementations (pi0 and pi0_fast) need to be synchronized to maintain consistency in the interface.
6. Error benchmark thread tasks can only inject collision_empty errors; other types of errors cannot be generated. Solution: Analysis revealed that all thread-based clean trajectory phase_labels were ‘pre_reach’, lacking grasp/lift/transport stages, resulting in errors dependent on these stages (e.g., drop_in_transit) not being injected. The get_task_phase logic or thread task definition needs to be checked.
Key Insight: Error injection depends on the segmentation of task stages; incomplete stage identification can make certain error types detectable. It’s necessary to verify all tasks cover expected stages during opportunity graph generation.
7. gpumon.py counts bash/ffmpeg/claude processes as GPU processes inheriting CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES, and displays DDP worker processes separately from the parent process. Solution: Modified the get_proc_gpus() function to require both CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES environment variable and open/dev/nvidia* file descriptors. Added parent chain traversal in get_gpu_procs(), merging child processes with the same GPU set with their ancestors.
Key Insight: Detecting GPU usage in processes requires checking both environment variables and actual device file descriptors to avoid false positives.
8. All 317 RoboCasa tasks encountered errors downloading MimicGen data, unclear whether it’s a code issue or data availability problem. Solution: System analysis showed that 257 tasks did not register mg_path (yellow warning), while 60 tasks had mg_path but box_links_ds.json contained 0 MimicGen download links (red error). Confirmed this was an upstream data publishing issue, not a code defect.
Key Insight: When all downloads fail systemically, it’s necessary to check data availability before attempting code fixes. Using grep to search box_links_ds.json for ‘/mg/’ yielded zero results, confirming the absence of download links.
9. CALVIN RLDS dataset conversion failed, causing KeyError: ‘image’. The actual field names should be ‘rgb_static’ and ‘rgb_gripper’. Solution: Read features.json to confirm the actual schema, modify the key names for frame extraction, and remove missing environment_state and relative_action field definitions from the dataset.
Key Insight: Before converting the dataset format, it’s necessary to verify the schema of the source data. RLDS features.json is the authoritative source for determining the actual field names.
10. How to implement interactive keyboard navigation in a monitoring tool that needs to be updated regularly, while avoiding blocking input### GPU Monitoring Tool (Tianhe Server)✅ Fixed issue with duplicate GPU processes and added interactive navigation features 03:01:28.167 | claude_code
Users reported that gpumon.py displayed approximately 35 duplicate GPU processes, although there were only 8 unique processes. AI identified two reasons: misdetection of ENV-only processes and duplicate DDP worker threads. The fix involved using CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES and /dev/nvidia* file descriptors for detection, while removing duplicates in the parent chain. Additionally, an interactive feature similar to nvitop was added: arrow keys for navigation, command scrolling, and visual highlighting. The number of processes was reduced from 35 to 8 unique entries.
RoboCasa Dataset Management ✅ Fixed issue with failed downloads of the MimicGen dataset in all tasks
03:00:36.287 | claude_code
Users encountered an error saying “No dataset available for this task!” when trying to download the MimicGen pre-trained data using download_datasets.py. AI analyzed the download script, registry, and box_links_ds.json file. Two issues were identified: 257/317 tasks lacked mg_path registration (which is expected), but more importantly, box_links_ds.json contained zero MimicGen download links, despite having 350 human data links. It was confirmed that this was a problem with upstream data publishing, not a code flaw. It is recommended to contact the maintainer to understand the data publishing schedule.
