summary: Haiyong Wang — 早期研究人员
title: Haiyong Wang — Research Scientist Profile

content:
Daily Report — 2026-03-15 Daily Overview Completed Work: Implemented full-stack optimization for CalendarPro (semantic routing, hybrid classification, system prompt splitting, provider retry, scheduling scoring, learning loop, caching). Major refactored common libraries (extracted generic modules/packages, integrated output directories, unified research tools). Also conducted a comprehensive academic graph of robotics technology/transferable AI fields. Implementation Methods: Implemented 5 parallel proxies for CalendarPro, created a universal/package that can be installed via pip, adopted a modular architecture, and re-exported compatible code. Implemented student discovery functionality based on homepage scraping. Developed a three-level evaluation process with citation impact analysis. Used ArXiv/Semantic Scholar API to create batch profiles of researchers. Impact: CalendarPro’s intent understanding accuracy and task execution reliability improved significantly. Eliminated workaround methods in sys.path and code duplication between four tools. Created a production-ready unified research workflow with fast domain understanding. Successfully mapped out the mentor relationships and research trajectories of 13 leading robotics technology researchers, including Sergey Levine, Chelsea Finn, Pieter Abbeel, Yuke Zhu, Yunzhu Li, Mingyu Ding, Ruoshi Liu, Xiaolong Wang, and Shuran Song. Completed 7-stage parallel implementation of comprehensive CalendarPro optimization (with 230 tests). Unified the research toolkit into a single CLI. Integrated paper discovery/analyzer/citation functions. Extracted shared tools into universal/packages. Eliminated over 400 lines of duplicate code. Organized all output directories into a structured output/hierarchy. Profiled 13 leading robotics technology/AI researchers. Established a systematic academic research tracking system.
Tasks Architecture and Strategy ✅ Full Optimization of CalendarPro (7 Stages) — Implemented semantic routing thresholds, hybrid keyword+embedding routing, system prompt splitting (reduced from 530 lines to basic code+11 segments), provider retry logic with exponential backoff, scheduling scoring system, loop that learns automatically from mismatches, and ThoughtStore caching. 5 parallel proxies were used for 230 tests. ✅ Unified Research Scout + Profiler + Citation Graph Tools — Integrated three independent research tools into a single CLI: research_scout.py now includes paper discovery functionality, researcher profiling through ‘profile’ subcommands, citation graph analysis through ‘citations’ subcommands, three-level evaluation process with citation impact analysis, and support for multiple backend LLMs (claude_cli/anthropic/openai). Deleted duplicate ‘New feature/’ directories. ✅ Extracting Shared Tools into Universal/Packages — Created a pip-installable universal/package containing 6 modules (io, cache, jsonUtils, llm, hugo, paths). Eliminated over 400 lines of duplicate code. Re-exported summarize/llm_backends.py and research/cache.py. Eliminated workaround methods in sys.path. Updated pyproject.toml and all documentation. ✅ Organizing Output Directories into Unified Output/Hierarchy — Moved all tool-specific output paths to a unified output/hierarchy organized by type (reports/, logs/, cache/, data/, site/). Created centralized path constants in common/paths.py. Updated all imports and documentation. ✅ Tracking Robotics Technology Research and Profiling of Researchers — Created a robotics technology leader project. Profiled 13 researchers (Sergey Levine with an h-index of 166, Pieter Abbeel with an h-index of 164, Chelsea Finn, Yuke Zhu, Yunzhu Li, Mingyu Ding, Ruoshi Liu, Xiaolong Wang, Shuran Song). Identified doctoral mentors. Generated detailed reports with breakthrough papers and research topics. Identified Open X-Embodiment, MineDojo (Best Paper at NeurIPS 2022), Value Iteration Networks (Best Paper at NeurIPS 2016). ✅ Implementing Student Discovery Functionality Based on Homepage — Added source/status fields to the StudentCandidate model. Created research/homepage discovery.py files for text extraction using HTMLParser and LLM for student name extraction. Redesigned the discover_students() function. Adopted a homepage-first+co-authored supplement strategy. Set a polite rate limit (1 request/2 seconds). ✅ Updating Comprehensive Documentation (CLAUDE.md, TUTORIAL.md, README.md) — Rewrote the root CLAUDE.md with MCP server documentation and subproject references. Updated summarize/CLAUDE.md to fit the new import structure. Rewrote research/CLAUDE.md, covering analyzer architecture and unified CLI. Completely rewritten the Chinese TUTORIAL.md, increasing from 10 chapters to 13 chapters. Added multi-source search/analyzer/citation sections and 4 new workflow examples. Updated the README.md for missing tools. Implementation and Fixes ✅ Deploying Researcher Profiles in Separate Hugo Sections — Created a website/content/research/ section for researcher profiles (separated from bugJournal temporal reports). Added deploy_to_hugo() function in research/output.py. Updated the research menu items in config.yml. Added –deploy and –hugo-site CLI flags. ✅ Analyzing CLAUDE.md and Similar Projects — Analyzed CalendarPro’s CLAUDE.md (highly comprehensive, no changes needed). Studied similar open-source projects (FluidCalendar, CoPaw, Khoj, OpenDAN, Cal.com, Leon, Rasa, Home Assistant). Concluded that CalendarPro’s energy-aware scheduling+three-tier architecture+double verification combination is unique. ✅ Fixing Import Errors in mcp_server.py — Corrected function name mismatch issues: replaced global_load_known_arxiv_ids with global_load_known_paper_ids in 4 places. Problems and Solutions Key Problems 1. Co-author analysis showed that all tested professors (Sergey Levine, Pieter Abbeel, Chelsea Finn) had no students, despite the correct implementation. Solution: Human-identifiable faculty pages clearly list student information. Implemented a homepage-based discovery mechanism, using LLM to extract student names from faculty pages, with co-author data as a supplement. Increased transparency through source tracing.
Key Insight: Faculty page is an authoritative source for student lists, more reliable than inference from co-author models. When heuristic algorithms fail due to lack of data, rely directly on the information source (faculty webpage) instead of debugging the scoring algorithm. Facts speak louder than reasoning.
2. Semantic Scholar matched 6 incorrect researchers due to ambiguous names (Ming Yu→Explosive Chemist, Xiaolong Wang→Veterinary Scientist/Geologist/Natural Language Processing Researcher). 5 out of 6 initial queries failed. Solution: Humans provided full names for clarification (Mingyu Ding). relied on ArXiv paper matching for accuracy. AI correctly identified data quality issues, identified incompatible research fields, and returned clear warnings instead of generating incorrect trajectories. Suggested ORCID+institution verification for common Chinese names. #### 3. Output files were scattered in tool-specific subdirectories (e.g., summarize/logs/, research/cache/, test/benchmark_results.csv), using hard-coded Path(file). This led to tight coupling and difficult disk space management.
Solution: Designed a unified output/hierarchy, organized by type (reports, logs, cache, data) and tool. Introduced standardized path constants in common/paths.py, replacing all hard-coded paths. Updated environment variable fallbacks.
Key Insight: When multiple tools have similar output patterns, a type-based hierarchy is easier to maintain than a tool-based one. Centralized path management makes refactoring simpler—changing one constant affects the entire system without breaking the code. Implementing in stages and setting validation checkpoints prevents cascading failures.
4. Two overlapping research tools in the same directory (monolithic research_scout.py and modular profiler package) led to code duplication, unclear single entry points, and undocumented functionality. The initial/init attempt was interrupted due to user suggestions that AI should focus on documentation improvements rather than architectural changes. Solution: Users provided a detailed 9-step integration plan, including precise function signatures, line count statistics, and architectural decisions. AI executed the plan: retained research_scout.py as a stable CLI entry point, refactored the modular package into an importable library, added profile/citations subcommands, implemented three-stage evaluation with citation analysis, and added support for multiple backend LLMs.
Key Insight: For complex architectural changes, a detailed written plan can prevent scope creep and ensure AI follows the plan. Hybrid integration (retaining a stable CLI while refactoring internal logic) reduces migration risk compared to complete rewriting. Human architectural vision combined with AI tactical execution is more effective than letting AI adjust on its own. Gradual migration is safer than one big change.
5. A new modular tool was completely omitted from CLAUDE.md, causing confusion about the capabilities of the codebase. AI initially ignored the “new features” directory entirely. Solution: Used proxy exploration to thoroughly map the package structure, then rewrote CLAUDE.md to include both tools separately, including details about architecture, usage, and configuration.
Key Insight: Document chaos occurs quickly when adding new features without updating guidance documents. Active documentation maintenance or automatic detection of undocumented code paths is necessary. Update CLAUDE.md immediately after structural changes to prevent information bias.
6. After three attempts at LLM repairs, Yuke Zhu’s trajectory analysis still couldn’t parse JSON data, resulting in incomplete reports. Solution: Accepted partial results (excluding the paper list for trajectory analysis) and recorded the reasons for failure. The analysis was completed, but the output quality decreased.
Key Insight: LLM repairs have limitations. When all three model layers fail, the underlying problem may be related to prompts/context rather than model capability. Continuous failures indicate the need to reconsider the input structure or decompose the problem.
7. Despite using the correct author names, arXiv search returned 0 results when searching for leaders in robotics, due to sorting/latestness issues in the search API. Solution: Switched from arXiv search API to Researcher Profiler, which uses data sources from arXiv and Semantic Scholar for stronger multi-source access.
Key Insight: API search behavior isn’t always intuitive. Multi-source data acquisition is more reliable than single API queries. When better data sources are available, there’s no need to debug the quirks of the API.
General Issues 8. The initially deployed researcher profile report in the bugJournal section mixed personal profiles with daily scout reports. There was no separation of semantic content. Solution: Humans pointed out that personal profiles should be placed in separate research/folder sections. Created a new website/content/research/ section and added dedicated menus and index.md files. Updated deploy_to_hugo() function to point to the new section.
Key Insight: Different content types should be organized in separate Hugo sections for better organization and navigation. Researchers’ personal profiles are reference materials, while bugJournal is a temporal report. Semantic classification is crucial for discoverability of content.
9. When running research_scout.py directly, the research analyzer tool reported “No module named research”. Solution: Changed the execution method from “python research/research_scout.py profile” to “python -m research analyze” to correctly identify the package structure.
Key Insight: Complex Python packages require the correct entry point. Using the -m flag ensures proper module resolution for packages with internal imports.
10. When reinstalling the package, pip install -e . failed on Windows systems due to file lock-up of gadget-mcp.exe. Solution: Confirmed that common/package imports can be directly taken from sys.path, as the editable installation already provides the root directory of the package. Skipped reinstallation and continued with the operation.
Human and Artificial Intelligence Approach Strategic Level Student Discovery Method Role Method Humans Recognized that faculty pages clearly list student information. Therefore, after the co-author analysis failed, proposed using homepage scraping as the main method, while leveraging the domain knowledge of professors maintaining authoritative student lists. AI Initially, only based on Semantic Scholar data for co-author analysis, using a four-dimensional scoring criteria (first author 40%, time concentration 25%, collaboration frequency 20%, latestness 15%). If possible, AI will continue to debug the scoring algorithm instead of changing the method. Difference Analysis: Humans directly identify the most reliable information sources based on their real-world knowledge of academic practices, while AI relies on quantitative signals in publication metadata. Humans know where the truth lies; AI defaults to using available data APIs and statistical inference. This shows that humans have an advantage in understanding authoritative sources, while AI shows stronger capabilities in processing structured data patterns.
Root Cause Analysis of CalendarPro | Role | Method | |——|——| | Humans | Users described high-level symptoms (“Overall experience is poor”, “Intent understanding is inaccurate”, “Task completion is unreliable”). Used Claude Local CLI (powerful model) to describe these symptoms, expecting “comprehensive improvement”. | | AI | Immediately conducted a systematic root cause analysis, breaking down system-level problems into 7 specific technical issues: problems with the intent classification layer (no confidence threshold, LLM timeout, no learning function); problems with the task execution layer (no provider retry mechanism, rough token estimation, incomplete proxy implementation). |
Difference Analysis: Users provide symptoms and background information; AI systematically debugs complex systems, identifying actionable technical problems. AI performs well in systematic debugging, while humans only see overall dysfunction and realize that the problem lies in process design rather than model capabilities.
Architecture Integration Strategy Role Method Humans Users specified “Keep research_scout.py as a unified entry point. Import from the modular package as library code.” Utilized modular code while retaining a single entry point. Provided a detailed 9-step plan, including specific file paths and line count statistics. AI Initially tried to improve the existing CLAUDE.md documentation, but did not propose architectural changes. Only executed unified processing after receiving a clear integration plan. AI focuses more on gradual documentation improvements. Difference Analysis: Humans saw the strategic value of a unified interface to improve the user experience and provided specific guidelines. AI focuses on gradual improvements, requiring architectural guidance for cross-domain changes. Humans provide “what,” while AI provides “how to do it.”
Citation Scoring Method for Paper Evaluation Role Method Humans Specified that citation count should only be used for sorting or tie-breaking, not affecting overall relevance scoring. Recognized that popularity and project relevance are independent. AI Initially proposed incorporating citation count into the overall evaluation score, confusing “important papers” with “relevant to the project”. Difference Analysis: Humans recognized that highly cited papers might not align with project priorities. AI defaults to treating citation count as a quality signal, not a discovery clue. This indicates that AI tends to optimize overall importance rather than specific context relevance.
Depth of Researcher Trajectory Analysis Role Method Humans Provided a list of original papers with basic metadata (title, year, citation count) and requested advanced trajectory analysis. AI Generated a comprehensive multi-paragraph description of breakthrough papers, explaining historical context, research topics, and methodological development processes. Difference Analysis: Humans required advanced analysis; AI generated multi-dimensional summaries, including causal reasoning about why breakthroughs occurred at those times. AI went beyond pattern recognition to organize historical context.### Role Method Human Immediately identifies the name “Ming Yu” as too generic during error review, providing the full name “Mingyu Ding”; aware that common Chinese names may require disambiguation issues. Artificial Intelligence Accepts the matching results from the first Semantic Scholar without questioning their correctness; only marks data quality issues when encountering clearly erroneous papers (e.g., in fields like explosive chemistry, veterinary science, etc.). Difference Analysis: Humans know in advance that common names need to be disambiguated; AI only notices problems after reviewing the results. Humans leverage prior knowledge of system issues; AI relies on empirical detection.
When to Trigger Citation Analysis in the Reporting Process Role Method Human Always automatically performs citation analysis on the top 5 papers; prioritizes convenience over explicit control based on actual usage patterns (which is necessary in most cases). Artificial Intelligence Proposes making citation analysis an optional feature with the –citations flag; for potentially expensive operations, it defaults to requiring explicit user selection. Difference Analysis: Humans consider the friction in real-world workflows, while AI focuses on theoretical flexibility and cost optimization. Humans prioritize reducing friction in common workflows rather than pursuing architectural purity.
Methods for Implementing the Refactoring Plan Role Method Human Provides a detailed multi-stage plan, including specific file paths, line count statistics, expected changes, and clear checkpoints—specifying exact steps. Artificial Intelligence First reads the files in parallel to understand the current state, then verifies that the import chain works properly after each stage. This adds verification steps and parallel I/O optimization, which were not included in the original plan. Difference Analysis: Humans use a specification with clear checkpoints; AI adds verification steps and parallel processing. Both emphasize phased execution of the system rather than atomic, massive changes. Humans’ detailed plan eliminates ambiguity and ensures consistent implementation.
Hugo Content Organization Role Method Human Specifies that researcher profiles should be placed in new research sections, not in the bugJournal, understanding the semantic differences between reference materials and time-sensitive reports. Artificial Intelligence May deploy content into the bugJournal according to the existing pattern, since the reports in research_scout.py are placed there. Both are considered “research content”. Difference Analysis: Humans distinguish between time-sensitive content (daily reports) and reference content (researcher profiles), needing to be placed in different sections; AI treats both as the same category and combines them together. Humans understand content classification for better navigation.
Implementation Level Handling Missing Conference Award Data Role Method Human It is assumed that AI may include award information in the training data; requests AI to identify papers with conference awards. Artificial Intelligence Recognizes knowledge limitations—cannot confidently confirm specific awards, so it returns an empty list with explanations instead of guessing, maintaining a conservative “unknown” response. Difference Analysis: Humans assume that AI’s training data may contain award information; AI understands the boundaries of uncertainty and chooses not to spread incorrect information. This shows that AI understands when to say “I don’t know”.
Document Language Selection Role Method Human Clearly requests “Please update tutorial.md in Chinese now,” recognizing that the target audience (Chinese researchers) needs tutorials written in their native language. Artificial Intelligence Always writes code/comments in English, but immediately converts them into comprehensive Chinese documentation when requested. For technical documents, English is used by default. Difference Analysis: Humans identify the needs of the end users based on the target audience; AI adopts a neutral default setting. This reflects humans’ understanding of user demographics, while AI adopts a language-independent approach.
Limitations of Artificial Intelligence Key Limitations In the initial analysis of researchers, the AI failed to actively detect ambiguities in the names of Semantic Scholars—accepting obviously incorrect matches (e.g., in fields like explosive chemistry, veterinary science, geology), and required observable symptoms or clear instructions to trigger error detection. Despite domain mismatches, no questions were raised about the matching results. A clear 9-step integration plan is needed to implement complex architectural changes; the initial attempt was interrupted due to user suggestions that the AI should focus more on document improvements rather than structural restructuring. Architectural guidance is needed for cross-cutting changes. The initial design only considered student collaborative research, ignoring the fact that teacher homepages are authoritative and clear sources for obtaining student lists; human advice was needed to change the approach. During analysis of the code library, the entire “new feature” directory was initially ignored; a clear user prompt was needed to discover an unrecorded analysis tool containing approximately 15 Python modules. Even after fixing the third-level LLM, it was still impossible to parse the trajectory analysis JSON files of Yuke Zhu, resulting in incomplete reports with only a list of papers and no analytical insights. Clear user modifications were needed to understand that researcher profiles should be placed in a different Hugo chapter from the daily research reconnaissance report, lacking semantic understanding of content classification (reference vs. time-related). Referring counts were used as part of the overall relevance score, but it was not realized that popularity and project-specific relevance are orthogonal dimensions; “important papers” and “project-related” were confused. General Limitations After large-scale refactoring, it is impossible to automatically test end-to-end functions—import operations were verified, but actual tool commands were not run using the new paths to confirm correctness. It is not possible to reliably verify conference awards (best papers, focus awards, oral awards) solely based on training data—real-time access to official conference websites or paper metadata APIs is required. Instead of guessing, an empty array with a disclaimer is returned. No optimization solutions for citation analysis were proposed (e.g., threshold for referring counts, selective flags); the rule of “always analyzing the top 5” was blindly implemented without considering the impact on API costs. English documentation is used by default, even though the code library belongs to a Chinese user project; clear instructions are needed to switch TUTORIAL.md to Chinese. It is impossible to automatically summarize the final state of multiple parallel agents; manual verification is required to ensure that all 230 CalendarPro tests passed after 5 agents completed their work. When pip installation fails due to Windows file locking, no alternative solutions were proposed, such as stopping the MCP server process or using the –user flag, until action was taken after the first failure. By default, citation analysis is optional (with the –citations flag), rather than being considered as part of the usage pattern, which tends to be automated for ease of use. Lessons Learned Key Lessons For large-scale architectural refactoring, a detailed written plan, including function signatures, line counts, and architectural decisions, can significantly improve the accuracy of AI execution and prevent scope creep. The combination of human architectural vision and AI tactical execution yields better results than AI’s improvisation. Large-scale refactoring should be processed in stages or by domain: CalendarPro adopted 7 stages, with 5 agents working simultaneously on different parts, followed by testing and verification. Phased execution with defined checkpoints can prevent difficult-to-diagnose cascading failures. Academic figure analysis systems must prioritize name disambiguation—common Chinese names cause serious disambiguation issues, requiring mandatory ORCID/institution matching. Aggregating researchers with the same name results in completely incorrect profiles that appear plausible until domain experts identify the incompatibility. Instead of spreading misinformation, it is better to return “data unavailable”. Multi-source data integration (web scraping, co-author relationships, ArXiv, Semantic Scholar) combined with clear source tracking can withstand failures or inaccuracies of a single data source. Teacher homepages are more accurate at identifying students than co-author analysis, as they clearly list current and past students along with their status. By adjusting import and re-export timing, backward compatibility can be maintained during large-scale code refactoring, avoiding circular dependencies. Centralized infrastructure modules (paths.py, cache.py, llm.py) enable atomic refactoring—changing one constant affects the entire code without breaking downstream dependencies. Modular architecture allows for clear integration: keeping research_scout.py as a single CLI entry point and the profiler package as an independent library code allows for better separation of different functions. Mixed integration methods (retaining a stable CLI while refactoring internal code) are easier to migrate than complete rewriting. Academic research analysis tools can quickly understand the current state of the field: Researchers analyzers can quickly reveal leaders in the field, current directions, and breakthrough achievements. A systematic framework can track the latest research directions. When multiple tools share similar output patterns, organizing by type (reports/, logs/, cache/) is easier to maintain than organizing by tool. Centralized path management makes refactoring the entire code library more atomic. Passing parameters in nested call chains requires backward-compatible default values (backend=‘claude_cli’) and step-by-step syntax testing (py_compile) to prevent cascading errors. Default values can maintain backward compatibility when adding parameters to deeply nested call chains. Before making architectural decisions, use parallel agents for exploration to fully understand the code library; sequential exploration would miss connections between components. Documentation like CLAUDE.md becomes outdated quickly as features increase, requiring continuous maintenance or automatic detection of unrecorded code paths. Documentation-driven development is very effective: updating CLAUDE.md and TUTORIAL.md immediately after code changes prevents documentation obsolescence and ensures that knowledge is passed down for future conferences. Document synchronization is crucial for maintainability. Heuristic algorithms rely heavily on data quality: when data is insufficient, using Semantic Scholar for student discovery fails; multiple sources or real data (e.g., teacher homepages) are needed. When the heuristic algorithm fails, direct information sources should be used instead of debugging the scoring algorithm. The upgrade process of LLM repairs (from haiku to sonnet to masterpiece) has limitations; persistent JSON parsing failures may indicate a problem with prompts or context, rather than model capabilities. When all three levels of models fail, the input structure or problem decomposition needs to be reexamined. Always ask users about design trade-offs (scoring weights, automatic features vs. active selection features, performance vs. convenience), rather than assuming default settings. User context reveals some implicit priorities, such as preferring automatic citation analysis over cost optimization. When extracting shared common features, it is better to adopt the method of merging the best implementations (choosing the most robust implementation from each source), which is better than arbitrary selection. API search behavior is not always intuitive; multi-source data acquisition is more reliable than single API queries. Summary of Practical Experience Hugo’s content organization benefits from semantic separation: reference materials and time-sensitive content should be placed in different sections for better navigation and user experience. The input point for Python packages is important: ‘python -m package’ is more reliable than executing scripts directly, suitable for complex module structures with internal imports. Using the -m flag ensures correct module resolution. Polite web crawling (requiring a rate limit every 2 seconds) is crucial when scraping academic homepages on a large scale, to avoid being blocked or causing server overload. Dialogue Summary CalendarPro ✅ Comprehensive Optimization and Related Project Research 21:29:45.386 | claude_code User feedback indicated that the experience with CalendarPro was unsatisfactory, with issues such as misinterpretation of intentions and failure to complete tasks. The AI analyzed the code library and identified 7 root causes (no confidence threshold, lack of retry mechanism, rough token estimation, LLM timeout, lack of learning loop, incomplete agent implementation), and designed a 7-stage optimization plan to address issues in intention classification and task execution. This plan was executed by 5 parallel agents: semantic routing threshold, hybrid keyword + embedding routing, split system prompt (530 lines → basic + 11 segments), provider retry mechanism, scheduling scoring, automatic learning from non-matchings, ThoughtStore caching. All 230 tests passed. Additionally, the existing CLAUDE.md was analyzed (very comprehensive, no need for modification), and similar open-source projects were studied (FluidCalendar, CoPaw, Khoj, OpenDAN, Cal.com, Leon, Rasa, Home Assistant), concluding that CalendarPro’s energy-aware scheduling, three-tier architecture, double verification, and unified life management are unique combinations in the open-source community.
Gadget Research Toolkit**✅ Unified Research Toolkit Integration, Documentation Writing, and Comprehensive Researcher Profiling System** 02:16:04.409 | claude_code A completely undocumented modular researcher profiling tool was discovered, along with the existing research_scout.py paper discovery script. Users requested integrating these two tools and adding citation graph analysis functionality. The AI explored these two code libraries through parallel agent tasks and then executed a detailed 9-step integration plan: removing duplicate “New feature/” directories, adding Semantic Scholar’s citation API (get_paper_by_id/citations/references), creating personal profiles and citation sub-commands, implementing three-stage evaluation, including citation impact analysis on the top 5 papers, adding support for multiple backend LLMs (claude_cli/anthropic/openai), changing the Chinese TUTORIAL.md from 10 chapters to 13 chapters. A robot domain leader project was created, and profiles were generated for 13 researchers: Sergey Levine (h-index 166, 160K citations), Pieter Abbeel (h-index 164, 177K citations, identifying three stages: manipulation/LfD → deep reinforcement learning revolution, including TRPO/GAE/MAML/SAC → generative model/LLM integration), Chelsea Finn (initially matched incorrectly, but recognized as a Stanford University professor), Yuke Zhu (Best Paper at MineDojo NeurIPS 2022), Yunzhu Li (Best Paper at Open X-Embodiment NeurIPS 2023), Mingyu Ding (Breakthroughs in VRDP, DaViT, CAE, Open X-Embodiment fields), Ruoshi Liu (Zero-1-to-3, Objaverse-XL, evolution from physics to 3D vision to embodied intelligence), Xiaolong Wang (name ambiguity issue, domain incompatibility), Shuran Song (FlingBot, UMI framework, video world model). Six researchers’ doctoral advisors were successfully found: Mingyu Ding → Jitendra Malik, Ruoshi Liu → Carl Vondrick, Xiaolong Wang → Abhinav Gupta, Shuran Song → Thomas Funkhouser, Yunzhu Li → Antonio Torralba, Yuke Zhu → Li Fei-Fei. Multiple Semantic Scholar name ambiguity issues were encountered, as well as JSON parsing failures in some analyses. All CLAUDE.md documents were updated, including the root directory, summary directory, and research directory. Result: A production-ready unified academic research toolkit, implementing a complete paper → researcher → citation workflow.**
Small Tool Kit Infrastructure ✅ Extracting Common/Packages and Merging Output Directories 21:37:49.486 | claude_code A comprehensive 4-stage refactoring was implemented, merging approximately 400 lines of repeated LLM calls, JSON parsing, atomic writing, and configuration loading into a new pip-installable common/package. Six modules were created (io, cache, jsonUtils, llm, hugo, paths), and a backward-compatible re-export scheme was provided (summarize/llm_backends.py, research/cache.py), eliminating the need for sys.path tricks. Due to Windows file locking issues, pip installation failed, but functions that could be directly imported from an editable installation file were verified. Then, a 7-stage output directory merger was executed: a common/paths.py was created, containing standard constants (GADGET_ROOT, OUTPUTS_DIR, REPORTS_DIR, LOGS_DIR, CACHE_DIR, DATA_DIR, SITE_OUTPUTS_DIR), all tool-specific paths were migrated to a unified output/directory structure, the.gitignore file was updated, and all documents were updated. All CLI commands and imports were verified to work properly. The main gadget CLAUDE.md was also updated: a reference to sub-project CLAUDE.md was added at the top, the Running Scripts section was reduced from 68 lines to 33 lines, the architecture section was updated, the modular structure of the research profiling module was described, the document length was reduced from 175 lines to 146 lines, while improving clarity.**
Small Tool Research Profiling System**✅ Student Discovery Based on Homepages and Hugo Deployment** 20:04:52.986 | claude_code After the co-author analysis failed to find students associated with renowned professors, an 8-step plan was implemented as the primary method for student discovery on the homepage. The research/homepage_discovery.py script was created, utilizing HTMLParser-based text extraction, two new prompt templates (EXTRACT_STUDENTS_PROMPT, PARSE_STUDENTS_LIST_PROMPT) for LLM-based student extraction, and various strategies for URL discovery. The discover_students() function was refactored to prioritize homepage information when combined with co-author information, merging results with source tracking (source=‘homepage’/coauthorship, status field). A polite rate limit was added (1 request every 2 seconds). According to user requests, individual researcher profiles were created in a separate section of the website/content/research/Hugo, rather than being mixed with bugJournal reports. The config.yml file was updated with research menu items, the_index.md file was created, the deploy_to_hugo() function was added to research/output.py, and the –deploy/–hugo-site CLI flags were incorporated. Batch analysis was performed on six researchers, identifying all doctoral supervisors, while encountering ambiguity issues with Semantic Scholar. The research/CLAUDE.md file was updated to document the homepage_discovery.py module, expanding the student discovery section from 2 lines to a complete two-stage process description.**
Token Usage Total: $0.64 — 1.3 million tokens
summary: The CalendarPro system has been fully optimized. A 7-stage parallel processing approach has been adopted, and it has successfully passed 230 tests. Additionally, a unified toolkit for developing small tools has been developed, integrating paper retrieval, performance analysis, and citation information into a single command-line interface. The shared tools have been extracted and placed in a public package, thereby avoiding the need to write more than 400 lines of duplicate code. All output directories are now organized into a structured hierarchical structure. Furthermore, performance analyses have been conducted on 13 leading researchers in robotics and artificial intelligence, enabling systematic tracking of their academic research progress.
title: Bug Journal 2026-03-15

content:
Daily Report — 2026-03-14 Daily Overview Completed tasks: Enhanced the GPU monitoring script for the robobrain_pi project, created a HDF5 camera data visualization tool for RoboTwin, and designed 4 sets of manipulation progress prediction comparative experiments for the pi05 policy model. Methods used: Used ANSI escape codes for alternative screen buffers and adaptive layouts, decoded JPEG images using OpenCV and concatenated multiple camera views, added an MLP decoder and special token mechanism in the JAX/Flax framework. Impact: The monitoring tool achieved a user experience comparable to nvitop, providing complete visualization of 4-camera data for 50 episodes, and established a complete framework for progress-conditioning action prediction experiments. Achieved adaptive UI for GPU monitoring scripts, HDF5 data visualization tool, and 4 sets of progress prediction experiments for the pi05 model
Tasks Architecture & Strategy ✅ Design of pi05 model progress prediction framework — Designed 4 sets of comparative experiments: last_token vs special_token × time vs distance ✅ Implementation of pi05 model modifications (6 files) — Modified pi0_config.py/model.py/tokenizer.py/robotwin_policy.py/config.py/pi0.py to include an MLP decoder, special tokens, conditional injection, and auxiliary loss ❌ Data conversion script updates — Need to modify convert_robotwin_democlean_to_lerobot.py by adding progress-related fields to enable training Implementation & Fixes ✅ HDF5 camera data visualization script — Created visualize_hdf5_cameras.py to concatenate 50 episodes of 4-camera data into a 2x2 grid MP4 video ✅ UI enhancements for GPU monitoring script gpumon.py — Added alternative screen buffers, adaptive column/row sizes, process deduplication, and noise filtering Problems & Solutions Critical Issues 1. Training data not converted to lerobot format, missing progress-related fields causing experiments not to start Solution: Need to modify convert_robotwin_democlean_to_lerobot.py by adding keys such as manip_progress_time/distance_left/distance_right/target_endpose/target_joint
Key Insight: Model experiments depend on the integrity of the data pipeline; data format conversion is a blocking step
General Issues 2. os.get_terminal_size() cannot read environment variables COLUMNS/LINES in sub-processes, resulting in non-adaptive table widths Solution: Try reading COLUMNS/LINES environment variables first, then fall back to os.get_terminal_size() if failed
Key Insight: Environment variables are more reliable for cross-process communication than system calls
Human vs AI Approaches Strategic Level Gradient strategy selection in pi05 experiment design Role Approach Human Directly chose option A (all stop_gradient) based on the reasoning ’ safest, does not affect action prediction, can be adjusted later’ — reflects a conservative starting strategy for iterative experiments AI AI proposed 3 options (A full block, B full return, C hybrid) and analyzed trade-offs, preferring option A but emphasizing the uniqueness of special tokens Difference Analysis: Humans make quick decisions based on engineering experience, while AI tends to list options and trade-offs thoroughly, but ultimately humans’ concise decision-making strategy (conservative first, then iterate) is reasonable
Naming and organization of experimental configurations Role Approach Human Requires ‘config switches, easier to start experiments’ — reflects emphasis on experimental efficiency AI AI designed a unified ProgressConfig class and 4 independent named configurations (exp1~4), allowing direct selection through tyro CLI Difference Analysis: Humans focus on practicality and convenience, while AI translates this into a config class design and CLI interface, both agreeing on the goal of ’easy experiment switching’
AI Limitations Critical Limitations AI did not proactively anticipate data pipeline issues after modifying the model — should have checked if data loading supported new fields immediately after modification, rather than waiting for user errors AI did not run actual training smoke tests when modifying pi05, only tested configuration imports, leading to missing dependencies in the data conversion script Learnings Key Learnings Integrity checks for complex model experiments should cover model code, configuration, data pipeline, and training scripts, not just model code verification Using stop_gradient in JAX/Flax for auxiliary tasks is a common multi-task learning pattern, allowing use of features while avoiding interference with the main task Special token mechanism requires 3 steps: tokenizer registers token IDs, model extends embedding layers, and prompts are concatenated during data loading Conversation Summaries Robobrain-PI (GPU Monitoring) ✅ UI enhancement for GPU monitor script with adaptive layout 09:24:23.170 | claude_code User requested an alternative screen buffer similar to nvitop and adaptive column/row sizing for gpumon.py. Implemented ANSI escape codes for screen buffer switching, dynamic width/height calculation from COLUMNS/LINES environment variables, proportional column sizing, and process deduplication. The script now provides a professional terminal UI that exits cleanly without polluting the terminal history.
RoboTwin VLA (HDF5 Visualization) ✅ Visualize 4-camera HDF5 data as MP4 videos 13:16:50.918 | claude_code User needed to visualize observation data from 50 HDF5 episodes (front/head/left/right cameras). Created visualize_hdf5_cameras.py that decodes JPEG-encoded frames, tiles them into 2×2 grids with labels, and writes 30fps MP4 files. Successfully processed all 50 episodes (~2.3MB each) to all_videos/ directory.
RoboTwin VLA (PI05 Experiments) 🔄 Designed and implemented 4 progress prediction experiments for pi05 14:21:07.908 | claude_code User requested 4 contrastive experiments for manipulation progress prediction: (1) last_token+time, (2) special_token+time, (3) last_token+distance, (4) special_token+distance. After discussing architecture (MLP 2048→256→out, λ=0.1, stop_gradient strategy), modified 6 files to add ProgressConfig, 3 special tokens, MLP decoder, condition injection logic, and auxiliary MSE loss. Created 4 training configs (pi05_robotwin_prog_exp1~4). Verified all imports but discovered data pipeline blocker at the end.
Token Usage Total: $6.79 — 10.5M tokens
summary: The script for GPU monitoring features an adaptive UI, a HDF5 data visualization tool, as well as four sets of progress prediction experimental frameworks for the pi05 model.
title: Bug Journal 2026-03-14

content:
Key Learnings### Practical Learning Outcomes ArXiv HTML rendering is now more suitable for full-text extraction than PDF: Faster, with no OCR errors, and retains the structure. For papers with pre-Html, PDF can be used as an alternative. This indicates that the evolution of infrastructure requires regular review of “best practices”. Numerical records without semantic tags can lead to ambiguity: “1736651” could refer to a count/id/size. Always use clear labels: “Author 1736651” or “1,736,651 papers”. The extra two words are less costly but can avoid misinterpretation of the entire category. For academic analysis tools, narrative sections prefer Chinese output, while technical terms prefer English. A dual-language approach: write the structure in English (breakthroughs, why not before, impact), and provide explanations in Chinese. When there is selection logic upstream (annual stratification → ≤10 years), downstream stages should not use different criteria for re-filtering. This can lead to inconsistent patterns (fast mode seeing different papers than detailed mode). Multi-agent exploration pattern: Use the Explore agent to understand the code library, the Plan agent for design, and implement them in sequence through user verification. This clearly separates concerns and allows for planning before execution. Dialogue Summary Gadget Research Tools ✅ Research Tool Design and Complete Implementation 20:08:05 | claude_code A complete academic researcher analysis tool was designed and built for the robotics field. Initial discussions covered requirements (analyzing why leaders are influential, breakthrough work, recursive student discovery, fast/detail modes). A 15-module Python package was implemented, including ArXiv/Semantic Scholar API, LLM abstraction (claude CLI), Chinese prompts, BFS recursion, scoring algorithms, and CLI functions for analysis, display, listing, and configuration. Directory structure, requirements.txt, and documentation were created. The tool supports caching, hierarchical selection by year, prioritizes awards, and generates JSON and Markdown reports.
✅ Critical Bug Fixes and Feature Enhancements 20:45:09 | claude_code Multiple critical issues were fixed: (1) Data source priorities reversed—S2 as the main source (comprehensive historical coverage), ArXiv providing rich metadata. (2) Hierarchical selection by year was implemented: grouped by year, sorted by award weight and citation count, ≤10 years/year, displayed in chronological order. (3) An LLM-based award recognition phase was added, using training data. (4) Log ambiguity issues were resolved: “[S2] Get Papers: 1736651” → “[S2] Get Papers by Author 1736651…”. (5) JSON parsing was refactored, using a progression from haiku to tanka to opus, saving failed responses to disk. (6) Detailed mode was fixed: downloading all filtered papers, not just the top ten globally.
✅ Researcher Trajectory Analysis 20:28:15 | claude_code Detailed academic trajectory analyses were generated for Sergey Levine and Pieter Abbeel. Levine: Career phases from 2016-2025, breakthrough works (Horizon Reduction offline RL, Q-learning Adjoint Matching, π0.5 VLA, Natural Language Actor-Critic, Multistep Quasimetric Learning), with time dependencies explained (data size, GPU computing, underlying models). Abbeel: 3 phases (robotics 2012-2014, deep RL 2015-2018, expansion after 2019), 5 breakthroughs (TRPO, MAML, SAC, DDPM, Decision Transformer), h-index 164, 177K citations. ✅ Documentation Creation 20:58:09 | claude_code Comprehensive documentation was created: (1) Chinese tutorial file tutorial.md, covering quick start, installation, core commands, parameter settings, analysis modes (fast and detailed analysis), student discovery algorithms (4 signals + weights), scoring system (5 dimensions, 4 levels), output format, caching mechanisms (5 namespaces and validity periods), and common examples. (2) CLAUDE.md document for future AI-related content, including core commands, 9-step process, design choices (CLI-based LLM, JSON parsing strategy, caching/validity periods, rate limits, atomic writes), and output structure.
RoboTwin VLA ✅ Implemented and debugged new variable collection system 15:34:27.123 | claude_code Users requested implementation of the previously designed scheme. Claude added import statements for the pickle module, modified setup_demo() function to track state, removed override logic for get_critical_region_label(), and added override logic for take_picture() and move() functions to capture EE positions and replace pkl files with new variables. After testing, users reported two issues: critical_region still stored in HDF5 (basic classes would无条件存储 it), and progress_distance took negative values (curve trajectory). Claude solved these issues by adding pkl_data.pop() and np.clip() functions.
🔄 Post-processing workflow for Place Dual Shoes task 03:39:55.636 | claude_code Users requested removal of critical_region variable and addition of 5 new variables (manip_progress_time, manip_progress_distance_left/right, target_endpose, target_joint). Claude used the Explore agent to understand the code structure (place_dual_shoes.py, _base_task.py, pkl2hdf5.py), then used the Plan agent to design post-processing schemes. The override logic for move() functions tracked EE positions at each frame and updated pkl files after completion. The Plan scheme was recorded, but users did not continue implementing it.

title: Bug Journal 2026-03-13

content:
Daily Report — 2026-03-12 Daily Overview What was done: Cross-platform solutions were implemented for three independent projects: upgrading the sandbox permissions architecture of macOS wallpaper applications, optimizing the output of GPU monitoring tools, and fixing dependency chains in the deep learning evaluation environment. How it was done: System-level log analysis was used to identify issues with the macOS TCC permission mechanism. Dependency version matrices were utilized to track ABI compatibility in the PyTorch ecosystem. The CUDA toolchain was configured to address issues with C++ extensions during local compilation. Impact: macOS applications now have production-grade file access persistence capabilities. Two research projects on the tianhe server have resumed operation (although the checkpoint path issue has not been fully resolved). MacBook What was done: Sandbox migration and permission system restructuring were completed for desktop-video applications. A critical bug was fixed, allowing history records to open old files. How it was done: The App Sandbox was enabled to make security-scoped bookmarks effective. Bookmark parsing logic was revised, and an NSOpenPanel reauthorization mechanism was added. Impact: The user history function is now fully functional. The app adheres to macOS best practices, paving the way for its release on the App Store. tianhe What was done: Conflicts in PyTorch dependencies for the RoboTwin project were resolved. Compilation issues with curobo were addressed, and the readability of GPU monitoring tools was improved. How it was done: The pip version matrix was used to match torch/torchvision. The CUDA toolchain path (CUDA_HOME and CPATH) was configured in the conda environment. The process aggregation logic was rewritten to limit the output width. Impact: The robot strategy evaluation environment has mostly resumed its functionality. GPU resource monitoring is more efficient, but the checkpoint path mapping issue remains unresolved. The sandbox permissions architecture of macOS wallpaper applications was fixed, and issues with PyTorch dependencies and CUDA compilation in the deep learning evaluation environment were resolved.
Tasks Architecture & Strategy ✅ Fixed the issue where historical records could not open old videos — Enabled the App Sandbox, corrected bookmark parsing logic (added non-scoped fallback), added a retry mechanism for permission errors (NSOpenPanel reauthorization), updated localization and ChangeLog. ✅ Resolved conflicts in PyTorch versions for the RoboTwin evaluation environment — Upgraded torchvision from 0.21.0 to 0.22.1+cu126 to match torch 2.7.1. This resolved issues with operator registration for torchvision::nms. ✅ Recompiled curobo to match the current PyTorch ABI — Deleted the old.so file, installed ninja, set CUDA_HOME and CPATH, and recompiled from source using pip install -e . ❌ Resolved the issue where the RoboTwin checkpoint path did not exist — The eval.sh parameter specified checkpoint_id=5000, but the directory contained only 15000/25000/29999. The session was interrupted when analyzing the parameter mapping logic. Implementation & Fixes 🔄 Optimized the output format of GPU monitoring tools — Limited the output to 80 columns, aggregated GPU lists by process, and filtered out noise from multiprocessing workers. Users reported that understanding errors caused the session to be interrupted. Problems & Solutions Critical Issues 1. macOS non-sandbox applications cannot persist file access permissions. Old videos in history records report Operation not permitted errors. Solution: Enabled App Sandbox permissions (com.apple.security.app-sandbox and files.user-selected.read-write). Fixed ensureFileAccess to add non-scoped bookmark fallback parsing. Added NSOpenPanel reauthorization mechanism for showVideo/showImage.
Key Insight: macOS TCC requires the App Sandbox to create security-scoped bookmarks. Non-scoped bookmarks can only parse URLs without granting access rights, resulting in inability to open old files. A combination of sandbox and downgrade paths is needed.
2. Torch 2.7.1 and torchvision 0.21.0 are incompatible, causing torchvision::nms operator registration failures. Solution: Upgraded torchvision to 0.22.1+cu126 to match torch 2.7.1.
Key Insight: Custom operators in torchvision link to the C++ API of torch during compilation. Incompatible versions cause operator registration failures. torchvision 0.21.0 is compatible with torch 2.6.x, not 2.7.x.
3. The pre-compiled curobo.so file is incompatible with the current PyTorch ABI. JIT compilation cannot find cuda_runtime.h. Solution: Deleted the old.so file, installed the ninja compiler, set CUDA_HOME to /path/to/conda.env and CPATH to /path/to/targets/x86_64-linux/include/. Recompiled using pip install -e .
Key Insight: C++ extensions need to be recompiled after PyTorch version upgrades. Nvidia packages installed via pip in the conda environment place CUDA headers in non-standard paths (targets/x86_64-linux/include/), requiring correct environment variables to allow JIT compilation to find them.
General Issues 4. The GPU monitoring tool’s output is too wide and processes are displayed repeatedly, making it difficult to read. Solution: Limited the output to 80 columns, aggregated GPU lists by process, and filtered out noise from multiprocessing workers and other sub-processes.
Key Insight: The original implementation grouped GPUs, causing a multi-gpu process to appear multiple times on multiple GPUs. It’s necessary to aggregate by process and display all used card numbers in the GPU column.
Human vs AI Approaches Strategic Level Explanation depth of macOS file permission issues Role Approach Human Asked a straightforward question like ‘Why is there a lack of permissions?’ and asked AI to explain the underlying principles of TCC and sandbox mechanisms. AI Provided technical solutions but did not explain the background principles. Human intervention was needed to provide a complete context. Difference Analysis: Humans guided AI to provide a more complete background explanation rather than jumping directly to solutions. Humans focus more on ‘why’, while AI focuses on ‘how to do it’.
Selection of repair strategies Role Approach Human Clearly chose a defensive approach of combining sandbox and NSOpenPanel reauthorization. AI Proposed two separate solutions (sandbox or reauthorization) but did not suggest a combination. Difference Analysis: Humans prefer defensive programming (multiple layers of protection), while AI tends to prefer a single optimal solution. Humans’ combination strategy addresses both root causes and provides a fallback path, making it more robust.
Implementation Level Understanding of GPU monitoring tool requirements Role Approach Human Corrected the statement ‘Each process should only appear once on one GPU’ to emphasize the many-to-many relationship between processes and GPUs. AI Misunderstood the requirement, aggregating multiple GPU processes into a single line and displaying card numbers separated by commas in the GPU column. Difference Analysis: Humans understood the many-to-many relationship between processes and GPUs better. AI’s optimization led to information loss (resources used by a process on GPU0 and GPU1 should be displayed in two lines).
AI Limitations General Limitations Failed to explain the technical background (macOS TCC/sandbox mechanism) actively. Human intervention was needed to provide a complete context. AI should explain the ‘why’ when proposing solutions. Misunderstood the GPU monitoring requirement, simplifying the many-to-one relationship between processes and GPUs, leading to information loss. Incorrectly understood the visualization needs for complex data relationships. When analyzing checkpoint path issues, went too deep into code details (continuous Read operations) without confirming the scope of the problem with the user. High-level understanding of parameter mapping logic was needed first. Learnings Key Learnings macOS TCC permission mechanism: Non-sandbox applications cannot create security-scoped bookmarks. They can only create non-scoped bookmarks (which only parse URLs without granting access rights), causing historical file access to fail after app restart. App Sandbox must be enabled to persist file access permissions. PyTorch C++ extension ABI compatibility: Custom operators in torchvision (such as torchvision::nms) link to the C++ API of torch during compilation. Versions must be exactly matched (torchvision 0.21 → torch 2.6, torchvision 0.22 → torch 2.7). Incompatible versions cause operator registration failures. Condna environment CUDA toolchain configuration: Nvidia packages installed via pip place CUDA headers in targets/x86_64-linux/include/ instead of the standard include/ directory. JIT compilation of C++ extensions requires setting CUDA_HOME to point to the conda env and CPATH to include the targets path. Defensive programming principles: The combination of sandbox and reauthorization is more robust than a single solution. Sandbox addresses the root cause (persistent permissions), while NSOpenPanel provides a fallback path (temporary authorization). Together, they ensure users can always access files. Conversation Summaries Desktop Video ✅ Fixed the permission issue where historical records could not open old videos 07:52:05.359 | claude_code Users reported that old videos could not be opened in historical records (Operation not permitted Code=257). Log analysis revealed that the App did not enable the Sandbox, causing security-scoped bookmarks to fail to create. ensureFileAccess only tried with.withSecurityScope parsing, resulting in non-scoped bookmarks always failing, ultimately leading to macOS TCC refusal to access. Three layers of fixes were implemented: enabling App Sandbox permissions, fixing bookmark parsing by adding non-scoped fallback, and adding NSOpenPanel reauthorization mechanism for showVideo/showImage. Code modifications, localization updates, and ChangeLog were completed, and the build verification passed.
robobrain GPU monitor 🔄 Optimized the output format of GPU monitoring tools 15:48:28.705 | claude_code Users requested that the gpumon.py output width be limited to 80 columns and that duplicate processes be displayed. AI rewrote the process list logic, aggregating each process into a single line, displaying card numbers separated by commas in the GPU column, and filtering out noise from multiprocessing workers and other sub-processes. Users corrected their understanding, pointing out that it should be ’each process appears once on one GPU’ instead of aggregated display, causing the session to be interrupted.
RoboTwin Policy Evaluation 🔄 Diagnosed dependency conflicts that caused eval.sh to fail 02:23:18.758 | claude_code Users ran bash eval.sh and encountered errors. AI identified three layers of dependency problems: 1) Torch 2.7.1 and torchvision 0.21.0 were not compatible, causing torchvision::nms operator registration failures; 2) The pre-compiled curobo.so file was incompatible with the Torch ABI. JIT compilation lacked ninja; 3) The checkpoint path did not exist (the parameter specified 5000, but there were only 15000/25000/29999). AI provided a version upgrade solution, and users chose to upgrade torchvision.
🔄 Implemented dependency fixes and resolved subsequent compilation issues 02:34:02.614 | claude_code The fix plan was executed: successfully upgraded torchvision to 0.22.1+cu126 and verified its compatibility with torch 2.7.1; installed ninja compiler; deleted the old.so file and recompiled curobo by setting CUDA_HOME to the conda env’s targets/x86_64-linux/include/ and CPATH; verified that curobo imported correctly. After running eval.sh again, the checkpoint path issue was encountered. AI began analyzing the parameters in deploy_policy.yml and eval_policy.py, causing the session to be interrupted.
Token Usage Total: $0.25 — 1.1M tokens
summary: Fixed the sandbox permissions structure in the macOS Wallpaper app, and resolved issues related to PyTorch dependencies and CUDA compilation for deep learning evaluation environments.
title: Bug Journal 2026-03-12

content:
Daily Report — 2026-03-11 Daily Overview Completed Work: Reorganized the MIHD project file system and conducted RM-IDEAL benchmark tests. Developed a containerized GPU monitoring tool. Fixed dependencies issues in VLA training and eliminated a 33% performance gap through systematic dependency alignment. Implementation Methods: Systematic file migration via code updates, GPU detection through file system checks, determination of torch/torchvision versions, comparison of configurations using parallel exploration agents, and editing pyproject.toml using the uv toolchain to achieve dependency alignment. Impact: The MIHD code repository structure was improved. Baseline metrics showed a Spearman correlation coefficient of 0.1804. K8s GPU monitoring solution was deployed. VLA training environment became stable. Bottlenecks in the JAX version (from 0.5.0 to 0.5.3) were identified and resolved, achieving an expected performance improvement of approximately 25%. DCC Completed Work: Reorganized the MIHD project files (executed an 8-step plan), fixed errors in patch queries (barcode alignment), and conducted RM-IDEAL benchmark tests between 151673-151508. Implementation Methods: File migration based on Bash, updated 14 file path references using the Edit tool, and executed CPU-based benchmark tests using a conda environment. Impact: The code repository structure became clearer. There was a hierarchy of outputs/{DLPFC,HD,rm_ideal}. Cross-sectional query functionality was restored, and baseline RM-IDEAL metrics were obtained (average Spearman correlation coefficient: 0.1804). tianhe Completed Work: Developed a nvitop-style GPU monitoring tool for K8s containers. Fixed version conflicts between pi05 and torch/torchvision. Resolved differences in training speed between openpi and pi05 through systematic dependency alignment. Implementation Methods: Used /proc/fd and CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES to create a GPU process mapping diagram. Fixed versions in pyproject.toml. Used parallel exploration agents to compare deep code. Systematically aligned 6 shared dependencies (JAX 0.5.0→0.5.3, torch 2.5→2.7.1, etc.) using uv lock/sync. Impact: GPU monitoring solution was deployed with PID namespace isolation. Problems with pi05 training were resolved. It was found that the JAX version was a bottleneck causing 20-30% performance issues. By aligning pi05 dependencies, training time differences were eliminated, achieving an expected performance improvement of approximately 25%. Multi-project productivity: MIHD file structure adjustment + benchmark tests, K8s GPU monitoring tool, VLA training optimization (fixed torch/torchvision issues and achieved 33% performance improvement through JAX/dependency alignment)
Tasks Architecture and Strategy ✅ MIHD file structure reorganization — Executed an 8-step plan to migrate outputs/ directories: benchmark_results→DLPFC, hd_results→HD, rm_ideal into cross-section/single-section. Updated 14 code files and multiple documents. Cleared old directories. ✅ GPU monitoring tool development (gpumon.py) — Built a nvitop-style GPU monitoring tool for K8s containers. Features include: /proc/fd + CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES GPU detection, dual-boundary tables, processes grouped by GPU, color progress bars, automatic filtering of monitoring processes. Supports real-time refresh and one-time mode. ✅ Analysis and resolution of openpi vs pi05 training speed differences — Investigated differences in training times between 15 hours and 20 hours (33% difference). Used parallel exploration agents to identify root causes: JAX version (0.5.0 vs 0.5.3 - main factor), num_workers (8 vs 16), lerobot git revs, IMAGE_KEYS differences, conda base environment. Systematically aligned 6 dependencies in pi05 to match openpi. Added override-dependencies to resolve lerobot’s torch constraint conflict. Completed uv lock/sync. ✅ MIHD patch query error fixing — Fixed 3 errors causing “patch mismatch” queries: inconsistent barcode sorting between cache and data, inconsistent data paths, coordinate system alignment issues. Updated 4 files and added clear barcode-to-index mappings. ✅ MIHD RM-IDEAL benchmark tests (151673-151508) — Ran RM-IDEAL cross-section benchmarks on CPUs using existing embeddings and cache data. Results showed an average Spearman correlation coefficient of 0.1804. Layer_4/Layer_1 performed well, while Layer_3/Layer_6 showed negative correlations. Data was saved in summary.csv file. ✅ Pi05 torch/torchvision version fix — Resolved training startup error “operator torchvision::nms does not exist.” Identified mismatch between torch 2.7.1 + torchvision 0.21.0. Added torchvision==0.22.1 to pyproject.toml, ran uv sync, and verified the upgrade. Training environment was restored. Implementation and Fixes ❌ v5 error recovery visualization — User requested a 720p version of the v5 error scene (the existing 480p video). The plan mode was rejected multiple times, and the task was abandoned. It might be an environmental issue or the user changed their intention. Issues and Solutions Key Issues 1. MIHD patch query returned incorrect physical location: “Patch does not match the current patch” Solution: The root cause was inconsistent barcode sorting between the cache (sc.read_10x_h5 order) and visualization (sc.read_visium order). This was solved by explicitly mapping barcodes to cache indexes: {str(b): i for i, b in enumerate(cache_barcodes)}. Also fixed data path and coordinate convention issues. Key Insight: Index-based searches fail when the order of data sources is different. Always use a key-based alignment method (in this case, barcodes) when connecting cached data and reloading at runtime.
2. K8s container PID namespace isolation prevented nvidia-smi from displaying process information Solution: Used /proc//fd/ to build a custom GPU monitoring tool, scanning /dev/nvidia* device files and CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES environment variables. Filtered out monitoring tools that used all GPUs (nvitop, gpustat, Claude). Created a rich interface for the nvitop-style user interface. Key Insight: Containerized GPU monitoring requires checking the proc file system. /proc/fd symbolic links can display opened devices; CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES is the actual information. Monitoring tools that use all GPUs are false positives—can be determined by checking CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES.
3. Despite identical hardware and configuration, training times for the same commands differed by 33% between the two projects (openpi 15 hours vs pi05 20 hours) Solution: Parallel exploration agents identified differences in JAX versions (0.5.0 vs 0.5.3) as the main cause. Systematically aligned 6 dependency versions: JAX 0.5.0→0.5.3, torch>=2.5.1→2.7.1, orbax-checkpoint 0.11.1→0.11.13, numpy, beartype, transformers. Added override-dependencies to resolve lerobot’s torch<2.7 constraint conflict. Key Insight: A slight upgrade of the JAX version (0.5.0→0.5.3) can bring about a 20-30% performance improvement, as the XLA compiler has optimization capabilities. Multi-project dependency changes are a hidden performance issue that requires regular alignment audits. uv’s override-dependencies can solve conflicts without splitting upstream code.
4. MIHD file structure became chaotic due to inconsistent naming (benchmark_results, hd_results, rm_ideal were distributed in different directories) Solution: Implemented a systematic 8-step migration plan: created new hierarchies (DLPFC, HD, rm_ideal/{cross_section,single_section}), migrated files, updated 14 code files and documents, and cleared old directories. Used bash mv and Edit tools for batch path updates. Key Insight: Large-scale refactoring requires planning (create directories first), then file migration, followed by code updates, and finally document modifications. For historical documents, sed can be used for batch archiving.
5. Pi05 training failed at startup: ‘RuntimeError: torchvision::nms does not exist’ Solution: Identified a mismatch between torch 2.7.1 + torchvision 0.21.0 (0.21.0 was compiled for torch 2.6.x). Added torchvision==0.22.1 explicitly in pyproject.toml, then used uv sync for the upgrade. After verification, torch was set to 2.7.1+cu126, and torchvision was set to 0.22.1+cu126. Key Insight: PyTorch and torchvision have strict version pairing requirements (2.7.x requires 0.22.x). Indirect dependencies resolved by uv/pip may choose incompatible versions—always specify critical binary coupling dependencies explicitly.
Common Issues 6. Pi05’s wandb log shows num_workers=16, while the default value in the code is 8 Solution: It was found that the CLI parameter –num-workers was set to 16, overriding the default value. It is recommended not to pass this parameter instead of modifying the code. Key Insight: Configuration parameters may come from multiple sources (code defaults, CLI parameters, configuration files). It is necessary to fully trace their origins before making code changes.
7. The initial gpumon.py layout had issues with line-end wrapping, column truncation, and overly crowded process lists Solution: Gradually improved: 1) Fixed column width calculation for GPU information, 2) Adopted table format layout, 3) Used nvitop’s dual-boundary style, 4) Filtered out monitoring tools’ processes, 5) Grouped processes under corresponding GPUs. Key Insight: Terminal user interface design requires multiple iterations. Refer to existing tools (such as nvitop) to meet user expectations. Automating noise filtering (monitoring processes) can improve the signal-to-noise ratio.
Human-AI Approaches Strategic Level MIHD file reorganization implementation method Role Method Human Humans provided a detailed 8-step plan: create directories first, migrate files by category, update code references, update documents, verify the final structure. There was a clear division between each stage. AI AI may start with exploration and then propose modifications step by step. No excessive upfront planning is needed; focus is on discovery-driven approaches. Difference Analysis: Humans’ systematic planning avoided problems during execution (e.g., updating code before file migration). AI tends to be exploratory and iterative in its approach. For large-scale refactorings with many interdependent relationships, human methods are more appropriate.
Error recovery v5 visualization plan rejection loop Role Method Human After multiple plan rejections, humans said “Just run it now” (desiring immediate execution rather than continuing planning). However, they still continued trying to exit the plan mode. AI AI interpreted “Just run it now” as “Exiting the plan mode and executing”. It continued trying the ExitPlanMode tool. It didn’t realize that the user might have environmental barriers or changed their intention. Difference Analysis: Communication issues: The user’s repeated rejections indicated an unspoken problem (unavailable GPU nodes? Task no longer needed?). AI should ask “What stopped us?” after two or three rejections instead of repeating the same approach.
Dependency alignment strategy Role Method Human Directly instructed: “Align all pi05 dependencies with openpi, and set num_workers to 8” AI Detailed analysis: compared two pyproject.toml files, categorized dependencies as shared (6 versions to update), pi05-specific (retain RoboTwin package), openpi-specific (not needed in pi05), and fixed git revs (retain lerobot)" Difference Analysis: AI broke down the request into specific editing actions (6 specific version changes + 1 new configuration section), instead of replacing everything. This allowed retaining project-specific dependencies and avoiding compatibility issues caused by changing lerobot’s git revs.
GPU monitoring interface design expectations Role Method Human Humans explicitly asked for “reference to nvitop’s layout”, “process grouping by GPU”, and “no processes showing unused GPUs”. There were clear reference points and priorities. AI AI’s initial design was a general TUI with process lists—no reference to industry-leading tools (nvitop). It was adjusted after multiple user modifications. Difference Analysis: Humans knew the established pattern (nvitop) and expected it. AI did not actively check the user experience patterns of existing tools. Lesson: When building CLI tools, it is necessary to study leading industry tools.
Implementation Level num_workers configuration fix Role Method Human Explicitly requested code changes to set num_workers to 8 AI Investigated actual execution and found that the default value was already 8. Then traced back to the CLI parameter –num-workers being 16. Suggested a non-invasive solution (omitting the CLI flag) Difference Analysis: After identifying the root cause of the problem, AI chose to correct the behavior rather than modifying the code, avoiding unnecessary code changes.
Limitations of AI Major Limitations Plan mode death spiral: In the v5 visualization task, AI tried the ExitPlanMode four times or more, but was rejected each time. It was only after multiple attempts that it asked the user “What stopped us from moving forward?” Instead, it should have detected the rejection loop earlier and switched to the clarification mode. General Limitations Initially editing pyproject.toml, it was not anticipated that lerobot’s torch<2.7 constraint conflict would occur. It was only after uv lock failed that this issue was discovered, and then it was passively added to override-dependencies. A dependency graph could have been analyzed in advance. Two parallel exploration agents were used to handle almost the same code base (openpi and pi05 shared the same OpenPI framework), which led to duplicate discoveries. If only one agent was used for sequential exploration, efficiency would be higher. The initial GPU monitoring design was generic and did not take into account domain characteristics. It did not actively check the user experience patterns of nvitop (leading industry tool). Multiple user modifications were needed to achieve the desired layout. When building CLI tools, it is necessary to study existing tools. In the training speed analysis, two parallel Explore agents were started, but the uv.lock files (real data from installed versions) were not compared initially. It is important to first check locked files like train.py, config.py, etc. when dealing with dependency issues.### Version Upgrade of JAX/XLA (0.5.0 → 0.5.3)
Upgrading to version 0.5.3 can improve training speed by 20-30% through compiler optimization. Even minor updates at the framework level are worth performing a performance audit in computationally intensive machine learning projects. Changes in dependencies between multiple projects can be a hidden performance issue that needs regular adjustment. Strict Version Pairing in PyTorch/torchvision For example, torch 2.7.x requires torchvision 0.22.x, while 2.6.x requires 0.21.x. If the versions do not match, C++ operator registration will fail. When using torchvision, it is necessary to explicitly specify the dependencies of both components, as indirect solutions may fail.
Barcode Sorting Issues in Spatial Transcriptomics The order of barcodes in the cache created by sc.read_10x_h5 may differ from the one used in sc.read_visium during runtime. Index-based searches may incorrectly map to the wrong positions. Always use a clear mapping from barcodes to indices: {str(b): i for i, b in enumerate(cache_barcodes)}.
User Barriers When Plans Are Repeatedly Rejected When plans are repeatedly rejected, users may face insurmountable obstacles such as unavailable environments, changes in priorities, or technical limitations. After 2-3 rejections, the focus should shift from “Let me fix the plan” to “What is preventing us from moving forward?”
RM-IDEAL Center Point Calculation For a given query domain, the center point is calculated by taking the embedding vectors of all points in that domain and calculating the average vector. Then, the matching between the center point and all reference points is compared using cosine similarity. This reduces the comparison from N pairs of M to 1 pair of M, improving efficiency and reliability.
uv’s Overlapping Dependency Tool uv’s overlapping dependency tool is a precise tool for resolving transitive dependency conflicts without splitting or patching upstream packages. This tool is particularly useful when fixing git revisions related to incompatible constraints.
Practical Experience Summary Before assuming a repair solution, it is necessary to track all source configuration parameters (code defaults, CLI parameters, configuration files, environment variables). This is because the actual values during runtime may differ from the results of code checks.
Dialogue Summary MIHD ✅ File structure reorganization, patch query fixes, and RM-IDEAL benchmark testing
00:20:01.434 | claude_code
Comprehensive maintenance of MIHD included three steps: 1) File reorganization—detailed 8-step plan to migrate output directories (benchmark_results → DLPFC, hd_results → HD, rm_ideal divided into cross-sections/single-sections), updated 14 code files and documentation, and batch updating of archived documents. 2) Patch query fixes—resolved issues caused by inconsistent barcode sorting between caching (sc.read_10x_h5) and runtime (sc.read_visium), inconsistent data paths, and inconsistent coordinate conventions. This was achieved by explicitly specifying the mapping from barcodes to cache indices in four files. 3) RM-IDEAL benchmark testing—CPU-based cross-section benchmarks were performed on 151673-151508 pairs, yielding baseline metrics (average Spearman r=0.1804, Layer_4/Layer_1 strongly positive correlated, Layer_3/Layer_6 negatively correlated). The user asked about the method for calculating center points—explained the method for compressing N pairs of M into 1 pair of M, thereby improving efficiency and reliability.
robobrain_pi (GPU Monitoring) ✅ Developing a GPU monitoring tool similar to nvitop for K8s containers
03:05:47.900 | claude_code
Users requested improvements to the interface of the GPU monitoring tool (initial version had line-breaking/truncation issues). The layout of nvitop was referenced, and information was displayed in a GPU-centric process grouping manner (non-GPU processes were not considered). This was achieved by checking/dev/nvidia* devices in /proc//fd/ and CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES environment variables. A double-border table layout similar to nvitop was implemented, with rich library support.
