Progress bars had color differentiation, and the monitoring tools (nvitop, gpustat, Claude) could be automatically filtered. The final gpumon.py supported real-time refresh (default 2 seconds) and one-time mode, grouping processes by corresponding GPUs.
error-recovery-benchmark (v5 Visualization) ❌ Attempting to visualize v5 error scenarios (task abandonment)
00:07:14.853 | claude_code
Users requested visualization of error scenarios. After investigating the code repository, it was found that all v5 tasks already had 480p MP4 videos. Users wanted to render them in 720p and selected “all v5 tasks” along with 3-5 samples. Existing videos were found, and it was suggested to run the 1_v5_execute_injections.py script again, specifying –render –resolution 720. The plan mode was entered, but users repeatedly refused to exit the plan mode, despite stating they wanted to run now. After more than 4 rejections, the user stopped responding. The task was abandoned, possibly due to environmental limitations or changes in priorities.
pi05 (VLA Training) ✅ Resolving version incompatibility issues between torch/torchvision
06:47:41.428 | claude_code
Users encountered a training startup error: “RuntimeError: operator torchvision::nms does not exist.” It was diagnosed that torch 2.7.1 and torchvision 0.21.0 were incompatible (0.21.0 was compiled for torch 2.6.x, incompatible with the operator registration in 2.7.1). Users confirmed they needed help. The pyproject.toml file was read, and a clear “torchvision==0.22.1” dependency was added. uv synchronization was performed (upgrading from 0.21.0 to 0.22.1), and torch 2.7.1+cu126 and torchvision 0.22.1+cu126 were confirmed. The training environment returned to normal.
VLA Training Performance (openpi vs pi05) ✅ Solving 33% of training time gap through dependency alignment
07:07:16.613 | claude_code
Users reported the same training command (pi05_robotwin2, batch_size=32, 30K steps), but ETA differences: openpi took 15 hours, while pi05 took 20 hours (a 33% difference). Parallel exploration agents were launched for in-depth comparison. In the first stage (initial analysis), it was found that train.py, configuration, and model architecture were the same. Differences were identified in lerobot’s git rev, IMAGE_KEYS (2 cameras vs 3 cameras affecting JIT), conda base environment, and dependency versions. In the second stage (root cause and fixes), it was determined that the JAX version (0.5.0 vs 0.5.3) was the main reason for the performance difference. Six shared dependencies in pi05 pyproject.toml were systematically aligned to match openpi (JAX, torch, numpy, orbax-checkpoint, beartype, transformers). [tool.uv] overlapping dependencies were added to resolve conflicts with lerobot’s torch<2.7 constraint. UV locking/synchronization was successful. Expected result: pi05’s current training time is equivalent to openpi’s 15 hours (approximately 25% faster due to JAX 0.5.3 XLA optimization).
summary: Multi-project productivity improvement: refactoring MIHD files and performance evaluation, development of K8s GPU monitoring tools, optimization of VLA training schemes (through improvements to the torch/torchvision library and leveraging JAX technology to optimize dependencies, resulting in a 33% speed increase).
title: Bug Journal 2026-03-11

content:
Daily Report — 2026-03-10 Daily Overview Completed Work: Cross-device multi-project work, including documentation cleanup (MIHD project cleanup: archived 16 files, README section optimized using RM-IDEAL algorithm), spatial transcriptomics debugging (fixed 3 critical barcode alignment errors), robotics learning optimization (VLA training loss analysis revealed 56% gradient dilution, Error Recovery Benchmark v5.0 architecture redesigned), infrastructure development (added GPU monitoring for Kubernetes containers), and environment setup (fixed CALVIN/Openpi/RoboTwin installation issues, adjusted Node.js proxy configuration). Completion Method: Used a systematic approach, combining parallel agent exploration with code audit, leveraging mathematical tensor dimension analysis for loss calculation, ensuring consistency between code and documentation through architectural pattern matching, and resolving environmental issues through hierarchical diagnostics. Utilized git archaeology techniques, barcode-based coordinate alignment, offline two-stage pipeline design, and localization propagation tracking. Impact: The number of MIHD documents was reduced from over 25 files to 4 core documents. Additionally, 1 critical ImportError and 6 dimensional differences were identified. Spatial visualization now correctly aligns coordinates. Robobrain Pi’s training efficiency improved significantly. The Error Recovery Benchmark architecture was redesigned to meet VLA’s time context requirements. GPU monitoring was added for containerized environments. Three independent research codebases were resolved (CALVIN, Openpi, RoboTwin). DCC Completed Work: MIHD spatial transcriptomics project: comprehensive documentation cleanup (archived 16 files, README section optimized using 145 lines of RM-IDEAL algorithm, 1 critical error and 6 differences found during code-document consistency audit), project file cleanup (developed a 4-step plan, created 6 reference guides, total size 48.9KB, but later deemed too detailed), output directory reorganization (DLPFC/HD separation), removal of outdated staig_fusion_e2e functionality (cleaned 14 files), and fixing 3 critical patch query visualization errors (barcode alignment, coordinate mismatch). Completion Method: Used parallel exploration agents for multi-file analysis, bash scripts for batch file operations, read-write tools to update documentation, systematic grep audits to ensure code-document consistency, and barcode-based coordinate alignment to fix issues. Plans were continuously refined based on user feedback. Impact: Established 4 core document structures (README/ Tutorials/Plans/Results). Fixed a serious ImportError that prevented staig_fusion_e2e from running. Created comprehensive reference documents (though later deemed too detailed by users). Improved the navigation of output files and resolved spatial alignment issues in patch queries. User feedback indicated they preferred “minimalistic” documentation over “comprehensive classification guides.” Impact: Redesigned the Error Recovery Benchmark architecture to meet VLA’s time context requirements (60% of existing code could be reused, detector/injector/verifier needed to be rewritten as Error Skills modules). gpumon.py addressed the gap in Kubernetes PID namespace isolation monitoring. Robobrain Pi’s training identified serious gradient dilution and category imbalance issues. ccusage increased from 46 models to 2696 models. CALVIN/Openpi/RoboTwin environments were ready for experimentation. CLAUDE.md documentation was completed, but lacked 4 make targets. A day of multi-project development, covering documentation cleanup, spatial transcriptomics debugging, robotics learning optimization, and troubleshooting issues in over 6 codebases
Tasks Architecture and Strategy - 🔄 Error Recovery Benchmark v5.0 Architecture Redesign – changed from online physical force injection methods (pulses/frictions/posture perturbations) to an offline two-stage process: Stage 0 collects clean trajectories (human demonstrations + 10 times each VLA strategy); Stage 1离线 scans injection opportunities; Stage 2 quota scheduler (100 opportunities per task × error type); Stage 3 context replay (N-1 frames before replay used for VLA context, injection at frame N). Defined 11 error types (E1-E11) × 3 deformation dimensions (D0/D1/D2) = 26 seed types. Analyzed v4 codebase: 60% reusable parts (EnvWrapper/StateExtractor), detector/injector/verifier needed to be completely rewritten as Error Skills modules. Identified 7 high-fidelity error types, 6 medium difficulty error types, 7 error types requiring natural capture. ❌ MIHD Code Documentation Consistency Audit – verified descriptions in README with actual code using 3 parallel exploration agents. Found 1 serious error: STAIGTrainerE2E.py removed relevant code, but Fusion.py and pipeline_config.yaml still referenced staig_fusion_e2e, causing ImportError. Identified 6 dimensional/architecture inconsistencies: MLP encoder not self-supervised (random initialization without training), attention fusion 256 vs 64 dimensions, adln_attention 256 vs 64 dimensions, llava_mlp 512 (256×2 series) vs 64 dimensions, element_wise_sum min(256,D1,D2) vs max(D1,D2), GCN 50 vs 64 dimensions. Users chose “remove all references” strategy, but refused to implement it.
✅ Fixing Coordinate Alignment Issues in MIHD Patch Query Visualization – resolved 3 serious errors in spatial transcriptomics visualization: (1) Cache creation (Dataset/DLPFC/) vs visualization loading path inconsistency; (2) Missing barcode alignment in scoring allocation (location-based matching failed when data was re-sorted); (3) Coordinates from cache instead of data.obsm[‘spatial’]. Used barcode-based alignment method with bc_to_cache_idx dictionary.
✅ Diagnosing Training Loss Calculation Issues in Robobrain Pi – investigated loss oscillation process and found 56% MSE loss due to zero-filled dimensions: 14-dimensional real actions filled to 32 dimensions for model compatibility, but loss calculation based on all 32 dimensions (18/32=56% loss caused by simple zero-to-zero mapping). Suggested masking actual dimensions: action_loss = mean(square(v_t[…, :14] - u_t[…, :14])).
✅ Simplifying MIHD Documentation: 25+ Files → 4 Core Documents – archived 16 files in docs/archived/ folder, removed overview.md file, merged into plans.md, updated CLAUDE.md and MEMORY.md indexes. Final structure: README.md (function introduction), tutorial/THOROUGH_TUTORIAL.md (how-to guide), docs/plans.md (project roadmap), docs/RESULTS.md (experiment results).
✅ Optimizing Hyperparameters of Robobrain Pi Task Completion Classifier – evaluated task_loss_weight (0.1) and task_pos_weight (1.0), found severe category imbalance (only 20 out of 200 frames were positive, ratio 9:1). Suggested keep task_loss_weight between 0.1-0.15 (to avoid affecting 50-step actions’ losses), increase task_pos_weight to 5.0 (to address imbalance).
✅ Fixing npx ccusage Issue with LiteLLM Pricing – npx ccusage@latest fallback option was 46 models in cache (actual count 2696). Root cause: Node.js v24’s built-in fetch function didn’t use HTTPS_PROXY environment variable, downloading 1.4MB JSON file via proxy took ~28 seconds, exceeding timeout. Solution: NODE_OPTIONS="–use-env-proxy" npx ccusage@latest.
✅ Fixing RoboTwin Instruction Sampling Issues in Data Set Conversion – convert_robotwin_democlean_to_lerobot.py always selected first “seen” instruction instead of random sampling. Changed line 68 from instruction_data.get(‘seen’, [’’]) to np.random.choice(seen_instructions) to achieve random per-episode selection, consistent with reference implementation pattern.
✅ Developing GPU Monitoring Tool for Kubernetes Containers – nvidia-smi couldn’t display processes in Kubernetes containers (PID namespace isolation). Developed gpumon.py, combining psutil (container internal process information: CPU/memory/time) and nvidia-smi (GPU hardware status: memory/usage/temperature), using similar to nvitop rich library for UI visualization.
✅ Improving Clustering and Evaluation Sections in README – replaced short sections (L119-150) with 145 lines of “Clustering and Evaluation” section: evaluation flowchart, 3 clustering methods (KMeans/mclust/Leiden) and their selection logic and zero-variance backoff mechanism, spatial majority voting refinement, RM-IDEAL algorithm 5-step decomposition (WWL messaging K=3→Hamming distance→Wasserstein optimal transmission→RM=1-W), cross-section evaluation workflow (Spearman ρ, P@K, SL@K metrics), command reference.
✅ Fixing CALVIN calvin_agent Import Failure – install.sh ran in wrong conda environment; reinstalled calvin_models, installed in active univla environment using pip install -e . –no-deps to avoid PyTorch version conflicts.
✅ Fixing Openpi-moe EGL Device ID Conflicts – CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=6 limited single GPU as device 0, but MUJOCO_EGL_DEVICE_ID=6 exceeded mapping range, causing RuntimeError. Added os.environ.setdefault(‘MUJOCO_EGL_DEVICE_ID’, ‘0’) in script startup.
Implementation and Fixes ✅ Cleaning MIHD Project Files and Creating Reference Guides – divided into 4 steps: (1) archived 4 dead-code files + 11 outdated experiment directories + 34 timestamped summaries; (2) cleaned pipeline_config.yaml file (removed configurations containing only images); (3) updated 5 documents, marked archived content; (4) created 6 feature guides (GENE_ENCODER/FUSION_STRATEGY/EVALUATION/PIPELINE/VISUALIZATION/FEATURE_CATALOG, total 48.9KB). Users immediately requested simplifying documents to 4 core ones.
✅ Removing Outdated staig_fusion_e2e Function from MIHD – cleaned 14 files, removed all references to staig_fusion_e2e (core code, configuration, documentation), corrected 6 dimensional/description errors in READMEs (MLP random projection, GCN 50 dimensions, fusion strategy), added GCNGeneEncoder to init export functions.
❌ Reorganizing MIHD Output Directory – developed detailed plan, separating DLPFC/HD datasets, integrating RM-IDEAL evaluation results into output directory/outputs/{DLPFC,HD,rm_ideal/{cross_section,single_section},figures,logs}. Approved by users, but failed due to repeated ExitPlanMode rejections.
✅ Improving CLAUDE.md Documentation on Error Recovery Benchmark – after multiple attempts, successfully added 4 missing VLA targets to Commands section: analyze-rejected, vla-server-pi0, vla-inject, vla-natural.
🔄 Fixing Chinese Character Rendering Issues in tmux – root cause was Windows client code page configuration (not server/tmux). Since terminal encoding wasn’t UTF-8, Chinese characters appeared as underscores. Solution required changing settings in Windows or executing chcp 65001 before SSH connection.
Problems and Solutions Key Problems 1. Error Recovery Benchmark v4’s physical force injection couldn’t generate a time context compatible with VLA – because initial state errors lacked the behavior history required by VLA modelsSolution: Designed v5.0 architecture with an offline two-stage process: Stage 0 collects clean trajectories (human data + 10×VLA strategy successful runs) → Stage 1离线 scans each frame opportunity → Stage 2 quota scheduler (100 opportunities per task × error type) → Stage 3 context replay (replay N-1 frames to build VLA context window, inject error at frame N, allowing VLA to freely infer from N frames). Shifted from force-based injection to behavior-based Error Skills module (EEF motion/claw control), handling detection/injection/verification independently for each error type. Key Insight: VLA models depend on time context – errors must “naturally occur” from correct trajectories. Offline scanning + quota method efficiently balances datasets while maintaining temporal realism.
2. MIHD spatial transcriptomics patch query visualization showed incorrect coordinate alignment – query patches didn’t match expected spatial positions. Solution: Identified 3 root causes: (1) Data loading inconsistencies between dataset/DLPFC/path and top-level paths; (2) Location-based allocation without barcode mapping (cache order after scanpy operation didn’t match data loading order); (3) Using cache coordinates instead of data.obsm[‘spatial’] for marking. Resolved these issues with consistent pathing, bc_to_cache_idx dictionary for precise barcode alignment, and barcode-based coordinate searching.
Key Insight: Spatial transcriptomics workflow requires explicit barcode alignment at each node (cache→load→render). Implicit location matching fails when data is reloaded from different sources or re-sorted by scanpy. Never assume index preservation between I/O boundaries.
3. Robobrain Pi training loss fluctuated significantly – suspected issue with dimension processing in action prediction. Solution: In-depth analysis revealed that 14-dimensional real actions (right arm 6DOF+claw+left arm 6DOF+claw) were filled to 32 dimensions for model compatibility, but MSE loss was calculated across all 32 dimensions. This meant 56% of losses (18/32) came from zero-filled dimensions, where the model learned trivial noise-to-zero mappings, severely diluting gradient signals. Suggestion: action_loss = mean(square(v_t[…, :14] - u_t[…, :14]))
Key Insight: Zero filling for compatibility often dilutes gradient signals in loss calculations. 56% of gradients were wasted learning trivial mappings—this was a subtle but critical error affecting training efficiency.
4. STAIGTrainerE2E.py file was deleted, but still referenced in Fusion.py and pipeline_config.yaml, causing ImportError when calling staig_fusion_e2e strategy in MIHD pipeline. Solution: Users chose “remove all references” strategy. Removed staig_fusion_e2e from Fusion.py, pipeline_config.yaml, data_preparer.py, pipeline runner, STAIGutils, MEMORY.md, and 8 other files. Also fixed 6 dimensional/description errors found during audit.
Key Insight: Isolated references are serious production-level errors. Systematic grep audits before deleting files are necessary. Automated import testing can detect these issues earlier. Code-document consistency audits using parallel exploration agents successfully identified 1 serious and 6 moderate issues.
5. RoboTwin dataset conversion always used first instruction in JSON, reducing training diversity for language conditional policies. Solution: Replaced instruction_data.get(‘seen’, [’’]) with np.random.choice(seen_instructions) to randomly select instructions per episode, consistent with reference implementation convert_aloha_data_to_lerobot_robotwin.py.
Key Insight: Dataset conversion should be enhanced at the source level. Randomizing instructions per episode increases training diversity without requiring multiple dataset versions, directly affecting model generalization to instruction interpretation. This is a simple change but has significant impact on training.
6. Robobrain Pi task completion binary classifier might learn a trivial “always predict 0” solution, despite task_loss_weight=0.1Solutions: The root cause is a severe category imbalance (only the last 20 frames out of approximately 200 are positive, with a ratio of 9:1 for positives vs negatives). Additionally, task_pos_weight=1.0 means there is no balance between the two categories. The solution requires adjusting two hyperparameters separately: keep task_loss_weight=0.1-0.15 (to prevent auxiliary tasks from overriding the 50-step action prediction), but increase task_pos_weight to 5.0 (to give enough gradient signal to the positive category by weighting each category). Key Insight: For unbalanced auxiliary classification tasks, when the task should not dominate the main goal, weighted scaling by category is more effective than overall loss scaling. Loss_weight and pos_weight have different purposes: loss_weight balances the importance of the task, while pos_weight balances the contribution of each category within the task. Both need to be adjusted independently.
7. The Node.js ccusage tool cannot obtain the latest model price information from LiteLLM (1.4MB JSON), instead it reverts to using 46 cached models, rather than 2696 models Solution: The problem was diagnosed through hierarchical testing: (1) downloading the 1.4MB file via the campus proxy took 28 seconds, (2) Node.js encountered “getaddrinfo EBUSY” error due to hostname resolution failure, (3) It was found that Node.js v24’s built-in retrieval method (undici) does not follow the HTTPS_PROXY environment variable by default, unlike curl/axios. Solution: NODE_OPTIONS="–use-env-proxy" npx ccusage@latest
Key Insight: Node.js v24’s retrieval method is completely different from traditional HTTP clients like curl/axios/requests—it ignores the environment proxy variables by default. This is a compatibility issue that requires explicitly using the –use-env-proxy flag or using a global proxy library. This is particularly important in restricted network environments.
8. The MIHD documentation is overly detailed: 6 detailed reference guides were created (48.9KB), while users prefer a simpler document structure Solution: Users requested a simplification of the document structure after completing the 6 guides. A new plan was developed to re-integrate the guides into the README (including all functions), tutorials (how to use), plans (past/present/future), and results (all experiments). This has not yet been implemented.
Key Insight: AI tends to adopt a “comprehensive classification” approach rather than a “minimal use-oriented” document structure. Research code changes quickly—detailed guides will soon become outdated. A better approach is to have concise integrated documents and well-documented code. The user’s preferred granularity should be confirmed before creating a detailed structure. The user’s first indication (reducing from 25+ to 4) should always be a constraint.
9. The Kubernetes container nvidia-smi shows that all GPU processes are [not found], even though GPUs are being actively used** Solution: The root cause is PID namespace isolation. nvidia-smi returns the host PID (e.g., 3076643) when querying the kernel driver, but the container’s PID namespace has a different numerical ID (e.g., 990465). Solution: The gpumon.py tool was developed, combining psutil (to read the /proc files inside the container for process information: CPU/memory/command line/duration) and nvidia-smi (for GPU hardware status: memory usage/temperature), and visualizing the results with a rich user interface.
Key Insight: Container namespace isolation disrupts the mapping of process IDs across namespaces. The tool must collect information from each namespace and then aggregate it. This is why standard monitoring tools fail in containerized environments—they assume the PID spaces are unified.
10. The Openpi-moe serve_policy.py crashes because MUJOCO_EGL_DEVICE_ID must be between 0 and 0, but actually gets 6 Solution: CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=6 limits the process to use only one GPU as device 0, but the EGL renderer tries to use the original hardware index 6. Add os.environ.setdefault(‘MUJOCO_EGL_DEVICE_ID’, ‘0’) at the start of the script to align with CUDA’s remapping.
11. Despite the successful completion of install.sh, the CALVIN’s calvin_agent module still cannot be found Solution: It was found that the pip installation was performed in a conda environment different from the activated univla environment. An editable installation creates an.egg-info directory, but the availability of packages is limited to the Python environment where the installation was performed. Reinstall in the correct environment using pip install -e . –no-deps to avoid overwriting PyTorch.
General Issues 12. In the tmux session, Chinese characters appear as underscores, but other users on the same server can see the correct characters Solution: The issue was traced throughout the chain: the server’s locale was correct (C.UTF-8), tmux was configured for UTF-8, but the Windows client terminal did not pass UTF-8 encoding via SSH. The root cause was on the client side: Windows PowerShell SSH did not correctly negotiate UTF-8 encoding. Solution: Enable “Beta: Use Unicode UTF-8” in the Windows locale settings, or run chcp 65001 before the SSH connection.
Key Insight: Terminal encoding issues can propagate through multiple layers (client terminal → SSH protocol → tmux → shell). When one user encounters a problem while others do not, the client configuration should be investigated first. By comparing observations (“others can use it”), the problem can be narrowed down from the server to the client.
Human-AI Approaches Strategic Level The pace of decision-making in the redesign of the error recovery architecture Role Method Human Users have a clear view of the types of errors (11 types × D0/D1/D2, offline scanning + quotas, error skills module), but ideas are still developing. They repeatedly mention “new ideas” and reject AI’s questions/confirmations. Exploratory flexibility is needed without making premature commitments. AI AI attempts to converge quickly through AskUserQuestion and ExitPlanMode, trying to decide between “injection + natural” and “only 7 high feasibility types”. It hopes to transition from planning to execution. Difference Analysis: Users require exploratory flexibility until their ideas are mature—they don’t want to make forced decisions before they’ve thought them through. AI, on the other hand, seeks to converge quickly to an executable plan. Users rejected the AskUserQuestion/ExitPlanMode calls 3-4 times, indicating that AI progressed too quickly. This suggests that AI should wait for users to come up with ideas before making commitments.
Visual debugging of MIHD patch queries Role Method Human Users reported that “sometimes the patch queries don’t match the current patch” — this was observed through visual inspection and interactive testing. AI AI systematically traced the data pipeline (cache creation path vs visualization loading path, barcode sorting assumptions, coordinate system) and identified 3 different architectural errors. Difference Analysis: Humans identify symptoms through visual quality assurance; AI performs architectural debugging to find the root causes. Human insight is necessary, but AI’s systematic tracing goes beyond file I/O boundaries, identifying all potential issues. Even in automated processes, visual quality checks are valuable.
Document organization concept in the research code base Role Method Human Structure oriented towards minimal cognitive load and action: 4 files answering “what features are available” (README), “how to use” (tutorials), “what the plan is” (plans), “what the results are” (RESULTS). After AI created 6 guides, users clearly stated “I only need four”. Emphasis is placed on integration rather than separation, action-oriented rather than completeness. AI Comprehensive classification approach: 6 specialized guides created (GENE_ENCODER_GUIDE, FUSION_STRATEGY_GUIDE, EVALUATION_GUIDE, etc.), organizing features by type, and providing detailed API references, parameters, performance tables. Priority is given to logical classification and completeness. Difference Analysis: Humans prioritize minimal cognitive burden and action-oriented documentation (“I need to use X, where can I find it?”). AI prioritizes completeness and logical structure (“All encoder information is in one place”). Humans’ iterative rejections indicate that the research code needs maintainable documentation rather than an outdated comprehensive manual. A rapidly changing code base prefers integration over classification.
Diagnosis of Robobrain Pi loss calculation accuracy Role Method Human Users noticed loss oscillations and suspected dimension mismatch, specifically requesting verification that 14-dimensional actions would result in correct loss calculations. The problem was inferred based on training behavior patterns. AI AI traced the entire data flow (RealInputs filled with 14→32→DeltaActions masked only[:14]→normalization→loss for all 32 dimensions), quantified the exact gradient dilution ratio (from the filled 56%), and identified the root cause of the architecture issue (loss not masked to the actual dimensions). Difference Analysis: Humans identify anomalies through empirical observation; AI performs systematic architectural analysis to quantify the root causes. Complementary to each other: human intuition triggers investigation, while AI provides mathematical precision. Humans correctly identified the dimension structure issue before analysis; AI needed prompts to reconsider the tensor shape and adjusted the task_loss_weight suggestion from 0.5 to 0.1-0.15.- Offline scanning + quota scheduler architecture for balancing error datasets: Stage 0 collects clean trajectories (human data + 10 times more running data than VLA strategy success rate) → Stage 1 performs offline scanning per frame → Stage 2 allocates quotas (100 quotas per task × error category) → Stage 3 performs context replay. This method is more efficient than online generation, ensures balance in the dataset, and allows reuse of expensive clean trajectory data in multiple error injection experiments.
Error classification using deformation dimension classification: D0 (only position), D1 (only rotation), D2 (position + rotation) systematically describes the spatial deformations of all error types. This classification method is more reasonable than stage-based classification (arrival/capture/lift/transport/placement) because it can decompose 6DOF changes into orthogonal components, thus defining the verification logic more clearly. Zero padding can cause compatibility issues in the architecture: Padding to increase the model’s compatibility from 14 dimensions to 32 dimensions may severely dilute the gradient signals (wasting 56%). This happens if it is included in the loss calculation. Always only cover meaningful dimensions to avoid losing information. In multi-dimensional outputs, this is subtle but crucial—the model may converge, but since most of the gradient signals optimize trivial zero-to-zero mappings, the performance may not be good. For unbalanced auxiliary tasks, hyperparameters need to be adjusted independently: loss_weight (importance of balancing tasks) and pos_weight (category contribution within tasks) have different purposes and must be adjusted separately. For severely unbalanced auxiliary classification tasks, keep loss_weight low (0.1-0.15) to avoid overfitting the main goal; at the same time, increase pos_weight (e.g., 5.0) to address category imbalance. Overall scalability and weight distribution per category are independent control factors. Requirements for space transcriptomics barcode alignment: A clear barcode mapping is required at each process node (cache → load → render). Position-based matching fails when data is reloaded from different sources or sorted through scanpy operations. Never assume that the index remains unchanged between file I/O boundaries. Solution: Maintain a bc_to_cache_idx dictionary throughout the process and use barcode lookup for all coordinate assignments. Deep understanding of MIHD architecture: Three-stage process (encoder → fusion → clustering/ evaluation), 5 gene encoders (PCA 50d, MLP 128d random initialization, scGPT 512d, GCN 50d, none), 4 visual encoders (ResNet50, HIPT, UNI, UNI2), 10 fusion strategies (series/mean/element sum + attention variants + STAIG/QFormer). Divided into two stages: extraction in the first stage (cache to embedding cache/) and evaluation in the second stage (load + fusion + clustering + metrics). Transforming the dataset at the source level: Randomly sampling instructions from each set (selecting from available interpretations) can increase training diversity without using multiple dataset versions. A simple change can significantly affect the generalization of language-conditioning strategies. This shows its importance in pattern matching with reference implementations in the same codebase. Redefining GPU index mapping for CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES: Limit the index to the range 0-N for operations within the CUDA context. Dependent libraries (MuJoCo EGL, rendering framework) must use the redefined index instead of the original hardware ID. When using isolated single GPUs, always set the rendering device ID to 0. This is a subtle but critical issue for multi-GPU setups, as different tasks run on isolated device subsets. Applicability of injection vs. natural capture: Physically based injections (pulses/frictions/posture perturbations) can reliably generate 7 types of errors (falling, collision, positioning, partial stagnation). GRASP_FAILURE/sequence/trajectory/regression require temporal behavior of the strategy—only obtained through natural capture (VLA launch + PolicyErrorMonitor detection). A mixed approach is needed to fully cover error types. RM-IDEAL spatial niche matching algorithm: (1) Build a spatial adjacency graph (k-NN, n_neighbors=6), (2) WWL messaging (K=3 iterations summarizing neighbor tags), (3) Calculate Hamming distance cost matrix, (4) Wasserstein optimal transmission, (5) RM = 1 - W ∈ [0,1]. Cross-valuation: bidirectional query, comparing RM-IDEAL scores with embedding cosine similarity, evaluated using Spearman ρ, P@K, SL@K metrics. Kubernetes PID namespace isolation breaks monitoring: nvidia-smi queries kernel drivers return host PID, but containers have independent PID namespaces. Solution: Collect information in their respective namespaces and then aggregate it. For GPU monitoring: Use psutil to get process information (/proc parsing), use nvidia-smi to get hardware status, and visualize it through a rich user interface. General principle: Namespace isolation requires local data collection within the namespace. Documentation philosophy for research codebases: Users prefer a “minimally viable” structure (what/ how/plan/result) rather than a “comprehensive classification” (detailed feature guides). Reason: Research code changes quickly, and detailed guides become outdated. Instead of a thick reference manual, there should be concise integration documents and well-documented code. AI should default to minimal documentation and ask for comprehensive structures before creating them. Audit workflow for code and documentation consistency: (1) Start parallel exploration agents for each module, (2) Compare statements in the README with actual code (class names, dimensions, parameters, training process), (3) Classify differences (serious errors/mild omissions), (4) Let users choose the repair strategy (update documentation or fix code). In the MIHD project, 1 serious ImportError and 6 dimension mismatches were found. AI behavior during exploration phase: When users’ thinking is still developing (repeating “new ideas”), avoid forcing decisions through AskUserQuestion. Provide information, analyze trade-offs, and wait for autonomous decision-making. Continuous rejection of ExitPlanMode/AskUserQuestion calls is an explicit “pause” signal. Users need to explore the space, not decide the implementation path too early. Compatibility issues with Node.js v24 fetch proxy: The built-in fetch function does not follow HTTPS_PROXY/HTTP_PROXY environment variables, unlike curl/axios/requests. It is necessary to explicitly specify NODE_OPTIONS="–use-env-proxy" or use a global proxy library. This is very important for restricted network environments. This represents a change in behavior compared to traditional HTTP clients. Editable pip installation environment range: Access to packages depends on the Python environment where the installation command is executed. Although there may be artifacts (.egg-info), importing from other locations will fail. Always verify that “which python” matches the expected environment. Use –no-deps to install package structures without overwriting existing dependencies (this is important to avoid PyTorch version conflicts). MIHD clustering methods: KMeans (default option, fast), mclust (implementing R GMM through rpy2, suitable for STAIG, with zero-variance backoff mechanism → KMeans), Leiden (used for HD data without real data, automatically estimating clustering). Only applicable to k-NN majority voting for spatial refinement (refine_labels_spatial_majority) when used with STAIG fusion. Selection logic based on fusion strategy and data characteristics. Troubleshooting terminal encoding issues: Problems can propagate through multiple layers (client terminal → SSH protocol → tmux → shell). When one user encounters a problem while others do not on the same server, first investigate issues with the client side. By observing whether other users can use it, you can quickly narrow down the problem to a user-specific configuration issue. For Windows systems, check the code page (chcp 65001) or enable “Beta: Use Unicode UTF-8” in the region settings. MIHD (multimodal integration for high-definition spaceomics) • Comprehensive documentation refactoring and cleanup 2026-03-10 | claude_code Five-stage improvements: (1) Archive 16 files into 4 core documents (README/tutorial/plans/RESULTS). (2) Enhanced README document with 145 lines of clustering and RM-IDEAL evaluation sections (WWL messaging, Wasserstein transmission, cross-sectional metrics). (3) Audited code and documentation consistency through 3 parallel agents—founded 1 serious error (STAIGTrainerE2E.py missing but referenced → ImportError) + 6 dimension discrepancies. (4) Project cleanup: archived 4 deprecated code files + 11 outdated directories + 34 summaries, cleaned pipeline_config.yaml file, created 6 reference guides (48.9KB)—users immediately rejected these guides, finding them too detailed. (5) Started output/reorganization work (DLPFC/HD separation), but hindered by the cyclic rejection of ExitPlanMode. Additionally: removed staig_fusion_e2e function from 14 files, fixed 3 critical patch queries visualization errors (barcode alignment, coordinate system mismatch). Overall: reduced documentation from over 25 to 4, eliminated serious ImportError risks, improved spatial visualization accuracy, but also revealed tensions between AI’s comprehensive classification methods and users’ minimalist approach to documentation. RoboBrain Pi (two-handed VLA training) ✅ Training loss calculation debugging and optimization 2026-03-10 | claude_code Three-part optimization: (1) Identified loss oscillation issue—found that 56% of MSE losses decreased due to zero-padding of action dimensions (14-dimensional actual action padding was 32-dimensional to match the model, but all 32-dimensional values were calculated for losses). Suggested masking of actual dimensions. (2) Hyperparameter evaluation for task completion classifier—identified serious category imbalance (in the last 20 frames of a 200-frame sequence, the ratio of negative examples to positive examples was 9:1). When task_pos_weight=1.0, it could not balance the situation. Suggested setting task_loss_weight to 0.1-0.15 (to avoid overemphasizing 50-step action losses) and task_pos_weight=5.0 (to solve the imbalance problem). (3) Environmental troubleshooting: reinstalled uv package manager, set WandB offline mode, handled multi-user WandB account conflicts through WANDB_API_KEY environment variable, debugged Chinese terminal rendering issues (client UTF-8 encoding problem). Also verified that the task completion implementation was mathematically correct (CLS token + MLP header, allowing partial matching to handle parameter mismatches). Impact: identified two key training efficiency issues (gradient dilution + category imbalance), provided specific hyperparameter suggestions, and resolved availability issues in the development environment. Error recovery benchmark testing 🔍 v5.0 architecture redesign and planning 2026-03-10 | claude_code Main architectural changes in multiple stages: (1) Initial/init attempt to improve CLAUDE.md file (rejected multiple times before successfully adding 4 missing targets). (2) Users proposed a redesigned vision: switching from online physical force injection to an offline two-stage process (collecting clean trajectories → scanning opportunities → quota injection → context replay). Defined 11 error types × 3 deformation dimensions (D0/D1/D2) = 26 seed types. (3) Launched 3 parallel exploration agents to analyze v4 code base—discovered 60% reusable components (EnvWrapper, StateExtractor), while a complete rewrite was necessary (detector/injector/verifier → error skill module). (4) Provided detailed analysis of 24 feasibility options for generation: 7 options were highly feasible through injection, 6 were moderate, 7 required natural capture. Users repeatedly expressed “new ideas,” but refused AI’s AskUserQuestion attempts to force decisions. The plan is still in the exploratory stage—users need flexibility, and the vision is gradually becoming clear. Key insights: VLA’s temporal context dependency requires errors to “come from the correct trajectories,” rather than being a disturbance to the initial state. Offline scanning + quota architecture is more efficient and better balances the dataset. Infrastructure tools ✅ GPU monitoring and Node.js tools 2026-03-10 | claude_code Two infrastructure improvements: (1) Developed gpumon.py file for Kubernetes containers—due to PID namespace isolation, nvidia-smi displayed [not found] related processes (host PID 3076643 ≠ container PID 990465). Solution involved combining psutil (container internal process information: CPU/memory/time running) and nvidia-smi (GPU hardware status: memory/usage/temperature), with a rich TUI visualization interface. Users interrupted the operation during layout optimization. (2) Fixed issue where npx ccusage could not obtain LiteLLM model pricing (rolled back to 46→2696 models). Root cause: Node.js v24’s fetch function does not follow HTTPS_PROXY, and a 1.4MB JSON file took 28 seconds to load through the campus proxy, exceeding the timeout limit. Solution: NODE_OPTIONS="–use-env-proxy". Impact: filled the key monitoring gaps in containerized environments and restored the token pricing tool functionality in restricted networks. Robot Learning Environment (CALVIN, Openpi-moe, RoboTwin) ✅ Multi-project environment setup and debugging 2026-03-10 | claude_code Systematic debugging of three projects: (1) CALVIN: Fixed issue where calvin_agent import failed—install.sh ran in the wrong conda environment, reinstallation used pip install -e . –no-deps in the univla environment. (2) Openpi-moe: Identified issue with serve_policy.py crashing—CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=6 mapped GPU to index 0, but MUJOCO_EGL_DEVICE_ID=6 exceeded the mapping range. Solved by setting EGL device to 0 at script startup. (3) RoboTwin: Modified dataset conversion method, using random sampling instructions (np.random.choice) instead of always using the first instruction, to match the training diversity pattern of the reference implementation. Additionally: resolved terminal UTF-8 encoding issues (Windows client code page problem), queried location of Claude Code API configuration file. Impact: removed obstacles in three key research processes, allowing robot learning experiments to continue. All issues were attributed to subtle environmental/configuration mismatches, requiring careful layer-by-layer diagnosis.
summary: 在多个项目开发日中，我们进行的任务包括：文档整理、空间转录组学的调试、机器人学习的优化，以及多个代码库之间出现的环境问题的排查。这些工作涉及到超过6个不同的代码框架。
title: Bug Journal 2026-03-10

content:
Daily Report — 2026-03-09 Daily Overview Completed Work: Progressed in five main directions on three devices simultaneously: (1) Development of core algorithms for MIHD spatial transcriptomics project (cross-sample structure assessment + cross-modal enhancement architecture), (2) Development of VLA/robot learning system (documentation, data pipeline, theoretical analysis), (3) Transformation of gadget tool architecture (CLI to MCP server), (4) Cross-project integration (CalendarPro automated workflow), (5) Enhancement of research tools (multi-data sources + production-level logs) Completion Methods: Used WWL graph kernel to establish spatial structure assessment benchmarks, designed cross-attention mechanism based on spatial neighborhoods (and resolved CUDA hardware compatibility issues), systematically researched foundation model performance, created MCP protocol wrapper layer for tools, implemented cross-environment calls through conda subprocess, extended research_scout to three academic sources (arXiv/bioRxiv/PubMed), established production-level log system Impact: Established reusable cross-sample spatial assessment toolchain and cross-modal enhancement methods, revealed key findings that scGPT foundation model is inferior to traditional methods, made gadget tools accessible to all AI agents, implemented CalendarPro automated daily research and summary workflow, expanded research coverage from single arXiv source to three academic sources and established complete error tracking mechanism DCC Completed Work: Full process of MIHD spatial transcriptomics project: cross-sample RM-IDEAL experiments and visualization (151673-151508/151676), design and implementation of CrossModalEnhancer (including multiple rounds of CUDA debugging), maintenance of CLAUDE.md, research on scGPT literature Completion Methods: Conducted large-scale experiments in HPC environment (3639 spots), used conda environment management + multiple rounds of CUDA error debugging (final solution was mini-batch workaround and CPU training fallback), performed parallel web searches and WebFetch to obtain benchmark data, generated spatial heatmap visualizations Impact: Produced complete cross-sample evaluation report (7 spatial heatmaps + Chinese technical documentation), delivered functional CrossModalEnhancer module (integrated into Fusion.py), obtained key insights into scGPT performance (zero-shot worse than traditional methods), identified PyTorch CUDA version compatibility issues and established CPU training fallback process TzJsDesktop Completed Work: Full-stack integration and tool enhancement work: created workspace documentation (5 projects), implementation of gadget MCP server (9 tools), CalendarPro-gadget integration bridge (13 tests), enhancement of research_scout (log system + multi-data sources, 500+ lines of new code), maintenance and updates of documentation Completion Methods: Used parallel agent orchestration for code library exploration, systematically implemented each file and verified at each stage, achieved cross-repo integration through subprocess isolation, implemented comprehensive testing (import checks, CLI verification, log file verification), used urllib.request to create zero-dependency API client Impact: Established unified development environment and clear documentation, made gadget tools accessible to all AI agents in the workspace, implemented CalendarPro automated daily research workflow, transformed research_scout from single arXiv source to a three-source system with production-level observability, maintained synchronization of all tool documentation and code tianhe Completed Work: Progress in multiple VLA/robot projects: creation of UniVLA documentation, troubleshooting of CALVIN environment, data conversion from RoboTwin to LeRobot, theoretical analysis of Pi0 flow matching Completion Methods: Created comprehensive CLAUDE.md (build/test commands, architecture overview), diagnosed DNS resolution failures (Kubernetes environment), implemented convert_robotwin_democlean_to_lerobot.py (14-DOF, 50 episodes, 11,459 frames), detailed conditional flow matching (Beta(1.5,1) time sampling + Euler integration) Impact: Established document standards for complex VLA code libraries, supported reuse of demo_clean data for pi0.5 training, clarified details of flow matching implementation for robot action generation Completed core functions of MIHD spatial transcriptomics (cross-sample RM-IDEAL assessment, CrossModalEnhancer cross-modal enhancement, scGPT literature research), advanced multiple VLA/robot projects (UniVLA documentation, RoboTwin data conversion, flow matching analysis), implemented gadget tool MCP serverization and integration with CalendarPro, enhanced research_scout support for multi-data sources
Tasks Architecture and Strategy ✅ Run 151673-151508 cross-sample RM-IDEAL benchmark test — Conducted benchmark test, compared similarity of spatial niches between two DLPFC regions, used PCA+UNI2+STAIG_fusion to embed vectors. Generated detailed correlation metrics (Spearman ρ) for all 7 organizational layers. ✅ Implement CrossModalEnhancer module — Created model/CrossModalEnhancer.py (approx. 350 lines) containing CrossModalAttentionBlock and CrossModalEnhancer classes, supporting 3 modes (gene→image, image→gene, bidirectional). Integrated into Fusion.py scheduler. ✅ Debug CUDA/cuBLAS compatibility issues — Fixed vulnerability in PyTorch 2.9/CUDA 12.9 cuBLAS on RTX 2080 Ti, which caused crashes during large matrix operations. Final solution was to use mini-batch (512 samples) InfoNCE loss to avoid N×N full matrix backpropagation. ✅ Research scGPT clustering benchmark literature — Conducted systematic web search and literature extraction to evaluate scGPT and scGPT-space performance. Found that zero-shot scGPT performance is lower than traditional methods (PCA, scVI), while scGPT-space lacks independent SOTA comparison. ✅ Create CLAUDE.md for UniVLA repository — Provided comprehensive documentation covering build commands (torchrun, accelerate), linting (black/ruff), training phases (LAM, VLA pre-training, fine-tuning), evaluation (LIBERO, CALVIN, R2R, SimplerEnv) and architecture overview (VLM→PrismaticVLM→OpenVLA, visual/LLM backbone, action segmentation, draccus configuration, RLDS process). ✅ Refactor MCP tools to return content — Modified 5 MCP tools (summarize_export/merge, research_report, benchmark_run/report) to return complete content (markdown/JSON), with optional save parameters, bypassing cmd_* wrappers for file I/O control. ✅ Convert gadget tools to MCP server — Created mcp_server.py exposing 9 tools (summarize_export/merge/list_dates, research_search/report/list_projects, benchmark_run/report/system_info), implemented stdout capture and SystemExit handling. ✅ Integrate gadget tools into CalendarPro — Built external tool integration layer (src/tools/), implemented asynchronous subprocess running through conda run, created ResearchScoutTool and DailySummaryTool, created backend service with cron scheduling, wrote 13 unit tests. ✅ Add bioRxiv and PubMed support to research_scout — Implemented search_biorxiv() and search_pubmed(), using urllib.request (no additional dependencies required), added multi-source coordination functionality, implemented unified caching mechanism, expanded CLI features, retained paper_id/arxiv_id handling, maintaining backward compatibility. ✅ Generate RM-IDEAL spatial heatmap visualizations — Modified_viz_rm_ideal.py script to generate 7 PNG files (one for each niche label), showing actual values, RM-IDEAL scores, and similarity of embedded vectors, applicable to 151673↔151508. ✅ Write comprehensive cross-sample patch query documentation (Chinese) — Created docs/CROSS_SAMPLE_PATCH_QUERY_REPORT.md documenting complete methodology (WWL graph kernel, Wasserstein distance, dual-path evaluation), code paths, data flows, result analysis, and reproducibility guidelines. ❌ Use STAIG fusion for MIHD cross-sample patch visualization — Achieved patch query from 151673 to 151676, using UNI2+PCA+STAIG_fusion to embed vectors, but visualization failed due to GPU CUDA compatibility issues (PyTorch 2.9.0+cu129 incompatible with CUDA 13.1 driver). As a solution, CPU training was used as an alternative. ✅ Convert RoboTwin demo_clean to LeRobot format — Created convert_robotwin_democlean_to_lerobot.py script to convert 50 episodes (11,459 frames) from demo_clean (14-DOF HDF5 format) to LeRobot dataset structure, located in RoboTwin/data/place_dual_shoes_lerobot. ✅ Add log system to research_scout — Implemented RotatingFileHandler (5MB, 3 backups), migrated approximately 77 print statements to logger, added empty fields and zero-score warnings, enhanced error handling for API calls and caching operations. ✅ Design CrossModalEnhancer architecture — Designed cross-modal enhancement module using spatial k-NN neighbors as K/V sequence to avoid degradation of single-label attention mechanism. Designed symmetric InfoNCE loss and residual connection. ✅ Analyze implementation of Pi0 flow matching — Explained conditional flow matching in pi0.py: linear interpolation path x_t = t·noise + (1-t)·actions, Beta(1.5,1) time sampling, MSE loss ‖v_t - u_t‖², using Euler method to sample 10 steps from t=1 to 0. ✅ Analyze CALVIN training data flow — Detailed explanation of how finetune_calvin.py uses DiskCalvinDataset to load auto_lang_ann.npy, episode_.npz files, extract rgbstatic/rel_actions/robot_obs, and processes them through LAM encoder and ActionDecoder. Implementation and Fixes - ✅ Create CALVIN dataset extraction script — Created extract_eval_files.py script to extract only necessary evaluation files (validation/.hydra/merged_config.yaml, metadata) from the full dataset, reducing file size from 1.3GB to 600KB. ✅ Create CLAUDE.md at workspace level — Analyzed 5 independent projects (CalendarPro, OCR, gadget, get-shit-done, openclaw), created unified CLAUDE.md document including quick reference commands and architectural highlights. ✅ Update research_scout.py’s CLAUDE.md document — Identified and recorded some previously unrecorded features: author search (–author flag, search_arxiv_author), init –from-overview (creating projects based on LLM), increasing line count from approximately 2500 to approximately 2650 lines, adding author search caching mode. ✅ Update CLAUDE.md document containing cross-modal and RM-IDEAL content — Added CrossModalEnhancer to core modules, included RM-IDEAL benchmark commands in common commands, added cross-sample document references, and enriched descriptions of utilities (rm_ideal.py, niche_utils.py). ✅ Optimize CLAUDE.md module list — Compressed architecture section of each module from approximately 30 lines to approximately 10 lines, focusing on non-obvious module relationships. Retained all key architectural patterns. ❌ Solve CALVIN environment installation issues — Diagnosed DNS resolution failure (Errno -3), preventing pip from accessing pypi.tuna.tsinghua.edu.cn or pypi.org, but the problem persisted in Kubernetes environment. Proxy configuration (http_proxy=localhost:9997) was identified, but the issue was not resolved. Issues and Solutions Key Issues 1. Vulnerability in PyTorch 2.9/CUDA 12.9 cuBLAS on RTX 2080 Ti causes crashes during backpropagation of large matrices (comparative loss requires 3639×3639 logical vectors). Multiple architectural rewrites were attempted before finding the root cause. Solution: Redesigned comparative loss function, using small batch sampling (512 samples per batch) to avoid full N×N matrix calculations. Added gradient clipping and LayerNorm to improve stability. For cross-sample visualization tasks, when GPU incompatibility is confirmed, switch to CPU training as an alternative (PyTorch 2.9.0+cu129 compatible with CUDA 13.1 drivers).
Key Insight: Model architecture alone cannot solve GPU hardware/driver compatibility issues—algorithmic solutions are needed at the loss function level. Incompatibility between PyTorch CUDA version and driver version can lead to “illegal memory access” errors, which pass basic tests but fail in complex training loops. For medium-sized training tasks, using CPU as an alternative is a viable strategy.
2. MCP server stdout corruption: Gadget tools heavily use print(), but MCP uses stdout as a JSON-RPC transmission method Solution: Created capture_stdout() context manager to redirect print() output, wrapped all tool calls in safe_call(), captured stdout/stderr and converted SystemExit to RuntimeError, returning captured logs as the _logs field in JSON responses.
Key Insight: Isolating stdout is crucial when integrating existing CLI tools into protocol-based systems. Context managers provide clean, recoverable redirection without modifying the original code.
3. research_scout hard-codes arXiv_id throughout the codebase, hindering expansion of multi-source data Solution: Introduced paper_id as the standard ID, set source fields, retained arXiv_id as a backward-compatible alias, created _paper_id()/_paper_url() helper functions, updated prompts to use paper_id, checking both fields when matching LLM responses.
Key Insight: When expanding from single-source data to multi-source data, introducing new abstraction layers while keeping old fields as aliases simplifies future data addition.
4. In 151673-151508 cross-sample query, Layer_3 has low correlation (ρ=-0.21/-0.36), while Layer_1/4/5 show high positive correlation (ρ>0.4) Solution: Analysis showed that Layer_3 is the largest DLPFC layer with high internal heterogeneity, making it difficult for embeddings to capture uniform spatial structures. Smaller and more clearly defined layers (Layer_1: ρ=0.62, Layer_4: ρ=0.66) performed best.
Key Insight: The size and internal heterogeneity of organizational layers significantly affect the quality of spatial structure embeddings. Different layers with clear boundaries perform better in cross-sample matching than large heterogeneous regions.
5. When there is only one gene vector and one image vector for each position, single-label cross-attention degrades to linear projection Solution: Used spatial k-NN neighbors to construct K/V sequences, allowing cross-attention to operate within neighborhood contexts. Reused QFormerFusion’s batch neighbor construction infrastructure.
Key Insight: Spatial transcriptomics data provides natural sequential context through neighborhood structures. Utilizing spatial adjacency allows meaningful attention mechanisms to be implemented even without time or batch dimensions.
6. RoboTwin demonstration data (14-DOF, directory-based structure) is incompatible with existing LeRobot conversion script (16-DOF, zip-based structure)Solution: A new convert_robotwin_democlean_to_lerobot.py script was created. It reads data directly from the data/commands directory, changes ACTION_DIM/STATE_DIM to 14, uses the LeRobotDataset.create() method, and specifies the root parameter instead of using the HF_LEROBOT_HOME environment variable for modification. Key Insight: Changes in the robot data format (number of DOF, storage format) require a dedicated conversion script. The directory structure and HDF5 key names are project-specific.
7. Due to JSON parsing failure by the LLM, the motivation/innovation_point field is empty during the research_scout filtering stage. Solution: Empty field detection was added after _screen_papers() and _screen_papers_chunked(). Warnings with detailed counts and DEBUG level information were recorded. The _try_repair_result() function was enhanced to record a preview of the original response when repairs fail.
Key Insight: The silent.get() backtracking mechanism hides data quality issues. Explicit verification and logging at the aggregation point (after batch processing) can provide actionable debugging information without complicating the logic of each project.
8. The initial CrossModalEnhancer design triggered a cuBLAS GEMM error during the projection process when outputting the original modality dimensions (image is 1536d). Solution: The architecture was redesigned to hide the output of the dimension space (128d), avoiding problems with large matrix projections. The projection head was used in the contrast loss while maintaining the compactness of the core output.
Key Insight: Engineering limitations (GPU errors) may force architectural decisions to differ from ideal semantic designs. The actual system must balance theoretical elegance and implementation robustness.
General Issues 9. The FastMCP constructor rejected the ‘version’ parameter in the user’s conda environment. Solution: Use inspect.signature() to check the actual constructor. Remove the version parameter and only pass ’name’ and ‘instructions’, allowing the mcp library to handle version management internally. Key Insight: The SDK API is constantly evolving. When integration issues arise, it’s important to check the actual signature rather than relying on documentation or assumptions. The FastMCP API in the mcp library has changed between versions.
10. The CLAUDE.md document is outdated—missing author search functionality, init –from-overview feature, and incorrect line count statistics. Solution: Systematically searched the code repository using grep to find unrecorded functions (such as search_arxiv_author, create_project_from_overview). Verified the line count using wc -l, and made 6 targeted modifications to the document sections to update the content (instructions, code organization, key implementation details, file layout).
Key Insight: In actively developed tools, document evolution is inevitable. Systematic code searching (using grep to find patterns like –author, –from-overview) is more reliable than manual review.
11. DNS resolution failed in the Kubernetes Pod, preventing pip from accessing PyPI images. Solution: Identified the problem (name server 10.96.0.10, proxy localhost:9997), but could not solve it—possibly a cluster-level network configuration issue.
Key Insight: Kubernetes cluster DNS and egress proxy configurations can disrupt standard pip workflows. Cluster administrators may need to intervene or use cached wheels.
12. When running conda run with the –no-banner flag in the user’s conda version, an “unrecognized parameter” error occurred. Solution: Completely removed the –no-banner flag. Condna run works normally without this flag, and the banner output is sent to stderr (does not damage stdout). After testing, condna run -n calendarpro python -c ‘print(test)’ successfully ran.
Key Insight: If certain optional flags introduce compatibility issues, they should be omitted because they do not significantly affect functionality. Banners in stderr do not affect the stdout-based protocol.
Human-AI Approaches Strategic Level MCP Tool Design Concept Role Method Human Considers summarizing/researching as “Claude code enhancement skills”—should return content, not just write files. Clearly embodies this as an architectural principle. AI Initially implemented the tool as a script that writes files with metadata, without considering usage patterns in context. Difference Analysis: Human users understand the tool-assisted interaction model and expect a specific workflow. AI implements the tool based on existing script patterns, without considering integrated context.
CalendarPro Gadget Integration Methods: MCP vs. Subprocesses vs. Shared Code Role Method Human Although AI recommended using the MCP server as the main method, users chose subprocesses and conda run. Users understand that the gadget is a large CLI script (over 2800 lines), preferring zero-code modifications through subprocess boundaries, maintaining clear separation. AI AI initially recommended using the MCP server as the main method because it was considered the “simplest” method, as it could natively discover the tool. Only after explicit instructions did AI fully adopt the subprocess method and designed a detailed ToolRunner. Difference Analysis: Humans prioritize maintainability and separation of concerns (zero cross-repository dependencies), rather than the “simplicity” of the protocol. AI initially favored the elegance of the protocol. Human choices reflect a pragmatic system thinking about the long-term development of independent codebases.
Deciding Between GPU or CPU Training After CUDA Errors Role Method Human Immediately questioned “Why use CPU training? It can be run on GPU!"—preferred faster hardware and debugged GPU issues. AI After encountering CUDA errors, conservatively switched to CPU training to avoid failure, without thoroughly investigating the root causes of GPU issues. Difference Analysis: Humans strongly prefer optimal performance (GPU) and tend to debug problems; AI tends to avoid errors and chooses safer alternatives (CPU). Human intuition suggests that GPUs should work, which outweighs the initial signs of failure.
CrossModalEnhancer Output Dimension Design Role Method Human Originally planned to specify the output dimension to match the original modality (1536 dimensions), to maintain semantic space and prioritize theoretical rationality. AI After encountering cuBLAS errors in large matrix projections, adjusted to output in a hidden dimension space (128 dimensions) to avoid hardware limitations. Difference Analysis: Humans maintain an ideal architectural design from a theoretical perspective. AI adjusts the architecture based on discovered implementation limitations, prioritizing “workable code” over “elegant design” when there is a conflict.
Data File Redirection Strategy Role Method Human Directly suggested “Just cut and paste it here”—aware that the correct data already existed, using file system movement operations. AI Planned to delete old data and regenerate it from scratch—although more conservative, this approach would waste resources if the correct data was already available. Difference Analysis: Humans quickly recognized the validity of existing data and chose direct file system operations; AI defaults to regeneration to ensure correctness. There is a trade-off between human efficiency and AI caution.
Implementation Level scGPT Benchmark Research Method Role Method Human Raised a broad question: “What do people think of ‘searching for existing papers’ for scGPT and clustering benchmarks?” Expecting a comprehensive analysis. AI Broken down into multiple target keyword searches (zero sample, ARI/NMI metrics, specific comparisons), parallelly executed WebSearch and WebFetch, then synthesized the findings in a structured summary format. Difference Analysis: Humans raised questions at the conceptual level (“What do people think?”). AI transformed these questions into specific search strategies, including specific metrics, datasets, and comparison benchmarks, then reconstructed conceptual answers.
RM-IDEAL Visualization Requirements Role Method Human When noticed that the benchmark test script only generated numerical CSV outputs, immediately asked “Where is your visualization?"—expecting to present results in visual form as a standard deliverable. AI Initially ran the benchmark and reported numerical results (Spearman ρ, Precision@K), but did not generate visualizations, prioritizing quantitative metrics over visualization. Difference Analysis: Humans emphasized the importance of visual verification and ready-to-show results for analysis completion. AI defaulted to the correctness of numerical values, assuming visualization was an optional step.
Limitations of Artificial Intelligence Key Limitations - Due to insufficient experience with CUDA debugging, four architectural reconstructions were performed before identifying the cuBLAS large matrix error (from collecting neighbor data to project construction, from project construction back to collecting neighbor data, outputting results in a hidden dimension, using small batch losses). Failure to identify known GPU driver compatibility issues early on. The originally designed MCP tool was just a script that wrote files with metadata, without realizing that the tool should directly return content for Claude code use. When encountering CUDA errors, it was too conservative to choose CPU training, without thoroughly investigating GPU compatibility issues. Multiple solutions were tried before recognizing that PyTorch’s CUDA build version did not match the driver version. Initially, it was not realized that torch.use_deterministic_algorithms(True) conflicts with PyG’s scattering operations on CUDA, and multiple solutions were tried before identifying the core issue. Before understanding the stdout/stderr isolation requirements of the MCP server, this issue was not anticipated. It was only realized during the design of the wrapper layer, and no attention was paid to the gadget CLI tool during the initial exploration. Overall Limitations When a user proposed a large-scale refactoring task (“Organize all documents, merge duplicate functions, archive unused code”), although 3 parallel Explore agents were launched, the session was interrupted before the agent returned, indicating poor performance in handling multi-agent workflows across sessions. WebFetch encountered a 403 error on multiple important papers (bioRxiv scGPT-spatial preprint, ResearchGate link), and no alternative strategies such as using archive.org snapshots or having users manually download PDFs were attempted, limiting the depth of literature analysis. When running the RM-IDEAL benchmark test, no visual results were generated, and the user had to explicitly ask “Where are the visual results?” No anticipation was made for the need for visual verification in spatial analysis. When supporting bioRxiv/PubMed, no inquiry was made as to whether the user wanted to modify a single 2600-line file or refactor it into modular packages. The implementation was done directly according to the user’s request, without providing other architectural options. The DNS troubleshooting session failed to resolve the issue—no sufficient exploration of cluster network configuration was conducted, and no alternative wheel distribution methods were suggested. Lessons Learned Key Lessons - RM-IDEAL uses WWL (Wasserstein Weisfeiler-Lehman) graph kernel to calculate spatial niche matching: Processes data by iteratively hashing neighbor tags, then performs optimal transfer calculations between subgraphs based on node feature distributions (using Wasserstein distance). This method provides a topological benchmark for evaluating embedding quality.### Practice Insights When integrating new fusion strategies into existing processes, the first step is to register them in the apply_fusion scheduler. The second step is to add the needs_coords tuple in runner.py and evaluation_planner.py. The third step is to create a configuration dictionary entry in runner.py. The fourth step is to define the experiment in pipeline_config.yaml. This four-step integration approach is standard for all trainable fusion methods. When updating documentation, it’s recommended to use grep to find CLI flags and function names, so as to systematically identify missing content, rather than relying on manual code reviews. Dialogue Summary MIHD Spatial Transcriptomics ✅ Work throughout the day, covering cross-sample analysis, cross-modal enhancement, and literature review 23:31:05.195 | claude_code Comprehensive development was carried out in 6 sessions: (1) Using PCA+UNI2+STAIG_fusion to perform cross-sample RM-IDEAL benchmarks on 151673-151508/151676. High correlations were found between different layers (Layer_1/4 ρ>0.6), while the heterogeneous Layer_3 showed negative correlations. Seven spatial heatmaps were generated, and detailed Chinese documentation was written. (2) The CrossModalEnhancer was designed, using spatial k-NN neighbors as attention context to avoid degradation in single-label processing. (3) The CrossModalEnhancer was implemented (approximately 350 lines of code), supporting 3 modes, and integrated into 5 process files. After 4 architectural iterations, the error in cuBLAS for PyTorch 2.9/CUDA 12.9 was resolved, and batch size loss methods were adopted. (4) The CLAUDE.md file was updated, adding details about the CrossModalEnhancer, RM-IDEAL commands, and tools. (5) The architecture section of the CLAUDE.md file was streamlined from approximately 30 lines to about 10 lines. (6) A systematic review of scGPT was conducted, revealing that its performance in zero-shot clustering is inferior to traditional methods like PCA and scVI. GPU compatibility issues were also encountered, requiring CPU fallback for cross-sample visualization tasks.
UniVLA ✅ Created comprehensive CLAUDE.md documentation and analyzed the training process 03:35:00.014 | claude_code The CLI library of UniVLA was explored using the init skill, and a CLAUDE.md document was created. Command lines for building, linting, and testing were recorded (torchrun, accelerate, black/ruff), along with training stages (LAM, VLA pre-training, fine-tuning). Evaluation methods included LIBERO/CALVIN/R2R/SimplerEnv, and architectural designs (VLM→PrismaticVLM→OpenVLA, visual/language model architecture, action tokenization path, draccus configuration, RLDS process). The requirements for the CALVIN dataset and analysis of training data were discussed (using DiskCalvinDataset to load auto_lang_ann.npy, episode_*.npz files).
OpenPI (Pi0) ✅ Explained the stream matching implementation in pi0.py 11:37:19.597 | claude_code The user requested an explanation of the stream matching implementation in policy/pi05/src/openpi/models/pi0.py. The conditional stream matching was analyzed: linear interpolation of the path x_t = t·noise + (1-t)·actions, Beta(1.5,1) time sampling [0.001,1], target vector field u_t = noise - actions, MSE loss training, and Euler integration for sampling from t=1 to 0. The time conditioning method using sine embedding + MLP or adaRMS was explained.
VLA (RoboTwin) ✅ Converted RoboTwin demo_clean to LeRobot format 16:18:03.707 | claude_code The user collected 50 place_dual_shoes data frames using RoboTwin/collect_data.sh in the demo_clean/ directory. A plan was made to convert the files to LeRobot format (different from the existing zip-based script: 14-DOF instead of 16, extracting directories instead of compressing packages). The convert_robotwin_democlean_to_lerobot.py script was implemented, successfully converting 11,459 frames of data. The output directory issue was solved using the LeRobotDataset.create(root=…) parameter, and the dataset was moved from the cache to RoboTwin/data/place_dual_shoes_lerobot/.
CALVIN Environment 🔄 Resolved pip installation failure and created dataset extraction script 10:24:15.816 | claude_code The user encountered DNS resolution failures when running calvin/install.sh in the Kubernetes Pod (Errno -3: temporary name resolution failed). It was diagnosed that the domain server was 10.96.0.10 (cluster DNS), and the proxy address was localhost:9997. pypi.tuna.tsinghua.edu.cn or pypi.org could not be resolved. The cluster network issue was identified but not resolved. An extract_eval_files.py script was created to extract only the validation/.hydra/merged_config.yaml and metadata from the CALVIN dataset, reducing the file size from 1.3GB to 600KB.
Gadget MCP Server ✅ Converted gadget tool to MCP server for AI agent discovery 19:34:52.537 | claude_code The MCP server was designed, providing 9 gadget tools (summarize, research, benchmark). pyproject.toml, .mcp.json, and mcp_server.py files were created, along with stdout capture and SystemExit handling. Issues with FastMCP version parameters were fixed, the mcp package was installed, and all tools were verified. Distribution options (PyPI, Smithery) were discussed. Ultimately, it was decided to return complete markdown/JSON content by default, and optional saving parameters were added for 5 tools.
Workspace Documentation ✅ Created CLAUDE.md documents for multi-project workspaces 18:36:35.440 | claude_code The _init command was triggered. AI started 5 parallel Explore agents to analyze all projects (CalendarPro, OCR, gadget, get-shit-done, openclaw). A work-level CLAUDE.md document was generated, including an overview table, quick reference commands for each project, and architectural highlights (EventBus, singleton factory, multi-agent execution, Gateway RPC, etc.).
CalendarPro ✅ Integrated gadget research/summary tools into CalendarPro 18:59:53.249 | claude_code The user wanted CalendarPro to automatically run the gadget’s research_scout and daily_summary functions according to a schedule. After exploration and user feedback, the sub-process + conda run method was chosen instead of MCP. Six new files (protocol, runtime program, gadgetTools, two services) were modified, and two files were adjusted (configuration, coordinator). 13 unit tests were created, all of which passed. The –no-banner conda flag issue was fixed. The CLAUDE.md document was updated, adding new subsystems.
Gadget Research Scout ✅ Comprehensive improvements: logging, multi-source support, and documentation updates 22:09:15.410 | claude_code Improvements were made in three aspects: (1) The user requested an explanation of the research_scout’s functionality. AI described a 6-step process, and the user reported that the motivation/innovation fields were empty. Logging was added, and support for bioRxiv/PubMed was implemented. (2) A 5-stage plan was implemented: RotatingFileHandler logging was added (approximately 77 print statements migrated), null/failed fields/points were warned, arxiv_id was generalized to paper_id, maintaining backward compatibility, bioRxiv and PubMed search functions were added (using urllib.request, no additional dependencies were added), and multi-source coordination with the –source CLI flag was implemented. The number of files increased from 2155 lines to 2654 lines. (3) The CLAUDE.md document was updated, adding author search functionality (–author flag, search_arxiv_author), and the init –from-overview function (creating projects based on LLM), along with line count statistics.
Token Usage Total expenditure: $16.60 — 24 million tokens
summary: 已经完成了MIHD空间转录组的核心功能开发，包括跨样本RM-IDEAL评估、CrossModalEnhancer跨模态增强技术，以及scGPT文献调研工作。同时，推进了多个VLA/机器人项目的开发，如UniVLA文档处理、RoboTwin数据转换、流程匹配分析等。此外，还实现了gadget工具MCP的服务器化部署，并与CalendarPro进行了集成。最后，增强了research_scout对多数据源的支持能力。
title: Bug Journal 2026-03-09

content:
Weekly Report — 2026-W10 (2026-03-02 ~ 2026-03-08) Intensive multi-project research and development week spanning spatial transcriptomics evaluation infrastructure, robotics training pipelines, personal automation systems, research tooling, and application development. Major breakthroughs: resolved critical BC-RNN evaluation reliability crisis (fixed 4 cascading bugs making metrics trustworthy), completed MIHD cross-sample RM-IDEAL benchmark revealing layer-specific performance patterns and STAIG architectural limitations, achieved Desktop Video production-grade stability (eliminated 100% bookmark restoration failures + implemented webpage wallpaper functionality), optimized Phoenix GPU utilization 5%→80%+ via HDF5 preloading, and built CalendarPro autonomous butler system with context-aware intent classification (360 tests passing). Established large-scale training infrastructure monitoring 10+ concurrent Pi0.5 LoRA jobs while launching FLARE D0×4 training. Developed research scout tool from scratch with bilingual support and two-stage LLM evaluation pipeline. Systematically addressed technical debt through comprehensive code audits, documentation synchronization, and testing.
Weekly Overview Metric Value Date Range 2026-03-02 ~ 2026-03-08 Active Days 7 / 7 Total Conversations 56 Projects 28 Tasks Completed 86 Tasks In Progress 9 Total Tokens 387,284,984 Total Cost $266.78 Claude Code Token 374,571,228 Claude Code Cost $262.79 Codex Token 12,713,756 Codex Cost $3.99 Daily Average Cost $44.46 Project Progress MIHD Spatial Transcriptomics (6 days active) — 🔄 active Accomplishments:
Completed scGPT+UNI2 fusion experiments (5 methods × 11 sections): QFormer achieved best ARI 0.370 (+118% vs gene-only) Implemented complete cross-sample RM-IDEAL benchmark (781-line script, 459-line methodology doc) Completed RM-IDEAL bidirectional evaluation (151673↔151508 + 151673↔151676): Layer_1/5 best (ρ 0.42-0.66), Layer_3/6 negative correlation (ρ -0.21 to -0.36) Discovered STAIG architectural limitation: two-stage fusion ignores gene encoder, uses raw HVG matrix Demonstrated zero-shot scGPT fundamentally underperforms PCA (ARI 0.170 vs 0.261, 54% gap) Generated 39 visualizations (3 summary charts + 33 per-section plots + 3-panel comparison script) Created 676-line overview.md with research positioning and clinical vision narrative Upgraded CLAUDE.md to precise technical specification (added 5 subsections, corrected statistics) Researched QueST paper evaluation framework using WWL graph kernel + Wasserstein distance Clarified data preprocessing pipeline differences between ContraVAE and STHD projects Blockers:
