Documentation often incomplete or outdated. Use high-level encapsulation functions instead of manual low-level APIs. HPC cluster GPU access via Slurm job attachment (srun –jobid) more stable than direct SSH because leverages cluster’s resource management layer designed for long-running workloads. Job IDs persist across sessions enabling reattachment. In HPC clusters, GPU availability should be judged by memory headroom (50%+ free), not idle status. Multiple jobs can coexist on same node if memory allows, maximizing cluster utilization. Systematic documentation audit via /init skill highly effective at catching long-standing gaps accumulated during iterative development, especially for multi-tool projects where each tool documented independently. Explore agents excel in large project organization tasks: recursively traverse directories, read file headers for quick content identification, count code lines, compare documents for redundancy - order of magnitude more efficient than manual exploration. Windows OpenSSH does not support Unix domain sockets (ControlMaster/ControlPath), causing silent failures with cryptic errors (‘Bad file descriptor’, ’nonexistent pipe’). Solution: disable connection multiplexing options. Cross-platform compatibility issues often manifest as low-level errors that look like bugs. Debugging (debugging) ‘Tests passing’ ≠ ‘system operational’ - integration testing with real dependencies (model checkpoints, external APIs) essential before claiming completion. Synthetic unit tests validate code correctness but not end-to-end functionality. AI Usage Notes Effective Patterns:
✓ Parallel agent deployment for independent exploration (3 Explore agents for MIHD project survey, 2 agents for Error Recovery status verification) significantly accelerated information gathering ✓ Two-stage integration approach (implement infrastructure + validate with real dependencies) caught VLA operational readiness gaps that unit tests missed ✓ Systematic registry-based verification (DETECTOR_REGISTRY, CLASSIFIER_REGISTRY, ErrorType enum) accurately corrected 15+ documentation errors ✓ Resume-capable long-running experiments with JSONL append-only logging provided crash resilience for 6+ hour evaluations ✓ Code exploration via inspect.signature() and source code reading successfully navigated incomplete third-party library documentation (openpi, robomimic) Limitations:
✗ Over-optimistic definition of ‘completion’ - equated passing unit tests with operational readiness, missing need for VLA checkpoint validation and API integration testing beyond synthetic mocks ✗ Proposed multiple workarounds (separate eval scripts, pipeline mode switches, manual caching) before accepting need to fix root cause in canonical tooling ✗ Started VLA evaluation with domain-mismatched checkpoints without questioning validity, required human pause to identify baseline appropriateness issue ✗ Persisted in attempting ExitPlanMode after multiple user rejections without recognizing pattern that user prefers longer planning iteration ✗ Struggled to find image-only ablation studies in spatial transcriptomics literature - difficulty distinguishing ‘finding absent due to poor search’ vs ‘finding absent because unpublished’ ✗ Fixed BC-RNN obs mismatch in injection path but missed identical issue in natural capture path, requiring second fix iteration ✗ Used technical jargon (‘recovery training data’, ‘oracle recovery’, ‘LeRobot format’) without verifying user understanding, only explained when explicitly asked ✗ Assumed conservative single-GPU allocation despite 8 A800 GPUs available, needed human correction for efficient cluster utilization ✗ Spent extensive time trying SLURM permission variants despite early evidence no partitions accessible - should have pivoted to alternatives sooner Next Week Outlook Priority focus on unblocking execution: (1) resolve Pi0.5 GPU OOM issue to enable M14 full baseline evaluation (7,800 episodes); (2) restore cluster SLURM access permissions for large-scale experiments; (3) complete Pi0.5 Phoenix fine-tuning (2/9 tasks remaining) to establish domain-matched baseline; (4) analyze Vision Refinement full sweep results across 7 fusion methods to validate two-stage architecture; (5) execute M12 large-scale dataset construction (454→2000+ scenes) leveraging BC-RNN natural capture and MimicGen augmentation; (6) begin M13 taxonomy validation with FMEA mapping and statistical analysis; (7) implement gadget conversation summary feature to enhance daily report narratives. Critical dependencies: cluster access restoration, GPU memory optimization, and VLA fine-tuning completion gate downstream experiments.
Token Usage Statistics Total: $159.97 — 233.7M tokens
Peak Day: 2026-02-16 — $72.41 / 104.8M tokens
Daily Average: $26.66
summary: Intensive week advancing two major research projects: Error Recovery Benchmark transitioned from infrastructure (Phase I) to experimentation (Phase II) with VLA integration, natural error capture (271 scenarios), and baseline evaluation pipeline; MIHD spatial transcriptomics achieved critical debugging breakthroughs (coordinate system fix improving ARI 86%), architectural innovations (Vision Refinement two-stage fusion, embedding cache), and comprehensive documentation. Both projects now operationally ready for large-scale experimentation despite cluster access challenges.
title: Weekly Summary 2026-W08

content:
Daily Report — 2026-02-22 Daily Summary Completed Tasks: Progressed on three main projects: implemented the visual optimization architecture for MIHD and conducted tests; updated the tool documentation and generated a 3-week historical report; transitioned the error recovery benchmark test from Phase 1 infrastructure to Phase 2 experiments through M14 baseline evaluation and Pi0.5 fine-tuning preparations. Implementation Methods: Organized the project using systematic multi-agent exploration methods, making 10 code changes in 150 lines of code to achieve visual optimization; evaluated 7 fusion methods on parallel GPUs; executed M14 sanity checks using Slurm clusters and EGL rendering; designed a 7-step Phase 2 execution plan with dependency topology; set up a MimicGen dataset conversion process for Pi0.5 fine-tuning. Impact: MIHD gained a new experimental dimension (two-phase fusion) and a clearer project structure; the tool achieved complete documentation coverage and comprehensive work history archiving; the error recovery benchmark test resolved all Phase 2 issues, established a Slurm-based GPU access pattern, and initiated the VLA fine-tuning process for 9 Phoenix tasks. DCC Completed Tasks: Implemented visual optimization for MIHD, conducted preliminary tests, and completed comprehensive document organization (removed 3 redundant scripts, reorganized documentation, created unified plans.md file). Implementation Methods: Modified the run_benchmark.py file by adding the –vision_refine parameter and configuring the scan_cluster/stego_refine/byol_spatial options; performed PCA+UNI2+scan_cluster optimization on 151673 slices, initiated fusion experiments with 7 methods; used the Explore agent to generate cleanup plans, then executed file deletion, log migration, and documentation restructuring. Impact: Achieved two-phase fusion experiment (optimization + multimodal fusion), although preliminary results showed that optimization was not always better than direct connection; significantly improved project maintainability, reducing the root directory to only include 2 core files, and establishing a clear three-tier documentation structure. MacBook Completed Tasks: Updated tool documentation, added website tool coverage, and generated batch historical daily reports for the period from 2026-02-01 to 2026-02-21. Implementation Methods: Used the /init skill to systematically review the CLAUDE.md file, manually updated the website tool details in the README.md file; iteratively called the daily report generation tool to process past dates, combining multiple device sessions for each date into a comprehensive report. Impact: Completed the documentation writing for all three tool tools (summarization, testing, website), established a comprehensive 3-week work history archive covering error recovery benchmark tests, MIHD, and cross-project activities. tianhe Completed Tasks: Executed the second-phase baseline evaluation of the error recovery benchmark test (M14 sanity check + full evaluation), developed a comprehensive execution plan with dependency topology, updated the CLAUDE.md file for the Slurm workflow, initiated the Pi0.5 Phoenix fine-tuning process, and converted the MimicGen dataset. Implementation Methods: Created a three-level target topology diagram, executed the M14 sanity check (60 scenarios, 7 minutes), started the full 649 scenario evaluation using Slurm, and rendered with EGL; replaced the SSH-based GPU access method with the squeue+srun workflow; set up a conversion process from MimicGen to LeRobot for 9 Phoenix tasks (4500 demonstrations, 18.4GB data), completed the conversion for 7/9 tasks, and configured the pi05_base_mimicgen_phoenix training settings. Impact: Verified the end-to-end evaluation process and resolved issues in the Phase 2 experiment; established a stable GPU access pattern, avoiding SSH disconnection problems; identified a Pi0.5 memory shortage issue (150MB allocation failed), preventing large-scale baseline runs; prepared the infrastructure for Pi0.5 fine-tuning on 9 Phoenix operation tasks, with training capabilities for 4 GPUs. Completed the two-phase fusion implementation of MIHD visual optimization, conducted preliminary tests, and completed comprehensive file cleanup; updated tool documentation and generated batch historical reports; executed the second-phase M14 baseline evaluation of the error recovery benchmark test, optimized GPU scheduling, and set up the Pi0.5 fine-tuning process.
Tasks Architecture and Strategy - 🔄 Complete M14-cpu evaluation of error recovery benchmark test— Conducted a background evaluation of 649 scenarios × 2 strategies (random + BC-RNN) × 3 seeds on GPU 0, resulting in 3,894 episodes, using EGL rendering. Estimated time based on inspection to be 6.5 hours. ✅ Implementing a two-stage fusion architecture for visual refinement— Added the –vision_refine parameter to the run_benchmark.py file, providing three methods (scan_cluster/stego_refine/byol_spatial), inserting a refinement phase of approximately 50 lines of logic between visual encoding and fusion, modifying 10 functions including get_result_path, check_result_exists, load_existing_result, run_evaluation, etc., to support caching and naming of refinement parameters, and updated the CSV columns in experiment_logger.py. ✅ Evaluation of the M14 sanity check for error recovery benchmark test— Ran 10 scenarios × 2 strategies (random + BC-RNN) × 3 seeds on GPU 0 using Slurm, resulting in 60 episodes, verifying the collector’s recovery capability, error detection (20 classifiers), and JSONL output format. The entire process took less than 7 minutes, with a success rate of zero, meeting expectations. ✅ Phase 2 execution plan for error recovery benchmark test— Developed a 7-step plan covering M12 (large dataset), M13 (classification reasons), M14 (baseline evaluation), and M15 (practicality proof). This includes a list of files, GPU allocation strategy (memory threshold of 50% for 8 A800 GPUs), a 16-day critical path schedule, and Slurm usage patterns. ✅ Preliminary testing of visual refinement (151673 slices)— Performed PCA + UNI2 + scan_cluster refinement + concatenate operations on 151673 samples, refining the visual embedding from (3639,1536) to (3639,256), connecting with PCA gene_emb (50d) to 306d. Results showed: ARI=0.313, NMI=0.433, Silhouette=0.146, which is higher than the results of just scan_cluster (ARI 0.303), but lower than the results of direct concatenation (ARI 0.387). ✅ Verification of the status of the error recovery benchmark test experiment— Used a parallel task proxy to search for outputs, logs, and project summaries. It was found that 649 error scenarios (M1-M11 completed), MimicGen enhancement completed (+193 scenarios), and BC-RNN injection completed (+61 scenarios) had been generated. However, due to a Pi0.5 GPU memory shortage (150MB allocation failed on 2026-02-21 04:17), the M14 baseline evaluation had not yet begun. ✅ Creating a dependency topology for the error recovery benchmark test— Created a comprehensive 3-level dependency visualization (G1-G7 targets, M12-M15 milestones, S-level sub-targets), identified M14 as a bottleneck in the critical path, and added it to the project overview summary.md. 🔄 Setting up the Pi0.5 Phoenix fine-tuning process— Downloaded all 9 MimicGen core datasets (4500 examples, 18.4GB), created the convert_mimicgen_to_lerobot.py script, supporting flat/nested HDF5 layouts, completed the conversion for 7/9 tasks (three_piece_assembly_d0/d1 not completed), configured the pi05_base_mimicgen_phoenix training settings (batch size=64, 100,000 steps, cosine LR), created evaluate_mimicgen.py, following the Phoenix CVPR 2025 protocol, configured with 4-GPU training capabilities, and fixed dataset version compatibility (downgraded from 4.4.1 to 3.6.0). 🔄 Scanning for fully fused methods for visual refinement— Launched 7 multimodal fusion methods (mean, element sum, attention mechanism, basic contrast learning, adln_attention, llava_mlp, qformer) on 151673 samples and performed scan_cluster refinement. The concatenate operation was completed (ARI 0.313), while other methods ran in the background. The QFormer ran slowly due to high memory usage (19.4GB), but GPU utilization was low (4%, 55W). ✅ Reorganizing MIHD documentation— Created a three-tier documentation structure (research version, experimental version, archived version), moved GEMINI.md/AGENTS.md to the archived version, moved BENCHMARK_GUIDE.md to the documentation version, moved the research report to the research version, moved the experimental summary to the experimental version, and merged ENHANCEMENT_PLAN.md/ENHANCEMENT_PLAN_CN.md into docs/plans.md, including project overview, progress in Phase 1 and Phase 2, detailed information for the third and fourth batches of plans, architecture roadmap, and key metrics (current DLPFC ARI maximum is 0.528). ❌ Setting up VLA baseline evaluation (Pi0 + Pi0.5)— Attempted to start the Pi0 server (port 5555) and Pi0.5 server (port 5557) on GPU 1-2. Pi0 was blocked due to port conflicts, while Pi0.5 succeeded. It was clarified that there was ambiguity in checkpoint selection: pi0_libero (LIBERO fine-tuning version) and pi05_base (pre-trained version) were not suitable for this domain in the PickPlace task. Implementation and Fixes ✅ Generating gadget batch historical daily reports— Generated structured daily reports for the past 21 days (2026-02-01 to 2026-02-21), covering error recovery benchmark tests, MIHD, and other projects. Multiple exports, analysis, and merges were performed on each date, integrating multi-device dialogue logs into a unified report. ✅ Updating CLAUDE.md for error recovery benchmark tests to fit Slurm workflows— Replaced the unstable SSH-based GPU access method with Slurm workflows: source set-XY-I.sh → squeue → srun –jobid=. Updated the “server limitations” section in CLAUDE.md and added a three-step mode in memory/MEMORY.md, recording warnings about unstable SSH. ✅ Cleaning up MIHD project files— Deleted 3 redundant scripts (run_benchmark_legacy_backup.py 77KB, run_benchmark_refactored.py 15KB, run_benchmark_from_config.py 19KB), cleaned up all pycache directories, created output/log files, and moved 5 pipeline_.log files to appropriate locations (about 5MB), deleting 16 summary_20260219* files. ✅ Improving gadget CLAUDE.md and README.md documents— Used the /init skill to find missing website/tools documents, added Hugo blog descriptions, updated command references, added detailed information about incremental media compression processes, referenced benchmark flags in test/CLAUDE.md, and updated the tool table and website sections in README.md. ✅ Updating MIHD CLAUDE.md and Memory references— Removed the non-existent docs/PCA_WORKFLOW.md reference, updated file paths to docs/BENCHMARK_GUIDE.md and docs/archived/GEMINI.md, added examples of visual refinement (–vision_refine scan_cluster), and synchronized the paths of Memory files. Issues and Solutions Key Issues 1. Pi0.5 VLA server encountered a GPU memory shortage issue when loading models (150MB allocation failed on 2026-02-21 04:17), hindering the M14 baseline evaluation for 7,800 episodes (649 scenarios × 4 strategies × 3 seeds). Solution: The issue was identified, but could not be resolved during the meeting—it is necessary to switch to GPUs with more memory or reduce the batch size. This issue has been recorded for further processing; at the same time, fine-tuning of pi05_base on the MimicGen task has begun to create a baseline that matches the domain.
2. There was ambiguity in the VLA checkpoint selection: both pi0_libero (LIBERO fine-tuning version) and pi05_base (general pre-trained version) were not suitable for this domain in the PickPlace task, which may weaken the validity statement of the dataset. Solution: Users paused the execution to confirm the relevance of the checkpoints. It is necessary to decide whether to use the existing checkpoints directly for the general baseline, or to first fine-tune the PickPlace/Phoenix data. The fine-tuning process has been started as a solution. Key insight: The baseline evaluation requires careful selection of models: due to distribution bias rather than dataset quality issues, checkpoints that do not match the domain may perform poorly, thereby invalidating statements about the model’s usefulness. A baseline optimized for the task needs to be used to evaluate the model’s performance.
3. The ARI value obtained from the Vision Refinement scan clustering method was 0.313, which is lower than the 0.387 value of the direct concatenation method. This indicates that while 256-dimensional compression improved the performance of single-modal clustering (0.303), it lost valuable information for multimodal fusion. Solution: Continue testing the remaining 6 fusion methods (attention mechanism, adln_attention, llava_mlp, qformer, etc.) to determine if certain strategies can better utilize the improved 256-dimensional embeddings; consider adjusting the hidden dimension or trying other optimization methods (such as stego_refine, byol_spatial).
Key insight: Self-supervised clustering methods optimize the independence of clustering, but this may not align with the goal of multimodal fusion. Reducing the dimensionality can filter out features that are valuable for gene-visual complementation, even if these features are not important for image-based clustering.
4. The GPU allocation strategy was unclear: initially assumed that only GPU 0 was available, but in reality, there were 8 A800 GPUs on the Slurm nodes. Solution: Manually verified the status of all GPUs, used the nvidia-smi tool to check memory usage, and selected GPUs with memory usage exceeding 50%. The updated plan involves dynamically checking memory usage and accordingly allocating CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES to ensure parallel task execution.
Key insight: In a shared HPC environment, the availability of resources does not necessarily mean they are idle. Memory-based criteria (such as a 50% threshold) should be used instead of simple idle detection. If memory allows, multiple tasks can share a node, maximizing cluster utilization.
5. The SSH connection to the GPU node was unstable and often disconnected, causing long-running experiments to be interrupted. Solution: Adopted the workflow recorded in CLAUDE.md, using squeue to find running tasks, and then using srun –jobid= to connect to the allocated node. This avoids direct SSH use and leverages Slurm’s task management capabilities.
Key insight: Using srun –jobid for task connections is more stable than using SSH directly, as Slurm is designed for HPC workloads and can manage cluster resources effectively. Task IDs can be reused across different sessions, allowing for reconnections.
Common Issues 6. The MIHD project has accumulated redundant scripts (a total of 3 backup versions, 111KB in total), scattered log files (5 files, approximately 5MB), 16 temporary summary files, and documents distributed across the root and docs/ directories. Solution: Performed systematic cleanup using a parallel task proxy: removed redundant scripts whose functionality was replaced by run_benchmark.py/run_pipeline.py, created centralized storage for output/log files, deleted summary files (key data stored in experiment_comparison.csv), organized documents into a three-tier structure (research/experiments/archived), merged ENHANCEMENT_PLAN files into unified plans.md, and updated all references.
Key insight: Over time, redundant documents naturally accumulate during project iterations. It is necessary to conduct regular “clean-up days”: remove outdated backup files, relocate temporary output files, consolidate duplicate documents, and update references. The root directory should only contain core documentation files.
7. When starting the Pi0 VLA server, port 5555 was already occupied, leading to failure to start. Solution: This issue was discovered through srun output. You can choose to terminate the existing process, use a different port, or check if the existing server can still be used. The specific solution depends on the user’s decision.
8. Both the gadget project’s CLAUDE.md and README.md lack documentation about websites/tools, resulting in an incomplete project overview. Solution: Used the /init command to systematically audit the system for missing documentation. Added Hugo blog descriptions, updated command references, included detailed information about incremental media compression processes, referenced benchmark flags in test/CLAUDE.md, and updated the tool table and website sections in README.md.
9. The QFormer had high memory usage (19.4GB) during training, but low GPU utilization (4%, 55W), resulting in slow training progress.Solution: It was found that this is a characteristic of the QFormer architecture: the model and data are stored in VRAM, but the cross-attention computation is relatively simple. Most of the time, it is spent on CPU-intensive k-NN graph construction and loss calculation. This is expected behavior, and patience is needed. Key Insight: High memory usage does not necessarily mean high GPU load. The learnable query mechanism limits the training process to CPU preprocessing, while the GPU cores remain idle most of the time. VRAM is mainly used for storage rather than computation.
Version 4.4.1 of the dataset library is incompatible with the conversion process, resulting in import failures. Solution: Downgrade the version to 3.6.0 to restore compatibility with the MimicGen-to-LeRobot conversion script.
Methods for Humans and Artificial Intelligence Strategic Level Two-stage fusion architecture design for visual refinement Role Method Human The user explicitly proposed “first using methods like scan clustering for self-supervised processing to optimize visual embeddings, then using a multi-modal fusion strategy to combine visual embeddings with gene embeddings” – this is a novel experimental design, something that artificial intelligence has never proposed before Artificial Intelligence Artificial intelligence previously used scan clustering as an independent fusion strategy (parallel to gene embeddings), without considering it as a preprocessing step for visual embeddings Difference Analysis: The human’s two-stage thinking reflects a deep understanding of the nature of the method: self-supervised processing based solely on images is feature optimization, not fusion, and should be used at different stages of the process. Artificial intelligence’s understanding is more mechanical (only listing fusion options), lacking an understanding of method combination.
Selection of VLA checkpoints for baseline evaluation Role Method Human Humans pause the task and ask about the relevance of checkpoints: “What checkpoints are used for Pi0 and Pi0.5?” Before proceeding, it is necessary to understand the domain matching situation (LIBERO adjustment vs PickPlace task) Artificial Intelligence Artificial intelligence uses the existing symbolic link checkpoints to start the VLA server, without questioning the domain matching issue, prioritizing “fast running” over verifying its applicability Difference Analysis: Humans critically think about the effectiveness of the experiment (does the baseline match the target domain?), avoiding wasting computational resources on ineffective baselines. Artificial intelligence follows the conventions of the file system, without questioning.
GPU resource allocation strategy | Role | Method | |——|——| | Human | Humans clearly specified how to use all 8 GPUs, allowing the use of more than 50% of idle memory, not just idle GPUs. Resources are allocated reasonably based on actual memory space, rather than allocating them individually to a single GPU. | | Artificial Intelligence | Artificial intelligence initially assumed that a single GPU would be used for allocation (only GPU 0), and detected idle status in a binary manner, similar to the mode of a single-user workstation. |
Difference Analysis: Humans have domain knowledge of sharing high-performance computing clusters, and can accept over-allocated resources if memory allows, thereby maximizing throughput on shared infrastructure. Artificial intelligence defaults to an exclusive resource model, lacking supportive knowledge based on practical experience.
Plan structure and dependency ordering Role Method Human Humans first require a topology diagram (“Which targets need to be completed first”), then the execution plan. From bottom to top: understanding dependencies → designing a timeline. Artificial Intelligence Artificial intelligence tends to start exploring and implementing immediately. Without clear dependency analysis, it may start coding directly. Difference Analysis: Humans insist on the strategic planning stage before tactical execution, to prevent rework due to incorrect order. Artificial intelligence tends to act immediately, without sufficient preliminary design.
Project organization planning workflow Role Method Human Users provide goal-oriented instructions (delete unnecessary files, merge related files, organize output results, update plans), trust the exploration ability of artificial intelligence, and then skip plan approval, executing directly. They prefer “immediate execution” over formal review. Artificial Intelligence Artificial intelligence enters the planning mode, sending 3 exploration agents to conduct a comprehensive bottom-up exploration, generating a detailed 9-step plan, and expecting approval. The user interrupted ExitPlanMode twice, requesting execution in the next round. Difference Analysis: Users trust the judgment of artificial intelligence, believing that a complete plan review is unnecessary for familiar maintenance tasks. Artificial intelligence is too cautious, not adjusting the interaction pace according to the nature of the task and the user’s familiarity.
Parallel experiments initiation timing Role Method Human Users first see the results of visual refinement (ARI 0.313), and immediately request to run “all fusion methods”, showing a fast iterative experimental decision-making style. Artificial Intelligence Artificial intelligence directly executes the instructions, putting 7 fusion methods in parallel in the background; correctly identified the problem of QFormer having high memory but low utilization, believing this to be an architectural feature rather than a failure. Difference Analysis: The user’s experimental strategy is breadth-first (quickly covering the parameter space), while artificial intelligence tends to analyze each result in depth before moving to the next step. But artificial intelligence supports the user’s strategy well at the execution level (background parallelism, non-blocking waiting).
Limitations of Artificial Intelligence Key Limitations When starting VLA evaluation, mismatched domain checkpoints were used (LIBERO optimized model used in PickPlace task), without questioning its effectiveness. Humans had to stop and ask “Which checkpoint are you using?” Artificial intelligence did not consider the combinability when implementing visual refinement, simply passively implementing the user’s two-stage idea, without proposing other combinations (e.g., stego_refine + attention, byol_spatial + qformer), lacking creative exploration of method combinations. Although aware that SSH access via Slurm is more reliable, artificial intelligence still did not actively recommend this method. Humans needed to identify stability issues from experience and explicitly request changes to the workflow. Despite having 8 A800 GPUs available, artificial intelligence still assumed the use of a single GPU for allocation, requiring humans to correct it to effectively utilize cluster resources. This shows that artificial intelligence lacks intuition for optimizing high-performance computing resources. In project organization tasks, artificial intelligence conducted a comprehensive exploration, but the user interrupted ExitPlanMode twice, indicating that the presentation of the plan did not meet the user’s expected pace (users preferred “direct execution + immediate adjustment” over “complete plan + approved execution”). General Limitations Before starting the VLA server, no check was made on port availability, resulting in failed startup due to port 5555 conflict. It should have run lsof -i :5555 or similar checks first. When organizing MIHD files, the location of ENHANCEMENT_PLAN.md was misjudged initially (thought it was in the doc/ directory), and it was necessary to verify with the ls command. In large projects, file paths should be verified more carefully. Multiple attempts were made to interrupt ExitPlanMode, but the user kept interrupting, indicating that artificial intelligence did not anticipate the need for iterative plan adjustments before finalizing. When generating historical daily reports, there was no question as to why multiple iterations or sessions were needed on the same day, nor was there any suggestion to integrate report generation into a more efficient single process. The initial srun command timed out because the project directory was not switched first, and it took 3 attempts to debug it. This shows that the simulation of bash context was not sufficient. When running 7 parallel fusion methods, it was found that qformer ran for too long and the output file was empty. Artificial intelligence used a repeated polling method (sleeping for 30 seconds → checking output), but the output buffering in conda run kept the file empty. It should have been identified earlier that output redirection failed, and instead, checking the existence of .npz files should have been done instead of relying on logs. Lessons Learned Key Lessons The two-stage fusion (self-supervised refinement → multi-modal integration) created a new experimental dimension, connecting methods based solely on images and multi-modal methods. However, preliminary tests showed that among all fusion strategies, finely processed embeddings were not always better than the original embeddings. It is crucial to maintain consistency in the optimization goals. The choice of baseline model has a significant impact on the effectiveness of the experiment. Using checkpoints from different domains (such as LIBERO adjusted for the PickPlace task) will weaken its effectiveness. The baseline model must be clearly validated or fine-tuned to ensure fair evaluation. The implementation of the visual refinement process demonstrated the feasibility of adding intermediate processing steps in run_benchmark.py: adding approximately 50 lines of code at the appropriate position (after visual encoding and before fusion) could create a completely new experimental paradigm, while being fully compatible with existing cache/naming/logging systems. Accessing GPUs in the HPC cluster through Slurm job attachments (srun –jobid) is more stable than direct SSH, as it allows utilization of the cluster’s resource management system and is suitable for long-running scientific workloads. The job ID remains unchanged between sessions, allowing reattachment even after disconnection. The research project structure for the first or second phase (infrastructure building → verification experiments) benefits from clear milestone tracking. The completion of error recovery benchmark tests M1-M11 (649 scenarios) provided a solid foundation for the M14 baseline evaluation, although currently it is hindered by insufficient memory in Pi0.5. In a HPC cluster, the availability of GPUs should be determined based on memory space (at least 50% or more idle), not just based on the idle state. If memory allows, multiple jobs can coexist on the same node, enabling parallel task execution and maximizing cluster utilization. The dependency topology should be presented before the execution plan. Clearly visualizing M14 as a key path blocker helps in resource allocation and timeline estimation, leading to more strategic scheduling. The collector resume function makes fault-tolerant long-term evaluation possible. The rationality check of M14 verifies that interrupted evaluations can restart from the last checkpoint, which is important for full runs lasting up to 6 hours. The “cleanup day” mode for project maintenance is very effective: using 3 parallel exploration agents for a comprehensive project survey, followed by systematic cleanup (deleting redundant files, moving logs, reorganizing documents, updating references/memory), thus simplifying the root directory and making the document hierarchy clear and traceable. The systematic documentation audit carried out through the /init skill is very effective in identifying long-term gaps accumulated during iterative development, especially in multiple tool projects where each tool is recorded independently. The error detection process (20 classifiers, PolicyErrorMonitor) runs reliably in large-scale situations. During the rationality check, 37-74 errors were detected per set, proving that the classification method is ready for production use. The MimicGen-to-LeRobot conversion process requires careful handling of flat and nested HDF5 layouts. The 84×84 images used in the Phoenix paper were resized to 224×224 for training, and this preprocessing must be retained during the conversion process. JAX can automatically detect all visible GPUs for multi-GPU training, when batch_size can be divided evenly by the number of GPUs. Setting CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=0,1,3,4 and batch_size=64 allows for efficient 4-GPU parallelism without explicit configuration. Exploration agents perform well in large project organization tasks: they can recursively traverse directories, read file headers to quickly identify content, calculate line counts, and compare redundant parts in documents—their efficiency is several orders of magnitude higher than manual exploration. In the cleanup work of large projects, if the user has clear goals and is willing to take responsibility for the quality of the plan, then user-controlled planning (avoiding the approval of ExitPlanMode) may be more efficient. The role of AI becomes one of execution, rather than design. Practical Experience When a project accumulates a large amount of work history, generating historical reports in batches is valuable for creating searchable archives. Although these reports are mainly meta-work rather than main development, the gadget project now has a comprehensive report covering more than 3 weeks of project activities. QFormer and other fusion methods based on learnable queries exhibit the characteristics of “high VRAM + low GPU utilization” during training: parameters and intermediate results consume memory, but the actual computation (cross-attention mechanism) is relatively simple, with most of the time spent on CPU-intensive k-NN graph construction and loss calculation. This is an architectural feature, not a performance issue. Dialogue Summary MIHD ✅ Implementation, testing, and project file organization of the two-stage fusion for visual refinement 2026-02-22 | claude_code The user proposed an innovative visual refinement architecture: before multi-modal fusion, using scan_cluster/stego_refine/byol_spatial to refine visual embeddings. AI modified run_benchmark.py, adding the –vision_refine parameter (approximately 150 lines of code, distributed across 10 locations), creating a refinement stage between visual encoding and fusion. Tests were conducted on 151673 slices, using PCA+UNI2+scan_cluster refinement+concat, resulting in ARI=0.313 (higher than 0.303 based on images alone, but lower than 0.387 for direct concat). Seven methods of fusion scanning were started in the background, and concat was completed, while other steps are still underway. The user then requested a comprehensive project cleanup. AI dispatched 3 exploration agents, generating a detailed cleanup plan, including 3 redundant scripts, scattered logs, 16 summary files, and document integration. The user bypassed plan approval, requesting execution directly. Redundant scripts were successfully deleted, the pycache directory was cleaned up, logs were moved to outputs/logs/, documents were organized into three layers (research/experiment/archival), and the ENHANCEMENT_PLAN file was merged into the unified plans.md file, updating the references and memory information in CLAUDE.md. The root directory was simplified to 2 core files, making the document hierarchy clear and traceable.**
gadget**✅ Document improvement and batch generation of historical daily reports** 2026-02-22 | claude_code The user called the /init command, triggering the system’s code library analysis. AI found that both CLAUDE.md and README.md lacked relevant documentation. The user read the update.sh and config.yml files to understand the functions of Hugo blog, and then added comprehensive documentation on incremental media compression processes, deployment commands, and cross-references. Subsequently, the user requested batch generation of historical daily reports for multiple dates. AI gradually processed data for 21 dates from February 1st to February 21st, 2026, performing the workflow of export→analysis→merge, integrating multi-device dialogue logs (such as DCC, MacBook, Tandem) into a structured report. This work included error recovery benchmark tests (M13 error classification, M12 dataset construction, VLA investigation, second-phase plan, baseline evaluation infrastructure), MIHD (only image clustering implementation, STAIG debugging, coordinate correction, document sprint, PCA+UNI+STAIG benchmark test), and other projects. A 3-week work history archive with over 17 dialogue summaries was generated, documenting tasks, problems, analysis results of human and AI, and learning insights. Now the documentation covers all three small tools (summary, test, website) and provides complete historical background information.**
Error Recovery Benchmark Test 🔄 Execution of the second-phase baseline evaluation, infrastructure optimization, and setup of the Pi0.5 fine-tuning process 2026-02-22 | claude_code The user asked about the completion status of Pi0.5 and MimicGen. AI launched parallel task agents and found that the first phase was completed (649 error scenarios, M1-M11, plus 193 MimicGen enhancements, 61 BC-RNN injections), but due to insufficient GPU memory in Pi0.5 (failed allocation on February 21st, 2026, with 150MB), the M14 baseline had not yet started. The user reported unstable SSH issues and requested updating CLAUDE.md to Slurm workflow. AI recorded using squeue → srun –jobid mode instead of direct SSH. The user then requested the execution of the second phase. AI created a three-level goal dependency topology (G1-G7, M12-M15, sub-goals), identified M14 as a key obstacle, and inserted it into the project summary. Three exploration agents were launched to verify the preparation of the infrastructure, and then a 7-step execution plan was designed, including 16 days of critical paths, GPU allocation strategy (memory threshold of 50% for 8 A800 GPUs), and a list of file checks. The rationality check of M14 was successfully executed (10 scenarios × 2 policies × 3 seeds = 60 episodes, 7 minutes, as expected without SR), verifying collector recovery, error detection (20 classifiers), and JSONL output. A complete M14-cpu evaluation was started in the background (649 scenarios, 3,894 episodes, estimated to take about 6.5 hours), attempting VLA server settings but encountering port 5555 conflicts. Humans paused the question about checkpoint selection: pi0_libero (adjusted by LIBERO) versus pi05_base (pre-trained), neither of which was suitable for PickPlace tasks. A task-aligned baseline was determined. The fine-tuning process of Pi0.5 was initiated: downloading all 9 MimicGen Phoenix datasets (4500 demonstrations, 18.4GB), creating conversion scripts that support flat/nested HDF5 layouts, completing 7/9 conversions (three pieces_assembly_d0/d1 remaining), configuring the pi05_base_mimicgen_phoenix training settings (batch size=64, 100,000 steps, 4 GPUs), creating the Phoenix CVPR 2025 evaluation script, fixing dataset version compatibility (downgrading from 4.4.1 to 3.6.0), updating the project summary to v4.11, adding M16 milestones. An reproducible evaluation workflow was established, with stable Slurm-based GPU access, and a complete VLA fine-tuning infrastructure, ready for 4-GPU training after conversion.**

title: Bug Journal 2026-02-22

content:
Daily Report — 2026-02-21 Daily Overview Completed Work: Developed automated evaluation scripts for the pi0.5 VLA and BC-RNN baseline models, suitable for error recovery scenarios. Implementation Method: Created multi-step SLURM batch scripts and a tmux-based monitoring system with parallel execution strategies. Cluster access permissions were also adjusted. Impact: The complete evaluation infrastructure is now ready to be used once administrators restore GPU access permissions. Evaluation scripts were created for the pi0.5 and mimicgen baseline models, but they were not completed due to issues with cluster GPU access permissions.
Tasks Implementation and Fixes ❌ Evaluation Runs: Unable to submit SLURM jobs or SSH to GPU nodes (such as an49, an16, etc.) – The user group ‘sysu_gbli2’ has no permissions in any partition. pam_slurm_adopt prevents SSH connections when there are no active jobs. ✅ Evaluation Script Creation: Developed run_full_eval.sh (three-step SLURM process), launch_eval_tmux.sh (tmux-based parallel execution), and monitor_eval.sh (status checking tool) for running pi0.5 and BC-RNN evaluations. Issues and Solutions Key Issues 1. Unable to SSH to GPU nodes (such as an49, an16, etc.) due to pam_slurm_adopt restrictions. Active jobs are required to access these nodes. Although the user’s history shows attempts to access an49 directly, it does not exist in any SLURM partition. Solution: Confirmed that an49 has completely disappeared from the information output. Administrators should intervene, ensuring that the script can still execute properly.
Key Insight: Continuous ‘ssh an49’ commands in the bash history confirm previous attempts to access the node. The node needs to be removed from cluster management, which requires administrators to restore permissions.
2. All SLURM job submissions failed, indicating “The user group is not allowed to use this partition.” This issue occurred in xy-a800, ai, lava, and all other partitions. Solution: Created another tmux-based launcher (launch_eval_tmux.sh) that can be used after restoring direct SSH or srun access. The cause of the problem was recorded for administrators to address.
Key Insight: When cluster access is fundamentally unavailable, self-contained workaround plans should be prepared, along with clear documentation, so work can be resumed immediately after access is restored.
General Issues 3. Using –gres=gpu:1 and –gres=gpu:2 resulted in SLURM sbatch failures, indicating “Invalid general resource (gres) specification.” Solution: Found that the cluster did not have GRES configuration (all nodes had empty GRES fields). Disabled the –gres parameter and requested GPUs directly through node allocation.
Key Insight: Not all SLURM clusters have GRES configured. Some clusters expose GPUs directly on compute nodes without resource scheduling.
Human and Artificial Intelligence Approaches Strategic Level Cluster GPU Access Methods Role Method Human Humans know that direct SSH to an49 is possible, indicating that SLURM was never used for access before. Artificial Intelligence AI initially treated SLURM job submission as a standard high-performance computing access method. It systematically tested sbatch/srun in all partitions.** Difference Analysis: Human institutional knowledge indicates that the cluster previously allowed direct SSH access (bypassing SLURM). However, AI found that this method is now prohibited. However, continuous ‘ssh an49’ attempts in the bash history confirm previous access attempts.
Limitations of Artificial Intelligence General Limitations Spent a lot of time trying different SLURM permission variants (different partitions, explicit –account flags, sacctmgr queries). Although early evidence showed no partitions accessible, other methods should have been used earlier. Tried using –gres=gpu:1/2 without verifying the cluster’s GRES configuration. It took one submission cycle to discover that GRES was not configured (all GRES fields were empty). Lessons Learned Key Lessons Access patterns in high-performance computing clusters vary greatly. Always check SLURM (sbatch/srun/squeue) and direct SSH access. Verify GRES configuration before using –gres flags, and check bash history to understand historical access methods. When infrastructure obstacles are fundamental (no partition access, SSH blocked by pam_slurm_adopt), provide automated scripts that can be executed immediately, along with clear documentation. This way, work can be resumed immediately after administrators restore access. Dialogue Summary 🔄 Automation of pi0.5 and BC-RNN evaluation pipelines 04:45:52.536 | claude_code The user requested a script to run the full evaluation process for pi0.5 VLA and mimicgen BC-RNN baseline models. Claude created a comprehensive automated script (run_full_eval.sh with a three-step SLURM process, launch_eval_tmux.sh for tmux-based parallel execution, and monitor_eval.sh for status tracking). However, it was discovered that cluster GPU access was completely blocked – the user’s group had no permissions in any SLURM partition, and pam_slurm_adopt prevented SSH connections to GPU nodes. A script that can be executed immediately was provided, pending administrator intervention.

title: Bug Journal 2026-02-21

content:
Daily Report — 2026-02-20 Daily Overview Completed Work: Built an embedded cache infrastructure for the MIHD spatial transcriptomics workflow and initiated large-scale robot error scenario generation to expand the benchmark dataset. Implementation: Integrated the CacheManager into run_benchmark.py to enable immediate cache reuse; debugged the issue where observation results did not match during multi-strategy deployment; parallelized the processing of 5 data generation tasks using multiple GPUs for acceleration. Impact: Now MIHD evaluations can skip the reencoding process for hours through caching; the error recovery benchmark test has expanded from 454 scenarios to over 2000 target scenarios, supporting parallel execution of 4 strategies. DCC Completed Work: Designed and implemented an embedded cache system for the MIHD spatial transcriptomics benchmark workflow. Implementation: Added CacheManager integration to run_benchmark.py, supporting read/write operations for gene/visual embeddings, providing variation support (standard/frequency/strict state), and a mechanism for propagating coverage flags. Impact: Eliminated redundant instantiation of UNI2/SCAN encoders—now 1536-dimensional embeddings from the cache can be loaded directly without extracting them from images; allowed for quick experimentation with 9 fusion methods. tianhe Completed Work: Fixed errors in the observation process and initiated parallel GPU tasks to expand the error recovery benchmark test M12 dataset from 454 scenarios to over 2000 scenarios. Implementation: Fixed the mapping issue in the _to_robosuite_obs() function to resolve the mismatch between BC-RNN 65-dimensional and 37-dimensional observations; fixed the injection and natural capture paths; started 3 BC-RNN and 2 VLA server tasks on 5 GPUs simultaneously. Impact: Large-scale data generation was achieved—BC-RNN injection (200 iterations), natural capture (200 iterations), demonstration-based generation, and Pi0/Pi0.5 VLA capture can now run in parallel. In-depth infrastructure work was done on two projects: the spatial transcriptomics cache system and the robot error recovery data generation process.
Tasks Architecture and Strategy ✅ Added embedded cache support to run_benchmark.py — Integrated the CacheManager into MIHD’s run_benchmark.py, checking/loading/saving gene and visual embeddings before encoder instantiation, and providing variation support and coverage flags. 🔄 Started parallel M12 data generation tasks — Started BC-RNN injection (GPU0), BC-RNN natural capture (GPU1), demonstration-based generation (GPU2), Pi0 server (GPU4), Pi0.5 server (GPU5) to expand the error scenario dataset from 454 scenarios to over 2000 scenarios. ✅ Fixed BC-RNN observation dimension mismatch — Fixed the object state-to-object mapping in the _to_robosuite_obs() function to resolve the mismatch between 65-dimensional and 37-dimensional observations; used raw robosuite obs during both injection and natural capture processes. Implementation and Fixes ✅ Implemented save_images function for M15 training data — Added the save_images=True option to RecoveryDataCollector to save camera frames and successful recovery trajectories. ❌ Fixed MimicGen robomimic compatibility issues — Updated robomimic_utils.py to filter kwargs for robomimic 0.3.0 API, but it was hindered by mujoco_py dependency conflicts—decided to skip this step and compensate with more BC-RNN iterations. Problems and Solutions Key Problems 1. run_benchmark.py reinstantiates encoders and extracts embeddings every time it runs, wasting UNI2/SCAN features that have already been calculated. Solution: Integrated the CacheManager into has/load/save_*_embedding() methods; added cache checks before encoder instantiation and provided variation support (standard/frequency/strict state); propagated coverage flags at both call points.
Key Insight: Users’ expectations are correct—run_benchmark.py should have similar caching functionality. AI initially proposed workaround solutions (separate evaluation scripts, manual caching) rather than solving the root cause.
2. BC-RNN strategy expects input size to be 65, but the environment provides dimensions of 37, causing RuntimeError. Solution: The root cause is the mismatch in the number of objects (8 objects = 56 dimensions used in training, 4 objects = 28 dimensions in the evaluation environment). Fixed the _to_robosuite_obs() function to map ‘object-state’ to ‘object’, using raw robosuite obs from environment._env._get_observations(), instead of using StateExtractor.
Key Insight: The initial fix only applied to the injection path (_generate_from_single_rollout); the same problem exists in the natural capture path. When fixing the observation process, all code paths need to be checked.
3. staig_fusion ARI in eval_scan_fusion.py is 0.39, which is lower than expected. Solution: It was found that there was a double normalization error—staig_alignment_config was not passed, so STAIGTrainer applied StandardScaler after upstream preprocessing, while sc.pp.scale() already existed. Additionally, no previous 151673 staig_fusion baseline data was available for comparison.
Key Insight: “Lower than expected” without baseline data is speculative. Before assuming regression, the existence of baseline data must be verified. Also, independent evaluation scripts easily overlook configuration details that the standard process can handle correctly.
General Problems 4. MimicGen dataset enhancement failed, with the error “env_class unexpected keyword argument”. Solution: Fixed robomimic_utils.py to use inspect.signature() to filter kwargs based on robomimic 0.3.0 API signature, but it was still hindered by mujoco_py dependency issues in pip installations. Decided to skip MimicGen and expand the scale through more BC-RNN/VLA iterations.
Key Insight: Environmental isolation issues require deep dependency analysis. When encountering obstacles, alternative methods should be explored instead of forcing incompatible dependencies.
Human and Artificial Intelligence Methods Strategic Level Embedding Cache Architecture Decisions Role Method Human Questioned why run_benchmark.py would reinstantiate encoders when embeddings already existed; expected similar caching functionality in the process; preferred to fix run_benchmark.py itself rather than finding workaround solutions Artificial Intelligence Initially proposed creating a separate eval_scan_fusion.py script, then suggested using a pipeline approach, and finally proposed manual caching of SCAN embeddings—before accepting to fix run_benchmark.py, tried various workaround solutions Difference Analysis: Humans identified the real problem (lack of caching support in the standard tool) and insisted on fixing the root cause. Artificial intelligence tended to create new scripts and workaround solutions rather than modifying the existing infrastructure.
Implementation Level Observation Process Debugging Strategies Role Method Human Not explicitly shown in this meeting, but humans requested a detailed explanation of the STAIG fusion and observation processes Artificial Intelligence First fixed the injection path, then discovered the same problem in the natural capture path—required a second iteration to fully fix it Difference Analysis: Artificial intelligence’s step-by-step debugging overlooked the fact that the same observation process problem existed in both code paths. If a more thorough code analysis had been conducted from the start, these two problems could have been identified.
summary: Two projects are currently underway with intensive infrastructure construction: the spatial transcriptomics caching system and the robotic technology error recovery data generation process.
