content:
Daily Report — 2026-02-19 Daily Summary Completed Work: Development of two main projects: (1) MIHD spatial transcriptomics – comprehensive documentation of evaluation methods, visual encoder analysis, and image-based clustering enhancements, with review of over 25 papers; (2) Error recovery benchmark testing – phase II infrastructure construction (M14 baseline evaluation, M12/M13 plans) and strategic document optimization Completion Method: Combined multiple methods, including systematic literature research, exploring code libraries through parallel agents, implementing code step by step with resume functionality, strategic planning through iterative user communication, document restructuring, and cross-domain solution analysis Impact: MIHD: Created a complete knowledge base for spatial transcriptomics evaluation, identified visual limitations (morphological and biological clustering), and designed the seventh improvement target with a five-phase implementation roadmap; Error recovery: M14 baseline is ready (5448 cases, crash recovery capability), phase II roadmap is complete (v4.9→v4.10), documents are optimized by 51%, and all 87 checklist items are retained. Manual remote operation strategy was chosen to obtain high-quality recovery data DCC Completed Work: MIHD project work: Created 4 technical documents (RM-IDEAL metrics, visual encoder guidelines, literature review, UNI2 evaluation analysis), improved visualization, conducted image-based clustering literature research covering over 25 papers, designed the seventh improvement plan with 6 candidate solutions Completion Method: In-depth analysis of code libraries using Read/Grep tools, extensive review of spEMO/STAIG/HEST-1k/UNI/STEGO/SCAN/BYOL/F-SEG related papers, modified create_clustering_visualization() function to generate 1×3 layout visualizations, batch generation of 11 DLPFC visualizations, conducted cross-domain comparative analysis (FGVC, medical imaging, remote sensing) Impact: Created a complete reference document for spatial transcriptomics evaluation; identified reasons for UNI2 feature failures (ARI 0.11-0.16 vs 0.5+ in multimodal scenarios) – clustering based on morphological similarity rather than biological categories; developed a five-phase implementation roadmap with risk mitigation; updated ENHANCEMENT_PLAN_CN.md document with 500 additional lines for the seventh improvement plan TzJsDesktop Completed Work: Fixed issue where VS Code Remote-SSH connection could not connect to HPC cluster (tianhe/an49/an47) on Windows system Completion Method: Identified compatibility issues between ControlMaster Unix domain sockets and Windows OpenSSH; commented out ControlMaster/ControlPath/ControlPersist in ~/.ssh/config file; cleaned up expired socket files Impact: Restored SSH connections to tianhe and an49, enabling remote development on HPC cluster tianhe Completed Work: Error recovery benchmark testing phase II: Implemented M14 baseline infrastructure (support for resume, multi-run merging, error type analysis), designed M12 large dataset construction and M13 classification validation plan, conducted VLA model research survey, optimized project documents from 1554 lines to 763 lines (reduced by 51%), designed a comprehensive phase II research strategy with manual remote operation Completion Method: Added support for resume function in collector.py, extended analysis scripts to support multi-run merging and error type analysis; created 7 Makefile targets; researched 17 VLA/basic models through network search; used block reading for large files; deployed parallel Explore agent to collect code library information; clarified concept of recovery training data through iterative Q&A; moved VLM tutorial to separate file Impact: M14 is ready for operation: 454 scenarios × 4 strategies × 3 seeds = 5448 cases, with crash recovery capability and automatic resume; comprehensive VLA research results make model selection easier; documents can now be maintained, with all 87 checklist items retained; clear phase II roadmap defines path from infrastructure to paper-level experiments (G1-G4: dataset size, classification validation, baseline evaluation, FM practicality proof); manual remote operation strategy was chosen to obtain high-quality recovery demonstrations Two important research projects were completed: comprehensive MIHD spatial transcriptomics documentation and visual encoder analysis revealed fundamental limitations, as well as infrastructure construction for phase II of error recovery benchmark testing, with a strategic roadmap established
Tasks Architecture and Strategy - ✅ MIHD comprehensive evaluation documentation and literature review – Created a complete documentation system: RM-IDEAL scoring metrics (graphical kernel WWL), visual encoder process guide (patch extraction, alignment, caching, fusion), pathological base model survey (spEMO/STAIG/HEST-1k/UNI/UNI2), image-based clustering methods (STEGO/SCAN/BYOL/F-SEG). Analyzed 34 UNI clinical tasks and 8 UNI2 benchmark tests, finding that most literature overlooked image-based spatial domain evaluations. ✅ MIHD improvement plan update: Concept 7 Image-Based Clustering – Designed a five-phase implementation plan with 6 candidate solutions (STEGO feature optimization, SCAN neighbor mining, BYOL training during testing, DINOv2-GAT graph attention, TTT-MAE mask reconstruction, SFDA frozen encoder + adapter). Added dependency diagram, risk analysis, and verification commands (about 500 lines) to ENHANCEMENT_PLAN_CN.md. ✅ Implementation of Error Recovery Benchmark M14 Baseline Infrastructure – Added recovery function to collector.py (read episodes.jsonl file, rebuild state, skip completed scenarios). Extended 3_collect_data.py with –resume/–scene_offset/–scene_limit options. Redrewed 4_analyze_results.py to support multi-run merging and error type analysis. Created 7 Makefile targets for baseline evaluation of 454 scenarios × 4 strategies. 🔄 Error Recovery Benchmark M12: Large Dataset Construction Plan – Designed a 13-step plan including MimicGen enhancement (10→200 demonstration samples), BC-RNN/Pi0/Pi0.5 natural error capture, hierarchical training/testing segmentation, adjusting detector timing parameters. All code is written (6 files modified), but encountered MUJOCO_EGL_DEVICE_ID errors during execution. 🔄 Error Recovery Benchmark M13: Error Classification Validation Design – Designed a three-pronged validation method: (1) Mapping FMEA to ISO 15066/10218/IEC 61508 standards; (2) Verifying completeness through 6×3 phase × family matrix; (3) Using Cohen’s/Fleiss’ kappa for statistical validation to assess classifier reliability. The plan is ready but not yet implemented. ✅ Strategic Planning Document for Phase II of Error Recovery Benchmark Testing – Comprehensive update of proj全景总结.md (from v4.9 to v4.10): divided goals into Phase I (completed G1-G3) and Phase II (new G4-G7), added detailed sub-goals for M12-M15, created a five-phase execution schedule (Day 1 to Day 16), updated risk analysis, reduced code lines from 1554 to 763 (reduced by 51%), retained all 87 checklist items; moved VLM tutorial to separate file; increased statistical data to 53 files, approximately 13,300 lines. ✅ VLA and Basic Model Research Survey – Created a comprehensive 200-line survey covering 8 VLA models with error recovery capabilities (SC-VLA, CycleVLA, VLA-SCT, FailSafe, FPC-VLA, RoboFAC, VLAC, Self-Improving VLA) and 9 basic models (π0, π0-FAST, π0.5, OpenVLA, Octo, GR00T, HPT, RT-2). Included details of architecture, open-source status, usage instructions, fine-tuning commands, and integration suggestions. Implementation and Fixes ✅ Enhancing MIHD Visualization with H&E Reference Panel – Modified create_clustering_visualization() function to change its layout from 1×2 to 1×3, added H&E image panel for direct comparison of morphological features; used cached embedding data to generate visualizations for all 11 DLPFC regions (151508-151675); fixed issue where 151510 had missing low-resolution images. ✅ Fixing Windows SSH Connection Issues to HPC Cluster – Resolved VS Code Remote-SSH failure due to incompatibility between ControlMaster/Unix domain sockets and Windows OpenSSH; successfully connected to tianhe and an49. ✅ Improving Error Recovery Benchmark CLAUDE.md Documentation – Reduced project documentation from 205 lines to 163 lines: corrected inaccuracies in error type count (17→24) and verifier count (2→4), merged redundant architecture sections, reduced external project description from 48 lines to 18 lines, added test fixture documentation and data format references. Problems and Solutions Key Issues 1. How to prove that error recovery benchmark testing is useful beyond “generating scenarios” – Need evidence to show that the data truly improves the model, so it can contribute to papers Solution: Designed G4 experiment (dataset usefulness proof), divided into 4 steps: (1) Convert over 2000 scenarios to LeRobot format, including state information and image observations; (2) Fine-tune π0.5 on error scenarios for 2-4 hours using LoRA on A800; (3) Evaluate using stratified sampling within retained test samples; (4) Measure improvement effects (expected 15-30% SR gain, 10-20% RP gain, statistical significance analysis using bootstrap confidence intervals and Wilcoxon tests) Key Insight: Beneficialness of benchmark testing requires closed-loop verification: error data → model training → measurable performance improvement on unseen errors. This is a core paper contribution beyond scenario generation infrastructure—the “four-pronged validation,” which distinguishes publishable benchmarks from simple datasets.
2. UNI2 basic model accuracy in DLPFC spatial domain clustering is only 0.11-0.16 (compared to 0.5+ for gene+image multimodality), and brown repeating areas are evenly distributed in clustering results Solution: Identified 5 root causes: (1) Differences in training data domains (more cancer data, less brain data), (2) Incompatible pre-training tasks (rough tissue classification vs fine-grained layer detection), (3) Subtle differences in DLPFC layers (cell density/size, not color/texture), (4) Redundancy between features and gene expression, (5) Lack of spatial context. Analysis showed that UNI2 clustering is based on morphological similarity (blood vessels across layers), not biological categorization. Proposed 6 adaptation methods (STEGO, SCAN, BYOL, DINOv2-GAT, TTT-MAE, SFDA), in order of solution hierarchy: domain-specific pre-training → adaptive tuning during testing → feature optimization → task-specific fine-tuning. Added H&E reference panel in visualization to confirm hypothesis. Key Insight: Pure visual encoders cluster based on morphological features, not biological functions—a typical fine-grained recognition problem, common in computer vision (bird subspecies, rock types, cell morphology). Lower accuracy reflects structural grouping (similar things), not functional grouping (biologically similar things). General basic models learn coarse semantic information; for fine-grained tasks, domain-specific fine-tuning or feature optimization is needed.
3. Imbalance in error recovery benchmark dataset—90% scenarios belong to pre_reach/reach, transport/place, while transport/place is completely empty (0 scenarios) Solution: Used BC-RNN as strategy to achieve subsequent phase goals, reducing detector minimum step threshold from 100 to 5-50 to capture lift/transport failures. Used MimicGen to generate 200+ demonstration variants to increase trajectory diversity and trigger opportunities for subsequent phases. Key Insight: Phase coverage requires a strategy with sufficient success rate to proceed to later phases (optimal timing for BC-RNN), while adjusting detector timing—random strategy fails prematurely (never reaches mastery level), Pi0’s SR is 0% (never reaches mastery level), only BC-RNN can achieve transport/placement when failing. This is a “reachability problem” determined by success rate distribution.
4. How to rigorously verify that 24 error classification methods are principled correct, complete, and non-redundant for academic publication Solution: Three-pronged validation: (1) Mapping 8 categories to ISO 15066/10218/IEC 61508 industrial safety standards’ FMEA failure modes (providing theoretical basis), (2) Verifying completeness through 6 phases × 3 families = 18 cell matrices to ensure no blind spots, (3) Using Cohen’s/Fleiss’ kappa for statistical validation (goal: κ>0.6 within same category, κ<0.3 between categories) to verify type distinction Key Insight: Verification of academic classification methods requires theoretical basis (industry standards provide FMEA rationale) and empirical evidence (statistical independence tests proving no redundancy)—cannot rely solely on intuition for handling 24 types
5. M14 baseline evaluation (5448 cases, runtime 5-15 hours) may lose all progress if VLA server crashes during operation Solution: Implemented recovery function in collector.py: read existing episodes.jsonl files at startup, build a set of completed tuples (scene ID, strategy name, seed), skip these tuples in a triple loop, and rebuild all_episodes dictionary to maintain consistency of summary. Added –resume flag in 3_collect_data.py. Now with automatic recovery, ensures continued operation even if server crashes. Key Insight: For long-running data collection (over 1 hour), appending to JSONL logs + recovery logic is essential. Key is immutability: reading back to JSONL and rebuilding summary dictionary ensures consistent final results, whether running once or multiple recoveries
6. Users do not understand the concept of “recovery training data” in phase II plan, hindering approval of the plan Solution: Provided specific examples, comparing normal demonstrations (reset→success) with recovery trajectories (error state→adjustment→success); explained how it teaches the model “how to correct errors,” not just “how to operate correctly from scratch.” Users ultimately chose manual remote operation to obtain high-quality data, although this increases time cost. Key Insight: Before implementation discussions, technical terms must be explained with concrete examples; users prioritize data quality and real recovery demonstrations over automated agents, willing to invest manual work for real data
7. Errors like “client_loop: send disconnect: Bad file descriptor” and “tried to write to a nonexistent pipe” occurred when connecting VS Code Remote-SSH to HPC cluster Solution: Windows OpenSSH does not support ControlMaster/ControlPath/ControlPersist used by Unix domain sockets. Commented out these options in ~/.ssh/config file, and removed expired socket files from ~/.ssh/sockets/ directory. Connection restored immediately. Key Insight: Multiplexing of SSH connections is a Unix-only feature; when these options exist, Windows OpenSSH silently fails, resulting in unintelligible low-level errors, seemingly faults rather than functional unavailability
General Issues 8. Project summary document is too large (1554 lines, 33000 tags), making optimization difficult Solution: Use block reading, dividing the document into 400-line paragraphs for reading; use Explore agent to collect statistical data; move large VLM tutorial section (331 lines) to separate file; reduce document length to 763 lines (reduced by 51%), while retaining all 87 checklist items Key Insight: Large files require strategic reading: use specific offsets for block access, allowing full editing of the document, or grep searches for specific content. When refactoring the document, verify steps: calculate all checklist items before and after modification to ensure no content is lost
9. When running MimicGen with CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=3, MUJOCO_EGL_DEVICE_ID assertion failed Solution: EGL device ID must match GPU index in CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES, not physical GPU ID. When using CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=3, set MUJOCO_EGL_DEVICE_ID to 3 (or 0 if using single GPU) Key Insight: EGL device ID is relative to visible devices, not absolute GPU hardware index—robosuite’s EGL assertion is designed to ensure consistency of rendering targets
Human-AI Approaches Strategic Level Strategic planning workflow: Understand first, then plan| Role | Method | |——|——| | Human | Requires updating the strategic document (Project Overview.md) before any code implementation; interrupts the planning process multiple times to ask “What are the steps to obtain recovery training data?” to ensure clarity before approving detailed implementation; prefers a step-by-step understanding process with the ability to adjust direction at any time | | Artificial Intelligence | Directly proceeds to implementation planning and code generation, treating document updates as an afterthought; attempts to develop a complete detailed plan before submitting it for approval; tries to exit the planning mode before users fully understand the concepts; uses technical terms without verifying user comprehension |
Difference Analysis: Humans manage risks through a workflow where they first understand the concepts, then users decide on the direction, and finally develop detailed plans. They also use documentation-driven development for complex projects to ensure common understanding before execution. AI focuses more on delivering code and a complete plan immediately, rather than controlling consistency. For graduate projects that require feedback cycles from mentors, human methods can reduce the risk of rework.
Controlling Scope and Priority During Refactoring Role Method Human Requests the retention of all major/minor goal checklists when optimizing the 1554-line document; insists on retaining all goals even when AI suggests reducing them by 60%; stops network research in advance; chooses manual remote operation to obtain recovery data despite the high cost of time, prioritizing quality over automation Artificial Intelligence Initially proposes radical integration measures to eliminate redundancy (reducing from 1554 lines to 600-700 lines); conducts network searches to find benchmark patterns before establishing a specific current state plan; proposes automated options (using Oracle recovery as a “recommended solution”) Difference Analysis: Humans view the integrity of the roadmap as non-negotiable and value the completeness of operational checklists over the simplicity of documents. They recognize that checklists are tools for tracking progress (must be comprehensive), while explanatory text should follow the DRY principle. Humans value quality and real demonstrations over automated agents. AI focuses on reducing file size metrics and conducts external research before gaining a deep understanding of the code library status.✅ Strategic Document Optimization and Phase II Research Design 20:17:43.767 | claude_code The project overview summary.md has been optimized, reducing the number of lines from 1554 to 763 (a reduction of 51%). Redundant content has been removed, and statistical data has been updated. All 87 checklist items have been retained. Large files are processed using a block-based reading method. The Explore agent was launched to gather information about the code library (53 files, approximately 14,300 lines of error-prone code). The VLM tutorial (331 lines) has been moved to a separate document/vlm_tutorial.md, and the main document has been rewritten. A comprehensive Phase II research strategy was designed, including 4 goals: G1 – dataset size reaches over 2000, G2 – classification validation, G3 – benchmark testing of 4 strategies, G4 – FM fine-tuning validation. There are also 15 milestones. Users clarified their strategic choices through an iterative Q&A session: choosing manual remote operations to restore data (prioritizing quality over automation), and limited to the PickPlace scope. Since users interrupted ExitPlanMode, the plan is not yet finalized to ensure a thorough understanding of the concept of “restoring training data”.
✅ VLA Model and Base Model Research Survey 15:59:46.611 | claude_code A comprehensive web-based survey was conducted, covering 8 VLA models claimed to have error recovery capabilities (SC-VLA, CycleVLA, VLA-SCT, FailSafe, FPC-VLA, RoboFAC, VLAC, Self-Improving VLA) and 9 base models (π0, π0-FAST, π0.5, OpenVLA, Octo, GR00T N1, HPT, RT-2). A reference document of 200 lines was created, including details of the architectures, open-source status, usage instructions, and fine-tuning commands. It was found that FailSafe and VLA-SCT are the most easily integrated models (no training required, plug-in architecture). Recovery methods are divided into two categories: dual systems, active subtask backtracking, and reactive supervised guidance correction.
✅ CLAUDE.md Document Quality Improvement 20:03:03.225 | claude_code Targeted improvements were made, reducing the number of lines in the project documentation from 205 to 163, while retaining all key information. Inaccuracies in error type counts (from 17 to 24) and verifier counts (from 2 to 4) were corrected. Duplicative architectural sections were merged, and overly detailed external project descriptions were reduced from 48 lines to 18 lines. Missing test fixtures and data format-related documents were added.
Token Usage Total: $19.73 — 27 million tokens
summary: There are two important research projects: first, the comprehensive documentation of MIHD spatial transcriptomics data; second, analyzing the fundamental limitations of this visual encoder. In addition, we have completed the second phase of the error recovery benchmark test and formulated corresponding implementation plans.
title: Bug Journal 2026-02-19

content:
Daily Report — 2026-02-18 Daily Overview Completed Work: Two parallel research projects: fixed a spatial transcriptomics coordinate processing bug that caused visual embedding errors (DCC), and built a comprehensive policy benchmark test infrastructure with unified checkpoint management and visualization capabilities (tianhe) Completion Method: Deeply debugged the coordinate system through code inspection and verification, then cleared cache and ran experiments again; completed infrastructure work through symbolic linking, script development, and integration testing Impact: MIHD: After fixing the bug, the clustering performance based on UNI2 improved by 86%, verifying the results of multimodal fusion; Tianhe: Unified checkpoint management and visualization tools enabled systematic VLA cross-task generalization analysis DCC Completed Work: Diagnosed and fixed a coordinate swapping bug in the MIHD spatial transcriptomics process, which caused pxl_row/pxl_col to be mapped as X/Y instead of Y/X Completion Method: Modified run_benchmark.py and data_loader.py files to correct column names, cleared visual cache, and re-run 33 visual-only tests and 88 multimodal fusion tests using STAIG training, taking about 6 hours Impact: Now UNI2 embeddings can be correctly extracted from the correct image positions; ARI increased from 0.065 to 0.121, and the visual-only fusion reached 0.275 ARI, verifying the effectiveness of multimodal integration methods Tianhe Completed Work: Built a unified checkpoint management system with symbolic links, created a policy release visualization tool, and investigated the availability of VLA training data for PickPlace tasks Completion Method: Created a checkpoints/ directory structure connecting BC-RNN/VLA/Robomimic models, wrote visualize_policy_rollout.py script with MP4 rendering capabilities, updated configuration files and Makefiles, and searched for zhaoganlong’s dataset Impact: Now policy behaviors can be visualized via MP4 release, and the root cause of VLA failures was identified (lack of task-specific fine-tuning); it was found that MimicGen can expand 10 demonstration samples to 500 for future training Fixed a critical spatial coordinate bug in MIHD multimodal fusion benchmarks, and established infrastructure for robot policy visualization and checkpoint management
Tasks Architecture and Strategy ✅ Fixed MIHD coordinate swapping bug — Corrected the column mapping issue in tissue_positions_list.csv, swapping pxl_row_in_fullres(Y) and pxl_col_in_fullres(X) to pixel_x/pixel_y Implementation and Fixes 🔄 Ran MIHD visual and fusion experiments again — After fixing the coordinates, re-generated embedding data and benchmark results, conducting 33 visual-only tests and 88 multimodal fusion tests ✅ Created policy release visualization tool — Written visualize_policy_rollout.py script, supporting off-screen rendering, HUD overlay, MP4 export, and random/BC-RNN/VLA policies ✅ Built unified checkpoint management system — Created checkpoints/ directory with symbolic links to BC-RNN, VLA (Pi0/Pi0.5), and Robomimic models, ensuring consistent path references ✅ Investigated availability of VLA training data — Searched for PickPlace-specific VLA checkpoints and training data; only found 10 source demonstration samples, while Coffee/Stack tasks had 500 demonstration samples Problems and Solutions Key Problems 1. Due to the X/Y coordinate swapping in tissue_positions_list.csv parsing, visual embeddings are extracted from transposed image positions Solution: Changed column names to pxl_row/pxl_col (semantic names), swapped coordinate order (X, Y) = (pxl_col, pxl_row), cleared visual cache, and ran experiments again
Key Insight: Spatial data requires explicit Y as row and X as column semantics; a 10-fold scale factor verification can detect coordinate system errors early
2. Pi0_libero checkpoints have a 0% success rate on PickPlace tasks, but 96-100% on Coffee/Stack tasks Solution: Identified the root cause: Pi0_libero is fine-tuned for the LIBERO domain, lacking PickPlace-specific training data (only 10 source demonstration samples, while other tasks have 500); proposed a process of MimicGen → LeRobot → LoRA
Key Insight: VLA’s cross-task generalization fails when the task domains differ (LIBERO vs PickPlace has different object/scene layouts); fine-tuning requires more than 500 demonstration samples, which can be expanded from 10 source samples using MimicGen
General Problems 3. Unable to visually inspect VLA policy behavior on PickPlace tasks, making it impossible to diagnose the 0% success rate Solution: Created visualize_policy_rollout.py script, supporting EGL off-screen rendering, frame-by-frame capture, HUD overlay (policy/steps/reward/status), and MP4 export functionality for each release
Key Insight: Video releases can reveal behavior patterns (e.g., random failures vs purposeful failures), and success rate alone cannot distinguish
4. Uneven distribution of checkpoint paths in multiple HDD_POOL directories, making configuration management fragile Solution: Created a centralized checkpoints/ directory, using task-organized symbolic links (bc_rnn/pick_place, vla/pi0_libero, robomimic/lift), and updated all configurations to use relative paths
Key Insight: Symbolic link-based checkpoint organization allows atomic path updates, while clearly distinguishing original storage from project references
Human-AI Approaches Strategic Level Selecting VLA checkpoints for PickPlace Role Approach Human Questioned why Pi0_libero was loaded instead of specific PickPlace checkpoints, assuming zhaoganlong should have such checkpoints AI Initially loaded the only available VLA checkpoint Pi0_libero, without verifying task-specific training conditions Difference Analysis: Humans intuitively knew there should be task-specific fine-tuning; AI defaulted to available checkpoints without verifying task matching. No PickPlace-specific checkpoints were found.
Implementation Level UNI/UNI2 used in literature Role Approach Human Requested online search to verify how other papers extracted patches and processed spatial transcriptomics coordinates AI Focused on code-level debugging, without consulting literature for best practices Difference Analysis: Humans sought external validation from published methods; AI relied on internal code logic. Literature search confirmed 224x224 patches at 20x magnification, but lacked specific coordinate handling details.
AI Limitations General Limitations Before recommending Pi0_libero, no active search for task-specific VLA checkpoints was conducted, requiring manual prompting to investigate zhaoganlong’s complete checkpoint directory Multiple searches were needed to find that zhaoganlong’s dataset was organized by task (Coffee/Stack), with 500 generated demonstration samples per task, with initial focus on directly finding PickPlace Lessons Learned Key Lessons - In medical imaging, spatial coordinate systems require clear semantic naming (pxl_row=Y, pxl_col=X), verified with physical units (pixel × scaling factor = micron); generic pixel_x/pixel_y naming is error-prone. Integration of visual base models is crucial in multimodal learning, relying on spatial alignment; even if the architecture is correct, incorrect segment extraction (from transposed positions) can lead to half the performance. VLA models trained in one task domain (e.g., LIBERO) have little generalization ability in different operating environments, despite similar action spaces; task-specific fine-tuning is essential. MimicGen can expand 10 human demonstrations to 500 training examples through automatic mutation generation, matching the data size used for successful VLA fine-tuning (Coffee: 96%, Stack: 100%). Practical Lessons Checkpoint management based on symbolic links enables version control of path references while preserving the original data locations; this is important for collaboration on shared file systems. Dialogue Summary MIHD Spatial Transcriptomics 🔄 Fixed Coordinate Swapping Errors in Visual Segment Extraction 00:01:02.520 | claude_code Diagnosed and fixed a critical error where the columns (pxl_row, pxl_col) in tissue_positions_list.csv were incorrectly mapped to (pixel_x, pixel_y), causing visual segments to be extracted from transposed positions. Cleared visual cache and ran 121 experiments (33 visual-only experiments + 88 fusion experiments). Results showed UNI2 ARI increased from 0.065 to 0.121 (+86%). Users requested literature research on UNI/UNI2 usage patterns; online search confirmed 224x224 segments with 20x magnification, but lacked specific coordinate handling details. Due to STAIG training requirements, experiments lasted over 6 hours.
Error Recovery Benchmark Test 🔍 Investigation of VLA Training Data and PickPlace Fine-tuning Paths 04:52:08.850 | claude_code After discovering that Pi0_libero had a 0% success rate, users asked how to train VLA on PickPlace tasks. It was found that PickPlace had only 10 source demonstrations (6288 steps), while zhaoganlong’s successful Coffee/Stack training used approximately 500 demonstrations generated by MimicGen. There were no specific PickPlace VLA checkpoints; all VLA models were from the LIBERO domain or base weights. The identified training path was: 1) Run MimicGen to expand 10 demonstrations to 500, 2) Convert to LeRobot format using zhaoganlong’s script, 3) Run Pi0.5 LoRA fine-tuning. All necessary scripts and pipelines are provided in zhaoganlong’s repository.
✅ Checkpoint List and Availability Assessment 05:32:29.978 | claude_code Users requested a list of available VLA models and checkpoints. Four types of VLA models were found in the code (pi0, pi05, phoenix, flare), but only Pi0_libero and Pi05_base had actual checkpoints (each約12GB). Also found were BC-RNN PickPlace checkpoints (12 cycles, 14MB each) and Robomimic Lift checkpoints (7.6MB, 100% successful). The can/square/transport directory of robomimic was empty. All VLA checkpoints were located in zhaoganlong’s HDD_POOL directory, linked to the project via symbolic links.
✅ Policy Visualization Infrastructure Setup 00:26:49.679 | claude_code Established unified checkpoint management through checkpoints/ directory and symbolic links (bc_rnn/pick_place, vla/pi0_libero, robomimic/lift). Created visualize_policy_rollout.py (200 lines of code) that supports random/BC-RNN/VLA policies, with off-screen rendering, HUD overlay, and MP4 export capabilities. Updated benchmark_v4.yaml to use symbolic link paths, added 3 Makefile visualization targets. Successfully tested random policies, generating 2 MP4 files. Updated documentation in the project panorama summary.md, adding new v4.7 checkpoint management section.

summary: 我们已经修复了MIHD多模态融合基准测试中的关键空间坐标错误问题，并且已经建立了用于机器人策略可视化以及检查点管理的基础设施。
title: Bug Journal 2026-02-18

summary: Daily AI Dialogue Summary:
title: Defect record: 2026-02-17

content:
Key Limitations of AI- The definition of “completed” is too optimistic—unit tests will equate to operational readiness, ignoring the need for actual model checkpoints and API integration tests in VLA integration, not just mock tests. Initially, no attention was paid to the use of repack_transform in openpi configuration to rename keys, leading to incorrect assumptions about the format of input keys. This issue was only discovered after reviewing the actual LiberoInputs source code and make_libero examples. When writing VLA integration code, it wasn’t anticipated that the requirement to include images would be necessary; this issue was only discovered through runtime errors. There was also a lack of prior knowledge checks regarding the format requirements for VLA policy inputs. A dimensionality ablation plan was generated, but the user refused to execute it, thus abandoning it. The AI didn’t know why the plan was rejected, nor did it understand how the user wanted to proceed. General Limitations Despite multiple network searches and Stanford server explorations, it was still difficult to find the correct BC-RNN checkpoint URL. The official robomimic documentation refers to a “model zoo,” but doesn’t provide a direct checkpoint URL. When users refused to use multiple tools during checkpointing, the AI didn’t actively inquire about the users’ true intentions, but instead continued to call similar tools. Confusion between Claude Code CLI and Claude API occurred, lacking an accurate understanding of one’s own tool ecosystem. It was necessary for the user to explicitly instruct the code to generate rules for “updating the project panorama summary.md” and “storing checkpoints in specific locations.” Lessons Learned Key Lessons - Embedding Dimensionality Bottlenecks Can Cause Model Crashes: STAIG’s 64-Dimensional Embeddings Could Compress 1536-Dimensional Visual Features by 96%, producing embeddings with a standard deviation of 0.12, which are almost indistinguishable. Spatial refinement could eliminate a few clusters, while majority voting could resolve other issues. Other fusion methods (such as attention mechanisms or qformer: 256-512 dimensions) wouldn’t have these problems, indicating that 64-dimensional embeddings are inherently insufficient. Key requirements for VLA end-to-end integration: Ensure that policy observation input formats are correct, especially in the camera image transmission section. It’s necessary to explicitly use the include_images=True parameter in the state_extractor.extract() function and provide camera_names information for all policies that rely on cameras (such as VLA and VisuoMotor BC). When integrating third-party machine learning libraries (such as openpi and robomimic), it’s essential to check the actual API signatures using inspect.signature() and read the sample code from the source repository. Documentation often lacks completeness or is outdated. The openpi library’s create_trained_policy() provides advanced encapsulation, but manually using the low-level API can be error-prone. Pi0 (LIBERO checkpoint) has poor generalization ability in the robosuite’s PickPlace task (failure rate of 100%). The main failure modes include joint_limit_approach (47%) and grasp_wrong_pose (30%). This indicates that VLA’s cross-task generalization still presents challenges, but these failure data can provide valuable natural error data for benchmark testing. Capturing natural errors is more efficient than injecting them: 50 iterations generated 150 scenarios, while the injection method only generated 3 scenarios. The natural error mode is more diverse and realistic. Plugin-based architecture pattern in the benchmark system: Detectors, classifiers, and injectors all use the basic ABC+ registry pattern to achieve scalability. The error classification system uses CLASSIFIER_REGISTRY to register 19 classifiers, making it easy to add new error types without modifying the core logic. Document verification requires checking the actual code registry and enumeration values, not just reading the document strings or previous documents. DETECTOR_REGISTRY, CLASSIFIER_REGISTRY, and ErrorType enumeration are real reference standards. Through systematic searching, residual errors can be avoided. Communication architecture across Conda environments: VLA servers use TCP+pickle protocol to separate model inference (openpi/openpi05 environment) and benchmark framework (mimicgen_env). Length-prefixed frames prevent message fragmentation, and action chunking reduces the number of inference calls. Environment isolation is crucial for VLA/robot projects due to conflicting dependencies. Preprocessing of observation data must handle various input formats gracefully: StateExtractor (internal framework), raw env.step() (robosuite), and model-specific preprocessing (VLA servers). A robust key lookup mechanism plus a fallback mechanism can prevent silent failures. The dictionary format of observation data varies depending on the source, so a unified preprocessing layer is needed. Spatial majority voting refinement (nearest neighbor with a radius of 15) may amplify rather than fix clustering errors, especially when the underlying embeddings are already compressed. Refinement should be based on embedding quality metrics (for example, if the contour score is below a threshold, refinement should be disabled). The dual VLM backend design (Gemini API + Claude Code CLI) offers flexibility: The API is suitable for batch automation operations, while the CLI is suitable for interactive deep analysis and debugging. Both complement each other and can meet different usage needs. To generate natural errors, VLA integration requires architectural adjustments: (1) Adopt a policy server mode across Conda environments; (2) Support camera observations in environment wrappers; (3) Preprocess observation data (convert from quaternions to angular coordinates, BGR to RGB, action chunking); (4) Dual iteration mode (synthetic injection and natural capture). This is very different from the synthetic perturbation method used in demonstration replay. The system’s code library exploration strategy (starting from infrastructure checks, to model file locations, to confirmation of configuration parameters, and finally to user decision confirmation) can effectively support complex multi-step project planning, avoiding overlooking key dependencies. In data structure design, backward compatibility is an important consideration: By introducing optional and graceful downgrading of the new conversation_summaries field, it’s ensured that old reports can still be used normally. This approach applies to evolving data formats. Out of the 24 error types in the error classification system, only 5 were triggered in actual VLA iterations: joint_limit_approach, grasp_wrong_pose, misalignment_pre_grasp, overshoot, and premature_release. This indicates that certain error types require specific scenarios to manifest. Practical Experience Using GPUs for batch processing for visualization (5 GPUs, 20 videos, zero failures) demonstrated the value of parallel infrastructure. The subprocess.Popen + GPU allocation pattern is ideal for parallel tasks. The subtleties of handling false values in Python: 0 or default would be evaluated as default because 0 is a false value, which would cause errors with counters indexed by 0. PrematureReleaseClassifier always returns 0 when starting to capture at step 0. The solution is to explicitly use is None to check for optional 0-indexed values. Conversation Summary MIHD 🔄 STAIG Fusion Clustering Crash Diagnosis and Dimensionality Analysis 22:26:09.691 | claude_code The user found that the visualization in section 151672 only showed 2 clusters, not 5. It was discovered that the bottleneck of 64-dimensional STAIG embeddings (with a standard deviation of 0.12, compressing 1536-dimensional inputs by 96%) caused almost complete crashes. Spatial refinement eliminated the remaining minor clusters. Cross-method comparisons showed that other methods wouldn’t crash even when using 256-512 dimensions. The user decided to continue with the dimensionality ablation experiment, but didn’t explain why they rejected the plan.
Error Recovery Benchmark Test ✅ VLA End-to-End Testing + Critical Error Fixes + Large-scale Natural Error Capture 16:23:03.741 | claude_code The user requested VLA end-to-end testing (10 injection iterations). The Pi0 server was started, and environmental issues were resolved (–no-banner, port occupation issues). The first run failed due to a “observation/image” KeyError; the problem was solved by adding the include_images parameter to the get_current_obs() function in rollout_generator.py. Three injection scenarios were successfully generated. Then, 50 natural capture iterations were conducted, generating 150 scenarios. The results showed that Pi0 had a failure rate of 100% in the PickPlace task, with the main errors being joint_limit_approach (47%) and grasp_wrong_pose (30%). The total number of scenarios increased from 121 to 271, achieving M5/M9/M10 milestone goals. The project documentation and milestone status were updated. ✅ VLA policies are now operating normally through automated error injection 06:51:17.532 | claude_code The user provided a detailed plan to solve VLA integration issues (wrong openpi API, missing argparse, conda mismatch). All 79 tests passed. Batch visualization processing was performed (20 videos generated on 5 GPUs, no failures). The user requested end-to-end VLA testing, but it was interrupted midway.

The PolicyErrorMonitor coordinator, VLM analyzer, and error analysis module were integrated into collector.py, with only 3 lines of changes made. 32 unit tests were created, covering all classifiers, and 3 test failures were fixed (severity logic, discard threshold, duration calculation). The final result was 73/73 tests passed (32 new + 41 existing), and the v4.3 system was ready for production.

The system’s file system search confirmed that Pi0/Pi0.5/Phoenix models existed in the zhaoganlong directory (total 250GB HuggingFace cache), but BC-RNN, can/square/transport checkpoints were missing. title: Bug Journal 2026-02-16

content:
Weekly Report — 2026-W07 (2026-02-09 ~ 2026-02-15) Intensive multi-project development week spanning spatial transcriptomics benchmarking, error recovery policy framework design, and developer tool enhancements. Completed PCA+UNI+STAIG fusion benchmarks across 7 DLPFC sections with enhanced metric visualizations, designed comprehensive 7-phase MIHD enhancement plan integrating Q-Former fusion and QueST-inspired features, expanded error recovery scene library by 293% (30→118 scenes) and fixed critical visualization bugs, implemented atomic writes and multi-device sync for gadget/summarize tool, refactored CalendarPro with recurring task support, and published historical reports to Hugo website. Key technical achievements include validating checkpoint recovery mechanisms in production, solving OSC controller compensation issues in robotic simulation, and establishing config-driven hyperparameter control patterns across multiple projects.
Weekly Overview Metric Value Date Range 2026-02-09 ~ 2026-02-15 Active Days 4 / 7 Total Conversations 30 Projects 9 Tasks Completed 32 Tasks In Progress 3 Total Tokens 67,986,026 Total Cost $45.94 Claude Code Token 65,389,292 Claude Code Cost $44.61 Codex Token 2,596,734 Codex Cost $1.33 Daily Average Cost $11.49 Project Progress MIHD Spatial Transcriptomics Benchmark (4 days active) — 🔄 active Accomplishments:
Enhanced visualizations with metrics overlay (ARI/NMI/Silhouette for clustering, Spearman/P@K for RM-Ideal) Completed PCA+UNI+STAIG fusion benchmarks on 7/11 DLPFC sections with quantified clustering performance Launched RM-Ideal niche query evaluation with WWL graph kernel across 7 sections Designed comprehensive 7-phase enhancement plan (~2,850 LOC) covering normalization, Q-Former/LLaVA fusion, QueST-inspired niche querying, and batch correction Successfully recovered Q-Former experiments from process crash using checkpoint mechanism (saved ~9 hours GPU time) Monitored long-running DCC cluster experiments: pca_uni2_qformer progressed 6/11→8/11, scgpt completed 65/77 Blockers:
⚠️ 4 sections failed due to data integrity issues: 151510/151672 (missing spatial coordinates), 151674 (OpenCV image error), 151676 (NaN/model collapse) ⚠️ Q-Former requires ~22GB GPU memory preventing parallel execution; 11 scgpt_uni2_qformer experiments failed with CUDA OOM during parallel runs ⚠️ Enhancement plan implementation blocked pending user review Error Recovery Benchmark (1 days active) — 🔄 active Accomplishments:
Fixed critical visualization bug: identified OSC controller compensation issue, implemented neutral actions during force injection+settling (90mm displacement validated) Expanded scene library from 30 to 118 scenes (293% increase) with mixed error types (impulse+friction+pose_perturb) Generated 60 visualization videos with 8-GPU parallelization covering all scenes and force levels Implemented BC policy loading from config and trajectory saving (compressed NPZ: actions/rewards/EEF/gripper/objects) Designed policy-based generation framework: 25 error types taxonomy (3 Family→10 Category→25 Type), PolicyAdapter abstraction with robomimic integration gadget/summarize Tool Enhancement (1 days active) — ✅ completed Accomplishments:
Implemented atomic writes at 5 locations to prevent JSON corruption (tempfile + os.replace pattern) Added LLM structured output support (Anthropic tool_use schema, OpenAI json_object format) to reduce parsing failures Implemented multi-device rclone sync workflow with subdirectory isolation (logs/, reports/) and –sync flag Added smart skip logic reading _merged_devices list to avoid redundant API calls for already-summarized devices Changed default behavior to export-only (no API call unless –summarize specified) Converted test/ git submodule to regular files, updated documentation CalendarPro Refactoring (1 days active) — ✅ completed Accomplishments:
Reduced code duplication: 8 AI provider selection instances→1 (provider_selector.py), 3 time parsing functions→1 (time_utils.py) Implemented periodic self-check with recurring tasks feature (RecurringTaskStore + PeriodicChecker with 15-minute cycle) Cleaned up codebase: deleted redundant files, merged test files, unified keyword/meal time definitions All 26+18 unit tests passing after refactoring Hugo Website Publishing (1 days active) — ✅ completed Accomplishments:
Published 3 historical daily reports (2026-02-12/13/14) to bugJournal section with generated Hugo YAML front matter Deployed to GitHub Pages with 139 pages built successfully Key Tasks ✅ Fix visualization video neutral action problem (2026-02-15) — Identified root cause (OSC controller compensating injected force when tracking demo trajectory), modified Phase 3 to use neutral actions during injection+settling window, verified with 90mm displacement ✅ Implement policy-based error scene generation framework (2026-02-15) — Created policy_adapter.py, extended rollout_generator.py with generate_from_policy(), integrated robomimic BCPolicy, all 41 unit tests pass ✅ Design 7-phase MIHD enhancement plan (2026-02-12) — Created comprehensive implementation plan covering normalization, Q-Former/LLaVA fusion, QueST niche querying, batch correction, and config overhaul with dependency graph 🚫 Implement enhancement plan phases 1-7 (2026-02-12) — User stopped implementation before execution to review the plan; implementation ready to proceed pending user approval 🔄 Monitor MIHD Q-Former benchmark progress (2026-02-15) — Monitored pca_uni2_qformer experiments (6/11→8/11), each section ~90 minutes with 50 epochs, estimated 3 hours remaining for last 3 sections ✅ Recover from Q-Former process crash (2026-02-15) — Detected crash at 6/11 sections, restarted pipeline which auto-detected completed sections and resumed from 151672, verified GPU idle before restart ✅ Implement JSON atomic write to prevent file corruption (2026-02-15) — Added _atomic_write() helper using tempfile + os.replace pattern at 5 file write points to ensure write interruption doesn’t cause half-written JSON ✅ Implement multi-device rclone sync workflow (2026-02-15) — Modified _rclone_upload with subdirectory parameter, added _rclone_download_logs(), merge subcommand supports –sync flag for one-command download+merge ✅ Expand error scene generation (30→118 scenes) (2026-02-15) — Modified detectors to generate mixed specs (impulse+friction+pose_perturb), configured max_scenes_per_demo 3→10, covered 10 demos 🔄 Design policy-driven error injection framework (2026-02-15) — Designed 25 error type classification (3 Family→10 Category→25 Type), dual-layer detection (rule-based + VLM), PolicyAdapter abstraction ✅ Run STAIG fusion benchmarks on all DLPFC sections (2026-02-09) — Execute benchmarks with PCA+UNI+STAIG fusion configuration on 11 DLPFC sections using –skip_cached to preserve existing results ✅ Execute scGPT benchmark experiments (2026-02-15) — Ran scGPT Phase 2 evaluation (77 experiments), completed 65/77 successfully with 12 failures (11 CUDA OOM, 1 NaN) ✅ Add LLM structured JSON output support (2026-02-15) — Configured Anthropic tool_use schema and OpenAI json_object format, kept _parse_json_response as fallback ✅ Implement export phase smart skip for already-summarized devices (2026-02-15) — Read _merged_devices list from existing file, check if current device in list to skip API call, changed to per-device dict format ✅ Batch generate 60 visualization videos (2026-02-15) — Created batch_visualize.py with 8-GPU parallel rendering, generated 30 baseline + 30 randomized videos covering all 30 scenes ✅ BC policy loading and trajectory saving functionality (2026-02-15) — Implemented _run_episode returning (summary, trajectory) tuple, _save_episode writing compressed NPZ with complete state ✅ Research Q-Former and QueST architectures (2026-02-12) — Deep-dive into BLIP-2 Q-Former vs LLaVA MLP connector, QueST’s GIN layers, batch correction, k-hop subgraph pooling ✅ Run PCA + UNI + STAIG fusion benchmark on remaining sections (2026-02-10) — Execute benchmarking on 11 tissue sections, leveraging cache for previously computed results ✅ CalendarPro codebase refactoring (2026-02-15) — Deleted redundant files, merged tests, extracted shared utilities (time_utils.py, provider_selector.py), unified constants, all 26 tests passing ✅ Implement CalendarPro periodic self-check and recurring tasks (2026-02-15) — Created RecurringTaskStore + PeriodicChecker (15-minute cycle), integrated with Discord bot, added /recurring commands, 18 new tests passing 🔄 Launch RM-Ideal evaluation across 7 sections (2026-02-09) — Run RM-Ideal niche query evaluation with WWL graph kernel for all embedding methods across available sections ✅ Change default behavior to export-only (2026-02-15) — Changed no-subcommand behavior from cmd_legacy to cmd_export, added –summarize flag (default False), ensuring no API call unless explicit ✅ Convert gadget test/ submodule to regular files (2026-02-15) — Removed git submodule tracking, cloned content, removed embedded .git, updated all documentation references ✅ Update README and tutorial documentation (2026-02-15) — Updated 3 docs with merge –sync usage examples, default behavior description, rclone_remote field, expanded cloud sync section ✅ Add metrics overlay to clustering visualizations (2026-02-09) — Refactor utils/visualization.py and run_benchmark.py to display ARI/NMI/Silhouette scores as text box below clustering plots ✅ Add metrics overlay to RM-Ideal visualizations (2026-02-09) — Add Spearman correlation, P@K, and SameLabel@K metrics to spatial heatmap visualizations ✅ Generate clustering visualizations with metric annotations (2026-02-10) — Save visualization outputs with ARI/NMI/Silhouette overlays for all successful runs ✅ Improve CLAUDE.md documentation (2026-02-12) — Eliminated redundancy, added missing components (GCN encoder, fusion strategies, test commands), reduced from 579 to 188 lines ✅ Fix ccusage output format parsing bug (2026-02-15) — Added type check for list format, wrap as {“daily”: data, “totals”: {}} to prevent AttributeError ✅ Add _source_device field to gadget export JSON (2026-02-15) — Append device metadata (platform, user, hostname) to export JSON for quick identification of unsummarized logs ✅ Smoke test and end-to-end verification (2026-02-15) — Ran complete test workflow: GPU check → 41 unit tests → 4-step smoke test → policy generation test Problems & Solutions 1. Visualization videos showed no visible error effects despite logged force application and body displacement (15mm-117mm) [Error Recovery Benchmark] (2026-02-15) Solution: Root cause: OSC position controller (kp=150) actively compensated external forces when tracking demo trajectory. Fix: use neutral actions (zero vector) during injection+settling phases, allowing force to accumulate without controller interference. Validated with 90mm displacement in test cases.
2. MuJoCo automatically clears xfrc_applied after each physics step, causing force effects to disappear [Error Recovery Benchmark] (2026-02-15) Solution: Re-apply force every control step during duration window (mimicking validation code drop.py), ensuring sustained force effect across all 20 physics substeps per control step
3. Q-Former process crashed after completing 6/11 sections, potentially losing ~9 hours of GPU compute work [MIHD] (2026-02-15) Solution: Pipeline’s checkpoint recovery mechanism (checking for existing *_embeddings.npz files) automatically detected completed sections and resumed from section 151672, validating the 断点续跑 architecture
4. JSON file writes interrupted by Ctrl+C/crash/power loss lead to half-written invalid JSON causing parse failures [gadget/summarize] (2026-02-15) Solution: Implemented _atomic_write() function using tempfile + os.replace pattern at 5 file write points, ensuring either old complete file or new complete file exists, never half-written state
5. Multi-device workflow required manually copying log files from each device to one machine for merge [gadget/summarize] (2026-02-15) Solution: Export uploads to /logs/, merge uploads to /reports/, added –sync flag to auto-download all device logs from remote, simplifying to one-command workflow
6. scgpt_uni2_qformer experiments failed with CUDA OOM (11/12 failures) - GPU had only 2.31 MiB free while pca_uni2_qformer used 21.46 GiB [MIHD] (2026-02-15) Solution: Identified Q-Former training requires ~22GB GPU memory; determined parallel Q-Former experiments cause OOM on 32GB GPU, requiring sequential execution despite logical independence
7. LLM occasionally returns invalid JSON (markdown code blocks, extra text), causing json.loads to fail [gadget/summarize] (2026-02-15) Solution: Prioritized SDK-level structured output: Anthropic tool_use with submit_report schema, OpenAI response_format: json_object, kept 3-layer fallback parsing as degradation path
8. Same device running export –summarize multiple times on same day repeatedly calls API, wasting cost [gadget/summarize] (2026-02-15) Solution: Added _merged_devices list in JSON to track already-summarized devices, read existing file on export to check if current device in list, skip API call if present and reuse existing summary
9. EGL rendering assertion failed: MUJOCO_EGL_DEVICE_ID must be contained in CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES [Error Recovery Benchmark] (2026-02-15) Solution: Fixed batch_visualize.py GPU assignment: use relative GPU indexing (MUJOCO_EGL_DEVICE_ID=0 always, adjust CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES per process)
10. ProximityDetector and CollisionDetector only generated impulse type specs, leading to single-type scene library (all 30 scenes were impulse/tip_over) [Error Recovery Benchmark] (2026-02-15) Solution: Modified both detectors’ detect() methods to use _generate_mixed_specs() generating impulse+pose_perturb+friction candidates simultaneously, increased max_scenes_per_demo to 10
11. Section 151676 consistently failed with ‘Input X contains NaN’ error across pca_uni_staig_fusion and scgpt_uni_staig_fusion [MIHD] (2026-02-15) Solution: Identified as section-specific data quality issue (missing spatial coordinates or corrupted expression values) causing STAIG model collapse, not a code bug
12. CalendarPro had 8 instances of duplicate AI provider selection logic scattered across different files [CalendarPro] (2026-02-15) Solution: Created src/ai/provider_selector.py with unified get_first_available_provider() and generate_with_fallback() functions, consolidated error handling and timeout logic in one place
13. Metrics overlay not appearing on plots despite modifying utils/visualization.py [MIHD] (2026-02-09) Solution: Discovered duplicate create_clustering_visualization() function at line 1644 in run_benchmark.py that shadowed the utils version - modified both functions
14. Risk of overwriting existing good results from pca_uni_staig_fusion directory [MIHD] (2026-02-09) Solution: Used –skip_cached flag instead of –override to preserve cached embeddings for completed sections
15. Parallel GPU tasks caused OOM kill (exit code 137) when running 3 STAIG fusion experiments simultaneously [MIHD] (2026-02-09) Solution: Switched to sequential execution by chaining commands with && in a single Bash call
16. MIHD has inconsistent embedding normalization - UNI2/scGPT are L2-normed but HIPT/ResNet50/MLP/GCN are not [MIHD] (2026-02-12) Solution: Designed 3-point normalization system (post-encoder, pre-fusion, post-fusion) with configurable methods (L2, standard scaling, none) via new utils/normalization.py module
17. User’s Idea 3 was ambiguous - ‘Q-Former like LLaVA’ but LLaVA uses simple MLP while Q-Former is from BLIP-2 [MIHD] (2026-02-12) Solution: Asked clarifying question; user chose to implement both (Q-Former with cross-attention + LLaVA’s 2-layer MLP) to compare lightweight vs heavy fusion
18. 4 out of 11 sections failed: 151510 and 151672 missing spatial coordinates, 151674 OpenCV image error, 151676 model collapse with NaN loss [MIHD] (2026-02-10) Solution: Documented failure modes and skipped problematic sections; preserved successful results via caching mechanism
Learnings Architecture (architecture) Error injection pipeline is already policy-agnostic at action level. Extending from demo injection to policy injection only requires action source replacement (demo_actions[step] → policy.predict(obs)); detection-injection-validation pipeline reuses as-is with no modifications needed. Atomic write pattern prevents file corruption: write to temp file, flush, os.replace for atomic rename, cleanup temp on failure. Ensures either old complete file or new complete file exists, never half-written state. Apply to all JSON/config file persistence. Multi-device collaboration tool design pattern: local generation → upload to cloud subdirectories → one-command download+merge. Encapsulate complex workflow into single command via –sync flag, reducing mental burden from ‘manual copying’ to ‘one command’. Checkpoint/resume mechanisms validate their design during actual crashes. File-existence checks (is_completed()) successfully prevented re-running expensive experiments (~9 hours GPU work for 6 sections), proving the recovery architecture works in production. Lazy loading with fallback requires transactional initialization: create object in local scope, validate all steps, only assign to instance variable after complete success. Partial success creates broken state. Code duplication threshold: when same pattern repeats 8+ times, extract into shared utility. Beyond reducing repetition, centralized error handling/timeout logic prevents inconsistent behavior. Single Source of Truth applies to logic patterns, not just data. Dependency analysis is critical for multi-phase plans: normalization must precede fusion experiments, config foundation enables all subsequent work, batch correction requires normalized fusion output. Document dependencies explicitly. Debugging (debugging) OSC position controllers (kp=150) generate joint torques that far exceed typical external forces (15-20N), actively compensating disturbances when tracking trajectories. Neutral actions disable tracking, allowing force effects to accumulate. Validation uses neutral actions; visualization must too. MuJoCo automatically zeros xfrc_applied after each physics step (0.002s default) to prevent accumulation. Must re-apply force every control step for sustained effect. Robosuite’s env.step() contains 20 physics substeps; OSC controller tracks target pose at every substep with kp=150 stiffness. In large codebases with modular utilities, always grep for all occurrences of a function name before modifying. Local copies can shadow imports and cause silent modification failures (e.g., run_benchmark.py shadowing utils/visualization.py). When same data section consistently fails across multiple model configurations with NaN/zero-variance symptoms, root cause is likely data quality issue (missing coordinates, corrupted values) not code bug. STAIG model collapse is known failure mode for problematic inputs. MuJoCo EGL uses relative GPU indexing within CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES, not absolute system GPU IDs. For single-GPU processes, always set MUJOCO_EGL_DEVICE_ID=0 regardless of which physical GPU (adjust CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES per process). Tools (tools) Cloud LLM APIs now support structured output - prioritize SDK features over fragile parsing. Use Anthropic tool_use with schema definition, OpenAI response_format: json_object. Keep 3-layer fallback parsing as degradation path for legacy/non-compliant responses. Idempotency design through persistent state avoids redundant operations. Track processed state (e.g., _merged_devices list), check on re-run, skip if already completed. Saves API cost and time while making each contributor’s work visible (per-device dict format). Experiment management requires explicit caching strategy: –override for full re-run, –skip_cached for incremental completion, no flags to fail on duplicates. Always check existing results before re-running experiments. Writing plans to .md files and pasting in new sessions effectively preserves context across sessions, more robust than depending on tool call chains. Plan text pattern overcomes session boundaries and context window limits. External tool output formats may change with version/environment. Defensive programming should check types and handle gracefully (e.g., ccusage returning list vs dict). Add type checks after parsing external tool JSON. Domain Knowledge (domain) Q-Former training requires ~22GB GPU memory. On 32GB GPU, parallel Q-Former experiments cause OOM despite being logically independent. Memory-intensive deep learning experiments require sequential execution even when compute graph is independent. Deep learning benchmarks with large spatial transcriptomics data (4000+ spots, 1024-dim embeddings, STAIG training) require exclusive GPU access. Parallelize only CPU-bound tasks like RM-Ideal WWL graph kernels. Detector design pattern: let high-frequency triggered detectors propose multi-type specs to avoid single type consuming max_scenes_per_demo quota. Extend ProximityDetector and CollisionDetector to generate mixed specs (impulse+pose_perturb+friction) like GraspPrecondDetector. STAIG fusion requires spatial coordinate validation and image integrity checks as preprocessing steps. Missing coordinates or corrupted images cause runtime failures. Robust pipelines should validate prerequisites and fail gracefully with clear error messages. Normalization inconsistency across encoders (UNI2/scGPT L2-normed, HIPT/ResNet50/MLP/GCN not) hurts downstream clustering. Systematic 3-point normalization (post-encoder, pre-fusion, post-fusion) with configurable methods provides fine-grained control. Trajectory data saving should include complete state: actions/rewards + EEF position + gripper state + all object positions. Use NPZ compressed storage (~64KB/episode), controlled by config flag (evaluation.save_trajectories). AI Usage Notes Effective Patterns:
✓ Plan-driven development with step-by-step execution: user provides comprehensive plans with context and file-by-file changes, AI executes methodically with test verification at each step ✓ Systematic root cause analysis through parallel investigation: AI analyzed error messages, identified GPU memory contention, proposed sequential execution strategy for Q-Former OOM failures ✓ Proactive status checking before recovery actions: verified GPU idle (1 MiB usage), counted completed sections (6/11), then restarted pipeline which auto-detected completions ✓ Comprehensive test coverage after refactoring: CalendarPro 26+18 tests, Error Recovery 41 unit tests all passing after major changes ✓ Cross-module code search for function definitions: used grep to find duplicate create_clustering_visualization() in run_benchmark.py after utils/ modification didn’t take effect Limitations:
✗ Failed to recognize OSC controller compensation as root cause of invisible force effects until explicitly guided via comparison to validation code using neutral actions ✗ Judged visualization fix successful based on log numerical values (15mm-117mm displacement) but ignored core goal of visible movement in video, only discovered layer-2 problem after user challenge ✗ Cannot bridge session boundaries without explicit artifacts (plans, files); relies on user to paste plans into new conversations when context is lost ✗ Initial policy injection framework design was overly complex (8-step plan), converged to simple action source replacement only after user’s rhetorical question ‘why can’t inject to VLA’ ✗ Had ambiguous understanding of natural language instruction ‘default should not merge’, required user’s second clarification to distinguish –sync behavior from main entry switch ✗ Used –override flag by default without checking if valuable existing results would be lost (pca_uni_staig_fusion folder) ✗ Attempted to run 3 GPU-intensive deep learning tasks in parallel without considering GPU memory constraints, leading to OOM kill ✗ First approach to batch GPU assignment used incorrect EGL device ID mapping (didn’t understand relative vs absolute indexing), needed correction after error Next Week Outlook Continue MIHD Q-Former experiments (3 remaining pca sections + 11 scgpt retries after GPU becomes available), then proceed with 7-phase enhancement plan implementation pending user approval (~2,850 LOC across normalization/fusion/QueST features). For Error Recovery Benchmark, implement the designed policy-based generation framework integrating VLA/Pi0 policies with the existing detection-injection-validation pipeline. Address GPU memory management strategies to prevent OOM in parallel deep learning experiments. Fix remaining MIHD data integrity issues for sections 151510/151672/151674/151676 (missing coordinates, image errors). Monitor long-running DCC cluster experiments with checkpoint recovery validated in production. Consider Hugo website automation for daily report publishing workflow given successful manual deployment this week.
Token Usage Statistics Total: $45.94 — 68.0M tokens
Peak Day: 2026-02-15 — $32.59 / 49.7M tokens
Daily Average: $11.49
