Completed PCA+UNI+STAIG fusion benchmarks across 7 DLPFC sections with enhanced metric visualizations, designed comprehensive 7-phase MIHD enhancement plan integrating Q-Former fusion and QueST-inspired features, expanded error recovery scene library by 293% (30→118 scenes) and fixed critical visualization bugs, implemented atomic writes and multi-device sync for gadget/summarize tool, refactored CalendarPro with recurring task support, and published historical reports to Hugo website. Key technical achievements include validating checkpoint recovery mechanisms in production, solving OSC controller compensation issues in robotic simulation, and establishing config-driven hyperparameter control patterns across multiple projects.
title: Weekly Summary 2026-W07

content:
Daily Report — 2026-02-15 Daily Overview Completed Work: Parallel development in four main areas: (1) Deploying the Hugo website publishing system to generate daily reports; (2) Monitoring MIHD Q-Former and scGPT benchmark tests on GPU nodes in the DCC cluster; (3) Refactoring the CalendarPro code library and implementing regular self-checks; (4) Improving the robustness of the gadget/summarize tool, enabling atomic writing, structured LLM output, and multi-device rclone synchronization capabilities; (5) Expanding the error recovery benchmark scenarios from 30 to 118, using a policy-based generation framework. Methodology: Plan-driven development with systematic implementation: reading existing code, writing detailed plans with line numbers, executing modifications, conducting comprehensive testing (26+18+41 unit tests passed), deploying via automated scripts (Hugo’s update.sh script for multi-device synchronization with rclone), monitoring long-running GPU experiments, and using checkpoint recovery mechanisms. Impact: Established a complete workflow from AI conversation summaries to public websites; ensured expensive GPU experiments could continue without data loss in case of process crashes; reduced code duplication in CalendarPro (8 provider selection instances reduced to 1, 3 time parsing functions reduced to 1); eliminated the risk of JSON corruption in the gadget tool; simplified multi-device workflows from manual file copying to a single –sync command; expanded the benchmark scenario library by 293% through a policy-based injection framework. DCC Completed Work: Monitoring MIHD benchmark experiments: pca_uni2_qformer recovered from a crash on June 11, restarted until August 11; scgpt evaluation (65 successes, 12 failures: 11 due to GPU competition causing insufficient CUDA memory, 1 due to data issues with NaN values in section 151676). Methodology: Checking experiment progress via log files and NPZ files; detecting Q-Former process crashes, verifying GPU idle status (using 1 MiB of memory), restarting processes, automatically detecting completed sections and continuing from 151672; analyzing causes of failures (GPU memory conflicts between parallel Q-Former runs, data quality issues in specific sections). Impact: Verified the breakpoint resume mechanism in production environments—avoiding rerunning 6 completed sections (about 9 hours of GPU time); identified that Q-Former consumed approximately 22GB of GPU resources, preventing parallel execution; total progress was 240/286 experiments (about 84%), with 13.5 hours remaining for 10 Q-Former sections. MacBook Completed Work: Published three historical daily reports (February 12: MIHD enhancement design, February 13: seven-stage implementation, February 14: two-stage pipeline architecture) to the Hugo bugJournal section and deployed them on GitHub Pages. Methodology: Reading the report Markdown files in the gadget/summarize/reports/ section, extracting summaries from block citation lines, generating Hugo YAML headers (title/dates plus -05:00 timezone/keywords/summary/draft fields), writing them to content/bugJournal/, and executing the update.sh script (Hugo build + public upload to GitHub Pages). Impact: Hugo successfully built 139 pages of content; three new bugJournal entries are now available in the https://tzj2006.github.io/bugJournal/;找到，为发布AI对话摘要建立了标准工作流程；确认了网站目录的部署模式（公共子目录作为GitHub Pages repository, with source files in a separate repository. TzJsDesktop Completed Work: Dual-track work: (1) Refactoring the CalendarPro code library (deleting redundant files, merging tests, extracting shared utilities for time parsing and AI provider selection, unifying keyword/meal time definitions) + regular self-checks and repetitive tasks; (2) Improving the gadget/summarize tool (atomic writing, structured LLM output, multi-device rclone synchronization, intelligent skipping logic, ccusage fixes, device marking, converting tests/submodalities to regular files). Methodology: CalendarPro: Creating new utility modules (timeUtils.py, provider_selector.py), updating import statements for more than 8 files, running tests (26/26 passed); implementing RecurringTaskStore and PeriodicChecker for background scheduling. Gadget: Modifying daily_summary.py to use atomic writing at 5 locations, configuring Anthropic tool usage patterns and OpenAI json_object format, adding rclone subdirectory parameters, implementing intelligent skipping logic (reading _merged_devices list, checking current device, skipping API if already merged), packaging ccusage list output, performing git operations for submodality removal, updating README/CLAUDE.md/tutorial.md. Impact: CalendarPro: 26/26 tests passed; reduced code duplication (8 AI provider selection instances reduced to 1, 3 time parsing functions reduced to 1); implemented automatic meal/sleep scheduling and user-defined repetitive tasks (e.g., daily LeetCode), with background checks every 15 minutes. Gadget: Eliminated JSON corruption risks (atomic writing ensures either a complete old file or a complete new file, never a half-written state); reduced API costs (intelligent skipping avoided repeated summarization); simplified multi-device workflows from manual file copying to a single merge --sync command; reduced LLM JSON parsing failures through SDK-level structured output; simplified repository structure (no submodalities). tianhe Completed Work: Main development of error recovery benchmark tests: (1) Batch visualization (60 videos, 8-GPU parallelization); (2) Fixing visualization bugs (taking neutral actions during forced injection); (3) Scene generation expansion (from 30 scenes to 118 scenes, including various error types); (4) BC policy loading and trajectory saving; (5) Policy-based generation framework design (classification of 25 error types, rule-based classifier + VLM dual-layer architecture, PolicyAdapter abstraction). Methodology: Creating batch_visualize.py with GPU parallelization functionality (fixing EGL device ID relative indexing issues), generating 30 baseline videos and 30 randomized videos; modifying the third phase to use neutral actions during injection and stabilization periods (adding –settle_steps parameter), verifying with 3 test cases, showing 90 mm displacement; modifying ProximityDetector and CollisionDetector to generate mixed specifications (impulse, friction, posture perturbation), increasing max_scenes_per_demo from 3 to 10; implementing run_episode to return (summary, trajectory) tuples, _save_episode to write compressed NPZ files (action/reward/EEF position/clamp/object), 3_collect_data.py loads BC checkpoints from configuration; designing a 543-line Chinese plan with 3 families → 10 categories → 25 error types classification, rule-based classifier (state threshold), VLM analysis layer (video + natural language explanation), combined with robomimic’s PolicyAdapter pattern. Impact: Visualization process verified (60 videos covering 30 scenes × 3 demonstrations × power levels); identified and fixed the root cause of visualization bugs (neutral actions during interference eliminated OSC controller compensation); scene library expanded by 293% (from 30 to 118 scenes, including various error types); BC policy integration completed for evaluation processes; policy-based generation framework designed for future VLA/Pi0 integration, verifying that the existing detection-injection-validation process is independent of policies at the action level > Multi-project development day: publishing historical daily reports on the Hugo website, monitoring long-term MIHD benchmark experiments on the DCC cluster, completing the refactoring of the CalendarPro code library, including regular tasks, improving the gadgets/summary tools, supporting atomic writing and multi-device synchronization, enhancing error recovery benchmark testing with 118 scenes and policy-based generation framework. Tasks Architecture and Strategy ✅ Fixing visual video neutral action issues — Identified the root cause (OSC controller compensating for injection force while tracking demonstration trajectories), modified the third phase to use neutral actions during injection and stabilization periods, added –settle_steps parameter, verified with 3 test cases, showing displacement of 90 mm. ✅ Implementing policy-based error scenario generation framework — Created policy_adapter.py (230 lines of code), extended rollout_generator.py, added generate_from_policy function, replaced BCPolicy stub with robomimic integration, updated database deduplication logic, added CLI script and Makefile targets; all 41 unit tests passed. 🔄 Monitoring MIHD Q-Former benchmark progress — Monitored pca_uni2_qformer experiment (restarted at 15:55 after process stopped at around 15:18, completed 8/11 experiments by 18:00), each part took about 90 minutes, including 50 cycles, with the last 3 parts expected to take another 3 hours. ✅ Restoring from Q-Former process crashes — Detected Q-Former pipeline crash (process stopped, completed 6/11 parts), automatically detected completed parts after restarting the pipeline, continuing from 151672 (remaining 5 parts), confirmed GPU was idle before restart (using 1 MiB). ✅ Implementing JSON atomic writing to prevent file corruption — Added atomic_write() helper function, using tempfile and os.replace modes, replaced 5 file writing points (exporting logs, saving Markdown/JSON reports, publishing to Hugo, configuration), ensuring interrupted writes did not result in incomplete JSON data. ✅ Implementing multi-device rclone synchronization workflow — Modified _rcloneUpload function, adding subdirectory parameters (exporting to logs/, merging to reports/), added _rclone_download_logs() function for remote log downloads, merging commands support –sync flag, enabling one-click download and merge. ✅ Expanding error scenario generation capabilities (from 30 scenes to 118 scenes) — Modified ProximityDetector and CollisionDetector to generate mixed specification scenes (including impulse, friction, posture perturbation), increased max_scenes_per_demo from 3 to 10, covering 10 demonstration scenes, generating 100 new scenes. 🔄 Designing policy-based error injection framework (25 error type classifications) — Designed a classification system for 25 error types (3 families: manipulation/planning/progression → 10 categories → 25 types), using a dual-layer detection architecture (rule-based classifier + VLM analysis), implemented PolicyAdapter abstraction layer (supporting Random/BC-RNN/VLA), studied availability of MimicGen/Pi0 checkpoints. ✅ Performing scGPT benchmark experiments — Conducted scGPT second-phase evaluation (planned 77 experiments), successfully completed 65, with 12 failures (1 due to insufficient CUDA memory in scgpt_uni2_qformer, another due to NaN errors in scgpt_uni_staig_fusion/151676). ✅ Adding LLM structured JSON output support — Configured Anthropic API, using tool_use to generate structured JSON (defined submit_report tool structure), added OpenAI response_format: json_object parameter, retained parse_json_response as alternative. ✅ Implementing intelligent skipping for exported devices — Calculated paths before calling summarize, read _merged_devices list from existing files, checked if current device was in list to decide whether to skip API call, changed device summary to per-device dictionary format ({device_name: summary}), updated _merged_devices and_source_device fields. ✅ Generating 60 visual videos for error benchmarks — Created scripts/batch_visualize.py, using 8-GPU parallel rendering (fixed EGL device ID relative indexing issue); generated 30 baseline videos and 30 randomized parameter videos, covering 3 demonstration scenes and all 30 scenes at 3N/15N/20N levels. ✅ BC policy loading and trajectory saving functions — Implemented run_episode to return (EpisodeSummary, trajectory) tuples, _save_episode to write compressed NPZ files (action/reward/EEF position/clamp/off object), modified 3_collect_data.py to load BC checkpoints from configuration. ✅ CalendarPro code library refactoring — Deleted redundant files (check_add.py, check_methods.py, nul, gemini.bat, ai_planner directory, 4 duplicate Claude test files), merged test code into test_claude_provider.py, extracted shared utilities (time_utils.py, provider_selector.py), unified keyword constants and meal time definitions across modules, all 26 tests passed. ✅ Implementing CalendarPro periodic self-checks and regular tasks — Created RecurringTaskStore (JSONL-based custom regular task storage) and PeriodicChecker (background asynchronous loop running every 15 minutes), integrated with Discord bot, added commands (/recurring add/remove/pause/resume/list), implemented automatic meal and sleep scheduling, all 18 new tests passed. ✅ Changing default behavior to export only — Changed no-subcommand behavior from cmd_legacy to cmd_export, added –summarize flag in top parser (default False), ensuring API calls are not triggered unless –summarize is specified. ✅ Converting gadgets tests/submodalities to regular files — Removed git submodality tracking (git rm –cached test), cloned TzJ2006/test content, removed embedded.git files, updated all document references (README.md, CLAUDE.md) to separate from the standalone repository. Implementation and Fixes - ✅ Updating README and Tutorial Documents — Updated 3 documents (gadget/README.md, summarize/README.md, summarize/tutorial.md): added examples of using –sync, updated default behavior descriptions, modified description of rclone_remote field, expanded cloud synchronization section, updated quick command reference.### Smoke Testing and End-to-End Verification The complete testing process was completed: GPU checks → 41 unit tests → 4-step smoke testing (scene generation/analysis rejection/data collection/result analysis) → policy generation testing (random policy deployment twice → 1 scenario).### Key Learnings
It was only through comparing the verification code with neutral actions that it was recognized that controller compensation was the root cause of the invisible force effect. Visual repairs were judged based on log values, but the core objective of visualization was to see visible movements in videos. Only after a user’s challenge did the second level of problem (demonstration actions + OSC controller compensation) become apparent. Without clear markings, it was impossible to cross session boundaries (plans, files). Users needed to paste their plans into new conversations when context was lost. The initial BCPolicy rollback implementation lacked transactional nature (adapters were assigned before starting episode validation). Testing and fixing were required iteratively. When designing the policy injection framework, complexity was initially preferred (8-step plan, creating multiple new files). It was only after the user asked “why can’t it be injected into VLA” that it was realized that the core was simply replacing the action source, and the existing process was no longer related to the policy.### Summary of Dialogues Mini Tool (Daily Report Publishing Tool) ✅ Implemented the report publishing plan and deployed it on GitHub Pages 05:16:45.089 | claude_code The user provided the complete plan text in a new session, requesting immediate implementation. content:
Daily Report — 2026-02-12 Daily Overview Completed Work: Optimized the MIHD’s CLAUDE.md documentation and designed a multi-stage plan, including embedding standardization, Q-Former/LLaVA fusion strategy, and specific queries and batch corrections inspired by QueST. How It Was Done: Explored both MIHD and QueST code bases, studied the architecture of Q-Former, broken down 6 user ideas into interdependent implementation phases, and defined the scope through user questions. Impact: The documentation was reduced from 579 lines to 188 lines, with additional missing content added. An implementable plan was developed, process inconsistencies were resolved, and 4 new features were added (estimated to add about 2,850 lines of code), achieving fully configurable hyperparameter control. Improved the MIHD documentation and designed a comprehensive 7-stage enhancement plan, covering standardization, Q-Former fusion, and QueST-inspired functions.
Tasks Architecture and Strategy ✅ Designed a 7-stage MIHD enhancement plan — Created a comprehensive plan covering standardization, Q-Former/LLaVA fusion, QueST-specific queries, batch corrections, and configuration adjustments, with a dependency diagram. ❌ Implemented stages 1 to 7 of the enhancement plan — Users stopped implementation before proceeding to review the plan. Implementation is ready for approval. ✅ Studied the architecture of Q-Former and QueST — In-depth study of BLIP-2 Q-Former and LLaVA MLP connectors, QueST’s GIN layer, batch corrections through adversarial training, and specific queries using k-hop subgraph pooling. Implementation and Fixes ✅ Improved the CLAUDE.md documentation — Analyzed the existing CLAUDE.md file, removed redundant content, and added missing components (GCN encoder, fusion strategy, test commands, utils module). The number of lines was reduced from 579 to 188. Problems and Solutions Key Issues 1. MIHD’s embedding standardization inconsistency - UNI2/scGPT uses L2 norm, while HIPT/ResNet50/MLP/GCN do not, and no standardization after fusion. Solution: Designed a three-point standardization system (after encoding, before fusion, after fusion) and can configure different methods (L2, standard scaling, none) through the new utils/normalization.py module. Key Insight: Inconsistent standardization between encoders can affect downstream clustering results; the three-point method provides fine-grained control.
2. QueST uses adversarial training for batch corrections, but its effectiveness in spatial transcriptomics is unclear. Solution: Designed a plug-in BatchCorrection module, following QueST’s discriminator/generator pattern, and set evaluation metrics to verify its effectiveness. Key Insight: Porting batch corrections to the MIHD environment requires empirical validation, rather than assuming its applicability.
General Issues 3. User idea 3 is not clear enough - “Q-Former similar to LLaVA”, but LLaVA uses simple MLP, while Q-Former comes from BLIP-2. Solution: Proposed clarifying questions; users chose to implement both (Q-Former with cross-attention mechanism and 2-layer MLP of LLaVA) to compare lightweight and heavyweight fusion effects. Key Insight: Clarifying architectural ambiguity in advance can avoid wasting implementation effort on the wrong direction.
Human-AI Methods Implementation Level Scope of QueST ideas implementation Role Method Human Clearly decided to implement QueST-inspired functions in MIHD, rather than just understanding QueST methods AI Initially assumed the task was research or understanding, later needed clarification Difference Analysis: Humans clearly defined the implementation intent from the start; AI was initially in an exploratory research mode until it was corrected.
Fusion strategy between Q-Former and LLaVA MLP Role Method Human When faced with choices, users decided to implement both to compare lightweight and heavyweight fusion effects AI AI recognized the architectural differences and asked users to choose one Difference Analysis: Humans found comparative evaluation valuable; AI initially sought a single decision.
When to start implementation Role Method Human Users stopped the assistant’s work before starting implementation, wanting to review the plan first AI AI prepared to start implementation immediately after creating the plan Difference Analysis: Humans prioritized reviewing the plan; AI assumed it could continue only after approval.
Limitations of AI General Limitations AI did not actively clarify scope and architecture before exploring extensively (Q-Former vs LLaVA MLP, research vs implementation), wasting potential human resources on unnecessary research. AI assumed users would need to implement immediately after creating the plan, without confirming user consent, requiring user intervention to stop. Lessons Learned Key Lessons For complex multi-project plans, scope and architecture choices should be clarified before deep exploration — ask “both or one,” “understanding or implementation,” and “partial process or complete process” in advance. Dependency analysis is crucial for multi-stage plans — standardization must be done before fusion experiments, the configuration base allows smooth progress, and QueST’s batch corrections require standardized fusion outputs. Document improvement is important: MIHD’s CLAUDE.md was reduced by 67%, while adding missing content by removing redundancy, discovering information, and recording implementation history. Dialogue Summary 🔄 Designed a 7-stage enhancement plan, covering standardization, Q-Former, and QueST integration 17:34:50.362 | claude_code Users requested implementation plans for 6 ideas (embedding standardization, UNI2+scGPT experiment, Q-Former fusion, QueST-specific queries, batch corrections, configuration adjustments). The assistant explored both MIHD and QueST code bases, studied the architecture of Q-Former, proposed clarifying questions (users chose to implement Q-Former and LLaVA MLP simultaneously in MIHD, with a complete configuration process), and designed a comprehensive 7-stage plan with a dependency diagram (estimated to add about 2,850 lines of code). Users stopped the assistant’s work before starting implementation to review the plan.
✅ Improved the CLAUDE.md documentation 17:12:36.312 | claude_code Users called the/init skill to improve the CLAUDE.md documentation. The assistant explored the code base structure, analyzed the existing documentation (579 lines, with a lot of redundant content), and generated a streamlined version (188 lines). Added missing components (GCN encoder, test instructions, utils module), removed redundant content (intelligent run command appeared twice, s squidpy warning appeared three times), and deleted less valuable parts (file organization, implementation history, detailed examples).

title: Bug Journal 2026-02-10

content:
Daily Report — 2026-02-09 Daily Overview Completed Work: Modified the MIHD visualization process to display clustering metrics (ARI, NMI, Silhouette) and RM-Ideal metrics (Spearman, P@K) directly on the chart. Benchmarks were conducted in 7 DLPFC regions. Implementation Method: Reorganized the visualization functionality using matplotlib subplots with text overlay. Switched from parallel execution to sequential execution to handle GPU memory limitations. --skip_cached was used to preserve existing results. Impact: The visualization function now allows for immediate quantitative evaluation without needing to view the console. The optimal configuration (PCA+UNI+STAIG fusion) was identified, and a comprehensive RM-Ideal benchmark baseline was established. The MIHD benchmark visualization function has been enhanced with metric overlay capabilities. Comprehensive RM-Ideal evaluations are available in 7 DLPFC regions.
Tasks Architecture and Strategy ✅ Run STAIG fusion benchmarks in all DLPFC regions — Executed benchmarks for PCA+UNI+STAIG fusion configurations in 11 DLPFC regions using --skip_cached to preserve existing results. 🔄 Perform RM-Ideal evaluations in 7 regions — Conducted RM-Ideal niche query evaluations for embedding methods in all available regions using the WWL graph kernel. Implementation and Fixes ✅ Add metric overlay to clustering visualizations — Reorganized utils/visualization.py and run_benchmark.py to display ARI/NMI/Silhouette scores below the clustering graph. ✅ Add metric overlay to RM-Ideal visualizations — Added Spearman correlation, P@K, and SameLabel@K metrics to the heatmap visualization in evaluate_rm_ideal.py. Issues and Solutions Key Issues Despite modifying utils/visualization.py, metric overlay still does not appear on the chart. Solution: It was found that there is a duplicate create_clustering_visualization function on line 1644 of run_benchmark.py, covering the utils version. Both versions were modified accordingly.
Key Insight: In a large code base, it’s necessary to grep all files for function definitions to determine whether a single modification is effective.
There is a risk of overwriting existing good results in the pca_uni_staig_fusion directory. Solution: Use --skip_cached instead of --override to preserve cached embeddings from completed regions.
Key Insight: Before running experiments again, it’s essential to check existing results. Use the cache flag to distinguish between incremental completion and full reruns.
General Issues When running 3 STAIG fusion experiments simultaneously, OOM termination occurred due to parallel GPU tasks (exit code 137). Solution: Switch to sequential execution by using the && command in a single Bash call.
Key Insight: In deep learning benchmarks involving large amounts of spatial transcriptomics data, exclusive GPU access is required. Parallelization should only be performed on CPU-intensive tasks.
Regions 151510, 151672, and 151674 failed due to missing spatial coordinates or OpenCV errors. Solution: The script continued processing the remaining regions. These were identified as data integrity issues that required separate investigation.
Key Insight: A robust benchmark process should validate data prerequisites (spatial coordinates, image files) and gracefully fail when duplicates are encountered, providing clear error messages.
Human-AI Approaches Strategic Level Preserve existing good results. Role Method Human The human explicitly stated that there is a folder named pca_uni_staig_fusion containing very good results, and advised not to overwrite them. AI The AI initially ran with the --override flag, which would overwrite existing results. Difference Analysis: Humans knew which results were valuable and should be preserved. The AI reran everything without checking the existing work.
Detect duplicate function definitions. Role Method Human The human noticed that the metrics did not appear and suggested investigating why the modification didn’t work. AI The AI modified utils/visualization.py, but did not check if there were other functions with the same name in the code base. Difference Analysis: Humans’ domain knowledge helped identify that run_benchmark.py might have its own local copy. The AI relied on grep after the fact, rather than actively checking for shadow functions.
Implementation Level Identify the best configuration. Role Method Human The human asked to check the logs and run the exact same code to get the highest ARI and NMI scores. AI The AI systematically checked all summary CSVs and experiment logs to determine that uni_staig_fusion was the best configuration, followed by pca_uni_staig_fusion. Difference Analysis: Humans provided clear instructions, using metrics as selection criteria. The AI performed systematic comparisons and correctly identified pca_uni_staig_fusion after being clarified by humans.
AI Limitations Key Limitations Failed to detect duplicate function definitions. Modifications were made to utils/visualization.py, but run_benchmark.py was not checked for local copies, resulting in overwriting. The --override flag was used by default, without checking whether valuable existing results would be lost. General Limitations Attempted to run 3 GPU-intensive deep learning tasks in parallel, leading to OOM termination due to GPU memory limitations. Lessons Learned Key Lessons In a large code base with modular utility tools, always use grep to find all occurrences of function names. Local copies can overwrite imports, causing silent modifications to fail. In deep learning benchmarks involving spatial transcriptomics data (more than 4000 points, 1024-dimensional embeddings, STAIG training), exclusive GPU access is required. Parallelization should only be performed on CPU-intensive tasks like RM-Ideal WWL graph kernels. Experiment management requires clear caching strategies: use --override for full reruns, --skip_cached for incremental completion, and avoid using any flags to fail on duplicate items. Practical Experience Visualization refactoring pattern: Explicitly creating fig/axes makes it possible to add custom annotations (metric text overlay). However, Scanspy’s save= parameter does not support this. Conversation Summary 🔄 Run STAIG fusion benchmarks in all DLPFC regions 21:50:31.276 | claude_code The user requested to run benchmarks for STAIG fusion + UNI2 + GCN/PCA/None in all 12 DLPFC regions. Initial parallel execution led to OOM. Switching to sequential execution, but encountered data issues (missing spatial coordinates). The user explicitly requested to use the pca_uni_staig_fusion configuration with the highest ARI/NMI scores. --skip_cached was used to preserve existing results. Seven regions were successfully completed (four failed due to data issues). 🔄 Start RM-Ideal evaluation benchmarks in all sections 22:57:20.481 | claude_code The user requested to run RM-Ideal niche query evaluations on slides containing available embeddings. Seven parallel evaluation tasks were initiated (one per section), using the WWL graph kernel to calculate spatial segmentation matching scores. Each section took about 25 points per second, involving seven segmentation labels. The evaluations for five sections were completed within minutes, while two sections are still ongoing due to the use of multiple embedding methods.
✅ Add metric layers for clustering and RM-Ideal visualizations 21:46:17.845 | claude_code The user requested to overlay ARI/NMI scores on the clustering graph and p-values on the RM-Ideal heatmap. This was achieved by restructuring the visualization functions to use explicit matplotlib subplots with text annotations. content:
Weekly Report — 2026-W06 (2026-02-02 ~ 2026-02-08) Intensive week on MIHD spatial transcriptomics: discovered and fixed critical GCN+STAIG fusion bugs (6 issues including algorithmic alignment and hyperparameter mismatches causing 9x performance degradation), implemented RM-Ideal ground-truth evaluation framework, built public benchmark submission pipeline with ethical consent practices. Major learning: implementation bugs vs tuning issues require reference code comparison first.
Weekly Overview Metric Value Date Range 2026-02-02 ~ 2026-02-08 Active Days 5 / 7 Total Conversations 15 Projects 4 Tasks Completed 27 Tasks In Progress 1 Total Tokens 158,188,666 Total Cost $62.39 Claude Code Token 51,674,218 Claude Code Cost $27.00 Codex Token 106,514,448 Codex Cost $35.39 Daily Average Cost $12.48 Project Progress MIHD Spatial Transcriptomics (4 days active) — 🔄 active Accomplishments:
Implemented end-to-end GCN+STAIG fusion system with flexible architecture supporting multiple gene encoders (PCA/MLP/GCN/none) Discovered and fixed 4 critical algorithmic differences (adaptive edge dropout, feature clipping, edge weight direction, tau decay) by comparing with reference implementation Diagnosed hyperparameter mismatches causing ARI degradation from 0.452 to 0.05 (learning_rate, num_layers, num_epochs, weight_decay) Integrated RM-Ideal (Wasserstein Weisfeiler-Lehman) evaluation into MIHD for ground-truth spatial niche matching Fixed experiments_log.md documentation from 14 to 36 complete entries, regenerated all visualizations Achieved Spearman r=0.424 between embedding similarity and RM-Ideal ground truth on DLPFC section 151508 Blockers:
⚠️ Hyperparameter corrections not yet applied - expected to improve GCN+STAIG from ARI 0.05-0.09 to 0.35-0.45 ⚠️ End-to-end GCN training design documented but implementation incomplete Benchmark Infrastructure (1 days active) — ✅ completed Accomplishments:
Built complete public submission pipeline with relay-based ingestion (untrusted users → validation gateway → protected storage) Implemented interactive opt-in consent flow with explicit –upload/–no-upload flags Fixed inverted bar chart rendering bug via list serialization and CDN version pinning Created three-workflow GitHub Actions system (queue/ingest/deploy) with daily automated publishing Key Tasks ✅ Fix end-to-end STAIG fusion flexibility issues (2026-02-05) — Fixed apply_fusion() missing **kwargs causing NameError, added gene_preprocessing parameter supporting 4 modes (auto/standard/clip/none), implemented dual input mode for raw expression and pre-encoded embeddings ✅ Analyze GCN+STAIG poor performance and compare with original STAIG (2026-02-05) — Discovered 4 critical algorithmic differences and hyperparameter mismatches causing ARI drop from 0.452 to 0.05 via reference code comparison ✅ MIHD vs original STAIG hyperparameter diagnosis (2026-02-05) — Identified critical mismatches: learning_rate (0.001 vs 0.0005), num_layers (2 vs 1), num_epochs (550 vs 300), weight_decay differs by 10x ✅ Implement 4 STAIG alignment fixes (2026-02-05) — Added adaptive edge dropout, gene feature clipping, fixed edge weight calculation direction, and tau decay mechanism to STAIGTrainer.py ✅ Design GitHub Pages auto-update pipeline (2026-02-06) — Built three-workflow system with validation/sanitization scripts, hash-based deduplication, and privacy protections for crowdsourced benchmark leaderboard ✅ Implement RM-Ideal core functions in QueST (2026-02-08) — Added wwl_node_features(), _wasserstein_distance(), rm_score(), compute_rm_ideal_scores() to QueST src/utils.py ✅ Build MIHD RM-Ideal evaluation framework (2026-02-08) — Created RMIdealEvaluator class with caching, extended spatial_utils.py with grid connectivity and BFS k-hop, built CLI with niche query support ✅ Implement end-to-end GCN + STAIG Fusion training system (2026-02-05) — Created STAIGTrainerE2E.py with EndToEndSTAIGModel supporting joint gene-vision optimization 🔄 Design GCN end-to-end training approach (2026-02-05) — Compared with original STAIG project showing GCN embeddings trained end-to-end, created detailed design document but code not yet implemented ✅ Add interactive upload consent flow to benchmark CLI (2026-02-06) — Integrated opt-in prompts (default No), added explicit flags, implemented relay endpoint submission, ensured non-interactive runs require explicit flags ✅ Run and visualize evaluation on DLPFC section 151508 (2026-02-08) — Evaluated uni_staig_fusion embedding on Layer_3 niche, generated 3-panel spatial heatmap, achieved Spearman r=0.424 ✅ Fix inverted bar chart rendering bug (2026-02-06) — Resolved bar length inversion by converting Pandas Series to plain lists, pinning Plotly JS version, forcing numeric conversion, setting y-axis to zero ✅ Diagnose UNI2 loading issues (2026-02-05) — Fixed position encoding resampling error on GPU by passing complete timm_kwargs for proper model initialization ✅ Debug SameLabel metric in MIHD evaluation (2026-02-08) — Fixed bug where SameLabel@K was always 0 by separating eval_mask for Precision@K from all_valid_mask for SameLabel@K ✅ Update experiments_log.md (2026-02-03) — Rebuilt complete experiment log from CSV with all 36 experiments, including top 10 results table and detailed metadata ✅ Add GCN encoder support to benchmark script (2026-02-05) — Extended run_benchmark.py argparse and run_evaluation() to support GCN gene encoder with spatial graph construction ✅ General environment dependency check and repair (2026-02-05) — Iteratively installed missing packages for General environment to run GCN + UNI2 + STAIG_fusion, achieved 70-80% readiness Problems & Solutions 1. GCN+STAIG fusion performance extremely poor (ARI 0.05-0.09) compared to PCA+STAIG (ARI 0.45) [MIHD Spatial Transcriptomics] (2026-02-05) Solution: Two-phase diagnosis: (1) Compared with original STAIG discovering 4 algorithmic differences (edge dropout, feature clipping, edge weight direction, tau decay), (2) Analyzed original config revealing hyperparameter mismatches (learning_rate, num_layers, num_epochs, weight_decay)
2. apply_fusion() missing **kwargs parameter caused NameError preventing staig_fusion_e2e execution [MIHD Spatial Transcriptomics] (2026-02-05) Solution: Added **kwargs to function signature to accept dynamically passed parameters like gene_expr_raw
3. STAIGTrainerE2E hardcoded assumption that input is raw gene expression (3000-dim), incompatible with PCA/MLP/GCN encoder outputs [MIHD Spatial Transcriptomics] (2026-02-05) Solution: Added gene_preprocessing parameter (auto/standard/clip/none) to automatically select preprocessing strategy based on input dimensionality and source
4. Adaptive edge dropout implementation differs from PyG’s standard dropout_adj - requires custom logic based on edge probabilities [MIHD Spatial Transcriptomics] (2026-02-05) Solution: Implemented custom adaptive_dropout_adj() where mask = rand() >= edge_prob, following original STAIG’s counter-intuitive strategy (drop similar edges, keep dissimilar)
5. experiments_log.md severely incomplete (14 entries vs 36 actual experiments), missing best results (ARI 0.528) [MIHD Spatial Transcriptomics] (2026-02-03) Solution: Regenerated entire log from experiment_comparison.csv as source of truth, ensuring all 36 experiments documented
6. Public users cannot push to repository but need to contribute benchmark results [Benchmark Infrastructure] (2026-02-06) Solution: Designed three-tier architecture: users submit to relay endpoint → relay validates and forwards to repository_dispatch → GitHub Actions queue/ingest/publish
7. Bar charts rendered with inverse lengths (higher scores showed shorter bars) despite correct CSV values [Benchmark Infrastructure] (2026-02-06) Solution: Fixed by: (1) pinning Plotly JS to installed Python version, (2) converting all chart x/y/text to plain lists, (3) forcing numeric conversion, (4) setting yaxis rangemode=‘tozero’
8. Automatic benchmark uploads without user consent raised ethical concerns [Benchmark Infrastructure] (2026-02-06) Solution: Implemented interactive prompt (default No) after saved runs, added explicit –upload/–no-upload flags, required relay URL configuration
9. SameLabel@K metric returned 0.0 even though embedding clearly retrieved same cell types [MIHD Spatial Transcriptomics] (2026-02-08) Solution: Fixed by computing SameLabel over all_valid_mask (including niche) while keeping Precision@K on eval_mask (excluding niche to avoid self-match bias)
Learnings Architecture (architecture) When implementing published methods, compare line-by-line with reference code BEFORE claiming alignment - papers omit critical implementation details. Both algorithmic alignment and hyperparameter configuration are critical for deep learning model performance reproduction. Dramatic underperformance (0.05 vs 0.45 ARI) signals implementation bugs, not tuning issues. Deep learning project reproduction key is complete hyperparameter configuration alignment - even with correct code logic, subtle parameter differences (e.g., learning_rate from 0.0005 to 0.001) can cause cliff-like performance drops (ARI from 0.452 to 0.05). Flexible software architecture requires strategy pattern at key nodes: gene_preprocessing’s auto/standard/clip/none design demonstrates how to support multiple input types without breaking interfaces. API flexibility matters more than narrow optimization - E2E training should support both raw and pre-encoded inputs. End-to-end training vs two-stage training affects model performance and efficiency: original STAIG uses end-to-end approach (GCN embeddings trained within), while MIHD initially used two-stage (extract features then train) - core architectural difference. Crowdsourced data pipelines require three-tier architecture (untrusted submit → trusted gateway → protected storage) with validation/audit trails to prevent abuse without granting write access. Ethical data collection requires explicit opt-in consent with default-No prompts; automation scenarios need explicit flags rather than relying on interactivity detection. Domain Knowledge (domain) STAIG fusion demonstrates significant improvement: PCA alone achieves ARI 0.202, but PCA+UNI2+STAIG reaches ARI 0.528 on section 151508 (123% improvement over PCA alone). RM-Ideal (Region Matching with Ideal labels) uses WWL graph kernel: K-iteration neighbor label hashing → Hamming cost matrix → Wasserstein distance → similarity = 1-W. Captures spatial niche structure independent of learned embeddings. Debugging (debugging) Evaluation metrics for niche query require careful mask design: Precision@K uses eval_mask (~niche to avoid self-match), SameLabel@K uses all_valid_mask (including niche for retrieval rate), Spearman uses eval_mask (rank correlation on non-self spots). Tools (tools) Client-side visualization bugs often stem from serialization format mismatches rather than data correctness; explicit list conversion + version pinning prevents silent rendering failures. When loading pretrained models (like UNI2), explicitly pass architecture parameters rather than relying on framework automatic inference, especially for shape-sensitive components like position encoding. Privacy sanitization rules need precise boundary conditions; overly aggressive regex patterns can corrupt legitimate technical metadata sharing surface syntax with PII. Documentation should be generated programmatically from source data (CSV) to prevent manual sync errors and incomplete records. Environment dependency management best practice is incremental repair: directly run target program, install on-demand when encountering ModuleNotFoundError - more efficient than pre-scanning. AI Usage Notes Effective Patterns:
✓ Systematic file system exploration for verification and documentation location ✓ Incremental environment dependency repair (run-fail-fix iteration) ✓ Modular evaluation class design with caching strategy for expensive computations ✓ Two-tier solver implementation with dependency-free fallback (POT → scipy) ✓ Generated comprehensive bilingual documentation and structured experiment logs Limitations:
✗ Failed to proactively compare with reference implementation despite having filesystem access - needed explicit human direction to locate and compare original STAIG code ✗ Did not validate semantic reasonableness of outputs (reported SameLabel@K=0.0 without questioning contradiction with visual evidence) ✗ Designed restrictive E2E architecture assuming raw inputs only, missing flexibility requirements ✗ Token limits in Plan agent prevented complete exploration and design tasks ✗ Did not proactively identify security/access-control and ethical consent implications in public submission pipeline design ✗ Initially suggested binary-encoded plot data causing browser rendering mismatches Next Week Outlook Priority 1: Apply diagnosed hyperparameter corrections to GCN+STAIG fusion and validate expected improvement from ARI 0.05-0.09 to 0.35-0.45. Priority 2: Complete end-to-end GCN training implementation following original STAIG approach. Priority 3: Run ablation studies on the 4 STAIG alignment fixes to quantify individual contribution. Priority 4: Extend RM-Ideal evaluation across multiple embedding methods (pca_uni_staig_fusion, gcn_uni_staig_fusion) and tissue sections for comprehensive comparison. Priority 5: Continue MIHD performance optimization and prepare results for documentation/publication.

Peak Day: unknown — $30.48 / 71.0M tokens
Daily Average: $12.48

Major learning: implementation bugs vs tuning issues require reference code comparison first.
title: Weekly Summary 2026-W06

content:
Daily Report — 2026-02-08 Daily Overview Completed Work: Integrated the RM-Ideal (Wasserstein Weisfeiler-Lehman graph kernel) score calculation into the QueST code, and built a complete specific query evaluation framework for MIHD. Implementation: Migrated the WWL messaging algorithm from pseudocode, created a modular evaluation class with caching capabilities, and built a CLI tool with spatial visualizations using scanpy/matplotlib. Impact: Now it’s possible to use quantitative methods to evaluate the effectiveness of different embedding methods (uni_staig_fusion, pca_uni_staig_fusion) in capturing spatial organizational structures, using real structural similarity metrics for evaluation. The spatial-specific query matching evaluation of RM-Ideal has been implemented and integrated into the QueST and MIHD frameworks, enabling true benchmark comparisons of embedding quality.
Tasks Architecture and Strategy ✅ Implementing RM-Ideal core functions in QueST — Added methods wlw_node_features(), _wasserstein_distance(), rm_score(), and compute_rm_ideal_scores() to src/utils.py, and added compute_rm_scores() method in QueSTTrainer. ✅ Building MIHD’s RM-Ideal evaluation framework — Created utils/rm_ideal.py file and RMIdealEvaluator class, extended spatial_utils.py file, added build_grid_connectivity() and bfs_k_hop() functions, and built scripts/evaluate_rm_ideal.py CLI tool for specific queries. Implementation and Fixes ✅ Running and visualizing evaluations on DLPFC region 151508 — Evaluated the uni_staig_fusion embedding method, generated a three-panel spatial heatmap visualization, and found that the Spearman correlation coefficient between embedding similarity and RM-Ideal true value was r=0.424. ✅ Debugging SameLabel metric in MIHD evaluation — Fixed the issue where SameLabel@K always became 0 due to specific query points being excluded; separated the evaluation mask for Precision@K from all valid masks for SameLabel@K. ✅ Verifying existing RM-Ideal implementation in QueST — Found and verified that the RM-Ideal score function exists in lines 956-1225 of src/utils.py, and confirmed its correctness through 8 verification tests. Problems and Solutions Key Issues 1. SameLabel@K returns 0.0, even though the embedding clearly retrieves the same cell type Solution: The root cause was using ~niche_mask to exclude all specific query points, resulting in no points remaining in the Layer_3 area. This issue was resolved by calculating SameLabel using all_valid_mask while retaining Precision@K on the eval_mask.
Key Insight: Different evaluation metrics require different exclusion masks—retrieval rate needs a complete valid set, while ranking consistency requires excluding the query area itself.
2. The initial plan required implementing RM-Ideal from scratch, but code review showed it was already implemented Solution: Manual verification of the existing implementation instead of duplicating work; conducted comprehensive testing to confirm that the same/subdifferentiated subgraphs returned 1.0/0.0 respectively, as expected.
Key Insight: Always verify the status of the existing code library before implementing—documents may not match the actual code.
General Issues 3. There is a risk of node ID conflicts when combining query subgraphs with shared label dictionaries Solution: Apply an id offset of datacq.n_obs to all reference node IDs before creating the merged label dictionary to avoid key conflicts.
Key Insight: Node indices overlap when merging graphs from different AnnData objects—use deterministic offsets rather than relying on non-overlapping ID spaces.
4. Dependency issue with Wasserstein distance solver—POT library not installed in the quest conda environment Solution: Implemented a two-stage solver: first tried importing ot.emd2() (fast C backend), and if failed, used scipy.optimize.linprog and HiGHS solver (no additional dependencies required, suitable for small subgraphs with around 30-100 nodes).
Key Insight: Always provide an alternative solution without dependencies for optional performance optimizations—scipy is very common, while POT is not.
Differences Between Human and AI Approaches Strategic Level Design of evaluation metric range Role Method Human Humans choose “both” for intra- and cross-partition queries, as well as “specific query (QueST-style)”, instead of full-on full-point comparison AI AI asked specific questions about the range before designing the evaluation framework (intra vs external, specific query vs point-level) Difference Analysis: Humans have a clear architectural vision that matches QueST’s evaluation paradigm; AI correctly identified key design decisions and provided suggestions to confirm these decisions, rather than assuming they were correct.
Error diagnosis method Role Method Human Humans immediately noticed the anomaly: “But why is SameLabel@50 equal to 0?"—Realizing that perfect cell type retrieval (SameLabel=1.0) and zero retrieval (SameLabel=0.0) are contradictory AI AI initially reported the result as 0.0 without questioning, only investigating after humans pointed out the inconsistency Difference Analysis: Humans used domain knowledge to identify impossible results; AI focused on the correctness of implementation without verifying the semantic validity of the output.
Code duplication and verification Role Method Human Humans asked “Why should we search graphcompass?” and clarified that the goal was to integrate it into src/utils.py, rather than using external libraries directly AI AI initially wanted to search the graphcompass package to potentially reuse the existing WWL implementation, rather than implementing it from scratch Difference Analysis: Humans realized that the plan explicitly required implementing the pseudocode, while AI tried to minimize effort by finding existing implementations. Humans always focused on explicit requirements.
Limitations of AI Key Limitations Failed to verify the semantic validity of the output—reported SameLabel@K=0.0, unaware that this contradicted the established goals of the metric and the visual evidence of the heatmap General Limitations Failed to actively verify the status of the existing code library before planning implementation—assuming that code would be written based on documentation, rather than checking the actual files first Initial impulse was to search for external implementations (graphcompass), rather than following the explicit plan requirements, indicating a preference for code reuse over strict adherence to specifications Lessons Learned Key Lessons RM-Ideal (area matching with ideal labels) uses WWL graph kernel: K iterations neighbor tag hashing → Hamming cost matrix (normalized by K+1) → Wasserstein distance → Similarity = 1-W. It can capture spatial-specific structures independently of the learned embeddings. Careful design of masks for specific query evaluation metrics: Precision@K uses eval_mask (excludes specific query points to avoid self-matching), SameLabel@K uses all_valid_mask (includes specific query points to obtain retrieval rate), Spearman uses eval_mask (conducts ranking correlation analysis on non-self-matching points) Practical Experience - Migrating Graph Algorithms Between Frameworks: s squidpy.gr.spatial_neighbors() → sklearn NearestNeighbors + scipy sparse, torch_geometric.k_hop_subgraph() → networkx BFS. Pattern: Convert to the equivalent solution with minimal dependencies. Cache strategy for expensive calculations: RMIdealEvaluator pre-calculates all k-hop subgraphs during initialization (about 8 minutes, suitable for 4384 positions), saves them to .npy cache files, which can be loaded immediately during subsequent runs. Cache keys should include spatial graph parameters (n_neighbors, k_hop). Conversation Summary QueST ✅ Integrated RM-Ideal functions into QueST code 03:13:27.688 | claude_code Implemented plans to add 5 functions/methods: wwl_node_features() for messaging, _wasserstein_distance() with POT/scipy backup, rm_score() for calculating 1-W similarity, compute_rm_ideal_scores() for batch processing, and handling node ID offsets. QueSTTrainer compute_rm_scores() was added to the process. All implementations passed syntax validation. Key design: Hamming normalization via K+1, hash tables between shared subgraphs, results stored in dataq.obs, following the conventions of tutorial 2.
🔍 Understanding QueST’s RM-Ideal usage and query mechanism 05:16:22.872 | claude_code Users requested a comprehensive understanding of how QueST uses RM-Ideal scores. The assistant analyzed the QueST code library, showing that RM-Ideal is a real metric (WWL + OT, using cell type labels), while the query mechanism uses learned GIN encoder + cosine similarity. Discussed the possibility of integrating with MIHD, where embeddings could be queried and compared with other RM-Ideal gold standards.
✅ Verified existing RM-Ideal implementation 04:42:56.919 | claude_code Users requested integrating RM-Ideal scores into QueST, but the assistant found that the complete implementation already existed in lines 956-1225 of src/utils.py. Independent verification tests showed that rm_score returned 1.0 for the same subgraphs, 0.0 for completely different types, and handled edge cases correctly. All 8/8 tests passed.
🔄 Planning RM-Ideal integration methods 02:23:10.030 | claude_code Discussed whether to use the graphcompass library or implement from pseudocode. Users explicitly wanted direct integration into src/utils.py. A detailed implementation plan was written, including 5 steps (wwl_node_features, _wasserstein_distance, rm_score, compute_rm_ideal_scores, trainer integration). Users rejected ExitPlanMode.
MIHD ✅ Implementing RM-Ideal for subquery evaluation in MIHD 05:36:39.735 | claude_code Built a complete evaluation framework, including 3 files: extended spatial_utils.py with build_grid_connectivity() and bfs_k_hop(); created rm_ideal.py with WWL/OT core and RMIdealEvaluator classes; created evaluate_rm_ideal.py CLI with NicheQueryEvaluator, supporting subqueries defined by labels/spatial/indexing. Fixed errors in SameLabel metrics (eval_mask vs all_valid_mask). Successfully evaluated on 151508 Layer_3s, uni_staig_fusion showed Spearman r=0.424, generating 3-panel spatial visualizations.

summary: The RM-Ideal spatial niche matching evaluation algorithm has been implemented and integrated into the QueST and MIHD frameworks. This allows for realistic testing of the quality of embeddings.
