title: Bug Journal 2026-02-07

content:
Daily Report — 2026-02-06 Daily Overview Completed Work: Built an end-to-end benchmark submission process based on the AGENTS.md document. This includes fixing report errors and automatically deploying to GitHub Pages, while also considering user consent prompts. Implementation Methods: Created validation scripts, GitHub Actions workflows (queuing/ingestion/deployment), fixed errors in Plotly rendering, solved these issues by serializing lists and using CDN fixed versions. Added interactive CLI prompts for upload with clear indicators. Impact: Implemented a privacy-protected crowdsourcing benchmark leaderboard, achieved automatic daily publishing, and adopted ethical user consent practices. Implemented a public benchmark submission process with GitHub Pages deployment and interactive user consent flow
Tasks Architecture and Strategy ✅ Design and implement GitHub Pages auto-update process — Built three workflows: accept-submission.yml (queuing), daily-publish.yml (validation/ingestion/build), pages-deploy.yml (deployment); created validation/disinfection scripts based on hash deduplication and privacy protection. ✅ Add interactive upload consent process to the benchmark CLI — Integrated prompts to choose whether to upload (default: no upload), added –upload/–no-upload/–relay-url flags, implemented relay endpoint submission via scripts/submit_result.py, ensuring non-interactive runs require explicit flags. ✅ Fix errors in bar chart rendering in HTML reports — Fixed bar length reversal issue by converting Pandas Series to plain lists, fixed Plotly JS version to match Python package, forced numerical conversion, and set y-axis from zero. Implementation and Fixes ✅ Fix create_trend_chart regression issue and prepare push — Added compatibility backports to prevent AttributeError, cleaned up pycache files in git tracking, updated.gitignore file, prepared list of files for submission. ✅ Create AGENTS.md repository contributor guide — Generated a 329-word contributor guide including project structure, build commands, coding style, testing guidelines, and submission specifications suitable for benchmark repositories. ✅ Remove historical trend charts from reports — Deleted trend chart generation code and references as per user request. Issues and Solutions Key Issues 1. Public users cannot push data to the repository, but need to contribute benchmark results Solution: Designed a three-tier architecture: users submit data to relay endpoints → relays validate and forward data to repository_dispatch → GitHub Actions queue/ingest/publish. Relays hold write tokens, while users have no repository access rights.
Key Insight: Crowdsourcing data pipelines requires an untrusted ingestion path; GitHubActions combined with validation workflows can provide audit trails and abuse controls without granting contributors write access.
2. Bar charts show opposite lengths (bars for higher scores are shorter), even though CSV values are correct Solution: The root cause was mismatching binary encoded chart data (bdata) and CDN versions. Solutions include: (1) fixing Plotly JS to match installed Python version, (2) converting all chart x/y/text to plain Python lists instead of Pandas Series, (3) forcing flops_gflops to be numerical types upon loading, (4) setting yaxis rangemode=‘tozero’.
Key Insight: Binary serialization formats in client plotting libraries may cause silent rendering errors when CDN versions change; explicit list conversion and version fixing ensure visual consistency.
3. Automatically uploading benchmark data without user consent raises ethical issues Solution: Added interactive prompts after saving runs (default: no upload), added explicit –upload/–no-upload flags for automation, required configuring relay URL before prompts, allowing non-interactive runs to skip prompts unless –upload is specified.
Key Insight: Telemetry and data collection require explicit consent and clear disclosures; default no-upload prompts and automated modes with flags respect user preferences.
4. HOSTNAME regex is too broad, causing timestamps and version strings in valid data to be corrupted Solution: Narrowed down HOSTNAME_RE range from matching any token-separated string to requiring TLD suffix (.[A-Za-z]{2,}\b), avoiding misidentification of ‘13.0’ or ‘2026-02-07’ patterns.
Key Insight: Privacy disinfection rules require precise boundaries; overly aggressive regex patterns may damage legitimate technical metadata sharing surface syntax, leading to PII leakage.
General Issues 5. Trend charts were incomplete, leaving outdated method calls that caused AttributeError crashes Solution: Added compatibility backports create_trend_chart(), returning empty graphs so old or outdated call paths could gracefully fail instead of causing AttributeError.
Key Insight: Defensive programming with no-op templates prevents fragile failures during feature removal; compatibility layers allow smooth migration of paths.
Human and AI Approaches Strategic Level Public Contribution Model Design Role Method Human Human questioned if pushing data to GitHub was appropriate (“How would another public user push data to change the website?”) and proposed interactive consent prompts (“Ask users if they want to upload data after completing the benchmark”). AI AI initially proposed GitHub Pages process but realized the problem of untrusted contributors only when human questions arose; then designed a relay-based ingestion process. Difference Analysis: Humans immediately identified security/access control vulnerabilities in the initial design and prioritized user preferences with consent prompts; AI needed explicit prompts to address these issues.
Upload Consent UX Default Values Role Method Human Humans preferred explicit confirmation before uploading, suggesting optional behavior, i.e., “Ask users: Do you want to upload your test results?” AI AI proposed option-based and exit options in decision trees, then implemented default “yes/no” prompts based on human choices. Difference Analysis: Humans prioritized ethical data collection, using optional consent; AI provided neutral options until humans made explicit choices.
Bar Chart Visual Error Diagnosis Role Method Human Humans immediately identified bar length reversal in HTML output and requested fix for “numbers are correct, but reports are incorrect” issues. AI AI initially focused on data flow and CSV validation, ignoring errors occurring in client rendering rather than data correctness. Difference Analysis: Humans located problems by reviewing render outputs, focusing on the display layer; AI performed data validation before considering visualization-specific issues.
AI Limitations Key Limitation - Did not actively identify security/access control issues associated with “public users pushing to GitHub” until explicitly asked Initially suggested using binary encoded chart data, leading to rendering inconsistencies between browsers and CDN Plotly versions Initial hostname cleaning regex was too broad, causing legitimate version strings and timestamps to be corrupted until discovered through manual testing Overall Limitations Incomplete trend charts remained, leading to old method calls requiring compatibility backports, which users discovered after encountering AttributeError errors Learning Outcomes Major Takeaways Crowdsourcing data pipelines require a three-tier architecture (untrusted submissions → trusted gateways → protected data storage) with validation/audit mechanisms to prevent abuse without granting write permissions Ethical data collection requires explicit consent confirmation, defaulting to “no” option; automated scenarios require explicit flags rather than relying on interactivity detection Visual errors in client visualization often stem from serialization format mismatches rather than data correctness issues; explicit list conversion and version fixing can prevent silent rendering failures Privacy disinfection rules require precise boundary conditions; overly aggressive regex patterns may damage legitimate technical metadata sharing surface syntax, leading to PII leakage Practical Gains Feature removal refactoring should include compatibility stubs for gradual migration; no-op backports can prevent fragile AttributeError crashes due to old call paths Dialogue Summary ✅ Repository Contributor Guide and Benchmark Submission Process 04:04:15.843 | codex Created AGENTS.md contributor documentation. Fixed inverted pyramid chart rendering issues caused by list serialization and Plotly version fixes. Removed trend charts. Designed and implemented GitHub Pages auto-update process, including workflow scheduling, strict validation, hash-based deduplication, and privacy cleanup features. Added interactive upload consent prompts in the CLI, defaulting to optional consent with explicit automation flags. Fixed trend chart regression issues and prepared changes for submission.
Token Usage Total: $18.03 — 52 million tokens
summary: The process for submitting public benchmark results has been implemented. This process is deployed using GitHub Pages, and includes an interactive mechanism for user consent.
title: Bug Journal 2026-02-06

content:
Daily Report — 2026-02-05 Daily Overview Completed Work: Implemented an end-to-end multimodal fusion system. Fixed the STAIG implementation to match the original paper using 4 algorithms. Resolved flexibility and environmental issues. Identified the root cause of a 9-fold performance drop due to hyperparameters. Implementation Methods: Created a flexible fusion module. Compared it with the reference implementation to identify differences. Systematically fixed edge discarding/cropping/weight/tau decay issues. Added **kwargs and gene preprocessing parameters to support multiple encoders. Analyzed the original STAIG configuration to identify hyperparameter mismatch issues. Impact: Established a flexible architecture that supports multiple gene encoders. Implemented end-to-end training. Fixed critical errors (NameError, hardcoded preprocessing). It is expected that after alignment and hyperparameter corrections, the performance of GCN+STAIG will improve from ARI=0.05-0.09 to 0.35-0.45. Implemented and debugged the end-to-end GCN+STAIG fusion system. Identified and fixed 6 critical issues (4 algorithm alignments + 2 flexibility issues). Identified the hyperparameter mismatch issue that caused a 9-fold performance drop.
Tasks Architecture and Strategy ✅ Fixed the flexibility issues in end-to-end STAIG fusion — Fixed the NameError caused by the absence of **kwargs in the apply_fusion() function. Added gene preprocessing parameters for 4 modes (automatic/standard/cropping/no). Implemented dual-input mode support (original expression/predefined embeddings). Created test scripts and comprehensive documentation. ✅ Analyzed the poor performance of GCN+STAIG and compared it with the original STAIG — Compared with the reference code in /hpc/group/yizhanglab/zt81/STAIG/, identified 4 key implementation differences. Then identified the hyperparameter mismatch issue that caused ARI to drop from 0.452 to 0.05. ✅ Hyperparameter comparison and diagnosis between MIHD and the original STAIG — Identified key hyperparameter mismatches in the MIHD implementation (learning rate: 0.001 vs 0.0005, number of layers: 2 vs 1, number of iterations: 550 vs 300, weight decay difference by 10 times), causing ARI to drop from 0.452 to 0.05. ✅ Implemented 4 STAIG alignment fixes — Added adaptive edge discard, gene feature cropping, corrected the edge weight calculation direction, and tau decay mechanism in STAIGTrainer.py. Added approximately 150 lines of new code and detailed Chinese documentation. ✅ Implanted an end-to-end GCN + STAIG fusion training system — Created STAIGTrainerE2E.py and EndToEndSTAIGModel that support joint gene visual optimization. Updated run_benchmark.py and benchmark_config.yaml files. 🔄 Designed an end-to-end GCN training method — Explored the GCN implementation in MIHD (currently using random initialization). Compared it with the original STAIG project (GCN embeddings trained end-to-end in STAIG.train()). Created detailed design documents, but the code has not been implemented yet. ✅ Diagnosed UNI2 loading issues — Identified a position encoding resampling error on GPU due to UNI2 position encoding (shape ‘[1, 15, 15, -1]’ invalid). Confirmed that full timmkwargs need to be passed for correct model initialization. Implementation and Fixes ✅ Added GCN encoder support in benchmark test scripts — Expanded the argparse of run_benchmark.py and run_evaluation() functions to support GCN gene encoders with spatial graph construction capabilities. ✅ General environment dependency checking and fixing — Verified the general environment for running GCN + UNI2 + STAIG_fusion. Gradually installed missing packages (dateutil, scipy, pyarrow, packaging, scikit-learn, PIL, psutil, urllib3). Resolved dependency conflicts, achieving 70-80% readiness. ✅ Added CUBLAS deterministic settings — Added os.environ[“CUBLAS_WORKSPACE_CONFIG”] = “:4096:8” in 6 key files (run_benchmark.py, STAIGTrainer.py, ImageEncoder.py, GeneEncoders.py, Fusion.py, datasets.py) to ensure GPU computation determinism. ✅ Fixed the command line parameter parsing for E2E fusion — Updated argparse to support ’none’ gene encoders and ‘staig_fusion_e2e’ fusion strategy, adding parameter validation logic. ✅ Verified GPU usage in benchmark testing framework — Confirmed that when CUDA is available, benchmark tests use CUDA by default. Problems and Solutions Key Problems 1. The apply_fusion() function lacks **kwargs parameters, causing a NameError, which prevents the staig_fusion_e2e branch from executing. Solution: Added **kwargs parameters to the signature of the apply_fusion() function, allowing dynamic passing of parameters (e.g., gene_expr_raw).
Key Insight: Python function signatures must be complete—even if parameters are only used in internal branches, they must be declared in the signature or received via **kwargs.
2. STAIGTrainerE2E assumes that the input is the original gene expression (3000 dimensions), which is incompatible with the output of PCA/MLP/GCN encoders, forcing gene_encoder=‘none’. Solution: Added gene preprocessing parameters (automatic/standard/cropping/no). Automatically selected preprocessing strategy based on input dimensions and source; for original expression, used StandardScaler+Clip, while for predefined embeddings, only Clip was used.
Key Insight: Flexible preprocessing strategies should be determined based on data characteristics (dimensions, sources), rather than being hard-coded. Automatic modes can be intelligently selected based on dimension thresholds (>100). API design should prioritize flexibility over assumptions about data flows—end-to-end training can use pre-encoded features.
3. GCN+STAIG fusion performance is extremely poor (ARI 0.05-0.09) and unstable. In contrast, PCA+STAIG has an ARI of 0.45. Solution: Diagnosed in two stages: (1) Compared with the original STAIG implementation, identified 4 algorithm differences (edge discard, feature cropping, edge weight direction, tau decay); (2) Analyzed the original STAIG configuration, identifying hyperparameter mismatch issues (learning rate, number of layers, number of iterations, weight decay).
Key Insight: Correct code logic does not equal correct implementation—algorithm alignment and hyperparameter configuration are crucial for deep learning model performance. Implementing alignment requires line-by-line comparison with reference code, not just reading the paper. The significant performance drop (0.05 vs 0.45) indicates an implementation error, not a hyperparameter adjustment issue.
4. The ARI of MIHD STAIG implementation is 0.05, much lower than the original STAIG project’s 0.452. Solution: Compared with the original STAIG’s train_img_config.yaml file, identified key hyperparameter inconsistencies. Need to change the learning rate to 0.0005, number of layers to 1, number of iterations to 300, and weight decay to 0.00001.
Key Insight: Correct code logic does not equal correct implementation—hyperparameters are crucial for deep learning model performance and must be fully aligned with the original paper/project configuration to reproduce results.
5. The implementation of adaptive edge discard differs fundamentally from PyG’s standard dropout_adj—custom logic based on edge probability is required. Solution: Implemented a custom adaptiveDropoutAdj function, following the approach in the original STAIG net.py: lines 209-240, where mask = rand() >= edge_prob means edges with higher weights are more likely to be discarded.
Key Insight: The unconventional discard strategy in the original STAIG (discarding similar edges, retaining dissimilar ones) can prevent intra-domain overfitting and enhance cross-domain learning ability.
6. UNI2 causes a position encoding resampling error on GPU without parameters (invalid shape). Solution: Passed the complete timmkwargs (including img_size, patch_size, depth, num_heads, etc.) to allow timm to correctly initialize UNI2 model, avoiding reliance on automatic inference.
Key Insight: When loading a pre-trained model, explicitly passing architecture parameters is more reliable than relying on framework automatic inference, especially for shape-sensitive components like position encoding.
Common Problems 7. Environment dependency issues are widespread due to lack of packages and version conflicts. Solution: Adopt a pragmatic approach: directly run benchmarks and install missing packages as needed when encountering ModuleNotFoundError. Use conda install to install large conda-managed packages. Fix underlying dependencies first when uninstalling errors (e.g., six).
Key Insight: The best practice for environment repairs is to fix problems step by step, rather than doing a comprehensive check at once—this allows for quick identification of truly needed dependencies, avoiding over-installation.
8. argparse rejected ‘–gene_encoder gcn’, even though the GCNGeneEncoder class exists in the codebase. Solution: Add ‘gcn’ to the list of options and implement handling for GCN in run_evaluation(), while also dealing with spatial graph construction.
Key Insight: Implementing completeness requires model classes and CLI integration—lacking CLI support makes features invisible to users.
Human-AI Approaches Strategic Level Root Cause Diagnosis Strategy Role Method Human When performance is poor, humans guide AI to compare with the reference implementation (/hpc/group/yizhanglab/zt81/STAIG/), then directly compare the original STAIG configuration (train_img_config.yaml) to quickly identify hyperparameter differences. AI AI initially tried to analyze performance metrics and adjust hyperparameters without checking the reference code. Then conducted experiments by observing performance differences, and finally analyzed possible reasons, finally checking the configuration file. Difference Analysis: Humans intuitively know that configuration alignment should be done first (empirical approach), because obvious performance drops likely indicate an implementation error rather than tuning issues—comparison with the reference is the fastest way to find the truth. AI adopted an empirical-scientific approach (logical approach). When there is a reference implementation available, the human approach is faster.
E2E STAIG Design Defect Detection and Flexibility Requirements Role Method Human Users immediately question why gene_encoder=‘none’ is required, realizing that it should support any encoder type (PCA/MLP/GCN), and that E2E can handle pre-encoded features. AI AI implemented a narrow design, assuming that E2E meant handling the original gene expression, ignoring flexibility requirements. Difference Analysis: Humans immediately noticed violations of architectural constraints; AI focused on implementation details without questioning restrictive design assumptions.
Environment Dependency Repair Strategy Role Method Human Suggested running commands directly and fixing missing dependencies step by step, focusing on practicality. AI Initially wanted to create a comprehensive environment check script, listing all possible missing packages—this is an ideal preventive approach. Difference Analysis: Humans prefer a progressive, problem-oriented approach to repairs (quick iteration); AI prefers comprehensive pre-checks (solve issues all at once). In practice, the human approach is more efficient because it’s impossible to accurately predict all dependencies in advance.
Understanding End-to-End Training Role Method Human Checked the original STAIG’s example.py implementation and found that GCN embeddings were trained jointly in STAIG.train(). Suggested implementing this method in MIHD. AI Explored various ways to use GCN in MIHD (random initialization, pre-training, loading weights), but initially didn’t realize that the original STAIG implementation should be referenced. Difference Analysis: Humans are good at making analogies across projects and transferring knowledge (knowing what to check the original implementation); AI focuses on technical possibilities within the current project (exploring local solutions).
Implementation Level Package Management Tool Selection Role Method Human Clearly asked to try pip install first, then consider conda install. AI Switched between pip and conda without a clear priority, leading to dependency conflicts. Difference Analysis: Humans have a clear tool priority strategy (based on experience); AI lacks this awareness and needs clear instructions.
Limitations of AI Key Limitations Implemented STAIG without sufficient reference comparison, missing 4 key algorithm details (adaptive dropout, cropping, edge weight direction, tau decay), resulting in a 5-fold performance drop. Failed to design a flexible E2E architecture—made restrictive assumptions that end-to-end training requires raw inputs, not supporting pre-encoded features. Although access to the original STAIG code in the file system was possible, it was still necessary to have explicit user instructions to compare with the reference implementation, and no active checks were made to diagnose performance issues with the original STAIG configuration. The proxy tool encountered token limits and couldn’t fully explore the code and plan tasks—the Plan proxy was interrupted during the design of the end-to-end GCN method. General Limitations Environment dependency resolution isn’t intelligent enough—it requires trial and error when dealing with package conflicts in conda environments, unable to provide the best solution at once. Lack of a consistent package management tool selection priority strategy, randomly switching between pip and conda, leading to dependency conflicts. Lessons Learned Key Lessons When implementing published methods, compare line by line with the reference code before claiming consistency—the paper omits key implementation details such as adaptive dropout mechanisms. Algorithm consistency and hyperparameter configuration are crucial for reproducing deep learning model performance. The key to deep learning project replication is achieving complete consistency in hyperparameter configuration—even if the code logic is correct, subtle parameter differences (e.g., learning_rate changing from 0.0005 to 0.001) can lead to a sharp drop in performance (ARI dropping from 0.452 to 0.05). A flexible software architecture requires using strategy patterns at key nodes: the four-mode design of gene_preprocessing (auto/standard/crop/no) shows how modules can support multiple input types without breaking the interface. API flexibility is more important than narrow optimization—end-to-end training should support raw inputs and pre-encoded inputs, rather than forcing one method. A significant performance drop (0.05-0.09 vs 0.45 ARI) indicates an implementation error, not a hyperparameter adjustment issue—comparison with the reference is faster than blind optimization. Ablation studies reveal which changes are truly important—expected improvements should be gradually verified. The choice between end-to-end training and two-stage training affects model performance and training efficiency: the original STAIG used an end-to-end method (training GCN embeddings internally), while MIHD initially used a two-stage method (extracting features first, then training)—this is the core architectural difference. When loading a pre-trained model (e.g., UNI2), explicitly pass architecture parameters rather than relying on framework automatic reasoning, especially for shape-sensitive components like position encoding—clear parameters can avoid runtime errors. Practical Experience The best practice for managing environment dependencies is to fix problems incrementally, rather than conducting a comprehensive pre-check: directly run the target program and install missing packages as needed when encountering ModuleNotFoundError—this is more efficient than scanning dependencies in advance. Setting the CUBLAS_WORKSPACE_CONFIG environment variable can solve GPU computing determinism issues, but it must be set early (before importing) and comes with a slight performance penalty. Dialogue Summary ✅ STAIG Performance Analysis and Reference Comparison 15:25:19.757 | claude_code Analyzed the poor performance of GCN+STAIG (ARI 0.09-0.37, unstable) compared to the baseline of PCA (0.45+). User-guided comparison with the original STAIG code revealed 5 key implementation differences. Sub-agents identified issues including: (1) incorrect dropout mechanism (uniform vs adaptive), (2) missing feature cropping, (3) incorrect edge weight calculation order, (4) missing tau decay, (5) basic differences in GCN integration (joint training vs independent training). The user approved a plan to fix all 4 actionable issues.
✅ End-to-end STAIG fusion flexibility fixes and hyperparameter diagnosis 22:20:54.558 | claude_code The user provided a detailed repair plan; AI implemented three key fixes: added kwargs parameters to the apply_fusion() function to solve the NameError, introduced gene preprocessing parameters for 4 modes (auto/standard/crop/no), and implemented dual-input mode support (original expression/pre-defined embeddings). All tests passed. Running benchmarks showed that the end-to-end version had an ARI of 0.05, far lower than the original STAIG’s 0.452. Comparing the original STAIG configuration, hyperparameter inconsistency was found to be the root cause of the performance gap (learning rate, number of layers, number of epochs, weight decay all differed).✅ Implementation of STAIG consistency (4 fixes)**
15:46:42.580 | claude_code
All 4 key fixes have been implemented to maintain consistency with the original STAIG: (1) Using probabilistic edge dropping for adaptive dropoutAdj(), (2) Gene feature trimming after StandardScaler, (3) Correction of edge weight calculation (removing the negative sign in softmax_log), (4) Linear tau decay mechanism. The STAIGTrainer.py file has been updated, adding approximately 150 lines of new code, along with comprehensive Chinese documentation and validation scripts. All unit tests and integration tests have passed. Expected performance improvement: ARI increased from 0.09 to 0.35-0.45.
🔄 End-to-end GCN + STAIG fusion implementation
20:54:34.940 | claude_code
A complete end-to-end training system (STAIGTrainerE2E.py) has been implemented, allowing simultaneous gene visual optimization without using a two-stage approach. An integrated solution has been created, combining with the benchmark framework, configuration files, and comprehensive testing. Users identified a critical design flaw: the use of geneEncoder=‘none’ during implementation, instead of supporting flexible inputs (PCA/MLP/GCN). This meeting completed the technical implementation, but it was also shown that the architecture needs to be redesigned.
🔄 General environment check, UNI2 diagnosis, and end-to-end GCN training design
16:29:18.062 | claude_code
AI checked the general environment required for running GCN + UNI2 + STAIG_fusion, gradually installing missing dependencies, reaching 70-80% completion. A diagnostic error in UNI2’s position encoding resampling was identified, confirming the need to pass the full timm_kwargs parameters. According to user requests, CUBLAS deterministic settings were added to 6 key files. It was found that MIHD GCN uses random initialization, while the original STAIG uses end-to-end training. Users requested end-to-end GCN training; AI created detailed design documents but did not implement the code.
✅ Adding GCN encoder support in benchmarks
16:02:07.363 | claude_code
Users attempted to run the benchmarks with ‘–gene_encoder gcn’, but encountered an argparse error. Although GCNGeneEncoder exists in the codebase, the CLI does not support it. ‘gcn’ was added to the valid options list, and GCN processing was implemented in run_evaluation(), including spatial graph construction (50-dimensional embedding, 2 layers, 5 neighbors). A quick fix allowed existing features to be implemented via the CLI.
✅ GPU configuration verification
16:08:33.119 | claude_code
Users requested verification of whether the benchmark defaults to running on a GPU. The logic in config.yaml, benchmark_config.yaml, and run_benchmark.py was checked. The automatic detection function worked properly: using cuda if available, otherwise using cpu. The tasks were quickly verified, and the results were clear.
Token usage Total cost: $8.99 — 26.4 million tokens
summary: The end-to-end GCN+STAIG fusion system has been implemented and debugged. Six critical issues were identified and fixed (including four algorithm alignment problems and two flexibility-related errors). Additionally, a hyperparameter tuning issue was detected, which resulted in a performance degradation of nine times.
title: Bug Journal 2026-02-05

content:
Daily Report — 2026-02-03 Daily Summary Completed Work: Diagnosed and fixed incomplete benchmark test documentation in the MIHD spatial transcriptomics project. Methodology: Re-generated all visualization charts using batch scripts, compiled PDF reports, and reconstructed the experiment_log.md file from a CSV source. Impact: Now has complete documentation for 36 experiments, confirming the excellent performance of the STAIG fusion technique (ARI 0.528). Fixed incomplete benchmark test documentation by re-generating visualization charts and updating experiment logs.
Tasks Implementation and Fixes ✅ Updated experiment_log.md — Reconstructed the complete experiment log from the CSV file, including tables for the first 10 results and detailed metadata. ✅ Re-generated All Visualization Charts — Ran the visualize_all_models.py script to generate 19 PNG comparison charts and metric CSV files for parts 151508, 151673, and 151676. ✅ Compiled PDF Benchmark Report — Generated a 2-page PDF report with all cluster visualizations using the compile_visualization_pdf.py script. ✅ Created Summary Document — Generated the BENCHMARK_RESULTS_SUMMARY.md file, including bilingual overview, key findings, and document organization information. ✅ Diagnosed Incomplete Benchmark Test Documentation — Found that only 14 experiments were recorded in the experiment_log.md, missing some visualization charts, and the best result (ARI 0.528) was not recorded. Issues and Solutions Common Problems 1. Severe Incompleteness of experiment_log.md (36 experiments in CSV, but only 14 entries), missing best results Solution: Re-generated the entire log from the experiment_comparison.csv file as the real source, ensuring all 36 experiments are recorded.
Key Insight: The CSV file should be considered the real source; markdown logs should be generated programmatically to avoid manual synchronization errors.
2. Incomplete visualization charts (only 3 PNG charts for part 151508, no others) Solution: Ran the visualize_all_models.py script on all parts again to generate complete 19 comparison charts and compile a PDF report.
Key Insight: Generation of visualization charts should be automated as part of the benchmark testing process, rather than manually post-processing.
Learnings Main Takeaways The STAIG fusion technique showed significant improvement: ARI was only 0.202 when using PCA alone, while PCA+UNI2+STAIG achieved ARI 0.528 in part 151508 (a 123% increase). Practical Experience Documentation should be generated programmatically from the source data (CSV) to prevent manual synchronization errors and incomplete records. Discussion Summary ✅ Fixed incomplete benchmark test documentation and re-generated visualization charts 03:31:19.374 | claude_code Users found excellent benchmark test results in the CSV file (ARI around 0.45-0.53), but these results were missing in the experiment_log.md and visualization charts. AI diagnosed that there were only 14 markdown entries, while the CSV file contained 36 experimental records. The task successfully re-generated all visualization charts for three parts, compiled a PDF report, created a summary document, and reconstructed the complete experiment_log.md file from the CSV source.

title: Bug Journal 2026-02-03

content:
Daily Report — 2026-02-02 Daily Overview Completed Work: After fixing previous errors, I verified the benchmark test results and document integrity of the MIHD project. Methodology: By searching the file system, I found experimental logs, visualization data, and CSV results. Issues: It was confirmed that the benchmark test outputs existed, but there was a discrepancy between the expected ARI score of approximately 0.45 and the recorded ARI score of approximately 0.37. I investigated the benchmark test results of MIHD and found the missing documentation files.
Tasks Implementation and Fixes • Verifying the documentation of MIHD benchmark test results — Checked whether the corrected benchmark test results were correctly recorded in experiments_log.md, and found the visualization files. Problems and Solutions General Issues 1. The expected ARI score after the previous fix was approximately 0.45, but experiments_log.md showed an ARI of 0.37; it’s unclear where the visualization data is stored. Solution: Found the experiments_log.md containing the benchmark test entries. The visualization data was located in the outputs/benchmark_results/pca_uni2_staig_fusion/visualizations/ directory. The experiment_comparison.csv file with detailed metrics was also found.
Key Findings: Although the results were recorded, there is an unexplained gap between the expected and actual ARI scores. Further investigation is needed.

content:
Daily Report — 2026-02-01 Daily Overview Completed Work: Fixed the significant performance decline of the MIHD model, and aligned the GCN implementation with the original STAIG paper. Implementation Methods: Conducted root cause analysis through version comparison, parameter tracking, and systematic code fixes; conducted parallel proxy exploration to compare across projects. Impact: Improved model performance from ARI 0.09 to 0.28-0.45; clarified the architectural differences between STAIG and MIHD for future development. Debugged and fixed the performance decline of the MIHD spatial transcriptomics model (ARI decreased from 0.4 to 0.13), aligned the STAIG GCN, and identified environment/clustering methods as key factors.
Tasks Architecture and Strategy ✅ Debugged MIHD ARI performance decline (0.4→0.13) — Conducted root cause analysis through parameter analysis, identifying an incorrect gene feature dimension (3000D raw data vs 50D PCA). ✅ Fixed STAIGTrainer parameter handling issues — Corrected num_epochs and edge_weight_method, removed incorrect raw_gene_expr logic; restored default parameters (num_layers=2, tau=30.0, learning_rate=0.001). ✅ Aligned STAIG GCN in MIHD — Added apply_activation_to_last_layer parameter to GCNGeneEncoder, updated edge weight method, and provided CLI support for gcn encoder. ✅ Analyzed GCN implementations across projects — Deployed parallel exploration proxies to systematically compare the differences between STAIG and MIHD GCN architectures across 6 dimensions. Implementation and Fixes ✅ Benchmark GCN + UNI2 + STAIG fusion — Used kmeans clustering for multi-configured comparisons on Section 151676. ✅ Installed dependencies (torch_geometric, scikit-misc) — Set up necessary packages in the current environment for GCN testing. Problems and Solutions Key Issues 1. ARI decreased from 0.4+ to 0.13, unclear why previous configurations worked Solution: Version comparison showed that the code was modified, passing 3000D raw gene expression instead of 50D PCA embeddings to STAIG training; restored use of PCA embeddings.
Key Insight: run_benchmark_core.py contained incorrect “fix” code, passing 3000D raw gene expression when geneencoder=‘pca’, disrupting the workflow using 50D PCA features.
2. Even after fixing, test results showed ARI at 0.13 instead of expected 0.4 Solution: Found that tests were performed using kmeans clustering in the base/AI environment; historically successful cases were achieved using rpy2+mclust clustering in the General environment.
Key Insight: Clustering methods (mclust vs kmeans) have a significant impact on downstream metrics; environment configuration is crucial for reproducibility.
3. Configuration parameters (num_epochs, learning_rate, num_hidden) were not read correctly Solution: Updated STAIGFusionTrainer.init to read all training parameters from staig_alignment_config, not just tau and num_layers.
Key Insight: Incomplete configuration parsing led to incorrect hyperparameters being used despite correct YAML configuration.
4. Edge_weight_method default value was incorrect (‘softmax_log’ vs ‘softmax_neglog’) Solution: Changed default value from ‘softmax_log’ (distance large → weight large) to ‘softmax_neglog’ (distance small → weight large), matching proven high-performance configurations.
Key Insight: The direction of edge weights significantly affects graph-based learning; STAIG uses logarithmic distances, while optimal MIHD configurations use negative logarithmic distances.
5. MIHD GCNGeneEncoder architecture did not match the original STAIG implementation Solution: Added apply_activation_to_last_layer parameter (default True) to align with STAIG design, where activation functions are applied to all layers including the last layer.
Key Insight: Minor architectural differences (last layer activation) can accumulate and lead to differences from the reference implementation.
Human and Artificial Intelligence Approaches Strategic Level Root Cause Diagnosis Strategy Role Method Human Immediately assumed differences in environment and clustering methods based on historical knowledge of the General environment Artificial Intelligence Started debugging at the parameter level, traced back through code versions, and finally identified environmental issues after exhausting code-level fixes Difference Analysis: Humans used domain memory and pattern recognition to assume environmental issues early; AI adopted a systematic bottom-up approach, from code to environment.
Understanding the mismatch between 50D and 3000D dimensions Role Method Human Immediately realized that passing raw gene expression (3000D) instead of PCA embeddings (50D) would disrupt STAIG training Artificial Intelligence Identified dimensional differences through log analysis, but needed to test older versions to confirm this was the root cause Difference Analysis: Humans understood the dimensional requirements of a specific domain; AI verified this through empirical testing.
When to use git history for comparison Role Method Human Requested git diff comparison, selecting the successful version (2a9a4b1) to find changes Artificial Intelligence Initially focused on fixing current code parameters; only checked git history after user guidance, revealing some key default parameter changes Difference Analysis: Humans recognized the importance of version control archaeology earlier; AI needed explicit guidance to use git history to debug regression issues.
Limitations of Artificial Intelligence Key Limitations AI did not immediately realize that kmeans and mclust clustering methods could cause such a large metric difference (ARI 0.13 vs 0.4), requiring the user to point out environmental issues. AI tried to fix a lot of code before checking if tests were running in the wrong environment, wasting debugging time on non-related issues. General Limitations AI did not actively suggest using git blame/log to find the time when regression issues occurred, only using version control after explicit user instructions. AI’s parallel exploration proxies provided a comprehensive architecture comparison, but did not initially consider differences in activation functions as the most important divergence points. Lessons Learned Key Lesson Learned: In machine learning regression debugging, environment configuration (such as conda environments, clustering libraries) may be more important than code-level fixes; ensure the execution environment matches historical successful cases. When debugging performance regression issues, comparing git history with the last known good version is usually faster than directly modifying the current code; version tracing should be an early step in the workflow. Mismatch in input dimensions (50D PCA vs 3000D raw features) can lead to significant performance degradation in graph neural networks; ensure feature dimensions match model expectations. Minor architectural differences (such as last layer activation functions, edge weight directions, etc.) can accumulate and lead to significant differences when replicating research implementations; comprehensive parameter alignment is crucial. The choice of clustering methods (such as mclust vs kmeans) has a significant impact on spatial transcriptomics evaluation metrics; to replicate the results of biological information science papers, it is usually necessary to integrate with R using rpy2. The deployment of parallel proxies for cross-codebase comparison is very effective for systematic architecture analysis; two proxies that simultaneously explored STAIG and MIHD provided a comprehensive comparison result during one round of testing.
Dialogue Summary MIHD Spatial Transcriptomics ✅ Debugged ARI performance regression issue (from 0.4 to 0.13) and fixed STAIG trainer
05:23:52.274 | claude_code
Through systematic debugging, we found that the reason ARI decreased from 0.4+ to 0.13 was due to a code error, passing 3000D raw gene expression data instead of 50D PCA embedding data to STAIG training. We fixed the parameter reading logic, restored correct default values (num_epochs=550, tau=30.0, edge_weight_method=‘softmax_neglog’), and removed the raw_gene_expr logic. Verifying these fixes restored ARI to the range of 0.28-0.45. We found that the best results require a general conda environment with rpy2+mclust, rather than just a basic environment with kmeans.
MIHD and STAIG Architecture Comparison ✅ Aligned GCN implementations and compared benchmark tests
05:24:33.554 | claude_code
We deployed parallel exploration proxies to systematically compare STAIG and MIHD GCN implementations. We identified six key architectural differences: activation function for the last layer, graph construction efficiency (O(n²) vs O(n log n)), edge weight direction, temperature parameters, default number of layers, and pseudo-label generation methods. We achieved alignment by adding the apply_activation_to_last_layer parameter to GCNGeneEncoder, updating edge weight methods, and providing CLI support. Benchmark tests showed that GCN+UNI2+STAIG had an ARI value of 0.1325 in the kmeans environment, while the expected value in the correct environment was 0.4+.

content:
Today’s problem 3432. Count Partitions with Even Sum Difference
Intuition In this question, we need to partition the array into two parts. And the difference between these two parts are even.
Now, both parts must have the same module to 2. That is, they are both even or both odd. So, the sum of the array needs to be even.
Now, if the sum of the array is even, then every partition must have the same module to 2. Otherwise, the sum of the array would be odd.
Therefore, the answer would be 0 when the sum of the array is odd, and $n-1$ when the sum of the array is even.
Approach Return 0 when the sum of the array is odd, and $n-1$ when the sum of the array is even.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(n)$, n is the length of the array.
Space complexity: $O(n)$, n is the length of the array.
Code class Solution: def countPartitions(self, nums: List[int]) -> int: if sum(nums) % 2 == 1: return 0 return len(nums) - 1
summary: 1. Count Partitions with Even Sum Difference

content:
Problem Pop the Balloon
Intuition When popping a balloon, it does not affect the scores of other balloons, and the length of the list decreases continuously. This means we can use dynamic programming to solve this problem.
Approach Consider the following definition: $dp[i][j]$ represents the maximum score that can be obtained by popping all balloons between i and j. In this case, if n is the length of the list, then the answer is $dp[0][n-1]$.
Now let’s consider how to calculate $dp[i][j]$. As mentioned in Intuition, the number of balloons decreases continuously, so we can first calculate the results for shorter intervals, and then gradually extend the intervals to $0 \to n-1$.
Our solution is as follows:
First, iterate through the interval lengths.
Next, split the interval $i \to j$ into two sub-intervals: $i \to k-1$ and $k+1 \to j$.
Then, add the scores of the sub-intervals $i \to k-1$ and $k+1 \to j$, plus the score of popping the kth balloon, to get the total score of the interval $i \to j$.
Since the balloons in sub-intervals $i \to k-1$ and $k+1 \to j$ have already been popped, the increment of the score for popping the kth balloon is $nums[k] \times nums[i-1] \times nums[j+1]$.
Finally, note that the boundaries of the sub-intervals need to be handled carefully: since the current balloon being popped could be at the boundary of the interval $i \to j$, we need to iterate over k within the closed interval $[i, j]$.
The final answer is thus $dp[0][n-1]$.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N^3)$, where N is the length of the list. Space complexity: $O(N^2)$, where N is the length of the list. Code class Solution: def maxCoins(self, nums: List[int]) -> int: n = len(nums) nums = nums + [1] dp = [[0 for _ in range(n+1)] for _ in range(n+1)] for i in range(n): dp[i][i] = nums[i] * nums[i-1] * nums[i+1] for i in range(1, n): for l in range(n): if i + l == n: break r = i + l for k in range(l, r+1): dp[l][r] = max(dp[l][r], dp[l][k-1] + dp[k+1][r] + nums[k] * nums[l-1] * nums[r + 1]) # print(dp) return dp[0][n-1]
summary: Detailed explanation of dynamic programming for solving problems within a certain range, as well as how to derive the solution approach.
title: P312 Explode balloons

content:
Today’s Problem 2894. Difference between Divisible and Non-divisible Sums
Solution Approach Given an integer n and a divisor m, we need to calculate the difference between:
The sum of all numbers from 1 to n that are not divisible by m. The sum of all numbers from 1 to n that are divisible by m. We then divide the numbers from 1 to n into two groups based on whether they are divisible by m, and calculate the sum of each group before returning the difference.
Solution Methods There are two ways to solve this problem:
Method 1: Formula-based Approach Use the formula for the sum of the first n natural numbers: n * (n + 1) // 2 to find the total sum. Calculate the number of numbers from 1 to n that are divisible by [[[HYMTPH_19]]: k = n // m. The numbers that are divisible are: m, 2m, ..., km, and their sum is [[[HYMTPH_22]]. Subtract the sum of the divisible numbers from the total sum to get the sum of the non-divisible numbers, and then find the difference. Method 2: Brute-force Enumeration Iterate through all numbers from 1 to n. If a number is divisible by m, add it to [[[HYMTPH_26]]. Otherwise, add it to [[[HYMTPH_27]]. Return the difference, which is num1 - num2. Complexity Analysis Time complexity:
Method 1: $O(1)$ (using formulas, constant time) Method 2: $O(n)$ (linear iteration) Space complexity:
Both methods are $O(1)$ (only using a few variables) Code class Solution: def differenceOfSums(self, n: int, m: int) -> int: # Method 1: Formula-Based total_sum = n * (n + 1) // 2 k = n // m divisible_sum = m * (k * (k + 1) // 2) return total_sum - divisible_sum class Solution: def differenceOfSums(self, n: int, m: int) -> int: # Method 2: Brute-Force Iteration num1, num2 = 0, 0 for i in range(1, n + 1): if i % m == 0: num2 += i else: num1 += i return num1 - num2 Advertisement For more solutions, please visit My Blog
summary: 遍历数学公式！

content:
Today’s Problem 2131. The longest palindrome string obtained by connecting two-letter words
Idea There are two ways to form a palindrome string:
A certain string exists in the list as its reverse string. A certain string is itself a palindrome. Among them, strings that are palindromes themselves can only be placed right in the middle of the entire palindrome string.
Solution Method Therefore, we can use a hash table to solve this problem. Note: First, check the first case, and then check the second case. If the reverse string of a certain string exists in the list, add both of these strings to the result.
Then, check whether the string itself is a palindrome.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N)$, where N is the length of words.
Space complexity: $O(N)$, where N is the length of words.
Code class Solution: def longestPalindrome(self, words: List[str]) -> int: cnt = Counter(words) ans = 0 sp = 0 for word, t in cnt.items(): # print(word, t) if word[0] == word[1]: ans += (t - t % 2) sp |= (t % 2) else: ans += min(t, cnt[word[::-1]]) return (ans + sp) * 2 Extended Knowledge For more solutions, please visit my blog

content:
Today’s Problem https://leetcode.com/problems/zero-array-transformation-iii/
Idea This problem requires us to find the number of “useful” intervals, in other words, the minimum number of intervals needed to convert the array into a zero array.
The key to solving this problem is to change our perspective: if we consider each element in the array separately, we can use a greedy algorithm to solve it.
The reasoning is simple—to convert the entire array into a zero array, each element must be reduced to zero.
For each element, the optimal strategy is to choose an interval that covers that element and has its right endpoint as far to the right as possible. This is because the farther the right endpoint, the more elements can be covered in the future. Therefore, choosing an interval with a shorter right endpoint is always a better option.
Therefore, we need a data structure to dynamically maintain the “farthest right endpoint”. This data structure needs to support dynamic insertion and deletion of the maximum value. A heap (priority queue) is the best choice for storing the “right endpoints”.
Algorithm Based on the above analysis, we can construct the following algorithm:
First, sort the query array by the left endpoint of each query. This allows us to quickly find all queries whose left endpoint is less than or equal to the current index. Create a difference array to handle interval superposition operations; a max-heap to store the right endpoints of the queries; and an index to record the current position while traversing the queries. Traverse the numerical array: for each element, first insert all queries whose left endpoint is less than or equal to the current index into the heap. All elements in the heap can potentially reduce the value of the current element. Then, greedily select available queries from the heap to perform interval reduction operations. (Ensure the validity of the operation by verifying that the right endpoint at the top of the heap is greater than or equal to the current index. This ensures that the left endpoint is less than or equal to the current index, and the right endpoint is greater than or equal to the current index, thus ensuring that each operation is valid.) Finally, if the current element is still greater than 0, return -1; otherwise, return the number of remaining elements in the heap, which is the number of unused queries. Complexity Time complexity: $O(N \times log(m))$
Space complexity: $O(N + M)$
Code class Solution: def maxRemoval(self, nums: List[int], queries: List[List[int]]) -> int: queries.sort(key = lambda x: x[0]) diff = [0] * (len(nums) + 1) h = [] idx, now = 0,0 for i in range(len(nums)): while idx < len(queries) and queries[idx][0] <= i: heappush(h, -queries[idx][1]) idx += 1 now += diff[i] while h and now < nums[i] and -h[0] >= i: now += 1 diff[-heappop(h) + 1] -= 1 if now < nums[i]: return -1 return len(h) For more solutions, please visit my blog
summary: Greedy algorithm, analyzing each element individually!
title: LeetCode Daily 2025-05-22

content:
Today’s Problem https://leetcode.com/problems/construct-smallest-number-from-di-string/
Approach This problem requires us to find a number sequence under certain constraints.
First, it can be observed that the length of the sequence N is very small. Therefore, we can use Depth-First Search (DFS) to enumerate all possible cases and obtain the result.
Another approach is to use a stack. When the sequence increases, the optimal strategy to construct a valid sequence is to fill in the smallest available number at each step. When the sequence decreases, since we still want the overall sequence to be as small as possible, we need to fill in the smallest available number, which must be greater than the number at the subsequent position. Thus, the smallest number that can be filled at the current position is equal to the current index plus the number of consecutive ‘D’s that follow. Based on this, we can use a stack to temporarily store the numbers being processed. Whenever we encounter an ‘I’ or reach the end of the sequence, we can append the elements in the stack in reverse order to the result array.
Method For Solution 1 (DFS), we only need to enumerate all possible solutions and return the first one that meets the conditions.
For Solution 2 (stack), when we encounter an ‘I’, we add the element from idx + 1 to the result array. Then, pop all elements from the stack in reverse order and add them to the result array. Finally, empty the stack. When we encounter a ‘D’, push the element from idx + 1 onto the stack for later processing.
Tips DFS: Since the problem requires finding the smallest valid sequence, the first non-None result is the answer. This means that once a valid result is found, we can return it immediately without continuing the search. Stack: Here, I specifically added a ‘D’ at the end of the sequence. Intuitively, the last element of the sequence is the largest number in the entire sequence. To include it in the result array, we need to perform an ‘D’ operation. In this way, if the last character of the original sequence is ‘I’, we can directly add the largest number to the end of the result array, which is equivalent to performing an ‘D’ operation. Because the ‘I’ operation will empty the stack, leaving only one element in the stack, appending it in either order results in the same outcome. If the last character of the original sequence is ‘D’, then the largest number should be placed at the beginning of the consecutive ‘D’s. In this case, we need to perform an ‘D’ operation to place it in the correct position. This tip makes the code more concise and elegant, but it sacrifices readability. It is not recommended for actual use. Complexity DFS solution: Time complexity is $O(N!)$, where N is the length of the sequence.
Stack solution: Time complexity is $O(N)$, where N is the length of the sequence.
DFS solution: Space complexity is $O(N)$, where N is the length of the sequence.
Stack solution: Space complexity is $O(N)$, where N is the length of the sequence.
Code class Solution: def smallestNumber(self, pattern: str) -> str: arr = [] n = len(pattern) + 2 def dfs(arr): if len(arr) == n - 1: return arr now = len(arr) - 1 res = None if pattern[now] == 'I': for i in range(arr[now] + 1, n): if i not in arr: res = dfs(arr + [i]) if res is not None: return res else: for i in range(1, arr[now]): if i not in arr: res = dfs(arr + [i]) if res is not None: return res return res for i in range(1, n): ans = dfs([i]) if ans is not None: return ''.join(map(str, ans)) # return ans return None ```python3 [dfs] class Solution: def smallestNumber(self, pattern: str) -> str: arr = [] n = len(pattern) + 2 def dfs(arr): if len(arr) == n - 1: return arr now = len(arr) - 1 res = None if pattern[now] == ‘I’: for i in range(arr[now] + 1, n): if i not in arr: res = dfs(arr + [i]) if res is not None: return res else: for i in range(1, arr[now]): if i not in arr: res = dfs(arr + [i]) if res is not None: return res

content:
Today’s Problem https://leetcode.com/problems/zero-array-transformation-i/
Intuition The problem means that for each query, reduce the elements in the interval (l, r) by one. For batch modifications within the interval, we can consider using a difference array. Note that the problem states “selecting a subset of indices”, which means we don’t necessarily need to reduce all indices in the interval by one. Therefore, since we only care whether the final array is a zero array, and considering the meaning of “subset” in the problem, we can determine whether each element in the final array is less than or equal to zero, rather than strictly equal to zero.
Approach For each query, increment the value at position l in the difference array by one, and decrement it at position r + 1 by one. Then, when calculating the final result, find the prefix sum of the difference array to get the total change at each position. Finally, add the prefix sum of the difference array to the original array, and check if the value at each position is less than or equal to zero to get the answer.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N + M)$, where N is the length of the original array and M is the length of the query array.
Space complexity: $O(N)$.

content:
Today’s Problem https://leetcode.com/problems/painting-a-grid-with-three-different-colors/
Idea In this problem, we notice that $m$ is much smaller than $n$. Since $m \le 5$ and $n \le 1000$, we can enumerate all valid coloring patterns for each column, and then generalize this to the entire matrix. During this process, we discover a key property: no matter how a column is colored, its influence only affects the adjacent column, not the columns further away. This property makes dynamic programming possible.
Method Based on the above analysis, our solution steps are as follows:
First, find all valid coloring patterns for each column. This is done by using depth-first search to enumerate all possible combinations. Next, determine which two patterns can appear in adjacent columns. We check each pair of patterns to see if they satisfy the constraints for adjacent columns. Then, use dynamic programming to move from one column to the next. The DP transition equation is: $DP[col][case_x] = \sum DP[col-1][case_y]$, where $\forall \text{case_x}$ and $\text{case_y}$ can appear in adjacent columns. Finally, sum up the values of all cases in the last column of DP to get the answer. Complexity Time complexity: $O(3^{2m} \times n)$ Space complexity: $O(3^{2m})$ Code class Solution: def colorTheGrid(self, m: int, n: int) -> int: pat = [] col = [0, 1, 2] def dfs(x, s): if x == m: pat.append(s) return for i in col: if x == 0 or s[x - 1] != i: dfs(x + 1, s + [i]) dfs(0, []) # till this step, we find all valid patterns for a column and store it in the pattern list. l = len(pat) valid = [[True for _ in range(l)] for _ in range(l)] for i in range(l): for j in range(i + 1, l): for k in range(m): if pat[i][k] == pat[j][k]: valid[i][j] = False break # till this step, we find all the pattern pairs that is valid. dp = [[0 for _ in range(l)] for _ in range(n)] mod = 1_000_000_007 for i in range(l): dp[0][i] = 1 # for column 0, each pattern is valid. for i in range(1, n): for x in range(l): for y in range(x + 1, l): if valid[x][y]: dp[i][x] = (dp[i][x] + dp[i-1][y]) % mod dp[i][y] = (dp[i][y] + dp[i-1][x]) % mod # we elaborate to the next column according to the DP formula. ans = 0 for i in range(l): ans = (ans + dp[-1][i]) % mod # finally, we add up all the answers. return ans
summary: First, handle DFS. Then, deal with DP!

Today’s Problem https://leetcode.com/problems/check-if-grid-can-be-cut-into-sections
Intuition This question asks about merging sections. In this case, if we smash it into 1D array, it just means “Is there more than two gaps inside the section?”
Approach Therefore, we can sort the list, and then iterate the whole list and see whether there is a gap between the section we already iterated and the new section. If there is, then add 1, else, merge this new section to our old section. The thing is that you need to do it twice.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N)$, N is the length of rectangles. Space complexity: $O(1)$. Code class Solution: def checkValidCuts(self, n: int, rectangles: List[List[int]]) -> bool: N = len(rectangles) def get_res(a,b): rectangles.sort(key = lambda x: (x[a], x[b])) gapCnt,maxPos,l = 0,1,0 while(l < N): while(l < N and rectangles[l][a] < maxPos): maxPos = max(maxPos, rectangles[l][b]) l += 1 if l == N: break else: gapCnt += 1 maxPos = rectangles[l][b] # print(a,l) if gapCnt > 1: return True return False return get_res(0,2) or get_res(1,3)
summary: 1. Check if Grid can be Cut into Sections

content:
Intuition In this question, we find that if we flip a coin twice, then it is the same as flipping it zero times. Moreover, the only three ways to flip a coin is: Flip this coin Flip the coin before Flip the coin before this coin This means that if we pass this coin, we can no longer flip this coin. Approach In this case, we can iterate from front to end and flip every coin that is 0, and check whether the whole array is 1 at the end. Why is this method correct then? First we know that we cannot flip a coin after we pass this coin.
This means that we must flip this coin. If we do not flip this coin, then this coin will remain 0, which does not satisfy the quetion. Therefore, this step is required, missing this step will not give us the array we need.
We can also prove that this will lead us to the result for every array that can achieve this step. Because you have to flip this coin no matter what operation you did.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N)$, N is the length of the array. Space complexity: $O(1)$. Code class Solution: def minOperations(self, nums: List[int]) -> int: cnt = 0 for i in range(len(nums) - 2): if nums[i] == 0: cnt += 1 nums[i] = 1 nums[i + 1] = 1 - nums[i + 1] nums[i + 2] = 1 - nums[i + 2] if nums[-1] == 1 and nums[-2] == 1: return cnt return -1
summary: 1. Minimum Operations to Make Binary Array Elements Equal to One I

content:
Today’s Problem https://leetcode.com/problems/find-the-punishment-number-of-an-integer/description/
Solution Approach Note that for numbers less than $1000^2$, the maximum number of partitioning schemes is $2^5 = 32$. This means that if we use DFS to determine whether to split a number at a certain position, each number can be checked in at most 32 scenarios.
Since $N \leq 1000$, we can solve this problem using brute-force methods.
Solution Method Use DFS to determine whether a number can be considered a “penalty number”.
Complexity Analysis Time complexity: $O(N\times 2^{log(N)})$, where N has the same meaning as described in the problem statement. Space complexity: $O(1)$. Code class Solution: def punishmentNumber(self, n: int) -> int: def check(x, now, s, nows, cnt): if now == 0: return (s + nows) == x if s > x: return False flag = check(x, now // 10, s, nows + now % 10 * (10 ** cnt), cnt + 1) if flag: return True flag |= check(x, now // 10, s + nows, now % 10, 1) return flag ans = 0 for i in range(n + 1): if check(i, i*i, 0, 0, 0): ans += i * i return ans class Solution: def punishmentNumber(self, n: int) -> int: punishmentNumbers = [0, 1, 9, 10, 36, 45, 55, 82, 91, 99, 100, 235, 297, 369, 370, 379, 414, 657, 675, 703, 756, 792, 909, 918, 945, 964, 990, 991, 999, 1000] ans = 0 for x in punishmentNumbers: if x > n: return ans ans += x * x return ans Results of the ordinary solution: Results of the fastest solution:

content:
Today’s Problem https://leetcode.com/problems/minimum-operations-to-exceed-threshold-value-ii/description
Solution Approach Use a heap to simulate the operations required by the problem.
Solution Steps Use the heap to extract the two smallest numbers from the list. Place $2 \times \min(x, y) + \max(x, y)$ back into the heap. If all extracted values are greater than or equal to k, then the operation is completed. Complexity Analysis Time complexity: $O(N \times \log(N))$, where N is the length of the sequence. Space complexity: $O(1)$, as the heap is constructed in-place using heapify, without any additional storage space needed. Code class Solution: def minOperations(self, nums: List[int], k: int) -> int: ans = 0 heapify(nums) x = heappop(nums) while(len(nums) > 0 and x < k): y = heappop(nums) nx = min(x, y) * 2 + max(x, y) heappush(nums, nx) x = heappop(nums) ans += 1 return ans
summary: 1. The minimum number of operations that exceed the threshold II

Idea This problem requires removing all occurrences of the substring “part” from the string “s”. Therefore, we can continuously check whether “s” contains “part”, and if it does, remove it. This process continues until “part” no longer appears in “s”.
Methods Method 1: Use a stack to achieve this. Traverse the string and push characters onto the stack in order. When several characters at the top of the stack completely match “part”, pop those characters from the stack.
Method 2: Directly use Python’s built-in functions to find and remove “part” from the original string s.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N\times M)$, where N is the length of s, and M is the length of “part”. Space complexity: $O(1)$ Code class Solution: def removeOccurrences(self, s: str, part: str) -> str: st = [] N = len(part) for ch in s: st.append(ch) if len(st) >= N: flag = True for i in range(1, N + 1): if st[-i] != part[-i]: print(st[-i], part[-i]) flag = False break if flag: for i in range(N): st.pop() return ''.join(st) Real Python class Solution: def removeOccurrences(self, s: str, part: str) -> str: while part in s: s = s.replace(part,"",1) return s

content:
Today’s Problem https://leetcode.com/problems/count-number-of-bad-pairs/description/
Solution Approach The key to solving this problem is to identify pairs of elements where the difference in indices is equal to the difference in values.
If we subtract the index difference from the distance between two elements, any pair with an equal result is considered a “good pair”.
That is: $j - i = nums[j] - nums[i] \Longrightarrow nums[j] - j = nums[i] - i$.
This simplifies the problem to counting the number of pairs $(i, j)$ in the array such that the transformed values are the same (i.e., they are good pairs).
Finally, by subtracting the number of good pairs from the total number of pairs, we can determine the number of bad pairs.
Solution Steps Calculate the total number of pairs, which is $N \times (N - 1) / 2$, where N is the length of the array. Subtract the index i from each element nums[i]. Count the number of pairs whose transformed values are the same (i.e., good pairs). Subtract the number of good pairs from the total number of pairs to get the number of bad pairs. Complexity Analysis Time complexity: $O(N)$, where N is the length of the array. Space complexity: $O(N)$, where N is the length of the array. Code [[[HYMTPH_0]]! image.png
summary: 1. Count the number of bad pairs

content:
Today’s Problem https://leetcode.com/problems/tuple-with-same-product/description/
Idea The core of this problem is to find tuples with the same product. Suppose there are n pairs of numbers with the same product. Then, the answer is $(^2_n) \times 4$. This is because we can choose any two pairs from the array to form a tuple that satisfies the condition.
So, how do we prove that these two pairs must be different?
Since all elements in the original array are unique, there are no duplicate numbers in the array. Therefore, if $a \times b = c \times d$, and $a \ne c$, then it follows that $b \ne d$. In other words, the two pairs must be different.
Method Therefore, we simply need to iterate through the entire array and count the number of pairs with the same product.
Tips Among the four dictionary types: [[[HYMTPH_1]], [[[HYMTPH_2]], [[[HYMTPH_3]], and {}, dict has the fastest execution time.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N ^ 2)$, where N is the length of the array. Space complexity: $O(N)$, as we need to store the entire array. Code class Solution: def tupleSameProduct(self, nums: List[int]) -> int: nums.sort() cnt = dict() N = len(nums) for i in range(N): for j in range(i + 1, N): tmp = nums[i] * nums[j] if tmp not in cnt: cnt[tmp] = 1 else: cnt[tmp] += 1 ans = 0 for v in cnt.values(): ans += 4 * (v) * (v - 1) return ans
summary: 1. Tuples with the same product

content:
Today’s Problem https://leetcode.com/problems/divide-nodes-into-the-maximum-number-of-groups/description/
Solution Approach The key to this problem lies in the condition $|y - x| = 1$. With each step, the parity of the nodes changes. Therefore, when we encounter a situation where the parity of two nodes is the same (e.g., a cycle with three nodes and three edges), we should return $-1$. Otherwise, we simply need to enumerate the starting points of the graph and find the optimal starting node.
Thus, the solution approach is as follows:
Use DFS to determine if there are any parity conflicts in the graph. Use BFS to find the optimal starting point. Question: Why use BFS to find the starting point? Because we need to add the nodes that are connected to the current node into the next group.
Algorithm Steps Use DFS to determine if there are any parity conflicts in the graph. Use BFS to find the optimal starting point. Complexity Analysis Time complexity: $O(N^2)$ Space complexity: $O(N^2)$ Code class Solution: def magnificentSets(self, n: int, edges: List[List[int]]) -> int: vis = [0] * (n + 1) bvis = [0] * (n + 1) e = [[] for _ in range(n + 1)] for x,y in edges: e[x].append(y) e[y].append(x) ans = 0 clock = 0 def bfs(x): nonlocal clock clock += 1 bvis[x] = clock q = deque([(x,1)]) res = 1 while(len(q) > 0): now, dis = q.popleft() res = max(res, dis) for to in e[now]: if bvis[to] == clock: continue bvis[to] = clock q.append((to, dis + 1)) return res cur = 0 def dfs(x): nonlocal cur cur = max(cur, bfs(x)) # print(cur) tmp = 0 for to in e[x]: if vis[to] == 0: if vis[x] == 0: print("Warning!", x) vis[to] = -vis[x] tmp += dfs(to) else: if vis[to] != -vis[x]: return -1 return tmp for i in range(1, n + 1): if vis[i] == 0: cur = 0 vis[i] = 1 if dfs(i) < 0: return -1 # print("out: ", cur) ans += cur # print(bfs(5)) return ans
summary: 1. Divide the nodes into as many groups as possible.

content:
Today’s Problem https://leetcode.com/problems/making-a-large-island/description/
Idea This problem requires converting a 0 to a 1, in order to find the largest connected region. If we can calculate the size of each connected block of 0s in all four directions beforehand, then we can simply add up these sizes to get the answer.
Method First, calculate the size of each connected block and assign a label to each one, so that we don’t accumulate the same connected block multiple times.
Then, iterate through all the 0s and calculate the sum of their connected block sizes in all four directions after flipping them.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N^2)$ Space complexity: $O(N^2)$ Code class Solution: def largestIsland(self, grid: List[List[int]]) -> int: islandCount = [0,0] dx = [0,0,1,-1] dy = [1,-1,0,0] n = len(grid) m = len(grid[0]) def dfs(x, y, cnt): grid[x][y] = cnt islandCount[cnt] += 1 for i in range(4): nx = x + dx[i] ny = y + dy[i] if nx < 0 or ny < 0 or nx >= n or ny >= m or grid[nx][ny] != 1: continue dfs(nx, ny, cnt) cnt = 1 for i in range(len(grid)): for j in range(len(grid[0])): if grid[i][j] == 1: cnt += 1 islandCount.append(0) dfs(i, j, cnt) ans = max(islandCount) for i in range(len(grid)): for j in range(len(grid[0])): if grid[i][j] == 0: tmp = 1 vis = set([]) for k in range(4): nx = i + dx[k] ny = j + dy[k] if nx < 0 or ny < 0 or nx >= n or ny >= m or grid[nx][ny] in vis: continue tmp += islandCount[grid[nx][ny]] vis.add(grid[nx][ny]) ans = max(ans, tmp) return ans
summary: Manufacturing the largest island possible
title: LeetCode Daily 2025-01-31

content:
Today’s problem https://leetcode.com/problems/redundant-connection/description/
Intuition Note that in a ring, every node have more than one index. Therefore, if we delete all the index that has one index, the remaining points will be a ring.
Approach Note that the label starts from 1 and ends at n, you would like to decrease index by one.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N)$
Space complexity: $O(N)$
Code class Solution: def findRedundantConnection(self, edges: List[List[int]]) -> List[int]: N = len(edges) du = [0] * (N) E = [[] for _ in range(N)] for x,y in edges: x -= 1 y -= 1 du[x] += 1 du[y] += 1 E[x].append(y) E[y].append(x) q = deque([]) for i in range(N): if du[i] == 1: q.append(i) # print(du) while(len(q) > 0): x = q.popleft() du[x] = 0 for to in E[x]: if du[to] > 0: du[to] -= 1 if du[to] == 1: q.append(to) # print(du) loop = set([]) for i in range(N): if du[i] > 0: loop.add(i) for i in range(N - 1, -1, -1): x,y = edges[i] if x - 1 in loop and y - 1 in loop: return [x, y] return None
summary: 1. Redundant Connection
title: LeetCode Daily 2025-01-29

content:
Today’s Problem https://leetcode.com/problems/maximum-number-of-fish-in-a-grid/description/
Idea Find the size of the connected blocks.
Method Traverse the grid, accumulate the number of fish in each connected block, and finally return the maximum number of fish in the largest connected block.
Complexity Time complexity: $O(N\times M)$, with each cell being visited exactly once. Space complexity: $O(1)$, assuming no additional space is required for storing the original grid. Code class Solution: def findMaxFish(self, grid: List[List[int]]) -> int: ans = 0 dx = [0,0,1,-1] dy = [1,-1,0,0] for i in range(len(grid)): for j in range(len(grid[0])): if grid[i][j] == 0: continue tmp = grid[i][j] grid[i][j] = 0 q = deque([(i,j)]) while len(q) > 0: x,y = q.popleft() for k in range(4): nx = x + dx[k] ny = y + dy[k] if nx < 0 or ny < 0 or nx >= len(grid) or ny >= len(grid[0]) or grid[nx][ny] == 0: continue tmp += grid[nx][ny] grid[nx][ny] = 0 # print(i,j,nx, ny, tmp) q.append((nx, ny)) ans = max(ans, tmp) print(grid) return ans
summary: 1. The maximum number of fish that can be in a grid
